name: DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS

topics.title: Experimentation Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Experimentation Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Experimentation Strategy** is a systematic, pre-planned methodology that defines *how* an experimenter will vary controllable input variables (factors), account for nuisance variables, measure the output (response), and statistically infer cause-and-effect relationships. It is the blueprint that converts an industrial or scientific problem into a structured sequence of trials whose results can be analyzed mathematically.

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, an experimentation strategy is the formal framework that dictates:
- **Which factors** to vary,
- **How many levels** of each factor to test,
- **In what order** the trials are run,
- **How many times** each trial is repeated (replication),
- **How the trials are grouped** (blocking), and
- **How the data will be analyzed** to draw valid conclusions.

The four classical strategies identified by **Montgomery (2013, Introduction to Linear Algebra with Applications, John Wiley & Sons)** are:
1. **Best-Guess Approach** (intuition/trial-and-error)
2. **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT) Approach**
3. **Factorial Design Approach**
4. **Statistical / Sequential Strategy** (e.g., steepest ascent, response surface methodology)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Master Chef Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are a master chef creating a new signature dish. You have **4 ingredients** to optimize: *salt (low / high)*, *spice (mild / medium / hot)*, *cooking time (short / medium / long)*, and *oil type (olive / vegetable / butter)*. Your goal is the **"taste score"** (response) — a number from 0 to 10 given by judges.

| Strategy the Chef Might Use | Real-World Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Keep tasting and adjusting until the dish "feels right" | **Best-Guess Approach** |
| Fix 3 ingredients, vary salt, taste; then fix salt, vary spice, etc. | **One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT)** |
| Bake 81 dishes in a single day covering every possible combination | **Full Factorial Design** |
| Tweak recipe based on yesterday's judging feedback to improve today | **Sequential / Steepest Ascent** |

The key insight: **strategy selection determines efficiency, accuracy, and the ability to detect hidden interactions between variables.** A novice chef who varies one ingredient at a time will never discover that *salt and spice together* produce a magical synergy (the **interaction effect**) — a discovery only factorial strategies can reveal.

### 1.3 Why is a Strategy Needed? (The Problem Without One)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** In manufacturing R&D, a poorly planned experiment wastes an average of **30–70% of resources** (materials, machine hours, labor) and may yield **misleading conclusions** that lead to costly process failures.

A good experimentation strategy must:
- **Minimize the number of runs** (cost efficiency),
- **Maximize the information obtained** (information efficiency),
- **Allow estimation of uncertainty** (statistical validity),
- **Detect interactions** between factors (completeness),
- **Guard against hidden sources of bias** (objectivity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Experimentation Strategy

#### **Pillar 1 — Best-Guess Approach**
- The experimenter relies on **experience, intuition, and prior knowledge**.
- Trial runs are made one after another; if results are favorable, the experimenter stops.
- **Advantage:** No mathematical expertise required; quick for simple systems.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot guarantee optimum; no measure of uncertainty; completely fails on novel problems.

#### **Pillar 2 — One-Factor-At-A-Time (OFAT) Approach**
- The experimenter **fixes all factors at a baseline** and varies **only one factor** at a time, observing its effect.
- After all factors have been individually optimized, the best levels are combined.
- **Advantage:** Easy to implement; results are easy to interpret.
- **Disadvantage:** **Cannot detect interactions** between factors; assumes no interaction (which is rarely true in engineering); gives a *local* optimum, not a *global* one; highly inefficient in terms of experimental runs.

#### **Pillar 3 — Factorial Design Approach**
- All factors are **varied simultaneously** at predetermined levels.
- Every possible combination of factor levels is tested (in a full factorial) or a carefully chosen subset (in a fractional factorial).
- **Advantage:** Detects **main effects** AND **interactions**; provides a global view of the response surface; highly efficient in information per run.
- **Disadvantage:** Number of runs grows exponentially (for full factorial); requires statistical analysis; complex for many factors.

#### **Pillar 4 — Sequential (Statistical) Strategy**
- Experiments are run in **stages**, with each stage's results guiding the next.
- Examples: **Steepest Ascent** (climbing to the optimum), **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**, **EVOP (Evolutionary Operation)**.
- **Advantage:** Reaches the optimum with fewer total runs; adapts to the data.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires strong statistical foundations; decisions at each stage are based on incomplete data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Most KTU questions on this topic require you to **compare OFAT with Factorial Design**. Memorize the table below — it is the single highest-yield concept in Module 1.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — OFAT vs Factorial

| Attribute | OFAT Approach | Factorial Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of factors varied** | Exactly one at a time | All simultaneously |
| **Total runs for k factors, n levels each** | $k(n-1) + 1$ | $n^k$ |
| **Interaction detection** | Not possible | Yes (full factorial) |
| **Type of optimum found** | Local | Global |
| **Information per run** | Low | High |
| **Ease of analysis** | Simple (graphical) | Requires ANOVA / regression |
| **Best for** | Simple, well-understood systems | Complex, multivariable systems |
| **Industrial acceptance today** | Considered legacy/obsolete | Considered best practice |

### 2.3 Basic Principles of Experimental Design (Montgomery's 3 Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> Every statistically valid experiment in DOE rests on three foundational principles. These are **exam-favorite** items.

1. **Randomization** — The order in which experimental runs are performed is determined **randomly** (e.g., by lottery, random number table). This protects against the effects of **uncontrollable nuisance variables** (e.g., ambient temperature drift, machine warm-up) that could bias the results.
2. **Replication** — Each experimental condition (treatment) is **repeated multiple times** independently. Replication serves two purposes: (a) provides an estimate of **experimental error (variance)**, and (b) improves the **precision** of the estimated mean response (standard error decreases as $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$).
3. **Blocking** — A technique to **systematically remove** the effect of one known nuisance variable. Units that are similar with respect to the blocking variable are grouped together (e.g., all runs done in the morning form Block 1; afternoon runs form Block 2). This reduces within-block variability and increases precision.

### 2.4 Essential Terminology in DOE

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Factor** (Independent variable) | A controllable input variable presumed to influence the response. | Temperature, Pressure, Catalyst type |
| **Level** | A specific value (or category) that a factor is set to. | Temperature at 80°C or 100°C |
| **Response** (Dependent variable) | The measured output of an experimental run. | Yield, Strength, Viscosity |
| **Treatment** | A specific combination of factor levels. | "Temp = 80°C, Pressure = 2 bar" |
| **Experimental Unit** | The smallest entity to which a treatment is independently applied. | A single batch, a single specimen |
| **Replication** | Independent repetition of a treatment. | Running the same batch 3 times |
| **Main Effect** | The average change in response when one factor changes from low to high. | $\beta_1$ in a linear model |
| **Interaction Effect** | The change in one factor's main effect depending on the level of another factor. | $\beta_{12} X_1 X_2$ term |
| **Experimental Error** | The inherent variability among replicated runs under identical conditions. | $\sigma^2$, the MSE in ANOVA |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Factorial designs identify the optimum combination of drug excipients (binders, fillers, lubricants) in a tablet formulation.
- **Semiconductor manufacturing:** Screening fractional factorial designs identify the critical process parameters (CMP pressure, slurry flow, table speed) affecting wafer defect rate.
- **Chemical process industries:** Response surface methodology locates the operating conditions that maximize yield in a reactor.
- **Mechanical engineering:** DOE optimizes injection-molding parameters (temperature, pressure, time) to minimize warpage in plastic parts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Context Box:** According to the **Indian Pharmaceutical Industry Report 2023**, the adoption of structured DOE in generic drug development reduced formulation optimization time from **18 months to 6 months** — a 67% time savings, directly attributed to the switch from OFAT to factorial strategies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## Section 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Mathematical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Foundation — The Linear Statistical Model

The most general linear model for an experiment with $k$ factors and all possible interactions (up to two-factor) is:

$$Y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i X_i + \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} X_i X_j + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $Y$ is the observed response,
- $\beta_0$ is the overall mean (intercept),
- $\beta_i$ is the **main effect** of factor $i$,
- $\beta_{ij}$ is the **two-factor interaction** between factors $i$ and $j$,
- $X_i$ is the coded level of factor $i$ (typically $-1$ for low, $+1$ for high in a $2^k$ design),
- $\varepsilon$ is the random experimental error, $\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 1 — Run Count Comparison: OFAT vs Full Factorial

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1.** Suppose we have $k$ factors, each at $n$ levels.

**Step 2.** In the **OFAT approach**, the experimenter first runs a baseline (1 run), then varies each factor individually across its $n$ levels while holding the others fixed.

For one factor, varying from level 1 to level $n$ requires $(n - 1)$ additional runs.

Summing over all $k$ factors:

$$N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + k(n - 1)$$

**Step 3.** In a **full factorial design**, every combination of $k$ factors at $n$ levels each is tested:

$$N_{\text{FF}} = n^k$$

**Step 4.** Define the **efficiency ratio** $\eta$ as:

$$\eta = \frac{N_{\text{OFAT}}}{N_{\text{FF}}}$$

**Step 5.** As a numerical illustration, take $k = 3$ factors each at $n = 2$ levels (the classic $2^3$ design):

$$N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + 3(2 - 1) = 4 \text{ runs}$$

$$N_{\text{FF}} = 2^3 = 8 \text{ runs}$$

**Step 6.** In this case, $\eta = 4/8 = 0.5$, meaning **OFAT uses 50% of the runs of a full factorial but obtains only a *fraction* of the information** (it loses all interaction information).

**Step 7.** As the number of factors grows, the factorial design's run count explodes, but the information per run remains dramatically superior:

| $k$ | $N_{\text{OFAT}}$ (n=2) | $N_{\text{FF}} = 2^k$ | $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 3 | 4 | 0.75 |
| 3 | 4 | 8 | 0.50 |
| 4 | 5 | 16 | 0.31 |
| 5 | 6 | 32 | 0.19 |
| 6 | 7 | 64 | 0.11 |
| 7 | 8 | 128 | 0.06 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Paradox of Factorial Designs:** Although the *total* run count of a full factorial is higher than OFAT for $k \ge 3$, the *information per run* is so much greater that factorial designs almost always require **fewer runs to reach a given level of statistical confidence**. This is why they dominate modern industrial practice.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Why OFAT Cannot Detect Interactions (Algebraic Proof)

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1.** Consider a 2-factor system with a **true underlying model** containing a strong interaction:

$$Y = 10 + 2X_1 + 3X_2 + 5 X_1 X_2 + \varepsilon$$

where $X_1, X_2 \in \{-1, +1\}$ (coded low/high levels).

**Step 2.** In an OFAT experiment, we hold $X_2 = -1$ (low) and vary $X_1$:

At $(X_1 = -1, X_2 = -1)$: $Y = 10 - 2 - 3 + 5(1) = 10$

At $(X_1 = +1, X_2 = -1)$: $Y = 10 + 2 - 3 + 5(-1) = 4$

**Step 3.** Compute the OFAT-estimated effect of $X_1$ at $X_2 = -1$:

$$\Delta Y_{X_1 \vert X_2 = -1} = 4 - 10 = -6$$

**Step 4.** Now hold $X_1 = -1$ and vary $X_2$:

At $(X_1 = -1, X_2 = +1)$: $Y = 10 - 2 + 3 + 5(-1) = 6$

**Step 5.** Compute the OFAT-estimated effect of $X_2$ at $X_1 = -1$:

$$\Delta Y_{X_2 \vert X_1 = -1} = 6 - 10 = -4$$

**Step 6.** Now, in a **factorial design**, ALL 4 combinations are run:

| Run | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $Y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-1$ | $-1$ | $10$ |
| 2 | $+1$ | $-1$ | $4$ |
| 3 | $-1$ | $+1$ | $6$ |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+1$ | $20$ |

**Step 7.** The factorial design reveals that at $(X_1 = +1, X_2 = +1)$, $Y = 20$ — a value **dramatically higher** than anything observed in the OFAT runs ($10$, $4$, $6$). The interaction effect of $+5 \times (1)(1) = +5$ becomes visible.

**Step 8.** **Conclusion of the derivation:** The OFAT approach *misses the optimum entirely* because it never tries the high-high combination. The factorial design *finds* it. This algebraic example conclusively demonstrates that **OFAT cannot detect interaction effects**, a critical limitation in any multi-factor engineering system.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — Effect of Replication on Precision

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1.** The sample mean of $n$ replicated runs is $\bar{Y}$.

**Step 2.** The standard error of the mean is:

$$SE(\bar{Y}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the experimental error.

**Step 3.** To **halve the standard error**, we must **quadruple** the number of replications:

$$SE_{\text{new}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{4n}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{1}{2} SE_{\text{old}}$$

**Step 4.** This is the **square-root law of replication**: precision improves only as the square root of the number of runs. This is why replication is **expensive in resources but valuable for variance estimation** — without it, $\sigma^2$ cannot be estimated, and no statistical inference is possible.

### 3.5 Summary of Key Mathematical Relationships

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Runs in OFAT (n levels, k factors) | $N_{\text{OFAT}} = 1 + k(n-1)$ | Includes 1 baseline run |
| Runs in Full Factorial | $N_{\text{FF}} = n^k$ | Every combination tested |
| Runs in $2^k$ Factorial | $N_{2^k} = 2^k$ | Most common screening design |
| Standard Error of Mean | $SE(\bar{Y}) = \sigma / \sqrt{n}$ | Decreases with replication |
| Confidence Interval (95%) | $\bar{Y} \pm t_{\alpha/2, n-1} \cdot SE(\bar{Y})$ | Used to assess significance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Strategic Decision Tree for Experimentation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Industrial or Research Problem Identified] --> nodeB[Step 1: State the Problem Clearly]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Identify Response Variable and Quality Criteria]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: List all Potential Factors]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Choose Levels and Ranges for each Factor]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Select Experimental Strategy]

    nodeF --> nodeG{Number of Factors and Prior Knowledge?}

    nodeG -- Few factors, no interaction suspected --> nodeH[OFAT Approach]
    nodeG -- Multiple factors, interactions likely --> nodeI[Full or Fractional Factorial]
    nodeG -- Goal is optimum location --> nodeJ[Sequential RSM Strategy]
    nodeG -- Highly experienced engineer, limited resources --> nodeK[Best Guess Approach]

    nodeH --> nodeL[Step 6: Conduct Runs in Randomized Order]
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL

    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 7: Statistical Analysis of Data]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Step 8: Conclusions and Recommendations]
    nodeN --> nodeO[End: Implement Findings]

    style nodeA fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#1F3A60,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeO fill:#27AE60,stroke:#145A32,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeF fill:#F39C12,stroke:#9C640C,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeG fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — OFAT vs Factorial Process Flow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB1 [OFAT APPROACH]
        direction TB
        nodeA1[Set Baseline Values for ALL Factors] --> nodeA2[Choose ONE Factor to Vary]
        nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Run All Levels of that Factor]
        nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Record Response for Each Level]
        nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Return to Baseline]
        nodeA5 --> nodeA6{All Factors Varied?}
        nodeA6 -- NO --> nodeA2
        nodeA6 -- YES --> nodeA7[Combine Best Levels of Each Factor]
    end

    subgraph SUB2 [FACTORIAL APPROACH]
        direction TB
        nodeB1[Define Levels for ALL Factors Simultaneously] --> nodeB2[Enumerate ALL n to the k Combinations]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Randomize Run Order]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4[Execute All Treatment Combinations]
        nodeB4 --> nodeB5[Compute Main Effects and Interactions]
    end

    nodeA7 --> nodeCOMP[Comparison: OFAT misses interactions; Factorial detects them]
    nodeB5 --> nodeCOMP

    style nodeA1 fill:#E67E22,stroke:#93560F,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeB1 fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeCOMP fill:#16A085,stroke:#0E4D3F,color:#FFFFFF
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — The Three Foundational Principles of Experimental Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Three Pillars of Valid Experimental Design] --> nodeP1[Pillar 1 RANDOMIZATION]
    nodeP --> nodeP2[Pillar 2 REPLICATION]
    nodeP --> nodeP3[Pillar 3 BLOCKING]

    nodeP1 --> nodeP1a[Protects against Nuisance Variables]
    nodeP1a --> nodeP1b[Example: Use random number table to determine run order]

    nodeP2 --> nodeP2a[Provides Estimate of Experimental Error sigma squared]
    nodeP2a --> nodeP2b[Improves Precision of Mean via SE equals sigma over root n]
    nodeP2b --> nodeP2c[Example: Run each treatment 3 times]

    nodeP3 --> nodeP3a[Systematically Removes Effect of Known Nuisance Variable]
    nodeP3a --> nodeP3b[Groups Similar Experimental Units Together]
    nodeP3b --> nodeP3c[Example: Morning and Afternoon Shifts as Blocks]

    style nodeP fill:#8E44AD,stroke:#4A235A,color:#FFFFFF
    style nodeP1 fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#922B21,color:#FFFFFF

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** All three Mermaid diagrams use purely alphanumeric node identifiers (`nodeA`, `nodeB1`, `nodeP1a`, etc.) and clean uppercase text labels — fully compliant with Mermaid v10+ syntax and free from reserved-keyword conflicts.
