> **The No Interaction Assumption (NIA)** in the context of a $2^{k}$ (and higher) factorial design states that *one or more interaction terms* (typically the highest-order interaction present in the model, or any two-factor interaction that the experimenter deems physically/chemically negligible) are mathematically equal to **zero**. Formally, for a model containing factors $A, B, C, \dots$, we set the interaction parameter $\beta_{AB \dots} = 0$ for the specified terms. The reduced model becomes purely additive in the surviving effects.

In a $2^{2}$ design with factors $A$ and $B$, the full model is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_{A_i} + \tau_{B_j} + (\tau_{A\tau_{B}})_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

The **No Interaction Assumption** sets $(\tau_{A\tau_{B}})_{ij} = 0$ for all $i, j$, simplifying the model to:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_{A_i} + \tau_{B_j} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

This assumption is also called the **"additivity assumption"** because the response becomes a simple sum of main effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The No Interaction Assumption is the *cornerstone* that allows pooling of interaction degrees of freedom into the error term, making unreplicated $2^{k}$ designs fully analyzable. It is a direct consequence of the **Sparsity-of-Effects Principle** (Box & Wilson, 1951).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a chef testing how **Salt (A)** and **Chili (B)** affect customer ratings of a dish:

- **With interaction:** Adding salt improves taste. *But adding salt when chili is already very high may actually ruin the dish* (i.e., the effect of salt **depends on** the chili level). The factors *interact*.
- **Without interaction:** Adding salt improves taste by 2 rating points, and adding chili improves taste by 3 rating points — **regardless of how much of the other is present**. The effects are *additive*; they don't talk to each other.

In engineering terms, the *No Interaction Assumption* asserts that the effect of factor A on the response is the **same** at both the low and high levels of factor B. The response surface becomes a tilted plane rather than a curved (twisted) surface.

## 1.3 The Two Pillars Justifying the Assumption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Sparsity-of-Effects Principle:** Of the many effects studied, *only a few are active (non-zero)*. Most effects — especially the higher-order ones — are inert.
>
> **2. Hierarchy Principle (Hereditary Principle):** If a factor is active (its main effect is non-zero), then the lower-order effects involving it are *likely* also active, but the converse is rare. Therefore, three-factor and higher interactions can be assumed zero with high confidence.

These two principles are the **theoretical bedrock** for treating the No Interaction Assumption as a *practical engineering reality* rather than a mathematical trick.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the effect hierarchy.**
In a $2^{k}$ design, we have $k$ main effects, $\binom{k}{2}$ two-factor interactions (2FI), $\binom{k}{3}$ three-factor interactions (3FI), and so on. The total number of effects is $2^{k} - 1$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Sparsity Principle.**
Based on empirical engineering experience (e.g., Box, Hunter & Hunter, *Statistics for Experimenters*), the higher-order interactions become vanishingly small rapidly. For $k \le 4$ factors, the assumption is almost always that *all 3FI and above are zero*.

**Step 3 — Test or assume the 2FI terms.**
Two-factor interactions are kept in the model unless proven negligible. Methods for testing:
- Replication-based F-test.
- Normal / half-normal probability plot of estimated effects.
- Pareto chart of $|t\text{-ratios}|$.

**Step 4 — Pooling Procedure.**
Once an interaction is declared inactive (i.e., $SS_{\text{interaction}}$ is not significantly different from error), its sum of squares and degrees of freedom are **added to the error term** to form a *pooled error estimate*. This is called **pooling** and increases the power of subsequent F-tests.

**Step 5 — Re-test all main effects using the pooled error.**
The pooled mean square error $MS_{E,\text{pooled}}$ has more degrees of freedom, providing a sharper (lower variance) benchmark for detecting real effects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the assumption matters in unreplicated designs:** Without replication, there is no internal estimate of error. The *only* way to obtain a residual variance estimate in a single-replicate $2^{k}$ run is to *designate certain interaction terms as the error term*. The No Interaction Assumption is therefore **not optional but mandatory** for this class of designs.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are required for KTU ESE numerical questions on this topic. Memorize the symbols: $N$ = total observations, $n$ = number of replicates, $k$ = number of factors.

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Effect of factor $A$ (in $2^{k}$) | $A = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}}\left[\sum (\text{high }A) - \sum (\text{low }A)\right]$ | Units of response |
| Two-factor interaction $AB$ | $AB = \dfrac{1}{2n}\left[(ab) - a - b + (1)\right]$ | Yates' signed average |
| Contrast of effect $E$ | $C_{E} = \sum_{i=1}^{2^{k}} (\pm 1) \, T_{i}$ | $T_i$ = treatment total |
| Sum of squares for effect $E$ | $SS_{E} = \dfrac{C_{E}^{\,2}}{N}$ | $N = n \cdot 2^{k}$ |
| Total sum of squares | $SS_{T} = \sum y_{ij}^{2} - \dfrac{\left(\sum y_{ij}\right)^{2}}{N}$ | Correction factor $C$ |
| Treatment sum of squares | $SS_{\text{Trt}} = n \sum_{i=1}^{2^{k}} \left(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot}\right)^{2}$ | Aggregated |
| Pure error (from replicates) | $SS_{PE} = \sum_{i} \sum_{r} \left(y_{ir} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot}\right)^{2}$ | True replicate variation |
| **Pooled Error SS** | $SS_{E,\text{pooled}} = SS_{E} + \sum_{\text{inactive}} SS_{\text{interaction}}$ | **NIA in action** |
| **Pooled Error df** | $df_{E,\text{pooled}} = df_{E} + \sum_{\text{inactive}} df_{\text{interaction}}$ | Degrees add |
| Pooled Mean Square Error | $MS_{E,\text{pooled}} = \dfrac{SS_{E,\text{pooled}}}{df_{E,\text{pooled}}}$ | Used as denominator |
| F-statistic (effect $E$) | $F_{E} = \dfrac{MS_{E}}{MS_{E,\text{pooled}}} \sim F(\alpha, 1, df_{E,\text{pooled}})$ | Two-sided test |
| Standard error of effect | $SE(\text{Effect}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_{E,\text{pooled}}}{n \cdot 2^{k-2}}}$ | For t-ratios |
| t-ratio of effect | $t_{0} = \dfrac{\text{Effect}}{SE(\text{Effect})}$ | Compare to $t_{\alpha/2, df_{E,\text{pooled}}}$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **LaTeX safety note:** The contrast formula contains summation signs (no vertical pipes used). All fractions are written with $\dfrac{}{}$ to avoid markdown table corruption.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The No Interaction Assumption drives engineering practice in:

- **Pharmaceutical tablet formulation:** assuming the binder-lubricant interaction is zero simplifies formulation screening from $4 \times 4 = 16$ runs to 8 main-effect runs.
- **Semiconductor manufacturing:** in a $2^{6-2}$ fractional factorial, the assumption that 3FI is zero converts a 64-run full factorial into a 16-run screening design — an 75% experimental cost reduction.
- **Taguchi's robust design philosophy:** explicitly assumes the inner-array (noise) $\times$ outer-array (control) interactions are negligible, enabling the S/N ratio analysis.

The trade-off is: **cheap & informative if assumption holds; misleading if violated.** Hence, residual diagnostic checks (normal probability plot of effects) are mandatory after analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Interaction Effect in a $2^{2}$ Design

**Setup.** Let factors $A$ and $B$ each have two levels (low/high). The four treatment combinations are denoted $(1), a, b, ab$, with $n$ replicates per cell.

**Goal:** Derive the formula for the $AB$ interaction effect.

**Step 1 — Define the effect of $A$ at high $B$.**
The average response at high $B$ (across $A$ levels) is:

$$\bar{y}_{\cdot B^{+}} = \frac{\bar{y}_{b} + \bar{y}_{ab}}{2}$$

The effect of $A$ at high $B$ is:

$$A_{\text{at }B^{+}} = \bar{y}_{ab} - \bar{y}_{b}$$

**Step 2 — Define the effect of $A$ at low $B$.**
The effect of $A$ at low $B$ is:

$$A_{\text{at }B^{-}} = \bar{y}_{a} - \bar{y}_{(1)}$$

**Step 3 — Define interaction as the difference.**
The interaction $AB$ is *half the difference* between the two effects of $A$ at the two $B$-levels:

$$AB = \frac{A_{\text{at }B^{+}} - A_{\text{at }B^{-}}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AB &= \frac{(\bar{y}_{ab} - \bar{y}_{b}) - (\bar{y}_{a} - \bar{y}_{(1)})}{2} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{\bar{y}_{ab} - \bar{y}_{b} - \bar{y}_{a} + \bar{y}_{(1)}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of treatment totals.**
Multiplying numerator and denominator by $2n$ (since each $\bar{y}$ is a mean of $n$ observations):

$$AB = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ab - a - b + (1)\right]$$

This is the standard **Yates-algorithm** expression, where the lowercase symbols now represent the *totals* of $n$ replicates.

## 3.2 Worked Example: $2^{2}$ Design with Pooled Error

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A $2^{2}$ factorial design investigates the effect of **Temperature (A)** and **Catalyst Concentration (B)** on reaction yield. Two replicates are run. Test the assumption of no interaction and, if valid, pool the interaction into error. Data given below.

| Treatment | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 | Total $T_{i}$ | Mean $\bar{y}_{i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1) | 28 | 30 | 58 | 29.0 |
| $a$ | 36 | 38 | 74 | 37.0 |
| $b$ | 40 | 42 | 82 | 41.0 |
| $ab$ | 49 | 51 | 100 | 50.0 |
| **Sum** |  |  | **314** |  |

**Step 1 — Compute Grand Mean.**

$$\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = \frac{314}{8} = 39.25$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effects.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1}{2n}\left[(-1)(1) + (1)a + (-1)b + (1)ab\right] \\
  &= \frac{1}{4}\left[-58 + 74 - 82 + 100\right] \\
  &= \frac{34}{4} = 8.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{1}{4}\left[-(1) - a + b + ab\right] \\
  &= \frac{1}{4}\left[-58 - 74 + 82 + 100\right] \\
  &= \frac{50}{4} = 12.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
AB &= \frac{1}{4}\left[(1) - a - b + ab\right] \\
   &= \frac{1}{4}\left[58 - 74 - 82 + 100\right] \\
   &= \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of effects:** $A$ and $B$ are large (8.5 and 12.5). The $AB$ interaction is *negligibly small* (0.5), supporting the No Interaction Assumption.

**Step 3 — Compute Sum of Squares.**

Total observations $N = 8$. Compute:

$$\sum y^{2} = 28^{2} + 30^{2} + 36^{2} + 38^{2} + 40^{2} + 42^{2} + 49^{2} + 51^{2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum y^{2} &= 784 + 900 + 1296 + 1444 + 1600 + 1764 + 2401 + 2601 \\
&= 12790
\end{aligned}
$$

Correction factor:

$$C = \frac{(\sum y)^{2}}{N} = \frac{314^{2}}{8} = \frac{98596}{8} = 12324.5$$

Total SS:

$$SS_{T} = 12790 - 12324.5 = 465.5$$

Effect SS (using contrast / N formula):

$$SS_{A} = \frac{C_{A}^{\,2}}{N} = \frac{34^{2}}{8} = \frac{1156}{8} = 144.5$$

$$SS_{B} = \frac{C_{B}^{\,2}}{N} = \frac{50^{2}}{8} = \frac{2500}{8} = 312.5$$

$$SS_{AB} = \frac{C_{AB}^{\,2}}{N} = \frac{2^{2}}{8} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

Pure error (from replicates, $df = 4$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{PE} &= (28-29)^{2} + (30-29)^{2} + (36-37)^{2} + (38-37)^{2} \\
       &\quad + (40-41)^{2} + (42-41)^{2} + (49-50)^{2} + (51-50)^{2} \\
       &= 1+1+1+1+1+1+1+1 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Build ANOVA Table (before pooling).**

| Source | $SS$ | $df$ | $MS$ | $F_{0}$ | $F_{0.05,1,4}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 144.5 | 1 | 144.5 | 72.25 | 7.71 |
| $B$ | 312.5 | 1 | 312.5 | 156.25 | 7.71 |
| $AB$ | 0.5 | 1 | 0.5 | 0.25 | 7.71 |
| Pure Error | 8.0 | 4 | 2.00 |  |  |
| **Total** | **465.5** | **7** |  |  |  |

**Decision on $AB$:** $F_{0} = 0.25 < 7.71$. The $AB$ interaction is **not significant**. The No Interaction Assumption is statistically supported.

**Step 5 — Pool $AB$ into Error.**

$$
SS_{E,\text{pooled}} = SS_{E} + SS_{AB} = 8.0 + 0.5 = 8.5
$$

$$df_{E,\text{pooled}} = 4 + 1 = 5$$

$$MS_{E,\text{pooled}} = \frac{8.5}{5} = 1.7$$

**Step 6 — Re-test with Pooled Error.**

| Source | $SS$ | $df$ | $MS$ | $F_{0}$ | $F_{0.05,1,5}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 144.5 | 1 | 144.5 | 85.00 | 6.61 |
| $B$ | 312.5 | 1 | 312.5 | 183.82 | 6.61 |
| Pooled Error | 8.5 | 5 | 1.70 |  |  |
| **Total** | **465.5** | **7** |  |  |  |

**Conclusion:** Both $A$ and $B$ are *highly significant* ($F_{0} \gg F_{\text{crit}}$). Temperature and Catalyst Concentration significantly affect yield. The no-interaction model is **adequate and powerful**.

## 3.3 Derivation of Pooled $t$-ratio for Effect Significance

When the No Interaction Assumption holds, the variance of an estimated effect is:

$$\mathrm{Var}(\text{Effect}) = \frac{4 \, MS_{E,\text{pooled}}}{N}$$

Therefore the standard error of the effect is:

$$SE(\text{Effect}) = \sqrt{\frac{4 \, MS_{E,\text{pooled}}}{N}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{N}} \sqrt{MS_{E,\text{pooled}}}$$

The t-ratio for testing $H_0: \text{Effect} = 0$ is:

$$t_{0} = \frac{\text{Effect}}{SE(\text{Effect})} = \frac{\text{Effect} \cdot \sqrt{N}}{2\sqrt{MS_{E,\text{pooled}}}}$$

This is *mathematically equivalent* to the square root of the F-statistic:

$$F_{0} = t_{0}^{\,2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad t_{0} = \sqrt{F_{0}}$$

For our example, $A$:

$$t_{0,A} = \frac{8.5 \cdot \sqrt{8}}{2\sqrt{1.7}} = \frac{8.5 \times 2.828}{2 \times 1.304} = \frac{24.04}{2.608} = 9.22$$

And $t_{0.025, 5} = 2.571$. Since $9.22 \gg 2.571$, factor $A$ is overwhelmingly significant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Pooling a small, non-significant interaction into error *reduces* $MS_E$ and *increases* $df_E$, both of which *increase* the test's statistical power. This is the **central practical benefit** of the No Interaction Assumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram: Decision Procedure for No Interaction Assumption

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Run 2k Factorial Design] --> B{Replicated?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Compute SS and F-test for each interaction]
    B -- No --> D[Normal / Half-Normal Probability Plot of Effects]
    C --> E{Is F_interaction less than F_critical?}
    D --> F{Do effects fall on straight line except outliers?}
    E -- Yes --> G[Declare Interaction INACTIVE]
    E -- No --> H[Keep Interaction in Model]
    F -- Yes --> G
    F -- No --> H
    G --> I[Pool SS_interaction into SS_Error]
    I --> J[Re-test Main Effects using Pooled MS_Error]
    H --> K[Refit Full Model with Interaction]
    J --> L[Report Final Significant Effects]
    K --> L
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.2 Effect Hierarchy Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    N1[Order 1: Main Effects A B C D] --> N2[Order 2: Two Factor Interactions AB AC AD BC BD CD]
    N2 --> N3[Order 3: Three Factor Interactions ABC ABD ACD BCD]
    N3 --> N4[Order 4: Four Factor Interaction ABCD]
    N1 -.->|Largest expected magnitude| N5[Active Region]
    N2 -.-> N5
    N3 -.->|Often assumed ZERO NIA| N6[Pooled into Error]
    N4 -.->|Almost always ZERO| N6
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pooling Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Pre Pooling State]
        direction TB
        S1[SS_Error df = 4 MS = 2.00]
        S2[SS_AB df = 1 MS = 0.50 F = 0.25 NS]
    end
    subgraph A2[Post Pooling State]
        direction TB
        S3[SS_Pooled_Error = 8.0 plus 0.5 = 8.5]
        S4[df_Pooled = 4 plus 1 = 5]
        S5[MS_Pooled = 8.5 divided by 5 = 1.70]
    end
    A1 --> A2
```

## 4.4 Normal Probability Plot Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Inert Effects on Straight Line through origin] --> B[Normal Probability Paper]
    C[Active Effects] --> D[Depart from Line at upper right]
    B --> E[Identify and Label Outliers]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Outliers are Active Effects]
    F --> G[Remaining = Pooled Error]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the plot:** The No Interaction Assumption implies that *most* effects are inert (zero), so they fall on a straight line on normal probability paper. The *few* active effects (main effects and significant 2FIs) appear as outliers at the extremes. The slope of the inert line is a graphical estimate of $\sigma$, the standard deviation of effects.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the "No Interaction Assumption" in the context of $2^{k}$ factorial designs. State two principles that justify its use.*

**Model Answer:**
The No Interaction Assumption states that the interaction term(s) in a factorial model are equal to zero, i.e., $\beta_{AB\dots} = 0$, so that the response is a purely additive function of the main effects. It is justified by **(i) the Sparsity-of-Effects Principle** (most effects are zero) and **(ii) the Hierarchy Principle** (lower-order effects dominate). Under this assumption, the sum of squares of inactive interactions is pooled into the error term, providing additional degrees of freedom for testing the remaining effects. **[3 marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is meant by "pooling" of interactions? Under what condition is it statistically valid?*

**Model Answer:**
Pooling is the procedure of combining the sum of squares and degrees of freedom of a non-significant interaction with the error sum of squares, forming a single pooled error estimate $MS_{E,\text{pooled}}$. It is **statistically valid** only when the interaction being pooled has been *tested* (via F-test or normal probability plot) and found to be *insignificant* — i.e., its mean square does not significantly exceed the pure error mean square. Pooling increases $df$ for error, sharpening subsequent F-tests, but if the assumption of zero interaction is false, the pooled error is inflated and real main effects may be masked. **[3 marks]**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the No Interaction Assumption with reference to a $2^{2}$ factorial design. Show the reduced model and discuss the role of replication in validating the assumption.*

**Model Solution:**

In a $2^{2}$ design with factors $A$ (e.g., Temperature) and $B$ (e.g., Pressure), the full model is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_{A_i} + \tau_{B_j} + (\tau_{AB})_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

The No Interaction Assumption sets $(\tau_{AB})_{ij} = 0$ for all $i, j$, reducing the model to:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_{A_i} + \tau_{B_j} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Interpretation:** the effect of $A$ on the response is the *same* at the low level of $B$ as it is at the high level of $B$. The response surface is a tilted plane rather than a twisted (saddle-shaped) surface.

**Role of replication:** With $n \ge 2$ replicates per cell, the **pure error** $SS_{PE}$ can be computed directly from within-cell variation. The interaction $SS_{AB}$ is then tested against $SS_{PE}$ using the F-ratio:

$$F_{0} = \frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_{PE}} \quad \text{with} \quad df_{1}=1,\ df_{2}=2^{k}(n-1)$$

If $F_0 \le F_{\alpha, 1, 2^{k}(n-1)}$, the interaction is declared *inactive*, the No Interaction Assumption is **validated**, and $SS_{AB}$ is pooled into error. **[7 marks]**

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks]** *A $2^{3}$ factorial design with two replicates is run to study factors A, B, C. The data are shown below. Apply the No Interaction Assumption to the three-factor interaction (ABC) and perform the complete ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$.*

| Treatment | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| (1) | 12 | 14 | 26 |
| a | 18 | 20 | 38 |
| b | 22 | 24 | 46 |
| ab | 30 | 32 | 62 |
| c | 16 | 18 | 34 |
| ac | 22 | 24 | 46 |
| bc | 26 | 28 | 54 |
| abc | 34 | 36 | 70 |
| **Sum** |  |  | **376** |

**Model Solution:**

$N = 16$, $\bar{y}_{\cdot\cdot} = 376/16 = 23.5$

**Step 1 — Compute contrasts using Yates' signs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= -26 + 38 - 46 + 62 - 34 + 46 - 54 + 70 = 56 \\
C_B &= -26 - 38 + 46 + 62 - 34 - 46 + 54 + 70 = 88 \\
C_C &= -26 - 38 - 46 - 62 + 34 + 46 + 54 + 70 = 32 \\
C_{AB} &= 26 - 38 - 46 + 62 + 34 - 46 - 54 + 70 = 8 \\
C_{AC} &= 26 - 38 + 46 - 62 - 34 + 46 + 54 - 70 = -32 \\
C_{BC} &= 26 + 38 - 46 - 62 - 34 - 46 + 54 + 70 = 0 \\
C_{ABC} &= -26 + 38 + 46 - 62 + 34 - 46 - 54 + 70 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squares ($SS_E = C_E^2 / N$):**

$$SS_A = \frac{56^{2}}{16} = 196.0, \quad SS_B = \frac{88^{2}}{16} = 484.0$$

$$SS_C = \frac{32^{2}}{16} = 64.0, \quad SS_{AB} = \frac{8^{2}}{16} = 4.0$$

$$SS_{AC} = \frac{(-32)^{2}}{16} = 64.0, \quad SS_{BC} = 0, \quad SS_{ABC} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Pure Error (from replicates):** $SS_{PE} = 8$ with $df_{PE}=8$, $MS_{PE}=1.0$.

**Step 4 — Test ABC vs Pure Error:**
$F_{0,ABC} = 0/1.0 = 0$. Clearly not significant. The No Interaction Assumption for ABC is **validated**.

**Step 5 — Pool ABC into Error.** $SS_{E,\text{pooled}} = 8 + 0 = 8$; $df_{E,\text{pooled}} = 8 + 1 = 9$; $MS_{E,\text{pooled}} = 8/9 = 0.889$.

**Step 6 — Re-test all main effects and 2FI:**

| Source | $SS$ | $df$ | $MS$ | $F_{0}$ | $F_{0.05,1,9}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | 196.0 | 1 | 196.0 | 220.5 | 5.12 |
| $B$ | 484.0 | 1 | 484.0 | 544.5 | 5.12 |
| $C$ | 64.0 | 1 | 64.0 | 72.0 | 5.12 |
| $AB$ | 4.0 | 1 | 4.0 | 4.50 | 5.12 |
| $AC$ | 64.0 | 1 | 64.0 | 72.0 | 5.12 |
| $BC$ | 0.0 | 1 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 5.12 |
| $ABC$ (pooled) | 0.0 | 1 | — | — | — |
| Pooled Error | 8.0 | 9 | 0.889 |  |  |
| **Total** |  | **15** |  |  |  |

**Conclusion:** Factors $A$, $B$, $C$, and the $AC$ interaction are **highly significant**. The $AB$ interaction is marginal. The no-interaction model for $ABC$ is supported. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the Sparsity-of-Effects Principle and the Hierarchy Principle. How do they together provide the theoretical justification for the No Interaction Assumption in $2^{k}$ designs?*

**Model Solution:**

**Sparsity-of-Effects Principle (Box & Meyer, 1986):**
In any factorial experiment with $k$ factors, the *number of active (non-zero) effects is small* compared to the total number of effects ($2^{k}-1$). For most engineering and scientific systems, the response is dominated by a *few* main effects and *at most a handful* of two-factor interactions. The vast majority of effects — particularly higher-order interactions — are statistically indistinguishable from zero. Mathematically, the effect vector $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_{A}, \beta_{B}, \dots, \beta_{ABC\dots K})$ is **sparse**, with most components equal to zero.

**Hierarchy Principle (Hamada & Wu, 1992):**
If a $p$-factor interaction is active (non-zero), then the *lower-order* interactions and main effects involving those same factors are *very likely* also active. Conversely, a low-order effect being zero does *not* imply the higher-order effect is zero, but the *probability* of a high-order effect being active given that lower-order effects are zero is very small. This principle is **hereditary in reverse**: effects "switch on" from low order upward, not the other way.

**Combined Theoretical Justification for NIA:**

1. By **Sparsity**, only a few high-order interactions are likely non-zero → *most* can be set to zero.
2. By **Hierarchy**, even when lower-order effects are active, the high-order effects (3FI, 4FI) are the *least likely* to be active.
3. Therefore, designating 3FI and 4FI as "the error term" in an unreplicated $2^{k}$ design is **mathematically defensible and practically sound**.
4. The "cost" of occasional false-pooling (pooling a real interaction) is balanced by the "benefit" of having $2^{k}-1 - k - \binom{k}{2}$ additional error degrees of freedom.

**Engineering validation:** Empirical studies across hundreds of industrial screening experiments confirm that 3FI and higher terms are negligible in $\sim 90\%$ of cases. The NIA is therefore a cornerstone of efficient industrial experimentation. **[7 marks]**

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks]** *Describe the procedure of constructing a normal probability plot of effects to validate the No Interaction Assumption. How are active effects identified? What is the danger if the assumption is incorrectly applied?*

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure for Normal Probability Plot of Effects (Daniel, 1959):**

1. **Estimate all $2^{k}-1$ effects** from the $2^{k}$ treatment means (single replicate) or from $n$ replicates.
2. **Rank the effects** in ascending order of magnitude: $E_{(1)} \le E_{(2)} \le \dots \le E_{(2^{k}-1)}$.
3. **Compute the cumulative probability** for the $i$-th ranked effect:

$$P_{(i)} = \frac{i - 0.5}{2^{k} - 1}$$

4. **Plot $E_{(i)}$ versus the normal score** $z_{(i)} = \Phi^{-1}(P_{(i)})$, where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse standard normal CDF.
5. **Inspect the plot:**
   - Inert (zero) effects follow a **straight line** through the origin (the mean of effects) with slope equal to $\sigma$.
   - Active effects **deviate from the straight line**, appearing as outliers at the **upper-right** (or **lower-left**) extremes.

**Identification of Active Effects:**
- Effects lying on the line (or within random scatter around it) are *inert* → candidates for pooling into error.
- Effects lying *off the line*, especially at the extremes, are *active* → must be retained in the model.
- The straight line is fitted *only* to the inert points (often the central 60-70% of the ranked effects).

**Dangers of Incorrect Application of the No Interaction Assumption:**

1. **Masking of real effects:** If a real 2FI is wrongly pooled into error, $MS_{E,\text{pooled}}$ becomes inflated → real main effects may *fail* the F-test → *Type II error*.
2. **Confounded effect identification:** In fractional factorials (e.g., $2^{k-1}$), the No Interaction Assumption is required to *de-alias* main effects from 2FI. If the assumption fails, the *alias structure breaks down* and one cannot separate main effects from interactions.
3. **Biased estimates of main effects:** Pooling a non-zero interaction adds a systematic bias to $MS_E$, distorting the F-test for all main effects.
4. **Non-additive response surface mis-modeled:** The assumed tilted plane is wrong; the true saddle-shaped surface leads to *sub-optimal operating conditions* if used for process optimization.

**Mitigation:** Always perform **replication-based F-tests** when possible. When replication is impossible, use **half-normal plots** (Daniel, 1976) — they are more efficient than full normal plots in the small-effect regime. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — KTU 2024 Pattern:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update df:** When pooling, students often add $SS_{AB}$ to $SS_E$ but forget to add 1 to $df_E$. The pooled $df$ is critical for the correct $F_{0.05}$ critical value lookup. **[-1 mark]**
> 2. **Confusing "no replication" with "no error":** In an unreplicated $2^{k}$ design, the error term *does not exist* until the No Interaction Assumption designates an interaction as error. Students who leave the interaction out of ANOVA entirely *lose 3 marks*.
> 3. **Using $\bar{y}$ vs $T$ inconsistently:** The contrast $C_E$ uses *treatment totals*; the effect uses *treatment means*. Mixing them up produces errors in $SS_E = C_E^2 / N$ vs $SS_E = (\text{Effect})^2 \cdot N / 4$. **[-1 mark]**
> 4. **Misidentifying the contrast sign pattern:** The $+1$ / $-1$ signs for a 3FI like $ABC$ are *not* the same as the product of individual signs. Use Yates' algorithm rigorously. **[-1 mark per wrong sign]**

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **NIA Definition:** Setting specific interaction parameters to zero so the model is additive. Validated by F-test, normal probability plot, or replication evidence.
- **Two Pillars:** Sparsity-of-Effects (most effects are zero) and Hierarchy (low-order effects dominate). These *justify* the assumption empirically.
- **Effect formula in $2^{k}$:** $E = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot 2^{k-1}} \sum (\pm 1) T_{i}$.
- **SS formula:** $SS_{E} = C_{E}^{\,2} / N$ where $N = n \cdot 2^{k}$.
- **Total SS:** $SS_{T} = \sum y^{2} - (\sum y)^{2}/N$.
- **Pooled Error:** $SS_{E,\text{pooled}} = SS_{E} + \sum_{\text{inactive}} SS_{\text{interaction}}$; degrees add similarly.
- **F-test:** $F_{0} = MS_{E} / MS_{E,\text{pooled}}$ with $df_1 = 1$ and $df_2 = df_{E,\text{pooled}}$.
- **Standard error of effect:** $SE(\text{Effect}) = 2\sqrt{MS_{E,\text{pooled}}/N}$.
- **Normal probability plot:** Inert effects lie on a straight line; active effects are off-line outliers. Slope of inert line estimates $\sigma$.
- **When NIA is mandatory:** Unreplicated $2^{k}$ designs, $2^{k-p}$ fractional factorials, Plackett-Burman screening designs, Taguchi inner-outer array analysis.
- **When NIA is risky:** Strong chemical/physical a priori reason to expect interaction (e.g., enzyme kinetics, mixture experiments), or any process with a known synergistic/antagonistic mechanism.
- **Practical rule of thumb:** 3FI and higher are pooled in $k \le 5$ designs; 2FI must be tested before pooling. Always check normal probability plot to confirm.
- **Cost of false pooling:** Inflated $MS_E$ → reduced power → real effects missed (Type II error).
- **Cost of false retention of inert effect:** Wasted error $df$ → reduced power for *all* remaining tests.
- **Trade-off:** The No Interaction Assumption is the *single most important practical simplification* in industrial DOE, responsible for the success of fractional factorial and screening designs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Factorial Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Factorial Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **General Factorial Design (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> A *general factorial design* is an experimental strategy in which **all possible combinations of the levels of two or more factors** are investigated in a single experiment, allowing the simultaneous estimation of **main effects** of each factor and the **interaction effects** between factors. It is the most efficient design for studying multi-factor systems and forms the foundation for fractional factorial, response surface, and Taguchi methodologies.

A two-factor general factorial design is denoted as an **a × b design**, meaning factor *A* is run at *a* levels and factor *B* at *b* levels. When extended to *k* factors each at two levels, it is called a **2^k factorial design**, the workhorse of industrial experimentation.

### Specialised Variants of General Factorial Design

| Design Notation | Levels per Factor | Runs (no replicates) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2^2** | 2, 2 | 4 | Screening two factors |
| **2^3** | 2, 2, 2 | 8 | Three-factor screening |
| **2^k** | 2, 2, ..., 2 (k times) | 2^k | Classical screening design |
| **3^k** | 3, 3, ..., 3 (k times) | 3^k | Response surface entry design |
| **a × b** | a, b (mixed) | a·b | Asymmetric factorial |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Chef" Experiment

Imagine a chef trying to perfect a cake recipe. She wants to know the effect of **(i) oven temperature** (Low, High) and **(ii) baking time** (Short, Long) on the cake's softness score.

A **one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT)** approach would test temperature at one fixed time, then vary time at one fixed temperature. The chef would never discover that *high temperature AND short time* produces a uniquely moist cake — the famous **interaction effect**.

A **general factorial design** runs **all four combinations** in one go:

| Combination | Temperature | Time |
|---|---|---|
| (1) | Low | Short |
| a | High | Short |
| b | Low | Long |
| ab | High | Long |

By comparing all four corners, the chef identifies both individual contributions *and* the synergy between factors. This is the **philosophical essence** of factorial design — **interaction discovery through completeness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** General factorial designs are the *only* design class that can **unambiguously estimate all interaction effects**. OFAT designs are fundamentally blind to interactions and are considered inferior by modern DOE standards (Montgomery, *Design and Analysis of Experiments*).

## 1.3 Why Factorial Designs? (Key Justifications)

1. **Efficiency:** One experiment yields information on *k* factors plus their interactions — fewer runs than separate single-factor experiments.
2. **Interaction Detection:** Only factorial designs can estimate factor-factor interactions.
3. **Validity Range:** Results are valid over the entire range of factor levels tested (broader inference).
4. **Optimality under Randomisation:** The orthogonal structure maximises precision of effect estimates.

## 1.4 Standard Notation and Terminology

- **Treatment Combination:** A specific tuple of factor levels, e.g., (A₂, B₁).
- **Main Effect of A:** Average change in response when A is changed from low to high, *averaged over* the levels of B.
- **Interaction Effect (A×B):** Difference in the effect of A at different levels of B (or vice-versa).
- **Replicate:** An independent repetition of a treatment combination.
- **Experimental Unit:** The smallest entity to which a treatment is applied.
- **Balance:** Each level of every factor appears the same number of times — *a property all general factorial designs possess by construction.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Linear Statistical Model

For a **two-factor factorial design** with factor *A* at *a* levels, factor *B* at *b* levels, and *n* replicates per cell, the **fixed-effects linear model** is:

$$y_{ijk} = \mu + \tau_i + \beta_j + (\tau\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

with the standard *sum-to-zero* constraints:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{a}\tau_i = 0, \quad \sum_{j=1}^{b}\beta_j = 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\tau\beta)_{ij} = \sum_{j=1}^{b}(\tau\beta)_{ij} = 0$$

### 2.1.1 Component-Wise Interpretation

| Symbol | Meaning | Index Range |
|---|---|---|
| $y_{ijk}$ | Observed response | k = 1, ..., n |
| $\mu$ | Overall (grand) mean | scalar |
| $\tau_i$ | Main effect of i-th level of A | i = 1, ..., a |
| $\beta_j$ | Main effect of j-th level of B | j = 1, ..., b |
| $(\tau\beta)_{ij}$ | Interaction effect of (i, j) cell | jointly indexed |
| $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ | Random error, $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ | iid |

### 2.1.2 Assumptions of the Model

- Errors $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ are **independent and identically distributed (iid) Normal(0, σ²)**.
- Factor levels are **fixed** (we are not generalising to a random population of levels).
- Treatment combinations are assigned to experimental units **randomly**.
- The interaction term is **non-additive** — without it, the model collapses to a simple additive main-effects model.

## 2.2 Hypotheses for the General Two-Factor Factorial

The KTU board examination frequently tests hypothesis formulation. The three testable null hypotheses are:

$$H_0^{(\tau)}: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \cdots = \tau_a = 0 \quad \text{(no main effect of A)}$$

$$H_0^{(\beta)}: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_b = 0 \quad \text{(no main effect of B)}$$

$$H_0^{(\tau\beta)}: (\tau\beta)_{ij} = 0 \quad \forall i,j \quad \text{(no A×B interaction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Loss Warning:** Always specify *all three* hypotheses in two-factor ANOVA. A common student error is to mention only the two main-effect hypotheses and forget the interaction test. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for incomplete hypothesis statements.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** A dot in the subscript denotes averaging. For example, $y_{i\cdot\cdot}$ is the average over *j* and *k* for a fixed *i*. The grand mean is $\bar{y}_{\cdots}$.

| Quantity | Formula | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|
| **Grand Mean** | $\bar{y}_{\cdots} = \dfrac{\sum_{i}\sum_{j}\sum_{k} y_{ijk}}{abn}$ | — |
| **Cell Mean** | $\bar{y}_{ij\cdot} = \dfrac{\sum_{k} y_{ijk}}{n}$ | — |
| **Row Mean** | $\bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} = \dfrac{\sum_{j}\sum_{k} y_{ijk}}{bn}$ | — |
| **Column Mean** | $\bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} = \dfrac{\sum_{i}\sum_{k} y_{ijk}}{an}$ | — |
| **SS_Total** | $\sum_{i,j,k} y_{ijk}^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y_{ijk})^2}{abn}$ | $abn - 1$ |
| **SS_A (Main)** | $bn \sum_{i} (\bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2$ | $a - 1$ |
| **SS_B (Main)** | $an \sum_{j} (\bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2$ | $b - 1$ |
| **SS_Cells** | $n \sum_{i,j} (\bar{y}_{ij\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2$ | $ab - 1$ |
| **SS_AB (Interaction)** | $SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ |
| **SS_Error** | $\sum_{i,j,k} (y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{ij\cdot})^2$ | $ab(n-1)$ |
| **MS_Factor** | $SS_{Factor} / df_{Factor}$ | — |
| **F-Statistic** | $F_0 = MS_{Effect} / MS_E$ | $df_1, df_2$ |
| **Effect (2²)** | $A = \dfrac{(ab + a) - (b + 1)}{2n}$ | — |
| **SS from Effect (2²)** | $SS = 4n \cdot (\text{Effect})^2$ | 1 |
| **Yates' Step** | $T_1, T_2, \ldots \to (T_1+T_2), (T_1-T_2)$ | — |

### 2.3.1 Critical F-Values Frequently Required (α = 0.05)

| $df_1$ / $df_2$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **2** | 18.51 | 19.00 | 19.16 | 19.25 | 19.30 | 19.33 |
| **3** | 10.13 | 9.55 | 9.28 | 9.12 | 9.01 | 8.94 |
| **4** | 7.71 | 6.94 | 6.59 | 6.39 | 6.26 | 6.16 |
| **5** | 6.61 | 5.79 | 5.41 | 5.19 | 5.05 | 4.95 |
| **6** | 5.99 | 5.14 | 4.76 | 4.53 | 4.39 | 4.28 |

## 2.4 Computational Procedure (KTU Board Standard)

A standard KTU answer proceeds through the following **six steps**:

1. **State the model** and the assumptions.
2. **Tabulate the data** in cell format: rows = A levels, columns = B levels, with n replicates per cell.
3. **Compute cell totals, row totals, column totals, and the grand total.**
4. **Compute correction factor (CF)** and the sums of squares (SS_T, SS_A, SS_B, SS_AB, SS_E).
5. **Construct the ANOVA table** with SS, df, MS, and F-ratios.
6. **Compare F with F-critical** at α = 0.05 and state engineering conclusions.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** General factorial designs underpin the pharmaceutical formulation trials (drug × dose × time), semiconductor process optimisation (temperature × pressure × flow rate), and marketing A/B/n testing frameworks. The ability to detect **synergistic effects** makes factorial design indispensable wherever *trade-offs* exist between controllable input variables.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sum-of-Squares Identity

The fundamental decomposition underlying the ANOVA for factorial designs is:

$$SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$$

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Identity

We begin by considering the total sum of squared deviations from the grand mean:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{n} (y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{\cdots})^2$$

Now, introduce the cell mean and apply the **add-and-subtract** trick. Note that:

$$y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{\cdots} = (\bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots}) + (\bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdots}) + (\bar{y}_{ij\cdot} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot\cdot} - \bar{y}_{\cdot j\cdot} + \bar{y}_{\cdots}) + (y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{ij\cdot})$$

Each of the four bracketed terms corresponds, respectively, to **A main effect, B main effect, A×B interaction, and error**. Let us denote them:

$$(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{\cdots}) = A_{i} + B_{j} + AB_{ij} + E_{ijk}$$

### 3.1.2 Squaring and Summing

Squaring both sides and summing over all $i, j, k$:

$$SS_T = \sum A_i^2 + \sum B_j^2 + \sum AB_{ij}^2 + \sum E_{ijk}^2 + 2 \sum A_i B_j + \cdots$$

The **orthogonality** of factorial designs (each level appears an equal number of times) ensures that **all cross-product terms vanish**. In particular:

$$\sum_{i,j,k} A_i B_j = n \sum_i A_i \sum_j B_j = 0 \quad \text{(by sum-to-zero constraints)}$$

Similarly, all mixed terms vanish. Therefore:

$$SS_T = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$$

This identity is the **mathematical heart** of factorial ANOVA.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2² Factorial with Replication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU "Favourite" 14-mark problem.** Practice it until the procedure becomes automatic.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A chemical engineer studies the effect of **Reaction Temperature (A)** and **Catalyst Concentration (B)** on the *yield* of a process. Factor A has two levels ($A_1 = 80°C, A_2 = 100°C$) and factor B has two levels ($B_1 = 1\%, B_2 = 3\%$). The design is replicated **n = 2** times. The data are:

| Treatment | Replicate 1 | Replicate 2 |
|---|---|---|
| (1) = $A_1B_1$ | 10 | 12 |
| a = $A_2B_1$ | 14 | 16 |
| b = $A_1B_2$ | 8 | 10 |
| ab = $A_2B_2$ | 18 | 20 |

**Required:** Construct the ANOVA table and test for significance at $\alpha = 0.05$. ($F_{0.05, 1, 4} = 7.71$)

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Cell Totals Layout

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | Row Total $y_{i..}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 22 | 18 | 40 |
| $A_2$ | 30 | 38 | 68 |
| **Col Total $y_{.j.}$** | **52** | **56** | **108** |

**Grand Total:** $y_{...} = 108$. **Total Runs:** $N = abn = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 8$.

**Correction Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{(y_{...})^2}{N} = \frac{(108)^2}{8} = \frac{11664}{8} = 1458$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Raw Sum of Squares

$$\sum_{i,j,k} y_{ijk}^2 = 10^2 + 12^2 + 14^2 + 16^2 + 8^2 + 10^2 + 18^2 + 20^2$$

$$= 100 + 144 + 196 + 256 + 64 + 100 + 324 + 400 = 1584$$

$$SS_T = \sum y^2 - CF = 1584 - 1458 = 126$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Main Effect Sums of Squares

**For Factor A (rows):**

$$SS_A = \frac{\sum_i y_{i..}^2}{bn} - CF = \frac{(40)^2 + (68)^2}{(2)(2)} - 1458$$

$$= \frac{1600 + 4624}{4} - 1458 = \frac{6224}{4} - 1458 = 1556 - 1458 = 98$$

**For Factor B (columns):**

$$SS_B = \frac{\sum_j y_{.j.}^2}{an} - CF = \frac{(52)^2 + (56)^2}{(2)(2)} - 1458$$

$$= \frac{2704 + 3136}{4} - 1458 = \frac{5840}{4} - 1458 = 1460 - 1458 = 2$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Cell and Interaction Sums of Squares

**Cells (treatment combinations):**

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{\sum_{i,j} y_{ij.}^2}{n} - CF = \frac{(22)^2 + (30)^2 + (18)^2 + (38)^2}{2} - 1458$$

$$= \frac{484 + 900 + 324 + 1444}{2} - 1458 = \frac{3152}{2} - 1458 = 1576 - 1458 = 118$$

**Interaction:**

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B = 118 - 98 - 2 = 18$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Error Sum of Squares

$$SS_E = SS_T - SS_{Cells} = 126 - 118 = 8$$

**Verification via direct within-cell variance:**

For cell (1): $(10-11)^2 + (12-11)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
For cell a: $(14-15)^2 + (16-15)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
For cell b: $(8-9)^2 + (10-9)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
For cell ab: $(18-19)^2 + (20-19)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$

$$SS_E = 2 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | $F_0$ | $F_{0.05}$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A (Temperature)** | 98 | 1 | 98 | **98.00** | 7.71 | Significant |
| **B (Catalyst)** | 2 | 1 | 2 | **2.00** | 7.71 | Not Significant |
| **AB (Interaction)** | 18 | 1 | 18 | **18.00** | 7.71 | Significant |
| **Error** | 8 | 4 | 2 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | 126 | 7 | | | | |

### 3.2.8 Engineering Conclusions

- **Temperature (A)** is a dominant factor: raising temperature from 80°C to 100°C substantially increases yield.
- **Catalyst (B) alone** has no significant main effect.
- **A × B interaction IS significant** — the effect of B depends on the level of A. This is the *engineering insight* the factorial design was designed to uncover.

## 3.3 Effect Estimation in 2² Design (Yates' Method)

For a 2² design, the **effect estimates** (half of the *contrast* divided by *n*) are computed using the *standard order* (1, a, b, ab):

$$A = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ ab + a - b - (1) \right] = \frac{38 + 30 - 18 - 22}{4} = \frac{28}{4} = 7$$

$$B = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ ab - a + b - (1) \right] = \frac{38 - 30 + 18 - 22}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1$$

$$AB = \frac{1}{2n}\left[ ab - a - b + (1) \right] = \frac{38 - 30 - 18 + 22}{4} = \frac{12}{4} = 3$$

### 3.3.1 Cross-Verification of Sums of Squares

$$SS_A = 4n \cdot A^2 = (4)(2)(7)^2 = 8 \cdot 49 = 392 \;?$$

Wait — this formula uses **half-effects** convention; the correct KTU formula is:

$$SS_{Effect} = \frac{(\text{Contrast})^2}{4n} = \frac{(\text{Contrast})^2}{2^k \cdot n}$$

For A: $SS_A = \dfrac{(28)^2}{8} = \dfrac{784}{8} = 98 \quad \checkmark$

For B: $SS_B = \dfrac{(4)^2}{8} = \dfrac{16}{8} = 2 \quad \checkmark$

For AB: $SS_{AB} = \dfrac{(12)^2}{8} = \dfrac{144}{8} = 18 \quad \checkmark$

## 3.4 General 2^k Design Notes

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Total runs (no rep) | $2^k$ |
| Cell replications needed | At least 2 for error estimation |
| Number of effects | $2^k - 1$ |
| Effect df | 1 each |
| Standard order of runs | (1), a, b, ab, c, ac, bc, abc, ... |

The orthogonal-array structure of the 2^k design means the **interaction sum-of-squares decomposition is always unique** — no correlation between effect estimates, regardless of the number of factors.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Analytical Pipeline of General Factorial Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Stage 0 Problem Definition] --> A1[Stage 1 Select Factors and Levels]
    A1 --> A2[Stage 2 Construct Treatment Combinations]
    A2 --> A3[Stage 3 Randomise and Replicate]
    A3 --> A4[Stage 4 Conduct Experiment and Record y ijk]
    A4 --> A5[Stage 5 Compute Cell Row Column Grand Totals]
    A5 --> A6[Stage 6 Calculate Correction Factor CF]
    A6 --> A7[Stage 7 Decompose Total SS into Main and Interaction SS]
    A7 --> A8[Stage 8 Build ANOVA Table with df MS and F Ratios]
    A8 --> A9{Stage 9 Decision Node F calc greater than F crit}
    A9 -->|Yes| A10[Stage 10 Reject H0 Factor Significant]
    A9 -->|No| A11[Stage 11 Fail to Reject H0 Factor Not Significant]
    A10 --> A12[Stage 12 Engineering Interpretation and Optimisation]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[Stage 13 Optional Confirmation Runs]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree — Sources of Variation in a Two-Factor Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Total Variability SS Total] --> S1[Between Treatment Variability]
    S0 --> S2[Within Treatment Variability SS Error]
    S1 --> T1[Variability due to Factor A Main Effect]
    S1 --> T2[Variability due to Factor B Main Effect]
    S1 --> T3[Variability due to A times B Interaction]
    T1 --> N1[df a minus 1]
    T2 --> N2[df b minus 1]
    T3 --> N3[df a minus 1 times b minus 1]
    S2 --> N4[df ab n minus 1]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Yates' Algorithm for 2^k Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Input Treatment Totals in Standard Order] --> Y1[Column 0 Original Totals]
    Y1 --> Y2[Column 1 First Pairwise Sums Top Half]
    Y1 --> Y3[Column 1 First Pairwise Diffs Bottom Half]
    Y2 --> Y4[Column 2 Pairwise Sums of Column 1 Top]
    Y3 --> Y5[Column 2 Pairwise Diffs of Column 1 Bottom]
    Y4 --> Y6[Output Column 2 Effect Estimates after Division by 2 to the k minus 1 times n]
    Y5 --> Y6
    Y6 --> Y7[Verify with Direct Contrast Formulas]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mermaid Map — Main vs. Interaction Effects in 2² Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Two Factor Two Level Design 2 to the power 2] --> E1[Effect A Effect of Temperature]
    E0 --> E2[Effect B Effect of Catalyst]
    E0 --> E3[Effect AB Joint Effect Temperature times Catalyst]
    E1 --> R1[Increase temperature from 80 to 100 shift the yield line upward]
    E2 --> R2[Change catalyst from 1 percent to 3 percent shift the line upward by a small amount]
    E3 --> R3[Two non parallel lines on the response plot AB is significant]
    R3 --> E4[Engineering Insight Synergy must be exploited by jointly tuning factors]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The decision node in **Section 4.1** represents the hypothesis-testing step where $F_{calc} > F_{crit}$ triggers a rejection of the null hypothesis. The tree in **Section 4.2** illustrates how the *between-treatment* variability partitions into main and interaction components — a concept KTU examiners frequently test via Venn-diagram-like sketches.
