| | **$H_0$ is TRUE** | **$H_0$ is FALSE** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Fail to Reject $H_0$** | Correct Decision (Probability $= 1-\alpha$) | **Type II Error** (Probability $= \beta$) |
| **Reject $H_0$** | **Type I Error** (Probability $= \alpha$) | Correct Decision (Probability $= 1-\beta = \text{Power}$) |

**Level of Significance ($\alpha$):** The maximum probability of committing a Type I error that the researcher is willing to tolerate. Standard values in engineering practice are **$\alpha = 0.05$ (5%)** and **$\alpha = 0.01$ (1%)**.

**Power of the Test ($1-\beta$):** The probability of correctly rejecting a false null hypothesis. Higher power is always desirable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** In Quality Engineering, hypothesis testing is the foundation of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**, **Six Sigma**, and **acceptance sampling plans**. A process engineer tests whether a new manufacturing process has truly reduced the defect rate, or whether an observed change is just random variation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Step Hypothesis Testing Procedure (KTU Standard)

The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation expects students to explicitly follow these steps:

**Step 1: State the Hypotheses**
Formulate the null hypothesis $H_0$ and the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ based on the research question.

**Step 2: Select the Test Statistic**
Choose an appropriate test based on:
- Sample size ($n \geq 30$ is "large"; $n < 30$ is "small")
- Whether population standard deviation $\sigma$ is known
- Whether the population is approximately normal

**Step 3: Determine the Critical Value(s) and Decision Rule**
Using the significance level $\alpha$ and the chosen test distribution (Z, t, $\chi^2$, F), find the critical value(s) from the statistical table.

**Step 4: Compute the Test Statistic from Sample Data**
Substitute the sample observations into the formula for the test statistic.

**Step 5: Make the Statistical Decision**
- If the test statistic falls in the **critical/rejection region** $\Rightarrow$ Reject $H_0$
- If the test statistic falls in the **acceptance region** $\Rightarrow$ Fail to reject $H_0$

**Step 6: Draw the Engineering/Managerial Conclusion**
Translate the statistical decision into a real-world statement relevant to the problem context.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Each Test

### 2.2.1 Z-Test for Population Mean (Large Sample, $\sigma$ Known)

**Why Z?** When the sample size is large ($n \geq 30$), by the **Central Limit Theorem**, the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal, regardless of the population's shape.

**How it works:** We standardize the difference between the sample mean $\bar{x}$ and the hypothesized mean $\mu_0$ by dividing by the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. This gives a standard normal variable $Z \sim N(0,1)$.

### 2.2.2 t-Test for Population Mean (Small Sample, $\sigma$ Unknown)

**Why t?** When $\sigma$ is unknown and must be estimated from the sample by $s$, the resulting statistic follows Student's t-distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom. The t-distribution has **heavier tails** than the normal, accounting for the extra uncertainty of estimating $\sigma$.

### 2.2.3 Z-Test for Population Proportion

Used when comparing a sample proportion $p$ to a hypothesized proportion $P$. The sampling distribution of $p$ is approximately normal for large $np$ and $n(1-p)$, both exceeding **5**.

### 2.2.4 Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test for Variance

Tests hypotheses about population variance $\sigma^2$. The statistic follows a chi-square distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom.

### 2.2.5 F-Test for Equality of Two Variances

Compares the variances of two independent samples. The ratio of sample variances follows an F-distribution with $(n_1-1, n_2-1)$ degrees of freedom.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: Comprehensive Formula Reference for Hypothesis Testing**

| Test Name | Test Statistic Formula | Critical Value Source | Degrees of Freedom | Reject $H_0$ When |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Z-test (mean, $\sigma$ known, $n \geq 30$)** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | Standard Normal $Z_\alpha$ | Not applicable | $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$ (two-tail) |
| **t-test (mean, $\sigma$ unknown, $n < 30$)** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | Student's t-table | $df = n - 1$ | $\vert t \vert > t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$ |
| **Z-test (proportion)** | $Z = \dfrac{p - P}{\sqrt{PQ/n}}$ where $Q = 1-P$ | Standard Normal $Z_\alpha$ | Not applicable | $\vert Z \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$ |
| **$\chi^2$-test (variance)** | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | Chi-square table | $df = n - 1$ | $\chi^2 < \chi^2_{1-\alpha/2}$ or $\chi^2 > \chi^2_{\alpha/2}$ |
| **F-test (two variances)** | $F = \dfrac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}$ (with $s_1^2 > s_2^2$) | F-table | $df_1 = n_1-1$, $df_2 = n_2-1$ | $F > F_{\alpha/2, df_1, df_2}$ |
| **Paired t-test** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{d} - 0}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$ | Student's t-table | $df = n - 1$ | $\vert t \vert > t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$ |

**Complementary Formulas:**

$$P(\text{Type I Error}) = \alpha$$

$$P(\text{Type II Error}) = \beta$$

$$\text{Power of Test} = 1 - \beta$$

$$\text{Standard Error of Mean} = SE(\bar{x}) = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$\text{Standard Error of Proportion} = SE(p) = \sqrt{\frac{PQ}{n}}$$

$$\text{Confidence Interval for } \mu = \bar{x} \pm Z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

---

## 2.4 Critical Values for Common Significance Levels (Memorize These)

> [!TIP]
> **Table 2: Standard Critical Values to Remember**

| Significance Level ($\alpha$) | $Z_{\alpha}$ (one-tail) | $Z_{\alpha/2}$ (two-tail) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **0.10 (10%)** | 1.282 | 1.645 |
| **0.05 (5%)** | 1.645 | 1.960 |
| **0.01 (1%)** | 2.326 | 2.576 |
| **0.001 (0.1%)** | 3.090 | 3.291 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these values?** They represent the Z-scores that cut off the upper tail of the standard normal distribution with probability $\alpha$. For example, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$ means 2.5% of the standard normal distribution lies above 1.96.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Area | Test Used | Engineering Decision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Z-test for proportion | Decide if defect rate has changed after process improvement |
| **Concrete Strength Testing** | t-test for mean | Verify if concrete mix meets design strength |
| **Manufacturing Tolerance** | $\chi^2$-test for variance | Check if process variability is within specification |
| **Comparing Two Machines** | F-test | Determine if two production lines have equal precision |
| **Drug/Pharmaceutical Trials** | Paired t-test | Assess before-after treatment effect on same subjects |
| **Six Sigma DMAIC** | All tests combined | Validate root-cause hypotheses during Improve phase |

---

## 2.6 Relationship Between Hypothesis Testing and Confidence Intervals

A two-tailed hypothesis test at significance level $\alpha$ is mathematically equivalent to checking whether the hypothesized value $\mu_0$ lies **outside** the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Equivalence:**
> - If $\mu_0$ is **inside** the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ CI $\Rightarrow$ Fail to reject $H_0$
> - If $\mu_0$ is **outside** the $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ CI $\Rightarrow$ Reject $H_0$

This dual relationship is heavily tested in KTU Part A questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Z-Test Statistic from First Principles

**Starting Point:** We have a random sample $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 1: Sampling Distribution of the Mean**

By the Central Limit Theorem (CLT), for large $n$:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

**Conversion Logic:** The sample mean is itself a random variable, normally distributed with mean equal to the population mean $\mu$ and variance equal to $\sigma^2/n$.

**Step 2: Standardization**

To convert $\bar{X}$ to a standard normal variable, we subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation of $\bar{X}$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This transformation is called **standardization**. By definition, any normal random variable minus its mean, divided by its standard deviation, follows $N(0,1)$.

**Step 3: Substitution Under $H_0$**

When testing $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$, we substitute the hypothesized value:

$$Z_{\text{calculated}} = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Under the assumption that $H_0$ is true, the population mean *is* $\mu_0$, so replacing $\mu$ with $\mu_0$ gives us the test statistic.

**Step 4: Decision Rule**

For a two-tailed test at level $\alpha$:

$$\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } \vert Z_{\text{calculated}} \vert > Z_{\alpha/2}$$

**Conversion Logic:** If the observed $Z$ is so extreme that it lies in the tails (where only $\alpha \times 100\%$ of values would fall under $H_0$), we conclude that the data is inconsistent with $H_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the t-Test Statistic

**Step 1: When $\sigma$ is Unknown**

Replace $\sigma$ with the sample standard deviation $s$:

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The sample standard deviation $s$ is an **unbiased estimator** of $\sigma$. The denominator $n-1$ (instead of $n$) is **Bessel's correction** to ensure $E[s^2] = \sigma^2$.

**Step 2: Form the t-Statistic**

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3: Degrees of Freedom**

The t-distribution has $n-1$ degrees of freedom because we lose one degree of freedom in estimating $\bar{X}$ from the sample.

$$df = n - 1$$

**Step 4: As $n \to \infty$, $t \to Z$**

For large $n$, $s \approx \sigma$, and the t-distribution converges to the standard normal. This justifies using the Z-test for large samples even when $\sigma$ is unknown.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Z-Test for Mean (Large Sample)

**Problem:** A machine produces bolts with a mean diameter of 10 mm. The standard deviation is 0.5 mm. A sample of 64 bolts has a mean diameter of 10.1 mm. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the mean diameter has changed.

**Solution Following the 6-Step KTU Procedure:**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu = 10 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(Mean has not changed)}$$
$$H_1: \mu \neq 10 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(Mean has changed — two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Statistic**

Since $n = 64 \geq 30$ and $\sigma = 0.5$ is known, use Z-test.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = Z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert > 1.96$.

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

Given: $\bar{x} = 10.1$, $\mu_0 = 10$, $\sigma = 0.5$, $n = 64$.

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{10.1 - 10}{0.5/\sqrt{64}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.1}{0.5/8} = \frac{0.1}{0.0625}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = 1.60$$

**Step 5: Statistical Decision**

Since $\vert Z_{\text{calc}} \vert = 1.60 < 1.96 = Z_{\text{critical}}$, the test statistic does **not** fall in the critical region.

$$\Rightarrow \text{Fail to reject } H_0$$

**Step 6: Engineering Conclusion**

At the 5% level of significance, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the mean diameter of bolts has changed from 10 mm. The observed difference of 0.1 mm can be attributed to random sampling variation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: t-Test for Mean (Small Sample)

**Problem:** A new fuel additive is claimed to increase mileage. A sample of 10 cars shows a mean increase of 2.5 km/litre with $s = 1.2$ km/litre. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the additive is effective.

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu \leq 0 \quad \text{(Additive has no effect)}$$
$$H_1: \mu > 0 \quad \text{(Additive increases mileage — right-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Statistic**

Since $n = 10 < 30$ and $\sigma$ is unknown, use t-test.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For right-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $df = n-1 = 9$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = t_{0.05, 9} = 1.833$$

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

Given: $\bar{x} = 2.5$, $\mu_0 = 0$, $s = 1.2$, $n = 10$.

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2.5 - 0}{1.2/\sqrt{10}}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{2.5}{1.2/3.1623} = \frac{2.5}{0.3795}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = 6.587$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $t_{\text{calc}} = 6.587 > 1.833 = t_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**

At the 5% level of significance, there is **strong statistical evidence** that the new fuel additive significantly increases mileage.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Z-Test for Proportion

**Problem:** A manufacturer claims that only 5% of products are defective. In a sample of 200, 15 are found defective. Test at $\alpha = 0.01$ whether the true defect rate is greater than 5%.

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: P = 0.05 \quad \text{(Defect rate is 5%)}$$
$$H_1: P > 0.05 \quad \text{(Defect rate is greater — right-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Statistic**

Sample proportion:

$$p = \frac{15}{200} = 0.075$$

Use Z-test for proportion (since $nP = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 > 5$).

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For right-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.01$:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = Z_{0.01} = 2.326$$

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{p - P}{\sqrt{PQ/n}} = \frac{0.075 - 0.05}{\sqrt{(0.05)(0.95)/200}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{0.0475/200}} = \frac{0.025}{\sqrt{0.0002375}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.025}{0.01541} = 1.622$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $Z_{\text{calc}} = 1.622 < 2.326 = Z_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Fail to reject } H_0$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**

At the 1% level of significance, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the defect rate exceeds 5%. The manufacturer's claim is not contradicted by the sample data.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 4: Chi-Square Test for Variance

**Problem:** The manufacturer of a precision bearing claims that the variance of the inner diameter is 0.0004 mm². A sample of 15 bearings gives $s^2 = 0.0006$ mm². Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the variance is greater than claimed.

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \sigma^2 = 0.0004 \quad \text{(Variance equals claimed value)}$$
$$H_1: \sigma^2 > 0.0004 \quad \text{(Variance is greater — right-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Statistic**

Use $\chi^2$ test for variance.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For right-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $df = n-1 = 14$:

$$\chi^2_{\text{critical}} = \chi^2_{0.05, 14} = 23.685$$

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

Given: $n = 15$, $s^2 = 0.0006$, $\sigma_0^2 = 0.0004$.

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2} = \frac{14 \times 0.0006}{0.0004}$$

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.0084}{0.0004} = 21.0$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 21.0 < 23.685 = \chi^2_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Fail to reject } H_0$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**

At the 5% level of significance, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the variance exceeds 0.0004 mm². The bearing manufacturing process is consistent with the claimed precision.

---

## 3.7 Power of the Test — Detailed Calculation

The **Power** of a test is the probability of correctly rejecting $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true:

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = P(\text{Reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ is true})$$

**Example:** For a two-tailed Z-test of $H_0: \mu = 100$ vs $H_1: \mu \neq 100$ with $\sigma = 15$, $n = 36$, $\alpha = 0.05$, find the power when the true mean is $\mu = 103$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Critical values for $\bar{X}$**

$$\bar{X}_{\text{critical}} = 100 \pm 1.96 \times \frac{15}{\sqrt{36}} = 100 \pm 4.9$$

So we reject $H_0$ if $\bar{X} < 95.1$ or $\bar{X} > 104.9$.

**Step 2: Calculate $\beta$ (Type II error probability)**

Under the true mean $\mu = 103$, $\bar{X} \sim N(103, 15^2/36)$:

$$\beta = P(95.1 \leq \bar{X} \leq 104.9 \mid \mu = 103)$$

$$Z_1 = \frac{95.1 - 103}{15/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{-7.9}{2.5} = -3.16$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{104.9 - 103}{15/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{1.9}{2.5} = 0.76$$

$$\beta = P(-3.16 \leq Z \leq 0.76) = \Phi(0.76) - \Phi(-3.16)$$

$$\beta = 0.7764 - 0.0008 = 0.7756$$

**Step 3: Power**

$$\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = 1 - 0.7756 = 0.2244 \approx 22.44\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If the true mean has actually shifted to 103 (a 3-unit shift), this test has only a 22.44% chance of detecting it. Low power indicates we need a **larger sample size** to improve detection capability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Research Question"] --> B["Step 1: State H0 and H1"]
    B --> C{"Step 2: Check Sample Conditions"}
    C -->|"n >= 30, sigma known"| D["Use Z-test for Mean"]
    C -->|"n < 30, sigma unknown"| E["Use t-test for Mean"]
    C -->|"Categorical Data, np > 5"| F["Use Z-test for Proportion"]
    C -->|"Test about Variance"| G["Use Chi-square Test"]
    C -->|"Compare Two Variances"| H["Use F-test"]
    D --> I["Step 3: Get Critical Value from Z-table"]
    E --> J["Step 3: Get Critical Value from t-table"]
    F --> I
    G --> K["Step 3: Get Critical Value from Chi-square table"]
    H --> L["Step 3: Get Critical Value from F-table"]
    I --> M["Step 4: Compute Test Statistic from Sample Data"]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{"Step 5: Is Test Stat in Critical Region?"}
    N -->|"Yes"| O["Reject H0 at alpha significance"]
    N -->|"No"| P["Fail to Reject H0"]
    O --> Q["Step 6: State Engineering Conclusion"]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R["End: Decision Communicated"]
```

**Figure 1: The Complete Hypothesis Testing Workflow**

---

## 4.2 Critical Regions Visualization (Z-Distribution)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TwoTailed["Two-Tailed Test: alpha = 0.05"]
        Z1["Z = -1.96"] --- M0["Critical Region: Reject H0 if Z less than -1.96 or Z greater than 1.96"] --- Z2["Z = +1.96"]
    end
```

**Figure 2: Two-Tailed Critical Region in Standard Normal Distribution**

The left tail (area $= \alpha/2 = 0.025$) and the right tail (area $= \alpha/2 = 0.025$) together constitute the rejection region for a two-tailed test. The middle region (area $= 1-\alpha = 0.95$) is the acceptance region.

---

## 4.3 Type I and Type II Error Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["True Distribution Under H0: Centered at mu0"] -->|"Sample Falls Here"| B["Fail to Reject H0: Correct Decision 1 minus alpha"]
    A -->|"Sample Falls in Right Tail"| C["Reject H0: Type I Error alpha"]
    D["True Distribution Under H1: Centered at mu1"] -->|"Sample Falls Here"| E["Reject H0: Correct Decision Power = 1 minus beta"]
    D -->|"Sample Overlaps H0 Region"| F["Fail to Reject H0: Type II Error beta"]
```

**Figure 3: Visualizing the Two Distributions and Four Outcomes**

When $H_0$ is true, the sampling distribution is centered at $\mu_0$. When $H_1$ is true, the true distribution shifts to $\mu_1$. The overlap between these two distributions creates the possibility of both types of errors.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hypothesis Testing Subsystems

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        IN1["Raw Sample Data X1 to Xn"]
        IN2["Claimed Parameter mu0 or P0"]
        IN3["Significance Level alpha"]
    end
    subgraph HYPFORM["HYPOTHESIS FORMULATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        HF1["Null Hypothesis H0: theta = theta0"]
        HF2["Alternative Hypothesis H1: theta not equal theta0"]
    end
    subgraph TESTEXEC["TEST EXECUTION SUBSYSTEM"]
        TE1["Test Statistic Calculator: Z, t, chi-square, F"]
        TE2["Critical Value Lookup Module"]
        TE3["Comparison Engine"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        OUT1["Statistical Verdict: Reject or Fail to Reject H0"]
        OUT2["p-value Computation"]
        OUT3["Engineering Conclusion Text"]
    end
    IN1 --> TE1
    IN2 --> HF1
    IN3 --> TE2
    HF1 --> HF2
    HF2 --> TE3
    TE1 --> TE3
    TE2 --> TE3
    TE3 --> OUT1
    TE3 --> OUT2
    OUT1 --> OUT3
```

**Figure 4: Functional Block Architecture of the Hypothesis Testing Process**

This diagram shows the modular flow: **Inputs** (data, claim, $\alpha$) $\rightarrow$ **Hypothesis Formulation** ($H_0$, $H_1$) $\rightarrow$ **Test Execution** (statistic calculation + critical value lookup + comparison) $\rightarrow$ **Outputs** (verdict + p-value + interpretation).

---

## 4.5 Comparison of Test Distributions

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["Test Selection Logic"] --> B{"Is Population Standard Deviation sigma Known?"}
    B -->|"Yes"| C{"Is Sample Size n >= 30?"}
    B -->|"No"| D{"Is Sample Size n >= 30?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| E["Z-test for Mean"]
    C -->|"No"| F["Z-test or t-test Acceptable, prefer Z"]
    D -->|"Yes"| G["Z-test using Sample s"]
    D -->|"No"| H["t-test with df = n minus 1"]
    A --> I["For Proportions: Z-test if np > 5"]
    A --> J["For Variance: Chi-square test"]
    A --> K["For Two Variances: F-test"]
```

**Figure 5: Decision Tree for Selecting the Appropriate Test**

---

## 4.6 The p-Value Approach (Alternative to Critical Value Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Compute Test Statistic Z or t"] --> B["Calculate p-value: Probability of Observing This Extreme Value Under H0"]
    B --> C{"Compare p-value to alpha"}
    C -->|"p-value less than alpha"| D["Reject H0: Result is Statistically Significant"]
    C -->|"p-value greater than or equal to alpha"| E["Fail to Reject H0: Result Not Statistically Significant"]
```

**Figure 6: p-Value Decision Procedure**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The p-value is the **smallest level of significance** at which we can reject $H_0$. A p-value of 0.03 means there is a 3% probability of observing data as extreme as ours, assuming $H_0$ is true. Since 3% < 5% = $\alpha$, we reject $H_0$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the following terms with respect to hypothesis testing:**
**(a)** Null Hypothesis
**(b)** Type II Error

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** The null hypothesis is a default statement of "no effect", "no change", or "no difference" about a population parameter. It is the hypothesis that is directly tested using sample data. By convention, $H_0$ always contains the equality sign ($=$, $\leq$, or $\geq$).

*Example:* "$H_0: \mu = 50$ kg" states that the true population mean weight is 50 kg.

**(b) Type II Error ($\beta$):** A Type II error occurs when we **fail to reject a false null hypothesis**. In other words, the null hypothesis is actually false, but the sample data does not provide enough evidence to reject it. The probability of committing a Type II error is denoted by $\beta$.

*Example:* Concluding that a new drug is ineffective when it actually does work.

---

### **Question A2:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Distinguish between one-tailed and two-tailed tests with an example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | One-Tailed Test | Two-Tailed Test |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Alternative Hypothesis** | $H_1: \theta > \theta_0$ or $H_1: \theta < \theta_0$ | $H_1: \theta \neq \theta_0$ |
| **Direction** | Deviation matters in only ONE direction | Deviation matters in BOTH directions |
| **Critical Region** | Lies in ONE tail only | Split between BOTH tails |
| **Critical Value** | $Z_\alpha$ (entire $\alpha$ in one tail) | $Z_{\alpha/2}$ (each tail has $\alpha/2$) |
| **Example** | Testing if a new drug is *better* than standard | Testing if a manufacturing process has *changed* (better or worse) |

**One-tailed Example:** $H_0: \mu \leq 100$ vs $H_1: \mu > 100$ — testing if mean strength is **greater** than 100.

**Two-tailed Example:** $H_0: \mu = 100$ vs $H_1: \mu \neq 100$ — testing if mean strength is **different** from 100.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Structure:** Each module typically contains questions of 14 marks. Students must answer with internal choice, meaning they choose between two alternative questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** State the null and alternative hypotheses and explain Type I and Type II errors for the following scenario: A company claims that the average lifetime of its LED bulbs is at least 5000 hours. The quality control manager wants to verify this claim using a sample. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A random sample of 36 electric bulbs from a large batch shows a mean lifetime of 4800 hours. The population standard deviation is 240 hours. Test at the 5% level of significance whether the company's claim is valid. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Hypothesis Formulation:**

The company's claim is that average lifetime is at least 5000 hours, which becomes the null hypothesis (status quo). The QC manager wants to detect if it is less.

$$H_0: \mu \geq 5000 \text{ hours} \quad \text{(Company's claim is true)}$$
$$H_1: \mu < 5000 \text{ hours} \quad \text{(Company's claim is false — left-tailed)}$$

**Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Concluding that the mean lifetime is less than 5000 hours (rejecting $H_0$) when in reality it is $\geq$ 5000 hours. This means wrongly accusing the company of producing inferior bulbs.

**Type II Error ($\beta$):** Concluding that the mean lifetime is at least 5000 hours (failing to reject $H_0$) when in reality it is less than 5000 hours. This means failing to detect actual inferior quality.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu \geq 5000 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu < 5000$$

**Step 2: Test Selection**

Since $n = 36 \geq 30$ (large sample) and $\sigma = 240$ is known, use **Z-test**.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For left-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = -Z_{0.05} = -1.645$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $Z_{\text{calc}} < -1.645$.

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

Given: $\bar{x} = 4800$, $\mu_0 = 5000$, $\sigma = 240$, $n = 36$.

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{4800 - 5000}{240/\sqrt{36}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-200}{240/6} = \frac{-200}{40} = -5.0$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $Z_{\text{calc}} = -5.0 < -1.645 = Z_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at 5\% level of significance}$$

**Step 6: Engineering Conclusion**

There is **overwhelming statistical evidence** that the average lifetime of LED bulbs is less than 5000 hours. The company's claim is **not valid** at the 5% significance level. The quality control manager should take corrective action.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** A soft drink manufacturer claims that each bottle contains 500 ml on average. A random sample of 25 bottles has a mean of 495 ml and standard deviation of 8 ml. Test at 1% level of significance whether the manufacturer's claim is valid. Use t-test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturer claims that only 2% of items in a lot are defective. A sample of 500 items revealed 18 defectives. Test at 5% level of significance whether the defect rate is greater than 2%. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \mu = 500 \text{ ml} \quad \text{(Manufacturer's claim)}$$
$$H_1: \mu \neq 500 \text{ ml} \quad \text{(Mean content has changed — two-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Selection**

Since $n = 25 < 30$ (small sample) and $\sigma$ is unknown, use **t-test** with $df = n-1 = 24$.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.01$ with $df = 24$:

$$t_{\text{critical}} = t_{0.005, 24} = 2.797$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert > 2.797$.

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

Given: $\bar{x} = 495$, $\mu_0 = 500$, $s = 8$, $n = 25$.

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{495 - 500}{8/\sqrt{25}}$$

$$t_{\text{calc}} = \frac{-5}{8/5} = \frac{-5}{1.6} = -3.125$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $\vert t_{\text{calc}} \vert = 3.125 > 2.797 = t_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at 1\% level of significance}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**

There is **strong statistical evidence** at the 1% significance level that the average content of soft drink bottles is **not** 500 ml. The manufacturer's claim is rejected.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

$$H_0: P = 0.02 \quad \text{(Defect rate is 2%)}$$
$$H_1: P > 0.02 \quad \text{(Defect rate is greater — right-tailed)}$$

**Step 2: Test Selection**

Sample proportion:

$$p = \frac{18}{500} = 0.036$$

Since $nP = 500 \times 0.02 = 10 > 5$, use **Z-test for proportion**.

**Step 3: Critical Value**

For right-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$Z_{\text{critical}} = Z_{0.05} = 1.645$$

**Step 4: Compute Test Statistic**

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{p - P}{\sqrt{PQ/n}} = \frac{0.036 - 0.02}{\sqrt{(0.02)(0.98)/500}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.016}{\sqrt{0.0196/500}} = \frac{0.016}{\sqrt{0.0000392}}$$

$$Z_{\text{calc}} = \frac{0.016}{0.006261} = 2.555$$

**Step 5: Decision**

Since $Z_{\text{calc}} = 2.555 > 1.645 = Z_{\text{critical}}$:

$$\Rightarrow \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at 5\% level of significance}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**

There is **sufficient statistical evidence** at the 5% significance level to conclude that the defect rate **exceeds 2%**. The manufacturer's claim is rejected, and the production process needs investigation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Critical Value Selection:** Students frequently confuse $Z_{0.05}$ (one-tailed = 1.645) with $Z_{0.025}$ (two-tailed = 1.96). Read the question carefully — the wording "greater than" or "less than" indicates one-tailed, while "different from" or "has changed" indicates two-tailed.
> 2. **Test Selection Errors:** Never use a t-test when $n \geq 30$ and $\sigma$ is known — always use Z-test. Conversely, never use Z-test for small samples with unknown $\sigma$.
> 3. **Hypothesis Direction:** The inequality in $H_1$ must match the research claim. If the question says "test if mean has *decreased*", then $H_1: \mu < \mu_0$ (left-tailed), not the other way around.
> 4. **Conclusion Wording:** Never say "We accept $H_0$" — the correct phrase is "We **fail to reject** $H_0$". Acceptance implies proof, which statistical testing cannot provide.
> 5. **Arithmetic Mistakes:** The most common numerical error is forgetting to take the square root when computing the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Always show this step explicitly.
> 6. **Degrees of Freedom:** For t-tests, $df = n-1$, not $n$. Forgetting this leads to looking up the wrong critical value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

### **Core Vocabulary**
- **$H_0$ (Null):** Statement of no effect; contains equality; the default assumption.
- **$H_1$ (Alternative):** Research claim; contains strict inequality; complement of $H_0$.
- **$\alpha$ (Level of Significance):** Probability of Type I Error; set by researcher (typically 0.05 or 0.01).
- **$\beta$ (Type II Error Probability):** Probability of failing to reject a false $H_0$.
- **Power = $1 - \beta$:** Probability of correctly rejecting a false $H_0$.
- **Test Statistic:** Standardized value computed from sample data.
- **Critical Region:** Set of values for which $H_0$ is rejected.
- **p-value:** Smallest $\alpha$ at which $H_0$ can be rejected; compare with $\alpha$ to decide.

### **Test Selection Quick-Reference**
- **$n \geq 30$, $\sigma$ known** $\rightarrow$ Z-test for mean
- **$n < 30$, $\sigma$ unknown** $\rightarrow$ t-test with $df = n-1$
- **Categorical/Proportion data, $nP > 5$** $\rightarrow$ Z-test for proportion
- **Hypothesis about variance** $\rightarrow$ $\chi^2$-test with $df = n-1$
- **Comparing two variances** $\rightarrow$ F-test
- **Paired observations (before-after)** $\rightarrow$ Paired t-test on differences

### **Critical Values to Memorize**
- $Z_{0.10} = 1.282$, $Z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $Z_{0.01} = 2.326$, $Z_{0.005} = 2.576$
- For t-distribution with large $df$, values approach Z-values.

### **The Decision Rule Pattern**
- **Two-tailed:** Reject $H_0$ if $\vert \text{test stat} \vert > \text{critical value}$
- **Right-tailed:** Reject $H_0$ if $\text{test stat} > + \text{critical value}$
- **Left-tailed:** Reject $H_0$ if $\text{test stat} < - \text{critical value}$

### **Key Relationships**
- $\alpha \uparrow$ (looser test) $\Rightarrow$ Power $\uparrow$ (better detection), but Type I risk increases
- Sample size $n \uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Power $\uparrow$, and both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ decrease
- Confidence interval $(1-\alpha)$ and two-tailed test at $\alpha$ are equivalent

### **Common Engineering Application Domains**
- **Quality Control:** Acceptance sampling, process capability validation
- **Reliability Engineering:** Mean-time-to-failure (MTTF) testing
- **Six Sigma:** DMAIC hypothesis validation
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Drug efficacy trials
- **Manufacturing:** Machine calibration verification
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete strength, material testing

### **Common Mistakes to Avoid**
1. Confusing one-tailed and two-tailed critical values
2. Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ for degrees of freedom
3. Saying "accept $H_0$" instead of "fail to reject $H_0$"
4. Forgetting to state both hypotheses explicitly
5. Not stating the engineering conclusion in plain language
6. Mixing up $\sigma$ (population) and $s$ (sample) in the formula
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Confidence Intervals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Confidence Intervals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Confidence Interval (CI):** A confidence interval is an interval estimate of an unknown population parameter constructed from sample data, expressed as a range of plausible values accompanied by a **confidence level (1 − α)** that quantifies the probability the interval contains the true parameter value under repeated sampling.

Mathematically, a $(1-\alpha) \times 100\%$ confidence interval for a population parameter $\theta$ is given by:

$$\text{CI}(\theta) = \hat{\theta} \pm \text{Margin of Error (E)}$$

where $\hat{\theta}$ is the point estimator of $\theta$, and the **Margin of Error** is:

$$E = \text{Critical Value} \times \text{Standard Error}(\hat{\theta})$$

---

### 1.2 Key Terminology — The "Three Pillars" of CI

| Term | Symbol | Plain Meaning |
|------|--------|----------------|
| Confidence Level | $1 - \alpha$ | Probability the interval *captures* the true parameter (commonly **0.90, 0.95, 0.99**) |
| Significance Level | $\alpha$ | Risk of being wrong (commonly **0.10, 0.05, 0.01**) |
| Margin of Error | $E$ | Half-width of the interval; how far we are willing to "swing" from the point estimate |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Common Misconception Cleared:** A 95% CI does **not** mean "the probability the parameter lies in this *specific* interval is 95%." The correct Frequentist interpretation: *If we repeatedly draw samples and construct CIs, approximately 95% of those intervals will contain the true parameter.*

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Archery Target" Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The Archery Range**  
> Imagine an archer who shoots **n = 25 arrows** at an invisible target (the true population mean $\mu$). The arrows scatter around the **sample mean $\bar{x}$** (your best single guess). The **confidence interval** is a *bracelet* placed around the cluster of arrows. The **95% confidence level** means: if the archer repeated this 25-arrow session **100 times**, roughly **95 of those bracelets** would actually encircle the invisible target's center. The bracelet's *width* is controlled by:
> - **Spread of arrows** (standard deviation $\sigma$)
> - **Number of arrows** (sample size $n$)
> - **Confidence demanded** (critical value $z_{\alpha/2}$ or $t_{\alpha/2}$)

---

### 1.4 Why Confidence Intervals? (Limitations of Point Estimation)

Point estimators (like $\bar{x}$, $\hat{p}$, $s^2$) give **a single number** but no information about **reliability** or **precision**. CIs address three critical questions a point estimate cannot:

1. **How close is $\hat{\theta}$ to $\theta$?** (precision)
2. **How confident are we in this closeness?** (reliability)
3. **What is the smallest and largest plausible value of $\theta$?** (range)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly emphasizes that **interval estimation is preferred** over point estimation in DOE because it conveys the **uncertainty** inherent in sampling from a finite population.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Architecture of a Confidence Interval

The construction of **any** confidence interval follows a unified 5-step logical sequence:

1. **Identify the parameter of interest** $\theta$ (e.g., $\mu$, $p$, $\sigma^2$, $\mu_1 - \mu_2$).
2. **Select an appropriate point estimator** $\hat{\theta}$ that is **unbiased** and has a **known sampling distribution**.
3. **Determine the standard error** $\text{SE}(\hat{\theta})$ — the standard deviation of $\hat{\theta}$ over all possible samples.
4. **Choose a confidence level** $1-\alpha$ and obtain the corresponding **critical value** from the appropriate distribution table ($Z$, $t$, or $\chi^2$).
5. **Construct the interval** $\hat{\theta} \pm (\text{Critical Value}) \times \text{SE}(\hat{\theta})$.

---

### 2.2 Decision Logic — Which CI to Use?

The choice of CI depends on three diagnostic questions:

| Question | Possible Answers | Resulting CI Uses |
|----------|------------------|---------------------|
| What is the parameter? | $\mu$, $p$, $\sigma^2$, $\mu_1-\mu_2$ | Different formula |
| Is population $\sigma$ known? | Yes / No | $Z$ (known) vs. $t$ (unknown) |
| Is the underlying distribution Normal? | Yes / No (use CLT if $n \geq 30$) | Exact vs. approximate CI |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Inside the markdown table below, the absolute value bars are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table syntax. In your exam answer sheet, write them as standard $\vert \cdot \vert$.

| # | Parameter | Conditions | Confidence Interval Formula | Critical Value Source |
|---|-----------|------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ known, Normal or $n \geq 30$ | $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Standard Normal $Z$ table |
| 2 | Mean $\mu$ | $\sigma$ unknown, Normal | $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$-table with $n-1$ d.f. |
| 3 | Proportion $p$ | Large sample, $np \geq 5$, $n(1-p) \geq 5$ | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | Standard Normal $Z$ table |
| 4 | Difference of Means $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ known, independent | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$ | Standard Normal $Z$ table |
| 5 | Difference of Means $\mu_1 - \mu_2$ | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$ unknown, independent | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2, \, n_1+n_2-2} \cdot s_p \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_1} + \dfrac{1}{n_2}}$ | $t$-table with pooled d.f. |
| 6 | Variance $\sigma^2$ | Normal population | $\left( \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2, \, n-1}}, \ \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, \, n-1}} \right)$ | $\chi^2$ table with $n-1$ d.f. |
| 7 | Standard Deviation $\sigma$ | Normal population | $\left( \sqrt{\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2, \, n-1}}}, \ \sqrt{\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, \, n-1}}} \right)$ | $\chi^2$ table with $n-1$ d.f. |
| 8 | Sample Size (for $\mu$, given $E$) | $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$, round **up** | Always round up to next integer | Uses $Z$ critical value |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pooled Standard Deviation** $s_p$ for the two-sample $t$-CI (Row 5) is defined as:  
> $$s_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1 - 1)s_1^2 + (n_2 - 1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}$$

---

### 2.4 The Three Foundational Distributions — When to Use Each

**A. Standard Normal Distribution (Z)**
- Used when $\sigma$ is **known** AND the population is Normal OR the sample size is large ($n \geq 30$, by Central Limit Theorem).
- Common critical values:
  - $z_{0.10/2} = z_{0.05} = 1.645$ (90% CI)
  - $z_{0.05/2} = z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (95% CI)
  - $z_{0.01/2} = z_{0.005} = 2.576$ (99% CI)

**B. Student's t-Distribution**
- Used when $\sigma$ is **unknown** and is replaced by sample standard deviation $s$, with the population assumed Normal.
- Degrees of freedom: $\nu = n - 1$.
- As $n \to \infty$, $t_{\alpha/2, \, n-1} \to z_{\alpha/2}$.

**C. Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Distribution**
- Used for **variance and standard deviation** CIs.
- The statistic $\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-1}$ follows a chi-square distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom.
- The chi-square is **asymmetric**, so the CI uses *different* critical values at each end: $\chi^2_{\alpha/2}$ and $\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2}$.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Engineering Field | CI Application |
|-------------------|----------------|
| **Quality Control (Manufacturing)** | CI for mean tensile strength of bolts, CI for defect proportion |
| **Civil Engineering** | CI for mean concrete compressive strength before pouring |
| **Industrial Engineering** | CI for process capability, CI for mean cycle time |
| **Reliability Engineering** | CI for mean time between failures (MTBF) |
| **Six Sigma / DOE** | CI for treatment effect, CI for factor main effect |
| **Pharmaceutical** | CI for mean drug concentration in a batch |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Confidence Interval for Population Mean ($\sigma$ Known)

**Starting Point:** By the Central Limit Theorem, the standardized statistic:

$$Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \sim N(0, 1)$$

**Step 1 — State the probability statement:**

$$P\!\left( -z_{\alpha/2} \leq \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \leq z_{\alpha/2} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

> *Logic:* Since $Z$ is standard normal, the area between $-z_{\alpha/2}$ and $+z_{\alpha/2}$ under the standard normal curve is exactly $1 - \alpha$ (the central area).

**Step 2 — Multiply all three parts by the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:**

$$P\!\left( -z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \bar{X} - \mu \leq z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

> *Logic:* Multiplying by a positive constant preserves inequality direction.

**Step 3 — Subtract $\bar{X}$ from all parts:**

$$P\!\left( -\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq -\mu \leq -\bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $-1$ (reverses inequalities) and rearrange to isolate $\mu$:**

$$P\!\left( \bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right) = 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Write the final interval estimator:**

$$\boxed{\text{CI}(\mu) = \left[ \bar{x} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \ \ \bar{x} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \right]}$$

> *Logic:* This is now a *random interval* (before sampling) that contains the *fixed but unknown* $\mu$ with probability $1-\alpha$. After sampling, we *realize* a specific numerical interval.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sample Size Formula for Estimating $\mu$

**Goal:** Find the minimum $n$ such that the margin of error $E$ does not exceed a specified value.

**Step 1 — Start with the margin of error formula:**

$$E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sqrt{n}$:**

$$\sqrt{n} = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides:**

$$n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \sigma^2}{E^2}$$

> *Logic:* If the computed $n$ is non-integer, **always round UP** to the next integer. This guarantees the margin of error does not exceed $E$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — CI for Mean (σ Known)

> **Problem:** A bottling plant fills 500 mL bottles. From past data, the standard deviation is known to be $\sigma = 4$ mL. A random sample of $n = 36$ bottles has a mean fill of $\bar{x} = 498.5$ mL. Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean fill volume.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify knowns:**
- $n = 36$, $\sigma = 4$ mL, $\bar{x} = 498.5$ mL, confidence level $= 0.95$ so $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Find the critical value:**
- For $1 - \alpha = 0.95$, we need $z_{\alpha/2} = z_{0.025}$.
- From standard normal table: $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error:**

$$\text{SE} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{4}{\sqrt{36}} = \dfrac{4}{6} = 0.6667 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the margin of error:**

$$E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE} = 1.96 \times 0.6667 = 1.3067 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 5 — Construct the interval:**

$$\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm E = 498.5 \pm 1.3067$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI} = (497.19, \ 500.81) \text{ mL}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Interpretation:** We are **95% confident** that the true mean fill volume lies between **497.19 mL and 500.81 mL**. Since the lower limit is below the 500 mL target, this hints at potential underfilling, warranting process investigation.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — CI for Mean (σ Unknown, Use t-Distribution)

> **Problem:** A quality engineer measures the hardness (in HV) of 10 steel samples. The data gives $\bar{x} = 215$ HV, $s = 8$ HV. Assume hardness is approximately Normal. Construct a **99% confidence interval** for the true mean hardness.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $n = 10$, $\bar{x} = 215$, $s = 8$, $1-\alpha = 0.99$ so $\alpha/2 = 0.005$, degrees of freedom $\nu = n - 1 = 9$.

**Step 2 — Find the critical value:**
- From $t$-table: $t_{0.005, \, 9} = 3.250$.

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error:**

$$\text{SE} = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{8}{\sqrt{10}} = \dfrac{8}{3.1623} = 2.5298$$

**Step 4 — Compute the margin of error:**

$$E = t_{\alpha/2, \, n-1} \cdot \text{SE} = 3.250 \times 2.5298 = 8.222$$

**Step 5 — Construct the interval:**

$$\text{CI} = 215 \pm 8.222 = (206.78, \ 223.22) \text{ HV}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** Notice that $t_{0.005,9} = 3.250$ is **much larger** than $z_{0.005} = 2.576$. This is because with small samples, the extra uncertainty from estimating $\sigma$ inflates the critical value.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — CI for a Proportion

> **Problem:** In a batch of 200 manufactured items, 12 are found defective. Construct a **90% confidence interval** for the true proportion of defective items.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the sample proportion:**

$$\hat{p} = \dfrac{x}{n} = \dfrac{12}{200} = 0.06$$

**Step 2 — Verify conditions:**
- $n\hat{p} = 200 \times 0.06 = 12 \geq 5$ ✓
- $n(1-\hat{p}) = 200 \times 0.94 = 188 \geq 5$ ✓ (Normal approximation valid)

**Step 3 — Find critical value:**
- For $1 - \alpha = 0.90$, $\alpha/2 = 0.05$, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

**Step 4 — Compute standard error:**

$$\text{SE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.06 \times 0.94}{200}} = \sqrt{0.000282} = 0.01679$$

**Step 5 — Compute margin of error:**

$$E = 1.645 \times 0.01679 = 0.02762$$

**Step 6 — Construct the interval:**

$$\text{CI} = 0.06 \pm 0.02762 = (0.0324, \ 0.0876)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Interpretation:** With 90% confidence, the true defective proportion lies between **3.24% and 8.76%**. The production manager can use this to benchmark Six Sigma yield calculations.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 4 — Sample Size Determination

> **Problem:** A manufacturer wants to estimate the mean lifetime of LED bulbs. Past data shows $\sigma = 200$ hours. How many bulbs must be tested to ensure the margin of error is at most **40 hours** at **95% confidence**?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify knowns:**
- $E = 40$, $\sigma = 200$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{1.96 \times 200}{40} \right)^2 = (9.8)^2 = 96.04$$

**Step 3 — Round up:**

$$\boxed{n = 97 \text{ bulbs}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Practical Note:** If $\sigma$ is *completely unknown* and a pilot study yields $s$, we use $s$ in place of $\sigma$ in the formula. KTU examiners may give a pilot $s$ value.

---

### 3.7 Worked Example 5 — CI for Variance

> **Problem:** A precision instrument records 15 measurements of a reference voltage. The sample variance is $s^2 = 0.0025$ V². Construct a **95% CI for the population variance $\sigma^2$**, assuming the data is Normal.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $n = 15$, $s^2 = 0.0025$, $\nu = n - 1 = 14$, $1-\alpha = 0.95$, $\alpha/2 = 0.025$.

**Step 2 — Find chi-square critical values from the table:**
- $\chi^2_{0.025, \, 14} = 26.119$ (upper tail, area to right = 0.025)
- $\chi^2_{0.975, \, 14} = 5.629$ (lower tail, area to right = 0.975)

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistake:** Students often swap these two values. Remember — the **larger** critical value ($\chi^2_{\alpha/2}$) goes in the **denominator of the lower limit**, and the **smaller** critical value ($\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2}$) goes in the **denominator of the upper limit**.

**Step 3 — Construct the interval:**

$$\text{Lower Limit} = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2, \, n-1}} = \dfrac{14 \times 0.0025}{26.119} = \dfrac{0.035}{26.119} = 0.00134$$

$$\text{Upper Limit} = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, \, n-1}} = \dfrac{14 \times 0.0025}{5.629} = \dfrac{0.035}{5.629} = 0.00622$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI}(\sigma^2) = (0.00134, \ 0.00622) \text{ V}^2}$$

**Step 4 (Optional) — CI for standard deviation by taking square root:**

$$\text{CI}(\sigma) = (\sqrt{0.00134}, \ \sqrt{0.00622}) = (0.0366, \ 0.0789) \text{ V}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — CI Construction Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Parameter of Interest] --> B{What is the parameter?}
    B -->|Mean mu| C{Is sigma known?}
    B -->|Proportion p| D{Is n*p >= 5 and n*1-p >= 5?}
    B -->|Variance sigma squared| E[Use chi-square with n-1 dof]
    B -->|Difference of Means| F{Two independent samples?}

    C -->|Yes| G[Use Z distribution: xbar +/- z alpha/2 times sigma over sqrt n]
    C -->|No| H[Use t distribution with n-1 dof: xbar +/- t alpha/2 times s over sqrt n]

    D -->|Yes| I[Use Z: p-hat +/- z alpha/2 times sqrt p-hat 1-p-hat over n]
    D -->|No| J[Increase n or use exact binomial method]

    F -->|Yes and known sigmas| K[Use Z with combined SE]
    F -->|Yes and unknown equal sigmas| L[Use t with pooled variance and n1+n2-2 dof]
    F -->|Paired samples| M[Use t on differences: d-bar +/- t alpha/2 times s_d over sqrt n]

    E --> N[Lower Limit = n-1 s squared over chi-square alpha/2]
    E --> O[Upper Limit = n-1 s squared over chi-square 1 minus alpha/2]

    G --> P[Report CI with 1 minus alpha confidence level]
    H --> P
    I --> P
    J --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Conceptual Map of CI Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Data: x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Compute Point Estimate: xbar or p-hat or s squared]
    A --> C[Compute Standard Error: SE]
    B --> D[Center of Interval]
    C --> E[Margin of Error Calculation]
    F[Confidence Level 1 minus alpha] --> G[Critical Value Lookup: z or t or chi-square]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Lower Limit: Center minus E]
    E --> I[Upper Limit: Center plus E]
    H --> J[Final Confidence Interval L to U]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Engineering Interpretation and Decision]
```

### 4.3 Visual Block — Margin of Error Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lower Limit L] -->|Margin of Error E| B[Point Estimate xbar]
    B -->|Margin of Error E| C[Upper Limit U]
    D[Critical Value z or t] -->|Multiplied by| E[Standard Error SE]
    E -->|Equals| F[Margin of Error E]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Effect of Parameters on CI Width

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CI Width 2 times Margin of Error] --> B[Confidence Level 1 minus alpha increases]
    A --> C[Sample Size n increases]
    A --> D[Population sigma increases]

    B --> E[CI Width INCREASES: Higher confidence requires wider net]
    C --> F[CI Width DECREASES: More data means more precision]
    D --> G[CI Width INCREASES: More variability means more uncertainty]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Pedagogical Insight:** The Mermaid schematics above emphasize the three levers controlling CI width: **confidence demanded**, **sample size**, and **process variability**. In DOE, we manipulate sample size and reduce variability (via blocking, randomization) to obtain tighter CIs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**  
*Define a confidence interval. Explain the meaning of a "95% confidence interval" with reference to the long-run frequency interpretation. Why is it incorrect to say "the probability that $\mu$ lies in this specific interval is 0.95"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A confidence interval is a range of values, derived from sample statistics, constructed to estimate an unknown population parameter $\theta$ with a specified level of confidence $(1-\alpha)$. It is expressed as $\hat{\theta} \pm E$, where $E$ is the margin of error.

**Long-Run Frequency Interpretation (1 Mark):** A 95% confidence interval means that if we were to repeat the sampling procedure an infinite number of times and construct a CI from each sample, approximately 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter $\mu$, and 5% would not.

**Common Misconception (1 Mark):** Once a specific interval is computed (e.g., (497.19, 500.81)), the parameter $\mu$ either lies inside it or it does not — the probability is 0 or 1. The 95% refers to the *procedure*, not to a *realized* interval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**  
*What is the difference between $Z$-based and $t$-based confidence intervals for the mean? Under what conditions is each used?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | $Z$-based CI | $t$-based CI |
|--------|--------------|----------------|
| Standard Deviation | Population $\sigma$ **known** | Population $\sigma$ **unknown**, estimated by $s$ |
| Distribution Shape | Standard Normal $N(0,1)$ | Student's $t$ with $n-1$ d.f. |
| Conditions | Normal population OR $n \geq 30$ (CLT) | Population assumed Normal |
| Critical Value | $z_{\alpha/2}$ (constant: 1.96 for 95%) | $t_{\alpha/2, n-1}$ (decreases as $n$ grows, approaches 1.96) |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** When $\sigma$ is unknown — which is the **realistic engineering scenario** — we use the $t$-distribution to account for the additional uncertainty introduced by estimating $\sigma$ from the sample.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Format:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and are split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in the exam.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — CI for Mean & Sample Size

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A textile mill produces fabric rolls with a target weight of 50 kg. From historical records, the population standard deviation is $\sigma = 1.5$ kg. A random sample of $n = 49$ rolls yields a sample mean weight of $\bar{x} = 49.6$ kg.  
(i) Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true mean fabric weight.  
(ii) Construct a **99% confidence interval** for the true mean fabric weight.  
(iii) Compare the two intervals and explain the trade-off involved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The mill manager wants the margin of error in estimating the mean weight to be **at most 0.3 kg at 95% confidence level**. How many rolls must be sampled? If the manager wants the same precision at **99% confidence**, how many additional rolls must be sampled?

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:** $n = 49$, $\sigma = 1.5$ kg, $\bar{x} = 49.6$ kg.

**(i) 95% CI** — Step-by-step:

Step 1: Critical value $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (from Z-table).  *(0.5 Mark)*

Step 2: Standard error:

$$\text{SE} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \dfrac{1.5}{\sqrt{49}} = \dfrac{1.5}{7} = 0.2143 \text{ kg} \quad \text{*(0.5 Mark)*}$$

Step 3: Margin of error:

$$E_{95} = 1.96 \times 0.2143 = 0.420 \text{ kg} \quad \text{*(0.5 Mark)*}$$

Step 4: Interval:

$$\text{CI}_{95} = 49.6 \pm 0.420 = (49.18, \ 50.02) \text{ kg} \quad \text{*(0.5 Mark)*}$$

**(ii) 99% CI:**

Step 1: Critical value $z_{0.005} = 2.576$ (from Z-table).  *(0.5 Mark)*

Step 2: Margin of error:

$$E_{99} = 2.576 \times 0.2143 = 0.552 \text{ kg} \quad \text{*(0.5 Mark)*}$$

Step 3: Interval:

$$\text{CI}_{99} = 49.6 \pm 0.552 = (49.05, \ 50.15) \text{ kg} \quad \text{*(0.5 Mark)*}$$

**(iii) Comparison and Trade-off (1 Mark):**
- The 99% CI (width = 1.104 kg) is **wider** than the 95% CI (width = 0.840 kg).
- **Trade-off:** Higher confidence ⇒ wider interval. We sacrifice *precision* (narrowness) to gain *reliability* (higher probability of containing $\mu$).

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**(i) Sample size for 95% CI with $E = 0.3$ kg:**  *(3.5 Marks)*

Step 1: Apply the formula:

$$n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2 = \left( \dfrac{1.96 \times 1.5}{0.3} \right)^2$$

Step 2: Compute numerator:

$$1.96 \times 1.5 = 2.94$$

Step 3: Compute the ratio:

$$\dfrac{2.94}{0.3} = 9.8$$

Step 4: Square:

$$n = 9.8^2 = 96.04$$

Step 5: Round up:

$$\boxed{n_{95} = 97 \text{ rolls}}$$

**(ii) Sample size for 99% CI with $E = 0.3$ kg:**  *(3.5 Marks)*

Step 1: Apply the formula with $z_{0.005} = 2.576$:

$$n = \left( \dfrac{2.576 \times 1.5}{0.3} \right)^2$$

Step 2: Compute:

$$n = \left( \dfrac{3.864}{0.3} \right)^2 = (12.88)^2 = 165.89$$

Step 3: Round up:

$$\boxed{n_{99} = 166 \text{ rolls}}$$

Step 4: Additional rolls required:

$$\Delta n = 166 - 97 = 69 \text{ additional rolls}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing confidence from 95% to 99% requires **71% more samples** (69 additional rolls) for the same precision. This is a critical cost-vs-certainty trade-off in quality assurance.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — CI for Proportion & Variance

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** An electronic chip manufacturer inspects a batch of 400 chips and finds 32 defective.  
(i) Construct a **90% confidence interval** for the true proportion of defective chips.  
(ii) The industry standard defect rate is 5%. Using your CI, comment on whether the batch meets the standard.  
(iii) If the manufacturer wants the margin of error for the proportion to be at most 0.02 at 99% confidence, how many chips must be inspected?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A machine tool produces ball bearings with diameter (in mm). A sample of $n = 21$ bearings yields a sample variance of $s^2 = 0.04$ mm². Assume diameters follow a Normal distribution.  
(i) Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true variance $\sigma^2$.  
(ii) Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the true standard deviation $\sigma$.  
(iii) Engineering tolerance requires $\sigma \leq 0.25$ mm. Based on the CI for $\sigma$, does the process meet the tolerance?

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:** $n = 400$, $x = 32$ defectives.

**(i) 90% CI for $p$:**  *(2 Marks)*

Step 1: Sample proportion:

$$\hat{p} = \dfrac{32}{400} = 0.08$$

Step 2: Check conditions: $n\hat{p} = 32 \geq 5$, $n(1-\hat{p}) = 368 \geq 5$ ✓

Step 3: Critical value $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.

Step 4: Standard error:

$$\text{SE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.08 \times 0.92}{400}} = \sqrt{0.000184} = 0.01356$$

Step 5: Margin of error:

$$E = 1.645 \times 0.01356 = 0.02231$$

Step 6: Interval:

$$\boxed{\text{CI} = 0.08 \pm 0.0223 = (0.0577, \ 0.1023)}$$

**(ii) Industry Standard Comparison (2 Marks):**

The industry standard is 5% = 0.05. Since the **upper limit** of the 90% CI is 0.1023, which is **well above** 0.05, we **cannot conclude** that the batch meets the industry standard at the 5% defect rate. The actual defect rate could be as high as 10.23%, exceeding the standard significantly.

**(iii) Sample size for $E = 0.02$, 99% confidence (3 Marks):**

Step 1: Use pilot estimate $\hat{p} = 0.08$ (or use conservative $p = 0.5$ if no prior info).

Step 2: Critical value $z_{0.005} = 2.576$.

Step 3: Apply formula:

$$n = \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}^2 \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{E^2} = \dfrac{(2.576)^2 \times 0.08 \times 0.92}{(0.02)^2}$$

Step 4: Compute numerator:

$$6.6358 \times 0.0736 = 0.4884$$

Step 5: Divide by $E^2 = 0.0004$:

$$n = \dfrac{0.4884}{0.0004} = 1221.0$$

Step 6: Round up:

$$\boxed{n = 1221 \text{ chips}}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:** $n = 21$, $s^2 = 0.04$ mm², $1-\alpha = 0.95$, $\nu = 20$.

**(i) 95% CI for $\sigma^2$ (3 Marks):**

Step 1: Chi-square critical values (from table):
- $\chi^2_{0.025, \, 20} = 34.170$
- $\chi^2_{0.975, \, 20} = 9.591$

Step 2: Lower limit:

$$L = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2, \, n-1}} = \dfrac{20 \times 0.04}{34.170} = \dfrac{0.8}{34.170} = 0.02342$$

Step 3: Upper limit:

$$U = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, \, n-1}} = \dfrac{0.8}{9.591} = 0.08342$$

Step 4: Interval:

$$\boxed{\text{CI}(\sigma^2) = (0.0234, \ 0.0834) \text{ mm}^2}$$

**(ii) 95% CI for $\sigma$ (2 Marks):**

Take square roots of the limits from (i):

$$\text{Lower} = \sqrt{0.0234} = 0.1530 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Upper} = \sqrt{0.0834} = 0.2888 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI}(\sigma) = (0.153, \ 0.289) \text{ mm}}$$

**(iii) Tolerance Check (2 Marks):**

The tolerance requires $\sigma \leq 0.25$ mm. The 95% CI for $\sigma$ is **(0.153, 0.289)**. Since the **upper bound (0.289) exceeds 0.25**, and the interval *includes* values above 0.25, we **cannot conclude with 95% confidence** that the process meets the tolerance. The process variability is potentially too high, and the engineer should investigate the manufacturing setup for sources of excess variation.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> **Pitfall 1 (Common — loses 2 marks):** In the chi-square CI, students often *swap* the critical values, placing $\chi^2_{0.975}$ in the lower denominator and $\chi^2_{0.025}$ in the upper denominator. **Remember:** Larger $\chi^2$ in the **denominator of the smaller** quantity (lower limit).  
> **Pitfall 2 (Common — loses 1–2 marks):** Failing to verify the Normal assumption for the $t$ and $\chi^2$ CIs. KTU examiners deduct marks if the Normal assumption is not stated.  
> **Pitfall 3 (Common — loses 1 mark):** Forgetting to **round up** the sample size to the next integer. Always write: "Since $n$ must be a whole number, we round up to $n = 97$."  
> **Pitfall 4 (Common — loses 1 mark):** Not writing the **units** (mL, mm, HV, kg) in the final CI. Always include units — it's a free mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Critical Things to Remember for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **CI Formula (Master Pattern):** $\text{CI} = \hat{\theta} \pm (\text{Critical Value}) \times \text{SE}(\hat{\theta})$
- **Use $Z$ when $\sigma$ is known; use $t$ when $\sigma$ is unknown** (with $\nu = n-1$).
- **Critical $Z$ values to memorize:** $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.005} = 2.576$.
- **Sample size formula:** $n = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E} \right)^2$ — always **round up**.
- **Proportion conditions:** $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ for Normal approximation to hold.
- **Variance CI** is the only **asymmetric** CI — uses two *different* chi-square values.
- **Pooled variance** $s_p^2 = \dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1+n_2-2}$ for equal-variance two-sample $t$-CI.
- **Always include units** in the final interval.
- **Always state assumptions** (Normal population, random sample, independence).
- **Interpretation template:** "We are $X\%$ confident that the true [parameter] lies between [lower] and [upper] [units]."
- **Three CI-width levers:**
  1. Higher confidence → **wider** CI.
  2. Larger $n$ → **narrower** CI.
  3. Higher $\sigma$ → **wider** CI.
- **Frequentist interpretation:** The confidence refers to the *procedure*, not a *realized* interval.
- **Degrees of freedom reference:**
  - $t$-CI for mean: $\nu = n - 1$
  - $t$-CI for difference (equal variance): $\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2$
  - $\chi^2$-CI for variance: $\nu = n - 1$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Effect Model Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Random Effect Model Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Design of Experiments (DOE) framework, a **Random Effect Model (REM)** — also referred to as **Model II ANOVA** or **Components of Variance Model** — is a statistical model in which the levels of the treatment (factor) under investigation are considered to be a **random sample drawn from a larger population** of possible treatment levels, and the inference is extended to the entire population of treatments rather than restricted to the specific levels tested in the experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Canonical Form):**
> The linear statistical model for a one-way Random Effect Model is expressed as:
> $$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$
> where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, p$ (number of treatments/levels sampled) and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, n_i$ (number of replications within the $i^{th}$ level). The classical assumptions are:
> 1. $\tau_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$ — random treatment effects are independent and identically normally distributed with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{\tau}^2$.
> 2. $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ — random errors are iid normal with mean zero and variance $\sigma^2$.
> 3. $\tau_i$ and $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are **mutually independent** random variables.

The **two key variance components** of the model are:
- $\sigma_{\tau}^2$ = **variance component due to treatments** (between-group variance)
- $\sigma^2$ = **variance component due to error** (within-group variance)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition: "The Bakery Inspector Analogy"** 🥖
>
> Imagine you are a quality control manager at a large bakery chain. You want to know how much variability in cake moisture content is due to the **oven** being used.
>
> - **Fixed Effect Model thinking**: You would pick *5 specific ovens* (Oven A, B, C, D, E) and conclude ONLY about those 5 ovens. "Oven B makes drier cakes than Oven D." — Inference is restricted.
> - **Random Effect Model thinking**: You randomly sample 5 ovens from a warehouse of 200 ovens. The 5 ovens are a *representative sample*. You want to estimate the overall **variability in oven performance across the entire population of 200 ovens** — not just the 5 sampled. — Inference is broad.
>
> The key distinction: **Fixed = specific levels of interest. Random = levels are a sample of a larger population.**

### 1.3 Fixed vs Random Effect Model — The Critical Comparison

| Feature | **Fixed Effect Model (Model I)** | **Random Effect Model (Model II)** |
|---|---|---|
| Treatment levels | **Deliberately chosen** by the experimenter | **Randomly sampled** from a population |
| Inference scope | Only the tested levels | Entire population of levels |
| Treatment effect $\tau_i$ | Fixed unknown constant | Random variable: $\tau_i \sim N(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$ |
| Goal | Estimate treatment means $\mu_i$ | Estimate **variance components** $\sigma_{\tau}^2, \sigma^2$ |
| Hypotheses | $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \ldots = \mu_p$ | $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ |
| Error term in F-ratio | $MS_E$ (no error in numerator) | $MS_E$ in both numerator and denominator |
| Distribution of F | $F_{(p-1, N-p)}$ | $F_{(p-1, N-p)}$ (same form) |
| Example | Comparing 3 **specific** catalysts | Estimating variability among **any** randomly selected batches |

### 1.4 Why Use a Random Effect Model? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications (KTU 2024 Industrial Context):**
> - **Manufacturing**: Estimating batch-to-batch variability in pharmaceutical production.
> - **Textile Industry**: Variance in fabric strength across randomly sampled looms.
> - **Quality Control**: Assessing variability of measurements across different operators.
> - **Agriculture**: Variability in crop yield across randomly selected plots of land.
> - **Reliability Engineering**: Estimating component-to-component variability in mass production.

The **two primary objectives** of a random effect model are:
1. **Estimate variance components** ($\sigma_{\tau}^2$ and $\sigma^2$) — the absolute scientific goal.
2. **Hypothesis test** whether treatment variation is greater than zero — operational decision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Model Decomposition

The **one-way random effects model** partitions every observation $Y_{ij}$ as:

$$Y_{ij} = \underbrace{\mu}_{\text{grand mean}} + \underbrace{\tau_i}_{\text{random treatment effect}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ij}}_{\text{random error}}$$

**Where:**
- $\mu$ = **grand mean** (overall population mean — a fixed constant)
- $\tau_i$ = deviation of the $i^{th}$ treatment mean from the grand mean (a **random variable**)
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random experimental error (a **random variable**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus):**
> In the Fixed Effect Model, we impose the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{p} \tau_i = 0$ to make parameters estimable.
> In the Random Effect Model, $\sum \tau_i = 0$ is **NOT imposed as a constraint** — it emerges naturally because $\tau_i$ is a random variable with $E(\tau_i) = 0$. The constraint is replaced by a **probabilistic distributional assumption**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step ANOVA Construction Logic

**Step 1: Total Variability (SST — Sum of Squares Total)**
The total variation of all $N = \sum n_i$ observations about the grand mean $\bar{Y}_{..}$:

$$SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

**Step 2: Treatment (Between-Group) Variability (SSTr)**
The variation of treatment means $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ about the grand mean:

$$SS_{Tr} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} n_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

**Step 3: Error (Within-Group) Variability (SSE)**
The pooled variation of observations about their respective treatment means:

$$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$$

**Step 4: Fundamental Partitioning Identity (KTU Theorem)**

$$SS_T = SS_{Tr} + SS_E$$

**Step 5: Degrees of Freedom**
- $df_{Tr} = p - 1$ (treatment degrees of freedom)
- $df_E = N - p$ (error degrees of freedom)
- $df_T = N - 1$ (total degrees of freedom)

### 2.3 Expected Mean Squares (EMS) — The Heart of Random Effects

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EMS Table (Equally Replicated Case, $n_i = n$ for all $i$):**
>
> | Source | SS | df | MS | Expected Mean Square (EMS) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Treatments | $SS_{Tr}$ | $p-1$ | $MS_{Tr} = \dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{p-1}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ |
> | Error | $SS_E$ | $N-p$ | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{N-p}$ | $\sigma^2$ |
> | **Total** | $SS_T$ | $N-1$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why EMS differs from Fixed Model?**
> In the Fixed Effect Model, $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + \frac{\sum n_i \tau_i^2}{p-1}$ — contains treatment means.
> In the Random Effect Model, the $\tau_i$ are random, so taking expectations yields $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ — contains **variance components**, not treatment means.

For **unequal replication** ($n_i$ varies), the EMS of treatments becomes:

$$E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n' \sigma_{\tau}^2 \quad \text{where} \quad n' = \frac{1}{p-1}\left(N - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} n_i^2}{N}\right)$$

### 2.4 Hypothesis Testing in Random Effect Model

**Test Statistic:**

$$F_0 = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$$

**Null Hypothesis:** $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ (no treatment variability; all treatments are identical)
**Alternative Hypothesis:** $H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0$ (treatment variability exists)

**Rejection Criterion (Upper-Tail Test):**
Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, (p-1), (N-p)}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight (KTU Board Favorite):**
> The F-distribution is the **same** as in the Fixed Model case — $F \sim F_{(p-1, N-p)}$ — because under $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$, the ratio $MS_{Tr}/MS_E$ simplifies to a ratio of two independent chi-squared variates scaled by their respective dfs.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Model | $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ | $i=1..p$, $j=1..n_i$ |
| 2 | Treatment Mean | $\bar{Y}_{i.} = \dfrac{\sum_j Y_{ij}}{n_i}$ | Sample mean of $i^{th}$ group |
| 3 | Grand Mean | $\bar{Y}_{..} = \dfrac{\sum_i \sum_j Y_{ij}}{N}$ | Weighted by $n_i$ |
| 4 | $SS_T$ | $\sum \sum (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | $df = N-1$ |
| 5 | $SS_{Tr}$ | $\sum n_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | $df = p-1$ |
| 6 | $SS_E$ | $\sum \sum (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i.})^2$ | $df = N-p$ |
| 7 | $MS_{Tr}$ | $SS_{Tr} / (p-1)$ | $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ |
| 8 | $MS_E$ | $SS_E / (N-p)$ | $E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$ |
| 9 | F-statistic | $F_0 = MS_{Tr} / MS_E$ | $F \sim F_{(p-1, N-p)}$ under $H_0$ |
| 10 | **Variance Component $\hat{\sigma}^2$** | $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$ | ANOVA estimator (Method of Moments) |
| 11 | **Variance Component $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$** | $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n}$ | Can be **negative** (set to 0) |
| 12 | $n'$ (unbalanced) | $\dfrac{1}{p-1}\left(N - \sum n_i^2/N\right)$ | Effective replication |
| 13 | Confidence Interval for $\sigma_{\tau}^2$ | Uses Satterthwaite approximation | Wide intervals, often negative lower bound |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Engineers Use Random Effect Models:**
> 1. **Process Capability Studies**: Estimating batch-to-batch variance in pharmaceutical/chemical plants.
> 2. **Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA)**: Gauge R&R studies where operators/instruments are randomly sampled.
> 3. **Reliability Engineering**: Estimating variability in component lifetimes across randomly sampled production lots.
> 4. **Tolerance Stacking**: Computing combined tolerance when source components have random variation.
> 5. **Six-Sigma DMAIC Phase**: Establishing baseline process variability before improvement interventions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EMS for Treatments in Random Effect Model

**Step 1: Express $MS_{Tr}$ algebraically**

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{p-1} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} n_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2}{p-1}$$

**Step 2: Expand $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ using the linear model**

$$\bar{Y}_{i.} = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} Y_{ij} = \frac{1}{n_i} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (\mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij})$$

**Step 3: Take expectation of the linear combination**

$$E(\bar{Y}_{i.}) = \mu + E(\tau_i) + \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_{j=1}^{n_i} E(\varepsilon_{ij}) = \mu + 0 + 0 = \mu$$

**Step 4: Compute the deviation of treatment mean from grand mean**

$$\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..} = (\mu + \tau_i + \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}) - \mu = \tau_i + \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}$$

where $\bar{\varepsilon}_{i.} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_j \varepsilon_{ij}$.

**Step 5: Square the deviation**

$$(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2 = (\tau_i + \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.})^2 = \tau_i^2 + 2\tau_i \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.} + \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}^2$$

**Step 6: Take expectation and exploit independence**

Because $\tau_i$ and $\bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}$ are independent, $E(2\tau_i \bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}) = 0$. Therefore:

$$E[(\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2] = E(\tau_i^2) + E(\bar{\varepsilon}_{i.}^2) = \sigma_{\tau}^2 + \frac{\sigma^2}{n_i}$$

**Step 7: Substitute into $E(MS_{Tr})$ assuming balanced design $n_i = n$**

$$E(MS_{Tr}) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{p} n \left(\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \sigma^2/n\right)}{p-1} = \frac{n \cdot p \cdot \sigma_{\tau}^2 + p \cdot \sigma^2}{p-1} = \frac{n p \sigma_{\tau}^2 + p \sigma^2}{p-1}$$

**Step 8: Apply algebraic simplification using the identity $p = (p-1) + 1$**

$$\begin{aligned}
E(MS_{Tr}) &= \frac{n(p-1)\sigma_{\tau}^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2 + (p-1)\sigma^2 + \sigma^2}{p-1} \\
&= n\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \sigma^2 + \frac{n\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \sigma^2}{p-1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9: Final simplification (using the constraint-free REM assumption $\sum \tau_i$ free)**

The classical result for REM is:

$$\boxed{E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Variance Component Estimators (Method of Moments)

**Step 1: Set up the moment equations**

Equate the observed mean squares to their expected values:

$$MS_E = \hat{\sigma}^2$$
$$MS_{Tr} = \hat{\sigma}^2 + n \hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$$

**Step 2: Solve the first equation for $\hat{\sigma}^2$**

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the second equation and solve for $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$**

$$MS_{Tr} = MS_E + n \hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \frac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Variance Estimator (KTU Board Pitfall):**
> The estimator $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = (MS_{Tr} - MS_E)/n$ can be **negative** if $MS_{Tr} < MS_E$. This happens because of sampling variability. The convention is to **set $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = 0$** in such cases and report the estimate as zero. This is the **ANOVA Method of Moments Estimator**.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Standard)

> **PROBLEM STATEMENT:**
> An engineer studies the variability in compressive strength of concrete produced by 5 randomly selected mixing machines. Each machine produces 4 concrete cubes. The data (in MPa) is summarized as follows:
>
> | Machine $i$ | $\bar{Y}_{i.}$ (Mean) | $n_i$ | $s_i^2$ (Variance) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 32.5 | 4 | 4.2 |
> | 2 | 35.0 | 4 | 3.8 |
> | 3 | 30.0 | 4 | 4.5 |
> | 4 | 33.5 | 4 | 4.0 |
> | 5 | 34.0 | 4 | 3.5 |
>
> Test whether significant machine-to-machine variability exists at $\alpha = 0.05$. Estimate variance components.

**SOLUTION (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**
