$$p = 5, \quad n_i = 4, \quad N = 20, \quad \alpha = 0.05$$

**Step 2: Compute the grand mean**

$$\bar{Y}_{..} = \frac{\sum n_i \bar{Y}_{i.}}{N} = \frac{4(32.5 + 35.0 + 30.0 + 33.5 + 34.0)}{20} = \frac{4(165.0)}{20} = \frac{660}{20} = 33.0$$

**Step 3: Compute $SS_{Tr}$ (Between-Machine Sum of Squares)**

$$SS_{Tr} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} n_i (\bar{Y}_{i.} - \bar{Y}_{..})^2$$

$$= 4[(32.5-33.0)^2 + (35.0-33.0)^2 + (30.0-33.0)^2 + (33.5-33.0)^2 + (34.0-33.0)^2]$$

$$= 4[(-0.5)^2 + (2.0)^2 + (-3.0)^2 + (0.5)^2 + (1.0)^2]$$

$$= 4[0.25 + 4.00 + 9.00 + 0.25 + 1.00] = 4 \times 14.50 = 58.00$$

**Step 4: Compute $SS_E$ (Within-Machine Sum of Squares)**

$$SS_E = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (n_i - 1) s_i^2 = 3(4.2 + 3.8 + 4.5 + 4.0 + 3.5) = 3 \times 20.0 = 60.00$$

**Step 5: Compute the Mean Squares**

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{p-1} = \frac{58.00}{4} = 14.50$$

$$MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{N-p} = \frac{60.00}{15} = 4.00$$

**Step 6: Construct the ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Machines (Between) | 58.00 | 4 | 14.50 | $\sigma^2 + 4\sigma_{\tau}^2$ | 3.625 |
| Error (Within) | 60.00 | 15 | 4.00 | $\sigma^2$ | |
| **Total** | **118.00** | **19** | | | |

**Step 7: Formulate Hypotheses**

$$H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0 \quad \text{(no machine-to-machine variability)}$$
$$H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0 \quad \text{(machines differ in mean strength)}$$

**Step 8: Determine the critical value**

$$F_{0.05, (p-1), (N-p)} = F_{0.05, 4, 15} = 3.057 \text{ (from F-tables)}$$

**Step 9: Decision Rule**

$$F_0 = 3.625 > F_{0.05, 4, 15} = 3.057 \implies \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** There is significant evidence at the 5% level that machine-to-machine variability exists in concrete compressive strength.

**Step 10: Estimate Variance Components**

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = 4.00$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \frac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{14.50 - 4.00}{4} = \frac{10.50}{4} = 2.625$$

**Step 11: Engineering Interpretation**

Total variability in concrete strength = $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 + \hat{\sigma}^2 = 2.625 + 4.00 = 6.625$.

**Coefficient of variation due to machines** = $\sqrt{2.625/6.625} = \sqrt{0.3962} \approx 62.9\%$.

This indicates that **machine variation contributes ~39.6%** of the total variance — a substantial source of inconsistency that warrants quality control intervention.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Decision Tree: Fixed vs Random Effect Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: DOE Problem Statement"] --> B{"How were treatment levels selected?"}
    B -->|"Deliberately chosen (specific levels)"| C["FIXED EFFECT MODEL (Model I)"]
    B -->|"Randomly sampled from a larger population"| D["RANDOM EFFECT MODEL (Model II)"]
    
    C --> C1["Goal: Estimate treatment MEANS"]
    C1 --> C2["EMS contains: sigma^2 + (treatment sum of squares)"]
    
    D --> D1["Goal: Estimate VARIANCE COMPONENTS"]
    D1 --> D2["EMS contains: sigma^2 + n × sigma_tau^2"]
    D2 --> D3["Hypothesis: H0: sigma_tau^2 = 0"]
    D3 --> D4["F-test: MS_Tr / MS_E"]
    
    C2 --> E["ANOVA Table Construction"]
    D4 --> E
    E --> F["Inference / Engineering Decision"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Random Effect Model Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Population of Treatments (Infinite/Large)"] --> A2["Random Sampling: p levels selected"]
    A2 --> A3["Treatment i (i=1,2,...,p)"]
    A3 --> A4["n_i Replications per treatment"]
    A4 --> A5["Observations Y_ij"]
    
    A5 --> B1["Compute Grand Mean Y_bar_dot_dot"]
    A5 --> B2["Compute Treatment Means Y_bar_i_dot"]
    A5 --> B3["Compute Sum of Squares: SS_T, SS_Tr, SS_E"]
    
    B1 --> C1["ANOVA Table Assembly"]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    
    C1 --> C2["Degrees of Freedom: p-1, N-p, N-1"]
    C2 --> C3["Mean Squares: MS_Tr, MS_E"]
    C3 --> C4["F-Statistic: F0 = MS_Tr / MS_E"]
    C4 --> C5{"F0 > F_alpha, p-1, N-p?"}
    
    C5 -->|"Yes"| D1["Reject H0: sigma_tau^2 > 0 exists"]
    C5 -->|"No"| D2["Fail to Reject H0: no treatment variability"]
    
    D1 --> E1["Estimate variance components"]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2["sigma^2_hat = MS_E"]
    E1 --> E3["sigma_tau^2_hat = MS_Tr - MS_E / n"]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Variance Decomposition Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Observed Variance in Y_ij"] --> B["Between-Treatment Component: sigma_tau^2"]
    A --> C["Within-Treatment Component: sigma^2"]
    
    B --> B1["Source: Treatment factor variability"]
    B1 --> B2["Estimated by: MS_Tr"]
    B2 --> B3["Reflects: Differences between treatment means"]
    
    C --> C1["Source: Experimental error / residual"]
    C1 --> C2["Estimated by: MS_E"]
    C2 --> C3["Reflects: Random noise / within-group variation"]
    
    B3 --> D["Combined Variance: sigma_tau^2 + sigma^2"]
    C3 --> D
    D --> E["ANOVA Partitioning: SS_T = SS_Tr + SS_E"]
    E --> F["F-test: Ratio of Between to Within variability"]
```

### 4.4 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow (REM Specific)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0["H0: sigma_tau^2 = 0"] --> H1["H1: sigma_tau^2 > 0"]
    H1 --> S1["Compute F0 = MS_Tr / MS_E"]
    S1 --> S2["Look up F_alpha, p-1, N-p from F-tables"]
    S2 --> S3{"Compare F0 with Critical Value"}
    S3 -->|"F0 > F_critical"| R1["Reject H0 at level alpha"]
    S3 -->|"F0 <= F_critical"| R2["Fail to reject H0"]
    R1 --> OUT1["Conclude: Significant treatment variability exists"]
    R2 --> OUT2["Conclude: Insufficient evidence of treatment variability"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between Fixed Effect Model and Random Effect Model with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Fixed Effect Model (Model I):** Treatment levels are *deliberately chosen* by the experimenter; inference is restricted to the tested levels. Example: Comparing the yield of 3 *specific* fertilizers (NPK, Urea, DAP). $(1\text{ mark})$
- **Random Effect Model (Model II):** Treatment levels are *randomly sampled* from a large population; inference extends to the entire population. Example: Estimating variability in yield across 5 *randomly sampled* fertilizers from a market of 200. $(1\text{ mark})$
- In Fixed Model, the goal is to estimate treatment means; in Random Model, the goal is to estimate **variance components** $\sigma_{\tau}^2$ and $\sigma^2$. $(1\text{ mark})$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**State the assumptions of a one-way Random Effect Model and write the expected mean square equations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Linear model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$, where $i = 1, \ldots, p$; $j = 1, \ldots, n_i$ $(1\text{ mark})$
2. **Random treatment effects:** $\tau_i \sim N(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$, iid $(0.5\text{ mark})$
3. **Random errors:** $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, iid, independent of $\tau_i$ $(0.5\text{ mark})$
4. **Expected Mean Squares:**
   - $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ $(0.5\text{ mark})$
   - $E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$ $(0.5\text{ mark})$

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Module 3, Full Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the Random Effect Model with its mathematical formulation. Explain the concept of variance components and how they are estimated using the method of moments. State clearly the assumptions of the model.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An experiment is conducted to study the variability in tensile strength of steel wires produced by **6 randomly selected machines** in a factory. Each machine produces **5 wire specimens**. The collected data (in $\text{N/mm}^2$) yields the following summary statistics:
- Grand mean: $\bar{Y}_{..} = 450 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Treatment sum of squares: $SS_{Tr} = 320.5$
- Error sum of squares: $SS_E = 480.0$

**Tasks:**
1. Construct the complete ANOVA table.
2. Test $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ against $H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.
3. Estimate both variance components and interpret the result in engineering terms.

---

**SOLUTION (Question A - Full Marks Distribution):**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Random Effect Model Theory

**1. Mathematical Formulation** (2 marks):

The one-way Random Effect Model is expressed as:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where:
- $Y_{ij}$ = observation from the $j^{th}$ replication of the $i^{th}$ treatment
- $\mu$ = grand mean (fixed constant)
- $\tau_i$ = random treatment effect with $\tau_i \sim N(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$
- $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random error with $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$
- $\tau_i$ and $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are mutually independent

**2. Assumptions** (2 marks):

1. Treatment effects are **random, independent**, and **normally distributed** with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{\tau}^2$.
2. Errors are **normally distributed** with mean zero and constant variance $\sigma^2$.
3. Treatment and error components are **statistically independent**.
4. Treatment levels represent a **random sample** from a larger population.

**3. Variance Components and Estimation** (2 marks):

The two variance components are:
- $\sigma^2$ = error variance (within-treatment)
- $\sigma_{\tau}^2$ = treatment variance (between-treatment)

Equating observed MS to expected MS (Method of Moments):
$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$$
$$\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \frac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n}$$

**4. Hypothesis Test** (1 mark):

$H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ vs $H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0$; test statistic $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E \sim F_{(p-1, N-p)}$.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $p = 6$ machines, $n = 5$ replications per machine, $N = 30$ total.

**Step 1: Compute Degrees of Freedom** (1 mark)
- $df_{Tr} = p - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$
- $df_E = N - p = 30 - 6 = 24$
- $df_T = N - 1 = 29$

**Step 2: Compute Mean Squares** (1 mark)
$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{df_{Tr}} = \frac{320.5}{5} = 64.10$$
$$MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{480.0}{24} = 20.00$$

**Step 3: Construct ANOVA Table** (1 mark)

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Machines (Between) | 320.5 | 5 | 64.10 | $\sigma^2 + 5\sigma_{\tau}^2$ | 3.205 |
| Error (Within) | 480.0 | 24 | 20.00 | $\sigma^2$ | |
| **Total** | **800.5** | **29** | | | |

**Step 4: Hypothesis Test** (1.5 marks)

$F_0 = 64.10 / 20.00 = 3.205$

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 5, 24} = 2.621$ (from F-tables)

Since $F_0 = 3.205 > 2.621$, **reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Conclusion:** Significant machine-to-machine variability exists in tensile strength.

**Step 5: Estimate Variance Components** (1.5 marks)

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = 20.00 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$
$$\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \frac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{64.10 - 20.00}{5} = \frac{44.10}{5} = 8.82 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation** (1 mark)

Total variance: $\hat{\sigma}_{total}^2 = \hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 + \hat{\sigma}^2 = 8.82 + 20.00 = 28.82$.

Proportion of variance due to machines: $8.82 / 28.82 = 30.6\%$.

Approximately **30.6% of total variability** in tensile strength is attributable to machine differences. The engineer should focus on **machine calibration and standardization** to reduce this significant source of quality variation.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Module 3, Alternative, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of variance components in the context of a one-way Random Effect Model. How does the Expected Mean Square (EMS) approach differ between Fixed and Random Effect Models? Develop the EMS table for a balanced one-way Random Effect Model with $n$ replications per treatment.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A quality engineer wants to estimate batch-to-batch variability in tablet weight from a pharmaceutical plant. **4 batches** are randomly selected and **6 tablets** are weighed from each batch. The following ANOVA summary is obtained:

| Source | df | SS |
|---|---|---|
| Between Batches | 3 | 84.50 |
| Within Batches (Error) | 20 | 105.00 |

**Tasks:**
1. Complete the ANOVA table and compute the F-statistic.
2. State and test the appropriate hypothesis at $\alpha = 0.01$. Use $F_{0.01, 3, 20} = 4.94$.
3. Estimate the variance components $\hat{\sigma}^2$ and $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$.

---

**SOLUTION (Question B - Full Marks Distribution):**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Variance Components Theory

**1. Concept of Variance Components** (2 marks):

Variance components are the contributions to total variability made by different random sources. In a one-way random effect model, total variance decomposes as:
$$\text{Total Variance} = \sigma_{\tau}^2 \text{ (between-treatment)} + \sigma^2 \text{ (within-treatment)}$$

**2. EMS Difference: Fixed vs Random** (2 marks):

| Aspect | Fixed Model | Random Model |
|---|---|---|
| $E(MS_{Tr})$ | $\sigma^2 + \dfrac{\sum n_i \tau_i^2}{p-1}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ |
| Contains | Treatment mean squares | **Variance components** |
| Purpose | Test treatment equality | Test for variability |

**3. EMS Table (Balanced)** (3 marks):

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments | $\sum n_i(\bar{Y}_{i.}-\bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | $p-1$ | $SS_{Tr}/(p-1)$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ |
| Error | $\sum\sum(Y_{ij}-\bar{Y}_{i.})^2$ | $N-p$ | $SS_E/(N-p)$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| Total | $\sum\sum(Y_{ij}-\bar{Y}_{..})^2$ | $N-1$ | — | — |

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $p = 4$ batches, $n = 6$ tablets per batch, $N = 24$, $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Step 1: Complete ANOVA Table** (2 marks)

| Source | SS | df | MS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Between Batches | 84.50 | 3 | $\frac{84.50}{3} = 28.167$ |
| Within Batches | 105.00 | 20 | $\frac{105.00}{20} = 5.250$ |
| **Total** | **189.50** | **23** | |

**Step 2: F-Statistic** (1 mark)

$$F_0 = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E} = \frac{28.167}{5.250} = 5.365$$

**Step 3: Hypothesis Test** (2 marks)

$H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ (no batch variability)
$H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0$ (batch variability exists)

Critical value: $F_{0.01, 3, 20} = 4.94$

Since $F_0 = 5.365 > 4.94$, **reject $H_0$** at $\alpha = 0.01$.

**Conclusion:** Highly significant evidence of batch-to-batch variability at the 1% level.

**Step 4: Estimate Variance Components** (2 marks)

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = 5.250 \text{ (mg)}^2$$
$$\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = \frac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{28.167 - 5.250}{6} = \frac{22.917}{6} = 3.819 \text{ (mg)}^2$$

**Interpretation:** The batch-to-batch variance (3.819) is comparable to the within-batch variance (5.250), indicating that **batch origin is a major contributor** to tablet weight inconsistency. Process control should focus on batch uniformity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong EMS formula**: Students often write the Fixed Model EMS ($\sigma^2 + \sum n_i \tau_i^2/(p-1)$) instead of the Random Model EMS ($\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$). **Lose 2-3 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong denominator of F-test**: In Random Models, the F-test uses $MS_E$ in **both** numerator and denominator (NOT just the numerator). Some students incorrectly cite other denominators.
> 3. **Negative variance estimator**: Forgetting to set $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = 0$ when negative. **Lose 1 mark** if not handled.
> 4. **Confusing $n$ with $n'$**: In balanced designs, $n' = n$. In unbalanced, students must use the formula for $n'$.
> 5. **Wrong hypothesis**: Writing $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \ldots$ (Fixed Model format) instead of $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$.
> 6. **Forgetting to specify the F-distribution** $(F_{p-1, N-p})$ for the test statistic.
> 7. **Unit errors**: Variance components have **squared units** of the response variable. Always state this explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 KTU Module 3 - Random Effect Model: Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ **Core Idea:** Treatments are a *random sample* from a larger population; goal is to estimate **variance components**, not specific treatment means.
>
> ✅ **Linear Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ with $\tau_i \sim N(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$ and $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, independent.
>
> ✅ **Two Variance Components:** $\sigma_{\tau}^2$ (between-treatment) and $\sigma^2$ (within-treatment error).
>
> ✅ **Key EMS Equations (Balanced):**
>    - $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$
>    - $E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$
>
> ✅ **Method of Moments Estimators:**
>    - $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$
>    - $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2 = (MS_{Tr} - MS_E) / n$ (set to 0 if negative)
>
> ✅ **F-Test:**
>    - $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E \sim F_{(p-1, N-p)}$
>    - $H_0: \sigma_{\tau}^2 = 0$ vs $H_1: \sigma_{\tau}^2 > 0$
>    - Upper-tailed test: reject if $F_0 > F_{\alpha, p-1, N-p}$
>
> ✅ **Unbalanced Design:** Replace $n$ with $n' = \frac{1}{p-1}\left(N - \frac{\sum n_i^2}{N}\right)$.
>
> ✅ **Fixed vs Random REMINDER:**
>    - **Fixed** → specific levels, test means, $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + \sum n_i \tau_i^2 / (p-1)$
>    - **Random** → sampled levels, test variance, $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$
>
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Process capability, batch variability, gauge R&R, tolerance analysis, Six-Sigma DMAIC, reliability engineering.
>
> ✅ **Pitfall Avoidance:** Always check for negative $\hat{\sigma}_{\tau}^2$; always report variance components in **squared units** of response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Response Surface Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Response Surface Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques that are useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. It is formally defined as a "statistical method that uses quantitative data from appropriate experimental designs to determine and simultaneously solve multivariate equations" to optimize a response (output variable) influenced by several independent input variables.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, **RSM** is a strategic sequential procedure wherein:
1. A **first-order polynomial model** is initially fitted to identify the most influential factors.
2. The experimenter moves along the **path of steepest ascent (or descent)** to locate the optimal region.
3. A **second-order polynomial model** (the actual *response surface*) is then fitted within this optimal region.
4. The **canonical form** of the second-order model is used to characterize the surface (minimum, maximum, saddle point).

The general second-order response surface model for $k$ factors is:

$$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \epsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 treatment of RSM focuses on the transition from First-Order Designs → Method of Steepest Ascent → Second-Order Designs → Canonical Analysis for process optimization.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **chocolate chef in Kerala** trying to perfect the taste of a new chocolate bar. The two key "knobs" you can adjust are:
- **Cocoa content** ($x_1$) — between 60% and 90%
- **Sugar content** ($x_2$) — between 10% and 30%

The "response" ($y$) is the **customer satisfaction rating** out of 10.

**Without RSM (The Old Way):**
You randomly change the recipe, test it with a few customers, and hope for the best. This is like searching for a hidden treasure in a dark room by randomly poking the floor with a stick. You will *eventually* find gold, but you will waste a lot of time (and chocolate).

**With RSM (The Smart Way):**
1. **Screening Phase:** First, you run a few quick experiments to see if cocoa and sugar *actually matter* for taste. (First-Order Model)
2. **Climbing Phase:** Once you know they matter, you find the "steepest uphill direction" in the taste landscape and keep adjusting the recipe in that direction, step by step, until taste ratings stop improving. (Method of Steepest Ascent)
3. **Polish Phase:** Near the peak of "taste-mountain," you switch to a sophisticated curved surface (like a hill-shaped quadratic curve) to pinpoint the *exact* peak — not just "high cocoa" but precisely **78.4% cocoa, 18.7% sugar**. (Second-Order Model + Canonical Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** RSM treats your process as a "black-box function" $y = f(x_1, x_2, ..., x_k) + \epsilon$ and uses cleverly chosen experiments to map that function's shape, ultimately finding where $y$ is maximized (or minimized).

## 1.3 Terminology Glossary for KTU Board Exams

| Term | Precise Definition | Significance |
|------|--------------------|--------------|
| **Response Variable ($y$)** | The measured output / quality characteristic being optimized | The "target" we want to maximize or minimize |
| **Independent Variables ($x_i$)** | The controllable input factors | The "knobs" we tune |
| **First-Order Model** | A linear polynomial $\beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i$ | Used for **screening** — does the factor matter? |
| **Second-Order Model** | Includes squared terms $\beta_{ii}x_i^2$ and cross-products $\beta_{ij}x_i x_j$ | The actual **response surface** — captures curvature |
| **Steepest Ascent Path** | Direction $\nabla \hat{y}$ proportional to factor main effects | Walks the experimenter toward the optimum |
| **Canonical Form** | $y = y_s + \sum \lambda_i w_i^2$ after coordinate rotation | Reveals the *true* nature of the stationary point |
| **Stationary Point** | The point where $\partial y / \partial x_i = 0$ for all $i$ | The candidate optimum (may be max, min, or saddle) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Number of replicates** at center points: typically **3 to 5** in CCD
> - **Axial distance ($\alpha$)** for CCD: $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$ for **rotatability**
> - **Coded variables** $x_i \in [-1, +1]$ are used to standardize the analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Phases of RSM (KTU High-Yield Sequence)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU board questions on RSM typically test the **three-phase strategy** in order. Memorize this sequence verbatim.

### **PHASE 1: First-Order Model & Screening**

When far from the optimum, the response surface can be **approximated by a plane** (flat tilted surface). The model is:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_k x_k + \epsilon$$

- Designed using a **$2^k$ Factorial Design** (often with center points).
- The **center points** are critical — they allow estimation of *pure error* and detection of curvature.
- If curvature is significant, **STOP** — you cannot use first-order model; switch to second-order.

**Test for Curvature (Single Degree of Freedom):**
- Compute the average response at factorial points: $\bar{y}_F$
- Compute the average response at center points: $\bar{y}_C$
- Compute the difference: $\bar{y}_F - \bar{y}_C$
- Perform a $t$-test or include a *curvature term* in the model.

### **PHASE 2: Method of Steepest Ascent (Descent for minimization)**

This is a **sequential optimization procedure**. The direction of steepest ascent is proportional to the **regression coefficients** $\beta_i$ of the first-order model.

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Compute coded coefficients** $\hat{\beta}_i$ from the first-order model.
2. **Define the path direction** in coded units using the proportional change rule.
3. **Choose a step size** $\Delta$ for *one* factor (usually the one with the largest coefficient $\hat{\beta}$).
4. **Compute step sizes** for all other factors:

$$\Delta x_i = \left( \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_{\text{reference}}} \right) \times \Delta x_{\text{reference}}$$

5. **Conduct experiments** along the path until the response stops increasing.
6. **Switch to a new first-order design** centered at the new location and **repeat**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "steepest"?** At any point on a plane $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i$, the gradient vector $\nabla y = (\beta_1, \beta_2, ..., \beta_k)$ points in the direction of fastest increase. The "steepest ascent path" is the line that starts from the current center and travels in this gradient direction.

### **PHASE 3: Second-Order Model & Response Surface**

Once the experimenter is **near the optimum**, curvature becomes significant. A **second-order polynomial** is fitted:

$$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \epsilon$$

In **matrix notation** for $k=2$:

$$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, \quad \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

The fitted second-order model in coded variables is:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2 + \hat{\beta}_{11} x_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_{22} x_2^2 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_1 x_2$$

### **Stationary Point (Setting gradient to zero):**

The stationary point is found by solving $\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1} = 0$ and $\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1} = \hat{\beta}_1 + 2\hat{\beta}_{11} x_1 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2} = \hat{\beta}_2 + 2\hat{\beta}_{22} x_2 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_1 = 0$$

In matrix form:

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\beta}_1 \\ \hat{\beta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$ (vector of linear coefficients)
- $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\beta}_{11} & \hat{\beta}_{12}/2 \\ \hat{\beta}_{12}/2 & \hat{\beta}_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ (matrix of quadratic and interaction coefficients)

The **predicted response at the stationary point** is:

$$\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$$

## 2.2 Canonical Analysis (Characterizing the Stationary Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical analysis** is a coordinate transformation that rotates the axes to eliminate cross-product terms. The model becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \lambda_1 w_1^2 + \lambda_2 w_2^2 + \dots + \lambda_k w_k^2$$

where $\lambda_i$ are the **eigenvalues** of matrix $\mathbf{B}$, and $w_i$ are the **canonical variables** (rotated coordinates).

**Interpretation Rule (KTU High-Yield):**

| Eigenvalue Sign Pattern | Nature of Stationary Point |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| All $\lambda_i < 0$ | **Maximum** (a peak) |
| All $\lambda_i > 0$ | **Minimum** (a valley) |
| Mixed signs (some $+$, some $-$) | **Saddle Point** (a mountain pass) |
| All $\lambda_i = 0$ | **Ridge System** (degenerate case) |

The eigenvalues are found by solving:

$$\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Second-Order Model (general) | $y = \beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i + \sum \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \epsilon$ | $k$ factors, coded variables |
| 2 | Least Squares Estimator | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$ | Matrix form |
| 3 | Steepest Ascent Direction (2D) | $\Delta x_2 = \left(\hat{\beta}_2 / \hat{\beta}_1\right) \Delta x_1$ | Move along gradient |
| 4 | Stationary Point | $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ | Gradient = 0 |
| 5 | Predicted Response at $\mathbf{x}_s$ | $\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$ | Plug $\mathbf{x}_s$ into model |
| 6 | Canonical Form | $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \sum \lambda_i w_i^2$ | After rotation |
| 7 | Eigenvalue Equation | $\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ | Characterizes optimum |
| 8 | Rotatability (CCD axial) | $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$ | For $k=2$: $\alpha \approx 1.414$ |
| 9 | CCD Factorial Runs ($2^k$) | $2^k$ corner points | For $k=2$: 4 runs |
| 10 | CCD Total Runs | $2^k + 2k + n_c$ | Factorial + Axial + Center |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of RSM

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications (KTU-relevant examples):**
> 1. **Chemical Engineering:** Optimizing yield of a chemical reactor (temperature, pressure, catalyst concentration).
> 2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Optimizing surface roughness in CNC turning (feed, speed, depth of cut).
> 3. **Civil Engineering:** Optimizing compressive strength of concrete (water-cement ratio, curing time, admixture %).
> 4. **Food Technology:** Optimizing sensory quality and shelf-life of processed foods.
> 5. **Biotechnology:** Optimizing fermentation parameters for maximum biomass yield.

The **distinct advantage** of RSM over classical one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) methods is that RSM:
- Captures **interaction effects** between factors
- **Reduces the number of experiments** required
- Generates a **mathematical model** that can be used for prediction
- Identifies the **true optimum**, not just a "good" region
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Worked Derivation: Stationary Point for $k=2$

Consider the fitted second-order model:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 x_2 + \hat{\beta}_{11} x_1^2 + \hat{\beta}_{22} x_2^2 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_1 x_2$$

**Step 1: Take partial derivatives to find the stationary point.**

We need the gradient to be zero:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1} = \hat{\beta}_1 + 2\hat{\beta}_{11} x_1 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2} = \hat{\beta}_2 + 2\hat{\beta}_{22} x_2 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_1 = 0$$

**Step 2: Express as a system of linear equations in matrix form.**

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2\hat{\beta}_{11} & \hat{\beta}_{12} \\ \hat{\beta}_{12} & 2\hat{\beta}_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\hat{\beta}_1 \\ -\hat{\beta}_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{b}$$

**Step 3: Solve using Cramer's Rule for the 2-variable case.**

The determinant of $\mathbf{B}$ is:

$$|\mathbf{B}| = (2\hat{\beta}_{11})(2\hat{\beta}_{22}) - \hat{\beta}_{12}^2 = 4\hat{\beta}_{11}\hat{\beta}_{22} - \hat{\beta}_{12}^2$$

The solution is:

$$x_{1s} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_{12}\hat{\beta}_2 - 2\hat{\beta}_{22}\hat{\beta}_1}{4\hat{\beta}_{11}\hat{\beta}_{22} - \hat{\beta}_{12}^2}$$

$$x_{2s} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_{12}\hat{\beta}_1 - 2\hat{\beta}_{11}\hat{\beta}_2}{4\hat{\beta}_{11}\hat{\beta}_{22} - \hat{\beta}_{12}^2$$

**Step 4: Compute the predicted response at the stationary point.**

$$\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_{1s} + \hat{\beta}_2 x_{2s} + \hat{\beta}_{11} x_{1s}^2 + \hat{\beta}_{22} x_{2s}^2 + \hat{\beta}_{12} x_{1s} x_{2s}$$

Using matrix algebra, this simplifies elegantly to:

$$\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$$

## 3.2 Full Worked Derivation: Canonical Analysis for $k=2$

Starting from the second-order model in matrix form:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 1: Substitute $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w}$** to shift origin to the stationary point:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + (\mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w})^T \mathbf{b} + (\mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w})^T \mathbf{B} (\mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w})$$

**Step 2: Expand and simplify.** Using $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{b}$:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{b} + 2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{B}\mathbf{w}$$

Since $2\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{b}$, the linear terms in $\mathbf{w}$ vanish, leaving:

$$\hat{y} = \underbrace{\left(\hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}_s\right)}_{\hat{y}_s} + \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3: Diagonalize $\mathbf{B}$** via the orthogonal transformation $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{z}$:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \mathbf{z}^T \mathbf{P}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{P} \mathbf{z} = \hat{y}_s + \mathbf{z}^T \boldsymbol{\Lambda} \mathbf{z} = \hat{y}_s + \lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2$$

**Step 4: Compute eigenvalues** by solving $\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$:

$$\det \begin{bmatrix} \hat{\beta}_{11} - \lambda & \hat{\beta}_{12}/2 \\ \hat{\beta}_{12}/2 & \hat{\beta}_{22} - \lambda \end{bmatrix} = 0$$

This gives the characteristic equation:

$$(\hat{\beta}_{11} - \lambda)(\hat{\beta}_{22} - \lambda) - \left(\frac{\hat{\beta}_{12}}{2}\right)^2 = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 - (\hat{\beta}_{11} + \hat{\beta}_{22})\lambda + \left(\hat{\beta}_{11}\hat{\beta}_{22} - \frac{\hat{\beta}_{12}^2}{4}\right) = 0$$

Solving via the quadratic formula:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(\hat{\beta}_{11} + \hat{\beta}_{22}) \pm \sqrt{(\hat{\beta}_{11} - \hat{\beta}_{22})^2 + \hat{\beta}_{12}^2}}{2}$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A chemical engineer studies the effect of reaction temperature ($x_1$) and pressure ($x_2$) on yield ($y$). A Central Composite Design yields the following fitted second-order model (in coded variables):

$$\hat{y} = 75.0 + 4.0 x_1 + 2.5 x_2 - 3.5 x_1^2 - 2.0 x_2^2 + 1.2 x_1 x_2$$

**Task:** (a) Find the stationary point. (b) Predict the response at the stationary point. (c) Perform canonical analysis.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the coefficient vectors.**

$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.0 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.5 & 0.6 \\ 0.6 & -2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Recall: the off-diagonal of $\mathbf{B}$ is $\hat{\beta}_{12}/2 = 1.2/2 = 0.6$)

**Step 2: Find the stationary point using $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.**

Compute $\det(\mathbf{B})$:

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-3.5)(-2.0) - (0.6)^2 = 7.0 - 0.36 = 6.64$$

Compute $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{6.64} \begin{bmatrix} -2.0 & -0.6 \\ -0.6 & -3.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3012 & -0.0904 \\ -0.0904 & -0.5271 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$:

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.3012 & -0.0904 \\ -0.0904 & -0.5271 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4.0 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.2048 - 0.2260 \\ -0.3616 - 1.3178 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4308 \\ -1.6794 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2} \begin{bmatrix} -1.4308 \\ -1.6794 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.7154 \\ 0.8397 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\hat{y}_s$.**

$$\hat{y}_s = 75.0 + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b} = 75.0 + \frac{1}{2}(0.7154 \times 4.0 + 0.8397 \times 2.5)$$

$$= 75.0 + \frac{1}{2}(2.8616 + 2.0993) = 75.0 + \frac{1}{2}(4.9609) = 75.0 + 2.4804 = 77.48$$

So the predicted maximum yield is **77.48%** at $x_1 = 0.7154$ (temperature) and $x_2 = 0.8397$ (pressure).

**Step 4: Canonical Analysis — find eigenvalues.**

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(-3.5 + -2.0) \pm \sqrt{(-3.5 - (-2.0))^2 + (1.2)^2}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-5.5 \pm \sqrt{(-1.5)^2 + 1.44}}{2} = \frac{-5.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 + 1.44}}{2} = \frac{-5.5 \pm \sqrt{3.69}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-5.5 \pm 1.921}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{-5.5 + 1.921}{2} = -1.790, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{-5.5 - 1.921}{2} = -3.710$$

**Step 5: Interpret eigenvalues.**

Since **both $\lambda_1 < 0$ and $\lambda_2 < 0$**, the stationary point is a **MAXIMUM** (a peak of the response surface). 

The canonical form is:

$$\hat{y} = 77.48 - 1.790\, z_1^2 - 3.710\, z_2^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The smaller magnitude $|-1.790| < |-3.710|$ means the surface is "more curved" along $z_2$. So the system is more sensitive to changes along the $z_2$ direction. The yield surface is a "stretched" dome over the optimum.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Method of Steepest Ascent Derivation

**Setup:** A first-order model has been fitted in **coded variables** $x_i \in [-1, +1]$:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \hat{\beta}_i x_i$$

The gradient is:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = \left( \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, ..., \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} \right) = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, ..., \hat{\beta}_k)$$

**The direction of steepest ascent** in coded space is the unit vector parallel to $\nabla \hat{y}$:

$$\mathbf{d} = \frac{\nabla \hat{y}}{\vert\vert\nabla \hat{y}\vert\vert}$$

**Practical KTU rule:** Walk along the direction $(b_1, b_2, ..., b_k)$ normalized by the coefficient of the most influential factor. If $\hat{\beta}_1$ is the largest, the proportional step rule is:

$$\Delta x_i = \left(\frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_1}\right) \Delta x_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoding (converting coded to natural variables):** The natural variable step $\Delta X_i$ is computed as:
>
> $$\Delta X_i = \left(\frac{\Delta x_i}{2}\right) \times (X_{i,\text{high}} - X_{i,\text{low}})$$
>
> This conversion is **frequently asked** in KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: RSM Three-Phase Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Response y and Factors x1, x2, ..., xk"] --> B["Phase 1: Fit First-Order Model using 2^k Factorial with Center Points"]
    B --> C{"Test for Significance of Factors via ANOVA / t-test"}
    C -->|Not Significant| D["Drop Non-Significant Factors and Refit"]
    D --> B
    C -->|Significant| E{"Test for Curvature using Center Points"}
    E -->|Curvature Detected| F["Switch Directly to Second-Order Model"]
    E -->|No Curvature| G["Phase 2: Method of Steepest Ascent / Descent"]
    G --> H["Compute Direction Proportional to Regression Coefficients"]
    H --> I["Conduct Sequential Experiments Along Path"]
    I --> J{"Response Stopped Improving?"}
    J -->|No| I
    J -->|Yes| K["New Optimal Region Identified"]
    K --> L["Phase 3: Fit Second-Order Model using CCD or Box-Behnken"]
    L --> M["Solve for Stationary Point: gradient y = 0"]
    M --> N["Perform Canonical Analysis: Find Eigenvalues"]
    N --> O{"Eigenvalue Sign Pattern"}
    O -->|"All lambda Negative"| P["Optimum is MAXIMUM"]
    O -->|"All lambda Positive"| Q["Optimum is MINIMUM"]
    O -->|"Mixed Signs"| R["SADDLE POINT: Use Ridge Analysis"]
    P --> S["Report Optimum Coordinates and Predicted Response"]
    Q --> S
    R --> T["Move Away from Saddle toward True Optimum"]
    T --> L
    S --> U["Confirm via Confirmation Experiments"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of RSM Modeling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Space"]
        F1["Factor x1: Temperature"]
        F2["Factor x2: Pressure"]
        FK["Factor xk: Other"]
    end
    subgraph DESIGN["Experimental Design Layer"]
        D1["2^k Factorial: Screening"]
        D2["Central Composite Design: Surface"]
        D3["Box-Behnken Design: Alternative"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["Process / System Under Study"]
        PROC["Black-Box Process: y = f x1, x2, ..., xk plus noise"]
    end
    subgraph MODEL["Statistical Modeling Layer"]
        M1["First-Order Model: y = beta0 + sum beta_i x_i"]
        M2["Second-Order Model: y = beta0 + sum beta_i x_i + sum beta_ii x_i^2 + sum beta_ij x_i x_j"]
        M3["Canonical Form: y = y_s + sum lambda_i w_i^2"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output / Decision"]
        O1["Stationary Point Coordinates"]
        O2["Predicted Optimum Response"]
        O3["Type of Optimum: Max, Min, Saddle"]
    end
    F1 --> D1
    F2 --> D1
    FK --> D1
    D1 --> PROC
    D2 --> PROC
    D3 --> PROC
    PROC --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> O1
    M3 --> O2
    M3 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Canonical Analysis Interpretation Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Compute Matrix B from Quadratic and Interaction Coefficients"] --> B["Solve Characteristic Equation: det B minus lambda I equals 0"]
    B --> C["Obtain Eigenvalues lambda_1, lambda_2, ..., lambda_k"]
    C --> D{"Sign Pattern Analysis"}
    D -->|All lambda Negative| E["MAXIMUM: Response Surface is a Dome / Peak"]
    D -->|All lambda Positive| F["MINIMUM: Response Surface is a Bowl / Valley"]
    D -->|Mixed Positive and Negative| G["SADDLE POINT: Min in Some Directions, Max in Others"]
    D -->|All lambda Near Zero| H["FLAT REGION or RIDGE: Refine with More Data"]
    E --> I["Optimal Coordinates: x_s = minus half times B inverse times b"]
    F --> I
    G --> J["Use Ridge Analysis to Locate True Optimum"]
    J --> K["Re-Fit Model in New Region"]
    I --> L["Report y_s and Optimum Settings"]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Sketch: Response Surface Topographies (Verbal Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "sketch the response surface" in a board exam, use the following 2D topological descriptions (replace with hand-drawn parabolas/ellipses in the answer book):

| Surface Type | 2D Sketch Description | Eigenvalues |
|--------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Maximum** | Inverted paraboloid (dome) | $\lambda_1 < 0, \lambda_2 < 0$ |
| **Minimum** | Upright paraboloid (bowl) | $\lambda_1 > 0, \lambda_2 > 0$ |
| **Saddle** | Horse-saddle shape (tilted in 2 directions) | $\lambda_1 > 0, \lambda_2 < 0$ (or vice versa) |
| **Ridge** | Elongated hill (curved in one direction) | $\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 \neq 0$ |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Response Surface Methodology. What is the general form of a second-order response surface model?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. It uses quantitative data obtained from appropriately designed experiments to build an empirical model relating the response variable to one or more independent input variables.

**General Second-Order Model (1.5 Marks):** The general form of a second-order response surface model in $k$ factors is:

$$y = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum_{i<j} \beta_{ij} x_i x_j + \epsilon$$

where $\beta_0$ is the intercept, $\beta_i$ are linear coefficients, $\beta_{ii}$ are quadratic coefficients, $\beta_{ij}$ are interaction coefficients, and $\epsilon$ is the random error term.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the method of steepest ascent? When is it used in RSM?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):** The method of steepest ascent (or descent for minimization) is a sequential procedure used in RSM to move from a current operating point toward the optimal region of the response. The direction of movement is proportional to the regression coefficients of the fitted first-order model. If $\hat{\beta}_i$ are the estimated coefficients in coded variables, the path direction is taken along the vector $(\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, ..., \hat{\beta}_k)$.

**When Used (1 Mark):** It is employed in **Phase 2** of RSM, after a first-order model has been fitted and **before** a second-order model is required — specifically when the experimenter is **far from the optimum** and curvature is not yet significant.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the three-phase strategy of Response Surface Methodology in detail with a suitable flowchart. Describe how the method of steepest ascent is used to navigate the factor space. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A second-order response surface model fitted to data on the yield of a chemical process is:

$$\hat{y} = 50 + 3x_1 + 2x_2 - 2x_1^2 - 1.5x_2^2 + 0.8x_1 x_2$$

Determine: (i) the stationary point coordinates, (ii) the predicted response at the stationary point, and (iii) the nature of the stationary point using canonical analysis. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Phase Strategy of RSM (7 Marks):**

**Phase 1: First-Order Model and Screening (2.5 Marks)**
- Initially, the experimenter is far from the optimum, so the response is approximated by a **linear (first-order) model** in $k$ factors:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_k x_k + \epsilon$$

- A **$2^k$ factorial design with center points** is used. Center points (typically 3–5 replicates) allow estimation of pure error and **detection of curvature** via a single-degree-of-freedom test comparing the average factorial response $\bar{y}_F$ with the average center response $\bar{y}_C$.
- Factor significance is assessed using ANOVA / $t$-tests. Insignificant factors are dropped, and the model is refit.
- **Decision:** If curvature is significant, jump directly to Phase 3. If not, proceed to Phase 2.

**Phase 2: Method of Steepest Ascent/Descent (2.5 Marks)**
- The direction of steepest ascent in coded space is the **gradient of the first-order model**: $\nabla \hat{y} = (\hat{\beta}_1, \hat{\beta}_2, ..., \hat{\beta}_k)$.
- Choose a step size $\Delta x$ for one reference factor. The other factors move proportionally:

$$\Delta x_i = \left( \frac{\hat{\beta}_i}{\hat{\beta}_{\text{ref}}} \right) \Delta x_{\text{ref}}$$

- **Sequential experiments** are conducted along this path. If the response continues to increase, the experimenter steps further. When the response plateaus or decreases, a **new first-order design** is run centered at the new location.
- The loop continues until the experimenter is near the optimum, at which point curvature becomes significant and Phase 3 begins.

**Phase 3: Second-Order Model and Response Surface (2 Marks)**
- A **second-order polynomial** is fitted using a **Central Composite Design (CCD)** or **Box-Behnken Design (BD)**.
- The stationary point is found by setting $\nabla \hat{y} = \mathbf{0}$, yielding $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
- **Canonical analysis** is performed to classify the stationary point as maximum, minimum, or saddle using the **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{B}$.
- Confirmation experiments validate the predicted optimum.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify coefficient vectors (1 Mark)**

From the model:
$$\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.0 & 0.4 \\ 0.4 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

(off-diagonal: $\hat{\beta}_{12}/2 = 0.8/2 = 0.4$)

**Step 2: Compute $\det(\mathbf{B})$ and $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$\det(\mathbf{B}) = (-2.0)(-1.5) - (0.4)^2 = 3.0 - 0.16 = 2.84$$

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1} = \frac{1}{2.84} \begin{bmatrix} -1.5 & -0.4 \\ -0.4 & -2.0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5282 & -0.1408 \\ -0.1408 & -0.7042 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute $\mathbf{x}_s$ (1.5 Marks)**

$$\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5282 & -0.1408 \\ -0.1408 & -0.7042 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.5846 - 0.2816 \\ -0.4224 - 1.4084 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.8662 \\ -1.8308 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -1.8662 \\ -1.8308 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9331 \\ 0.9154 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute $\hat{y}_s$ (1 Mark)**

$$\hat{y}_s = 50 + \frac{1}{2}(0.9331 \times 3 + 0.9154 \times 2) = 50 + \frac{1}{2}(2.7993 + 1.8308) = 50 + 2.3151 = 52.32$$

**Step 5: Canonical analysis (2 Marks)**

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(-2.0 - 1.5) \pm \sqrt{(-2.0 + 1.5)^2 + (0.8)^2}}{2} = \frac{-3.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 + 0.64}}{2} = \frac{-3.5 \pm \sqrt{0.89}}{2}$$

$$= \frac{-3.5 \pm 0.9434}{2}$$

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{-3.5 + 0.9434}{2} = -1.278, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{-3.5 - 0.9434}{2} = -2.222$$

**Both eigenvalues are negative** → The stationary point is a **MAXIMUM**.

The canonical form is:

$$\hat{y} = 52.32 - 1.278\, z_1^2 - 2.222\, z_2^2$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal settings are $x_1 = 0.9331$, $x_2 = 0.9154$ (in coded variables), with predicted maximum response $\hat{y}_s = 52.32$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 3 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the Central Composite Design (CCD) for fitting second-order response surfaces. What is rotatability, and how is the axial distance $\alpha$ chosen for a rotatable CCD? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe canonical analysis of the second-order response surface. How are the eigenvalues used to classify the nature of the stationary point? Illustrate with a $k=2$ example. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Central Composite Design (CCD) (7 Marks):**

**Definition and Structure (3 Marks):** The Central Composite Design (CCD) is the most widely used experimental design for fitting a second-order response surface model. It consists of three distinct portions:

1. **Factorial portion:** A $2^k$ full factorial (or fractional factorial $2^{k-p}$ for large $k$) — the **corner points** of the design space, coded at $(\pm 1, \pm 1, ..., \pm 1)$.
2. **Axial (Star) portion:** $2k$ additional runs with one factor at $\pm \alpha$ and the others at $0$. These points lie on the axes of the design space.
3. **Center portion:** $n_c$ replicate runs (typically 3–6) at the origin $(0, 0, ..., 0)$, used to estimate pure error.

**Total runs:** $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$

**Rotatability (2.5 Marks):** A design is **rotatable** if the predicted variance $\text{Var}[\hat{y}(\mathbf{x})]$ depends only on the distance $d = \sqrt{\sum x_i^2}$ from the design center, not on the direction. This is a desirable property because it ensures the precision of $\hat{y}$ is the same in all directions from the center.

For a **rotatable CCD**, the axial distance is:

$$\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$$

Examples:
- $k = 2$: $\alpha = 2^{2/4} = 2^{0.5} \approx 1.414$
- $k = 3$: $\alpha = 2^{3/4} \approx 1.682$
- $k = 4$: $\alpha = 2^{4/4} = 2.0$

**Advantages of CCD (1.5 Marks):**
- Provides efficient estimation of all second-order coefficients.
- Can be built sequentially — the factorial runs from a previous $2^k$ design can be augmented with axial and center points.
- The orthogonality and rotatability properties are well-established.
- For $k \leq 6$, CCD is highly practical.

**(b) Canonical Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Concept and Setup (2 Marks):** Canonical analysis is a coordinate transformation applied to the fitted second-order model. It rotates the coordinate axes to align with the principal directions of the response surface, thereby eliminating the cross-product ($x_i x_j$) terms.

**Derivation of Canonical Form (2 Marks):** The second-order model in matrix form is:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{b} + \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{B} \mathbf{x}$$

Shifting the origin to the stationary point ($\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x}_s + \mathbf{w}$) and rotating coordinates ($\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{P}\mathbf{z}$), the model becomes:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2 + \dots + \lambda_k z_k^2$$

where $\lambda_i$ are the **eigenvalues** of matrix $\mathbf{B}$ and $z_i$ are the **canonical variables** (rotated coordinates).

**Eigenvalue Computation (1.5 Marks):** Eigenvalues are found from the characteristic equation:

$$\det(\mathbf{B} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$$

For $k=2$:

$$\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{(\hat{\beta}_{11} + \hat{\beta}_{22}) \pm \sqrt{(\hat{\beta}_{11} - \hat{\beta}_{22})^2 + \hat{\beta}_{12}^2}}{2}$$

**Classification Rule (1.5 Marks):**

| Sign of Eigenvalues | Type of Stationary Point | Geometric Shape |
|--------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| $\lambda_1 < 0, \lambda_2 < 0$ | **Maximum** | Inverted dome (peak) |
| $\lambda_1 > 0, \lambda_2 > 0$ | **Minimum** | U-shaped bowl (valley) |
| $\lambda_1 \cdot \lambda_2 < 0$ | **Saddle Point** | Horse-saddle |

**Example (illustrative):** If the fitted model is $\hat{y} = 80 - 3x_1^2 - 2x_2^2 + x_1 x_2$, then $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} -3 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{1 + 1}}{2} = \frac{-5 \pm 1.414}{2}$, giving $\lambda_1 = -1.79$ and $\lambda_2 = -3.21$. Both negative → **Maximum**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (MUST READ):**
> 1. **Sign Error in $\mathbf{B}$:** Students frequently forget to halve $\hat{\beta}_{12}$ when constructing $\mathbf{B}$. The off-diagonal entry is $\hat{\beta}_{12}/2$, NOT $\hat{\beta}_{12}$. This single mistake shifts the stationary point completely.
> 2. **Confusing Coded vs. Natural Variables:** Steepest ascent step sizes are computed in **coded variables**, then converted to natural variables. Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Misclassifying Saddle Points:** A saddle point is **not** an optimum — if eigenvalues have mixed signs, the experimenter must perform **ridge analysis** and move toward the true optimum, NOT report the saddle as the answer.
> 4. **Forgetting the $-\frac{1}{2}$ factor:** The stationary point formula is $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$, not $-\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
> 5. **Omitting Curvature Test:** When describing the three-phase strategy, always include the **curvature test using center points** — examiners specifically look for it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- [ ] **RSM Definition:** Statistical + mathematical technique for process optimization using experimental data.
- [ ] **First-Order Model:** $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum \hat{\beta}_i x_i$ — used for **screening** when far from optimum.
- [ ] **Second-Order Model:** $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \sum \hat{\beta}_i x_i + \sum \hat{\beta}_{ii} x_i^2 + \sum \hat{\beta}_{ij} x_i x_j$ — the actual **response surface**.
- [ ] **Three-Phase Strategy:** **Phase 1** (First-Order Screening) → **Phase 2** (Steepest Ascent) → **Phase 3** (Second-Order Surface + Canonical Analysis).
- [ ] **Curvature Test:** Compare $\bar{y}_F$ (factorial mean) with $\bar{y}_C$ (center mean). Significant difference → curvature exists → skip to second-order.
- [ ] **Steepest Ascent Direction:** Proportional to regression coefficients $\hat{\beta}_i$. Step size rule: $\Delta x_i = (\hat{\beta}_i / \hat{\beta}_{\text{ref}}) \cdot \Delta x_{\text{ref}}$.
- [ ] **Natural-to-Coded Conversion:** $\Delta X_i = (\Delta x_i / 2) \cdot (X_{i,\text{high}} - X_{i,\text{low}})$.
- [ ] **Stationary Point Formula (Matrix):** $\mathbf{x}_s = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
- [ ] **Predicted Optimum Response:** $\hat{y}_s = \hat{\beta}_0 + \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}_s^T \mathbf{b}$.
- [ ] **Canonical Form:** $\hat{y} = \hat{y}_s + \lambda_1 z_1^2 + \lambda_2 z_2^2 + \dots + \lambda_k z_k^2$.
- [ ] **Eigenvalue Rule:** All $\lambda < 0$ → **MAX**; All $\lambda > 0$ → **MIN**; Mixed → **SADDLE**.
- [ ] **B Matrix:** Diagonal: $\hat{\beta}_{ii}$ (quadratic coeffs). Off-diagonal: $\hat{\beta}_{ij}/2$ (half of interaction coeffs).
- [ ] **Central Composite Design (CCD):** $2^k$ factorial + $2k$ axial + $n_c$ center points. **Total:** $N = 2^k + 2k + n_c$.
- [ ] **Rotatability Condition:** $\alpha = (2^k)^{1/4}$ for a rotatable CCD.
- [ ] **Box-Behnken Design (BBD):** Alternative to CCD; uses midpoints of edges; requires **3 levels per factor**; no axial points.
- [ ] **Ridge Analysis:** Used when saddle points are encountered; identifies direction of steepest movement in the absence of a true optimum.
- [ ] **Confirmation Experiment:** Always conduct runs near the predicted optimum to verify the model — this is a **viva question** favorite.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark RSM question, expect either: (1) full stationary point + canonical analysis with numerical computation, OR (2) descriptive question on three-phase strategy, steepest ascent, or CCD. Practice **both** types. Always write the matrix forms explicitly — examiners award partial credit for correctly setting up equations even if arithmetic slips occur.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Steepest Ascent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method of Steepest Ascent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Method of Steepest Ascent** is a sequential first-order optimization procedure used in **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)** to move a process or system from an initial operating region toward the vicinity of the optimum by following the direction in which the response increases most rapidly. Mathematically, the path of steepest ascent is the direction of the **gradient vector** of the fitted first-order response surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The Method of Steepest Ascent is a *sequential* search technique employed when a first-order model is found to be **inadequate** (i.e., when significant *lack-of-fit* or *curvature* is detected) but a second-order model cannot yet be reliably fitted. The method helps locate a new region where the response is improved before fitting a higher-order polynomial.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a **hillside in dense fog** at night. You cannot see the mountain peak, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. Naturally, you would:

1. **Check which direction the ground rises most steeply** (this is your direction vector).
2. **Take a step in that direction** (this is your step size).
3. **Re-evaluate the slope** at the new point and repeat.

The **Method of Steepest Ascent** is the mathematical equivalent of this hill-climbing strategy. We use a *first-order model* (a tilted plane) to estimate the local slope, then move along that slope to find a region where the response (yield, quality, profit) is higher. Once the response stops improving, we know we are near the **peak (optimum)** and can switch to a second-order model to pinpoint it precisely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** In a chemical plant, an engineer may want to maximize the yield of a reaction. After running a **2<sup>k</sup> factorial design**, they observe that increasing both *temperature* and *pressure* improves yield, but a curvature test reveals the first-order model is insufficient. The engineer then uses **steepest ascent** to walk along the "yield hill" by simultaneously increasing temperature and pressure in a proportional ratio until no further yield improvement is observed.

## Key Terminology Standard Metrics

- **First-Order Model:** A linear polynomial $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_i$
- **Gradient Vector:** $\nabla \hat{y} = \left(\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k}\right) = (\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_k)$
- **Step Size (Δ):** The increment by which the most influential variable is changed.
- **Path Ratio:** $\frac{\Delta x_i}{\Delta x_j} = \frac{\beta_i}{\beta_j}$ (proportional movement along the gradient).
- **Origin (Center Point):** The central point of the initial factorial design from which the ascent begins.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Why Do We Need the Method of Steepest Ascent?

After a **2<sup>k</sup> factorial or first-order design** is conducted, three possible outcomes arise:

| Outcome | Diagnostic Test | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| 1. First-order model fits well | Non-significant lack-of-fit | Move directly to optimum using gradient |
| 2. First-order model has **lack-of-fit** but no curvature | Significant LOFL | Move along steepest ascent to new region |
| 3. **Pure curvature** present | Curvature test significant | Add axial/star points OR use steepest ascent |

The **Method of Steepest Ascent** is the standard remedy for **Outcome 2**, where the linear model is *directionally correct* but the current region is *far from the optimum*.

## Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Fit the First-Order Model** from the factorial design data:
$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k}\beta_i x_i$$

2. **Identify the Direction of Steepest Ascent** — this is the unit vector along the regression coefficients:
$$\mathbf{d} = (\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_k)$$

3. **Choose a Step Size (Δ) for the Most Influential Variable** — typically equal to a small, practical increment in that variable's natural units.

4. **Compute the Proportional Step Sizes** for all other variables:
$$\Delta x_i = \frac{\beta_i}{\beta_j^*} \cdot \Delta$$
where $j^*$ denotes the variable used to define the base step size.

5. **Conduct Experiments** along the path, taking new observations of the response $y$.

6. **Continue Until the Response Stops Improving** — the last "improving" point becomes the new **center** for the next design (typically a central composite design).

7. **Fit a Second-Order Model** in the new region to locate the precise optimum.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Expression |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat{y}$ | Predicted first-order response | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \beta_i x_i$ |
| $\beta_i$ | First-order regression coefficient | Estimated via least squares |
| $\nabla \hat{y}$ | Gradient vector | $(\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_k)$ |
| $\mathbf{d}$ | Direction of steepest ascent | $\mathbf{d} = (\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_k)$ |
| $\Delta x_i$ | Step size of variable $i$ | $\Delta x_i = \dfrac{\beta_i}{\beta_j^*}\Delta$ |
| $\Delta$ | Base step size (for variable $j^*$) | Chosen by experimenter (small practical value) |
| $x_{i,\text{new}}$ | New operating level of variable $i$ | $x_{i,\text{new}} = x_{i,\text{center}} + n\cdot\Delta x_i$ (where $n$ is the step number) |
| $S_y$ | Standard deviation of response (from replication) | Used to judge "improvement" vs. noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU:** The base step size $\Delta$ is **NOT** a mathematical constant. The experimenter selects it so that the smallest non-zero coefficient $\beta_i$ produces a *detectable, practical* change in $x_i$. This is a frequently tested conceptual question in Part A.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Engineering:** Maximizing yield of catalytic reactions by tuning temperature, pressure, and concentration.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Optimizing drug formulation parameters (pH, excipient ratio, mixing time).
- **Manufacturing:** Maximizing tool life by optimizing cutting speed, feed rate, and depth of cut.
- **Semiconductor Fabrication:** Optimizing deposition rate by adjusting gas flow ratios and substrate temperature.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation: The Direction of Steepest Ascent from the First-Order Model

### Step 1: Set Up the First-Order Model

Consider a system with $k$ independent variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k$ and one response $y$. The first-order model is:

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \beta_k x_k$$

This is a **hyperplane** in $(k+1)$-dimensional space, tilting in directions dictated by the coefficients $\beta_i$.

### Step 2: Compute the Gradient Vector

The gradient of $\hat{y}$ with respect to the input variables is:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = \left( \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} \right)$$

Computing each partial derivative (noting $\beta_0$ is a constant):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_1} &= \beta_1 \\
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_2} &= \beta_2 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
\frac{\partial \hat{y}}{\partial x_k} &= \beta_k
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the gradient vector simplifies to:

$$\nabla \hat{y} = (\beta_1, \beta_2, \ldots, \beta_k)$$

### Step 3: Interpret the Gradient as the Direction of Maximum Increase

The gradient vector always points in the direction of **maximum rate of increase** of the function. Thus, the path of **steepest ascent** is precisely the direction of this gradient.

### Step 4: Derive the Proportional Step Size Formula

Suppose the experimenter chooses a base step size $\Delta$ for a *reference variable* $x_{j^*}$. The corresponding step size for any other variable $x_i$ must be proportional to its regression coefficient relative to $\beta_{j^*}$. The ratio of movement along each axis is:

$$\frac{\Delta x_i}{\Delta x_j^*} = \frac{\beta_i}{\beta_{j^*}}$$

Solving for $\Delta x_i$:

$$\Delta x_i = \left( \frac{\beta_i}{\beta_{j^*}} \right) \cdot \Delta$$

This formula ensures that **the ratio of changes in the coded variables equals the ratio of their regression coefficients**, which is the formal definition of moving along the steepest ascent path.

### Step 5: General Point Along the Path

For the $n$-th step (where $n = 0$ is the original center), the new operating point is:

$$x_{i,n} = x_{i,\text{center}} + n \cdot \Delta x_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$$

### Step 6: Stopping Criterion Derivation

The process continues until the response $y_n$ at step $n$ is **no longer significantly greater** than the response at the previous step. A practical statistical test uses replication-based standard deviation $S_y$:

$$\text{Stop if} \quad \vert y_{n+1} - y_n \vert < \Delta_{\text{critical}}$$

where $\Delta_{\text{critical}}$ is typically **2 to 3 times $S_y$** (a noise threshold). This ensures we don't mistake random variation for real improvement.

---

## Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

### Problem Statement

A 2<sup>2</sup> factorial design was conducted to study the effect of **Reaction Temperature ($x_1$, °C)** and **Catalyst Concentration ($x_2$, %)** on the **Yield ($y$, %)** of a chemical process. The center point is at $x_1 = 80°C$, $x_2 = 5\%$. The fitted first-order model is:

$$\hat{y} = 45.0 + 2.5 x_1 + 1.0 x_2$$

with both coefficients significant at $\alpha = 0.05$ and the lack-of-fit test revealing inadequacy. The experimenter chooses a base step size of **Δ = 2°C** for $x_1$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the gradient direction.**

$$\nabla \hat{y} = (2.5, \; 1.0)$$

**Step 2: Compute step size for $x_2$ (catalyst concentration).**

$$\Delta x_2 = \left( \frac{\beta_2}{\beta_1} \right) \cdot \Delta = \left( \frac{1.0}{2.5} \right) \cdot 2 = 0.8 \, \%$$

**Step 3: Tabulate the path of steepest ascent.**

| Step $n$ | $x_1 = 80 + 2n$ | $x_2 = 5 + 0.8n$ | Observed $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (center) | 80 | 5.0 | 45.0 |
| 1 | 82 | 5.8 | 47.2 |
| 2 | 84 | 6.6 | 49.5 |
| 3 | 86 | 7.4 | 50.8 |
| 4 | 88 | 8.2 | 50.5 |
| 5 | 90 | 9.0 | 48.9 |

**Step 4: Decision Rule.**

Improvement continues from step 0 to step 3 (yield increases from 45.0 to 50.8). At step 4, yield drops to 50.5 — no meaningful improvement. Therefore, **step 3 is the new optimum region**, and a second-order design (Central Composite Design) should be centered at $(x_1, x_2) = (86, 7.4)$ to locate the precise optimum.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Procedural Flowchart of the Method of Steepest Ascent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Conduct 2k Factorial Design] --> B[Fit First-Order Model]
    B --> C{Test for Lack of Fit and Curvature}
    C -->|Model Adequate| D[Use First-Order Model Directly]
    C -->|Lack of Fit Detected| E[Compute Gradient Vector beta_i]
    E --> F[Choose Base Step Size Delta for Most Influential Variable]
    F --> G[Compute Proportional Step Sizes Delta x_i]
    G --> H[Take Step n=1: New Operating Point]
    H --> I[Run Experiment and Record y]
    I --> J{y Improved vs Previous Step?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Increment n and Take Next Step]
    K --> H
    J -->|No| L[New Center Located]
    L --> M[Fit Second-Order Model CCD in New Region]
    M --> N[Locate Precise Optimum]
    D --> N
```

## Diagram 2: Geometric Interpretation — Walking the Response Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph F1[First Order Region]
    P0[Center Point 0,0] -->|Path of Steepest Ascent| P1[Step 1]
    P1 --> P2[Step 2]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 - New Peak Region]
    end
    subgraph F2[Second Order Region]
    P3 --> P4[Central Composite Design]

    end

    style P5 fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
```

## Diagram 3: Functional Block Architecture — Steepest Ascent Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[Factorial Design Data] --> Module1[Module A: Regression Fitting]
    Module1 --> Output1[First Order Coefficients beta_i]
    Output1 --> Module2[Module B: Gradient Computation]
    Module2 --> Output2[Direction Vector d]
    Output3[Base Step Size Delta from User] --> Module3[Module C: Step Size Calculator]
    Output2 --> Module3
    Module3 --> Output4[Proportional Step Sizes Delta x_i]
    Output4 --> Module4[Module D: Path Generator]
    Module4 --> Output5[Sequence of Experimental Points]
    Output5 --> Module5[Module E: Response Evaluator]
    Module5 --> Decision{Improvement Detected}
    Decision -->|Yes| Module4
    Decision -->|No| Module6[Module F: New Center Selector]
    Module6 --> Output6[New Design Center for CCD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above block diagrams illustrate the *algorithmic workflow* of the steepest ascent procedure. In a typical 3-D visualization, the **first-order plane** (tilted) at the origin guides the initial direction, while the **curved second-order surface** (paraboloid) characterizes the response near the new optimum.
