sigma squared] eMS_A -- subtract --> eMS_AB eMS_AB -- subtract --> eMS_E eMS_A -.Test Factor A.-> Ftest1[F equals MS_A divided by MS_AB] eMS_B -.Test Factor B.-> Ftest2[F equals MS_B divided by MS_AB] eMS_AB -.Test Interaction.-> Ftest3[F equals MS_AB divided by MS_E] style eMS_A fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style eMS_B fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style eMS_AB fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style eMS_E fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style Ftest1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style Ftest2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style Ftest3 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Textual Block Diagram: Random-Factor Factorial Architecture ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ TWO-FACTOR FACTORIAL EXPERIMENT (a × b × n) │ │ │ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │ FACTOR A │ │ FACTOR B │ │ REPLICATION │ │ │ │ (Random or │× │ (Random or │× │ n per cell │ │ │ │ Fixed) │ │ Fixed) │ │ │ │ │ │ a levels │ │ b levels │ │ │ │ │ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ └──────┬───────┘ │ │ └────────┬────────┘ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────────┐ │ │ │ CELL OBSERVATIONS │ │ WITHIN-CELL │ │ │ │ Y_ijk for each │ │ VARIATION │ │ │ │ (i, j) combination │ │ SS_E (Error) │ │ │ └──────────┬──────────┘ └──────────┬──────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ANOVA TABLE → F-Tests → Variance │ │ │ │ → Variance Component Estimators │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q1.** *Distinguish between the **Fixed Effects Model (Model I)** and the **Random Effects Model (Model II)** in the context of a two-factor factorial design. State the practical implications of choosing one over the other.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Model I (Fixed) | Model II (Random) | |---|---|---| | **Nature of factor levels** | Specific, predetermined | Random sample from a population | | **Goal of inference** | Compare the chosen level means | Estimate the variance component of the factor | | **Hypothesis tested** | $H_0: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \dots = \alpha_a$ | $H_0: \sigma^2_\alpha = 0$ | | **Practical implication** | Conclusions apply *only* to the studied levels | Conclusions generalize to the entire population of levels | *(Award 1.5 marks for the distinction, 1.5 marks for the practical implication.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2.** *In a two-factor factorial experiment with **both factors random**, write down the Expected Mean Squares (EMS) for $MS_A$, $MS_B$, $MS_{AB}$, and $MS_E$.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** $$E[MS_A] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + bn\sigma^2_\alpha$$ $$E[MS_B] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + an\sigma^2_\beta$$ $$E[MS_{AB}] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$$ $$E[MS_E] = \sigma^2$$ *(Award 0.75 marks for each correct EMS expression.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **(a)** *In a study of the effect of **3 randomly chosen catalysts** (Factor A) and **4 randomly chosen reactors** (Factor B) on the yield of a chemical process, $n = 3$ replications are taken per cell. Construct the complete ANOVA table. State the model and the assumptions. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Using the EMS values, write the F-test statistic for testing the significance of Factor A, Factor B, and the A×B interaction. Also derive the variance component estimators. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Model Statement (2 marks):** $$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}, \quad i=1,2,3;\; j=1,\dots,4;\; k=1,2,3$$ Assumptions: 1. $\alpha_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2_\alpha)$ iid 2. $\beta_j \sim N(0, \sigma^2_\beta)$ iid 3. $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\alpha\beta})$ iid 4. $\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ iid 5. All four random variables are mutually independent. **ANOVA Table (5 marks):** | Source | SS | df | MS | |---|---|---|---| | Factor A (Catalyst) | $SS_A$ | $a-1 = 2$ | $MS_A = SS_A/2$ | | Factor B (Reactor) | $SS_B$ | $b-1 = 3$ | $MS_B = SS_B/3$ | | A × B Interaction | $SS_{AB}$ | $(a-1)(b-1) = 6$ | $MS_{AB} = SS_{AB}/6$ | | Error | $SS_E$ | $ab(n-1) = 24$ | $MS_E = SS_E/24$ | | **Total** | $SS_T$ | $abn - 1 = 35$ | — | **(b) F-Tests and Variance Component Estimators (7 marks):** **F-Tests (Random Model II):** - For Factor A: $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_A}{MS_{AB}} \sim F_{a-1,\,(a-1)(b-1)}$ - For Factor B: $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_B}{MS_{AB}} \sim F_{b-1,\,(a-1)(b-1)}$ - For A×B: $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} \sim F_{(a-1)(b-1),\,ab(n-1)}$ **Variance Component estimators (3 marks):** $$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2_{\alpha\beta} = \frac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2_\alpha = \frac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{bn}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2_\beta = \frac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)** **(a)** *A textile engineer investigates the effect of **3 fixed temperatures** (Factor A — fixed) and **5 randomly selected operators** (Factor B — random) on fabric strength, with $n = 4$ replications. State the mixed model. Construct the ANOVA table with the appropriate EMS. (7 marks)* **(b)** *Specify the correct F-tests for the main effects and interaction. Explain why the F-test denominator differs from the Random Model II. (7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Mixed Model Statement (3 marks):** $$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$ - $\alpha_i$ is **fixed** with constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{3}\alpha_i = 0$. - $\beta_j \sim N(0, \sigma^2_\beta)$ is **random**. - $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2_{\alpha\beta})$ is **random**. - $\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ is **random**. **ANOVA Table with EMS (4 marks):** | Source | df | MS | EMS (Mixed: A fixed, B random) | |---|---|---|---| | Factor A (Fixed Temp) | 2 | $MS_A$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + \frac{bn\sum\alpha_i^2}{a-1}$ | | Factor B (Random Operator) | 4 | $MS_B$ | $\sigma^2 + an\sigma^2_\beta$ | | A × B | 8 | $MS_{AB}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$ | | Error | 45 | $MS_E$ | $\sigma^2$ | **(b) F-Tests and Justification (7 marks):** **F-Tests (3 marks):** - Test Factor A (fixed): $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_A}{MS_{AB}} \sim F_{2,\,8}$ - Test Factor B (random): $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_B}{MS_E} \sim F_{4,\,45}$ - Test A×B interaction: $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} \sim F_{8,\,45}$ **Justification (4 marks):** The F-test denominator is selected by matching the EMS of the numerator to that of a lower-order MS that differs *only* by the variance component being tested. In the **Random Model II**, $MS_B$'s EMS contains the interaction variance $\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$, requiring $MS_{AB}$ as the denominator. However, in the **Mixed Model**, Factor A is fixed (so $\sigma^2_\alpha$ does not exist), and consequently the cross-product variance $\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$ drops out of $E[MS_B]$. Thus $E[MS_B] = \sigma^2 + an\sigma^2_\beta$, whose **next lower MS in the hierarchy is $MS_E$** (with EMS $= \sigma^2$). The interaction term is *absorbed* into the average over the fixed factor. Hence, $MS_B$ must be tested against $MS_E$, **not** $MS_{AB}$, in the Mixed Model. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Wrong F-denominator:** Using $MS_E$ instead of $MS_{AB}$ for testing Factor A in the Random Model — **deduct 2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting the constraint $\sum \alpha_i = 0$** for fixed factors in the Mixed Model — **deduct 1 mark**. > 3. **Confusing Model I, II, and Mixed** — always identify the *type* of factor *before* constructing F-ratios. Many students memorize the Random Model table and apply it blindly to the Mixed Model, losing **3 to 4 marks**. > 4. **Negative variance component estimators:** If $MS_{AB} < MS_E$, then $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\alpha\beta} < 0$ is reported — the convention is to **report it as zero** and re-state. This is a high-frequency conceptual error. > 5. **Degrees of freedom errors** in the Mixed Model are very common — practice drawing the table before the exam. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Two-Factor Factorial with Random Factor):** - ✅ **Model equation:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$ - ✅ **Three model types:** Fixed (Model I), Random (Model II), Mixed - ✅ **Random Model II assumption:** All effects ($\alpha_i, \beta_j, (\alpha\beta)_{ij}, \varepsilon_{ijk}$) are **mutually independent, normally distributed, with mean zero**. - ✅ **EMS for Random Model II:** - $E[MS_A] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + bn\sigma^2_\alpha$ - $E[MS_B] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + an\sigma^2_\beta$ - $E[MS_{AB}] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$ - $E[MS_E] = \sigma^2$ - ✅ **EMS for Mixed Model (A fixed, B random):** - $E[MS_A] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta} + \frac{bn\sum\alpha_i^2}{a-1}$ (non-random term appears!) - $E[MS_B] = \sigma^2 + an\sigma^2_\beta$ (interaction term **vanishes**!) - $E[MS_{AB}] = \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\alpha\beta}$ - $E[MS_E] = \sigma^2$ - ✅ **F-test denominators (Random Model II):** $MS_A \to MS_{AB}$; $MS_B \to MS_{AB}$; $MS_{AB} \to MS_E$. - ✅ **F-test denominators (Mixed Model):** $MS_A \to MS_{AB}$; $MS_B \to MS_E$ (different from Random Model); $MS_{AB} \to MS_E$. - ✅ **Variance component estimators (Random Model II):** Equate MS to EMS, then solve: - $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$ - $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\alpha\beta} = (MS_{AB} - MS_E)/n$ - $\hat{\sigma}^2_\alpha = (MS_A - MS_{AB})/bn$ - $\hat{\sigma}^2_\beta = (MS_B - MS_{AB})/an$ - ✅ **Negative estimator rule:** If any numerator is negative, set that variance component to **zero** and re-estimate. - ✅ **Degrees of freedom:** $a-1$, $b-1$, $(a-1)(b-1)$, $ab(n-1)$, $abn-1$ for A, B, A×B, Error, Total respectively. - ✅ **KTU exam mantra:** "*Identify factor type → Write EMS → Match numerator and denominator EMS → Compute F → Estimate variance components.*"

topics.title: Two Factor Mixed Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Two Factor Mixed Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Two-Factor Mixed Model (Mixed Effects Model)** is an Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) design in which one of the two treatment factors is a **fixed factor** (its levels are deliberately chosen and exhaustive of the researcher's interest) while the other factor is a **random factor** (its levels constitute a random sample drawn from a much larger infinite population of possible levels). The interaction term between these two factors is, by mathematical convention, treated as a **random effect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> A Two-Factor Mixed Model ANOVA is used when Factor A has fixed levels (e.g., specific temperatures, catalysts, drugs) and Factor B has random levels (e.g., batches, operators, days, machines selected at random). The interaction (A×B) is always modeled as a random effect.

### 1.2 The Linear Statistical Model

The standard mathematical representation of a balanced Two-Factor Mixed Model is:

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

Where the indices and parameters represent:

- $Y_{ijk}$ = the response variable for the $k^{th}$ replicate of the $i^{th}$ level of Factor A and $j^{th}$ level of Factor B
- $\mu$ = the **grand mean** (overall true mean response)
- $\alpha_i$ = the **fixed effect** of the $i^{th}$ level of Factor A, with the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{a} \alpha_i = 0$
- $\beta_j \sim N(0, \sigma_{\beta}^{2})$ = the **random effect** of the $j^{th}$ level of Factor B
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2})$ = the **random interaction effect** between level $i$ of A and level $j$ of B
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^{2})$ = the **random error term**
- $i = 1, 2, \ldots, a$; $j = 1, 2, \ldots, b$; $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$

All random effects $\beta_j$, $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$, and $\epsilon_{ijk}$ are assumed to be **mutually independent**, normally distributed with mean zero.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Experiment"**
>
> Imagine a coffee chain wants to test **3 specific coffee blends** (Fixed Factor A: Espresso, Cappuccino, Latte — these are the *exact* drinks of interest) sold across **5 randomly selected store branches** (Random Factor B: randomly chosen outlets from their 200+ stores nationwide).
>
> - **Fixed (Blend):** The owner CARES about these 3 specific blends. He wants to know *which blend is best* — the conclusion applies only to these three.
> - **Random (Branch):** The 5 stores are just a *sample* of all stores. The owner wants to generalize: "Performance varies between branches, and we need to estimate that variability."
> - **Interaction (Blend × Branch):** Some blends may sell better in some branches than others (e.g., Latte sells more in urban branches). This *interaction* itself is a random phenomenon.
> - **Error:** Day-to-day fluctuation within a single store on a single blend.

This is precisely the **Mixed Model** situation — one factor's levels are deliberately chosen, the other's are randomly sampled.

### 1.4 Comparison with Other Two-Factor Models

| Model Type | Factor A | Factor B | Interaction | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Effects Model** | Fixed | Fixed | Fixed (no random component) | Comparing specific treatments |
| **Random Effects Model** | Random | Random | Random | Variance component estimation |
| **Mixed Model** | Fixed | Random | Random (always) | Generalization from random batches |
| **Random-Fixed Mixed Model** | Random | Fixed | Random (always) | Mirror case; same analysis logic |

> [!WARNING]
> A common student misconception is that the interaction is "fixed" in the mixed model. **By KTU convention, the interaction in a mixed model is ALWAYS random** because one of the parents (Factor B) is random. This single fact dictates the entire EMS structure and F-test denominators.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Sets of Hypotheses

Because the factors have different statistical natures, **three distinct hypothesis systems** must be tested:

**For the Fixed Factor A:**
$$H_{0}^{(A)}: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \ldots = \alpha_a = 0$$
$$H_{1}^{(A)}: \text{At least one } \alpha_i \neq 0$$

**For the Random Factor B:**
$$H_{0}^{(B)}: \sigma_{\beta}^{2} = 0 \quad \text{(B contributes no variability)}$$
$$H_{1}^{(B)}: \sigma_{\beta}^{2} > 0$$

**For the Random Interaction A×B:**
$$H_{0}^{(AB)}: \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = 0 \quad \text{(No interaction present)}$$
$$H_{1}^{(AB)}: \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the **asymmetric null hypotheses**: A is tested on the mean scale ($\alpha_i = 0$), while B and AB are tested on the variance scale ($\sigma^2 = 0$). This is a recurring KTU board exam question.

### 2.2 Why the Expected Mean Squares (EMS) Matter — The "Why" of the Whole Topic

The F-statistic in ANOVA is a ratio of two independent mean squares. For a valid F-test, **the numerator MS must differ from the denominator MS by exactly the variance component you wish to test, and nothing else**. The EMS table reveals this:

| Source | EMS (Two-Factor Mixed: A Fixed, B Random) | What it tests |
|---|---|---|
| Factor A (Fixed) | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + nb \cdot \theta_{\alpha}$ | Tests fixed main effect of A |
| Factor B (Random) | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + na\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ | Tests random main effect of B |
| Interaction A×B | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ | Tests random interaction |
| Error | $\sigma^{2}$ | Baseline error |

Where the **fixed-effect quadratic form** is:
$$\theta_{\alpha} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{a} \alpha_i^{2}}{a-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "**n**" multiplier attached to $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ and "**nb**" / "**na**" attached to the main effects come from the number of observations contributing to each mean square in a balanced design. This is why **balance is critical** in mixed models — unbalanced data break the orthogonality that makes EMS clean.

### 2.3 The F-Test Denominator Rules (HIGH-YIELD)

| Effect being tested | Numerator MS | Denominator MS | F-ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| Factor A (Fixed) | $MS_A$ | $MS_{AB}$ | $F_A = MS_A / MS_{AB}$ |
| Factor B (Random) | $MS_B$ | $MS_{AB}$ | $F_B = MS_B / MS_{AB}$ |
| Interaction A×B | $MS_{AB}$ | $MS_E$ | $F_{AB} = MS_{AB} / MS_E$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When interaction is present (i.e., $F_{AB}$ is significant):** The interaction MS must be used as the error term for the main effects. This is the cardinal rule distinguishing mixed-model F-tests from fixed-model F-tests, where $MS_E$ alone is used.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{(\sum\sum\sum Y_{ijk})^{2}}{abn}$ | Anchor for all SS computations |
| $SS_{Total}$ | $\sum\sum\sum Y_{ijk}^{2} - CF$ | Total variability |
| $SS_A$ (Fixed) | $\dfrac{\sum_{i} T_{i\cdot\cdot}^{2}}{bn} - CF$ | Variability due to A |
| $SS_B$ (Random) | $\dfrac{\sum_{j} T_{\cdot j\cdot}^{2}}{an} - CF$ | Variability due to B |
| $SS_{AB}$ (Interaction) | $\dfrac{\sum_{i}\sum_{j} T_{ij\cdot}^{2}}{n} - CF - SS_A - SS_B$ | Interaction variability |
| $SS_E$ | $SS_{Total} - SS_{Total\;cells} = SS_{Total} - \dfrac{\sum\sum T_{ij\cdot}^{2}}{n} + CF$ | Pure error |
| $df_A$ | $a-1$ | Degrees of freedom for A |
| $df_B$ | $b-1$ | Degrees of freedom for B |
| $df_{AB}$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ | Degrees of freedom for AB |
| $df_E$ | $ab(n-1)$ | Degrees of freedom for error |
| Variance component $\hat{\sigma}^{2}$ | $MS_E$ | Error variance estimate |
| Variance component $\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ | $\dfrac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n}$ | Interaction variance estimate |
| Variance component $\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2}$ | $\dfrac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an}$ | B variance estimate |
| EMS for A | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + nb\theta_{\alpha}$ | For F-test of fixed A |
| EMS for B | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + na\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ | For F-test of random B |
| EMS for AB | $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ | For F-test of interaction |
| EMS for Error | $\sigma^{2}$ | Baseline |

> [!NOTE]
> If a variance component estimate turns out **negative**, KTU convention (and Hartley–Rao's MINQUE philosophy) is to **set it to zero** — variances cannot be negative. This is a frequent 3-mark KTU question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Two-Factor Mixed Models are workhorse tools across industries:

- **Pharmaceutical manufacturing:** Fixed = drug formulation; Random = production batches drawn from the year → assess if the formulation effect is consistent *across batches*.
- **Agriculture:** Fixed = fertilizer type; Random = randomly chosen fields → generalize field-to-field variability.
- **Semiconductor fabrication:** Fixed = lithography setting; Random = wafers/lots → generalize process variability.
- **Software engineering performance testing:** Fixed = algorithm variant; Random = randomly sampled input datasets → estimate generalization performance variance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, ANOVA Construction, and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Numerical Example — A Chemical Yield Study

**Problem Setup (KTU 2024 Module-4 style problem):**

A chemical engineer studies the effect of **3 catalyst types (Factor A — Fixed)** on yield, with the experiment run on **4 randomly selected batches (Factor B — Random)**, with **2 replications per cell** ($n = 2$).

Raw data table (yield in %):

| Catalyst \ Batch | B₁ | B₂ | B₃ | B₄ | Row Sum $T_{i\cdot\cdot}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A₁ | 72, 73 | 75, 74 | 70, 71 | 73, 72 | 580 |
| A₂ | 80, 79 | 82, 81 | 78, 77 | 81, 80 | 638 |
| A₃ | 68, 67 | 70, 69 | 66, 65 | 69, 68 | 542 |
| **Col Sum $T_{\cdot j\cdot}$** | **439** | **451** | **427** | **443** | **G = 1760** |

Cell totals $T_{ij\cdot}$:

| | B₁ | B₂ | B₃ | B₄ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A₁ | 145 | 149 | 141 | 145 |
| A₂ | 159 | 163 | 155 | 161 |
| A₃ | 135 | 139 | 131 | 137 |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Sum of Squares Derivation

**Step 1 — Grand Total and Correction Factor:**

$$G = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}\sum_{k} Y_{ijk} = 1760$$

$$CF = \frac{G^{2}}{abn} = \frac{(1760)^{2}}{(3)(4)(2)} = \frac{3{,}097{,}600}{24} = 129{,}066.67$$

**Step 2 — Total Sum of Squares:**

First, $\sum\sum\sum Y_{ijk}^{2}$ is computed:

$$\sum\sum\sum Y_{ijk}^{2} = 10513 + 11101 + 9941 + 10513 + 12641 + 13285 + 12013 + 12961 + 9113 + 9661 + 8581 + 9385 = 129{,}707$$

Then:

$$SS_{Total} = 129{,}707 - 129{,}066.67 = 640.33$$

**Step 3 — Sum of Squares for Fixed Factor A:**

$$SS_A = \frac{\sum_{i} T_{i\cdot\cdot}^{2}}{bn} - CF$$

$$\sum_i T_{i\cdot\cdot}^{2} = (580)^{2} + (638)^{2} + (542)^{2} = 336{,}400 + 407{,}044 + 293{,}764 = 1{,}037{,}208$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1{,}037{,}208}{(4)(2)} - 129{,}066.67 = \frac{1{,}037{,}208}{8} - 129{,}066.67$$

$$SS_A = 129{,}651.00 - 129{,}066.67 = 584.33$$

**Step 4 — Sum of Squares for Random Factor B:**

$$SS_B = \frac{\sum_{j} T_{\cdot j\cdot}^{2}}{an} - CF$$

$$\sum_j T_{\cdot j\cdot}^{2} = (439)^{2} + (451)^{2} + (427)^{2} + (443)^{2} = 192{,}721 + 203{,}401 + 182{,}329 + 196{,}249 = 774{,}700$$

$$SS_B = \frac{774{,}700}{(3)(2)} - 129{,}066.67 = \frac{774{,}700}{6} - 129{,}066.67$$

$$SS_B = 129{,}116.67 - 129{,}066.67 = 50.00$$

**Step 5 — Sum of Squares for Sub-Totals (Cell Totals):**

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{\sum_{i}\sum_{j} T_{ij\cdot}^{2}}{n} - CF$$

$$\sum_i\sum_j T_{ij\cdot}^{2} = (145)^{2} + (149)^{2} + (141)^{2} + (145)^{2} + (159)^{2} + (163)^{2} + (155)^{2} + (161)^{2} + (135)^{2} + (139)^{2} + (131)^{2} + (137)^{2}$$

$$= 21{,}025 + 22{,}201 + 19{,}881 + 21{,}025 + 25{,}281 + 26{,}569 + 24{,}025 + 25{,}921 + 18{,}225 + 19{,}321 + 17{,}161 + 18{,}769 = 259{,}405$$

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{259{,}405}{2} - 129{,}066.67 = 129{,}702.50 - 129{,}066.67 = 635.83$$

**Step 6 — Interaction Sum of Squares:**

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B$$

$$SS_{AB} = 635.83 - 584.33 - 50.00 = 1.50$$

**Step 7 — Error (Within-Cell) Sum of Squares:**

$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells}$$

$$SS_E = 640.33 - 635.83 = 4.50$$

**Verification (sanity check):**

$$SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E = 584.33 + 50.00 + 1.50 + 4.50 = 640.33 \;\; \checkmark$$

### 3.3 ANOVA Table Construction and F-Tests

**Degrees of Freedom:**

$$df_A = a - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2$$

$$df_B = b - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

$$df_{AB} = (a-1)(b-1) = (2)(3) = 6$$

$$df_E = ab(n-1) = (3)(4)(1) = 12$$

**Mean Squares:**

$$MS_A = \frac{SS_A}{df_A} = \frac{584.33}{2} = 292.17$$

$$MS_B = \frac{SS_B}{df_B} = \frac{50.00}{3} = 16.67$$

$$MS_{AB} = \frac{SS_{AB}}{df_{AB}} = \frac{1.50}{6} = 0.25$$

$$MS_E = \frac{SS_E}{df_E} = \frac{4.50}{12} = 0.375$$

**F-Ratios (using correct denominators per mixed-model rules):**

$$F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_{AB}} = \frac{292.17}{0.25} = 1168.67$$

$$F_B = \frac{MS_B}{MS_{AB}} = \frac{16.67}{0.25} = 66.67$$

$$F_{AB} = \frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} = \frac{0.25}{0.375} = 0.667$$

**Complete ANOVA Table:**

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | EMS | F-ratio | $F_{0.05}$ Critical | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Factor A (Fixed) | 584.33 | 2 | 292.17 | $\sigma^{2}+n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}+nb\theta_{\alpha}$ | 1168.67 (on $MS_{AB}$) | $F_{0.05,2,6}=5.14$ | Reject $H_0$ |
| Factor B (Random) | 50.00 | 3 | 16.67 | $\sigma^{2}+n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}+na\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ | 66.67 (on $MS_{AB}$) | $F_{0.05,3,6}=4.76$ | Reject $H_0$ |
| Interaction A×B | 1.50 | 6 | 0.25 | $\sigma^{2}+n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ | 0.667 (on $MS_E$) | $F_{0.05,6,12}=3.00$ | Fail to reject $H_0$ |
| Error | 4.50 | 12 | 0.375 | $\sigma^{2}$ | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **640.33** | **23** | | | | | |

### 3.4 Variance Component estimation

**Step 8 — Estimating $\sigma^{2}$ (Error variance):**

$$\hat{\sigma}^{2} = MS_E = 0.375$$

**Step 9 — Estimating $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ (Interaction variance):**

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = \frac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{0.25 - 0.375}{2} = \frac{-0.125}{2} = -0.0625$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = 0 \quad \text{(Set to 0; negative value is set to zero per KTU convention)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A negative estimate is **mathematically meaningless** as a variance. The KTU board expects you to report it as 0 and continue. Do NOT write a negative variance in the exam.

**Step 10 — Estimating $\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ (Random factor B variance):**

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} = \frac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an} = \frac{16.67 - 0.25}{(3)(2)} = \frac{16.42}{6} = 2.736$$

### 3.5 Interpretation of the Results

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:**
>
> 1. **Interaction (A×B) is NOT significant** ($F_{AB} = 0.667 < 3.00$, $p > 0.05$). The catalyst effect does NOT depend on the batch — catalysts behave consistently.
> 2. **Catalyst (Fixed A) is highly significant** ($F_A = 1168.67 \gg 5.14$). At least one catalyst gives a different mean yield.
> 3. **Batches (Random B) are highly variable** ($\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} = 2.736$ vs $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = 0.375$). Batch-to-batch variability dominates — the engineer should target **batch standardization** as a process improvement lever.

### 3.6 Why the Interaction MS Becomes the Denominator — A Step-by-Step Logical Proof

Reading the EMS table:

- $E(MS_A) = \sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + nb\theta_{\alpha}$
- $E(MS_{AB}) = \sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$

Their ratio's expectation isolates the fixed-effect term:

$$\frac{E(MS_A)}{E(MS_{AB})} = \frac{\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + nb\theta_{\alpha}}{\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}}$$

If $H_0^{(A)}: \theta_{\alpha} = 0$, then the ratio's expectation is **exactly 1** — which is what F-test theory requires. The interaction MS cancels out the common $\sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ from both numerator and denominator, isolating $\theta_{\alpha}$ alone. Using $MS_E$ as denominator would have left an un-cancelled $n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ term, **inflating the denominator artificially** and creating a conservative (but biased) test. This is the entire **why** of mixed-model F-tests.

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of the Two-Factor Mixed Model Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OBSERVATION Y_ijk]:::obsNode
    A --> B[GRAND MEAN mu]
    A --> C[MAIN EFFECT A_i FIXED]
    A --> D[MAIN EFFECT B_j RANDOM]
    A --> E[INTERACTION alpha-beta_ij RANDOM]
    A --> F[ERROR epsilon_ijk]
    C --> G[Hyp A: alpha_1 = alpha_2 = ... = alpha_a = 0]
    D --> H[Hyp B: sigma_beta_sq = 0]
    E --> I[Hyp AB: sigma_alphaBeta_sq = 0]
    G --> J[F_A = MS_A OVER MS_AB]
    H --> K[F_B = MS_B OVER MS_AB]
    I --> L[F_AB = MS_AB OVER MS_E]
    J --> M[DECISION on A FIXED]
    K --> N[DECISION on B RANDOM]
    L --> O[DECISION on INTERACTION]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    MSab[MS_AB sigma_sq + n sigma_ab_sq estimate]:::stepB

    MSab --> DELTA1[sigma_ab_sq hat = MS_AB - MS_E divided by n]
    MSb --> DELTA2[sigma_b_sq hat = MS_B - MS_AB divided by an]
    MSa --> DELTA3[theta_alpha hat = MS_A - MS_AB divided by nb]

    DELTA2 --> TRIM
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the linear statistical model for a two-factor mixed model ANOVA where Factor A is fixed and Factor B is random. Clearly specify the distributional assumptions for each term.*

**Model Answer:**

The two-factor mixed model is given by:

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

where:
- $\mu$ is the overall mean; $\alpha_i$ is the **fixed** effect of $i^{th}$ level of A with $\sum_{i=1}^{a}\alpha_i = 0$
- $\beta_j \sim N(0, \sigma_{\beta}^{2})$ is the **random** effect of the $j^{th}$ level of B
- $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2})$ is the **random interaction** (always random since B is random)
- $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^{2})$ is the random error
- All random components are mutually independent
- Indices: $i = 1,\ldots,a$; $j = 1,\ldots,b$; $k = 1,\ldots,n$

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the interaction term treated as a random effect in a mixed model ANOVA, and what is its consequence on the F-test for the main effects?*

**Model Answer:**

The interaction $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ is treated as random because one of its parent factors (Factor B) is random. A random parent's interaction is also random by definition.

**Consequence on F-tests:** The interaction mean square ($MS_{AB}$) contains both $\sigma^{2}$ and $n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ in its expectation, while $MS_E$ contains only $\sigma^{2}$. When the interaction exists, the main effect MS ratios are constructed using $MS_{AB}$ (not $MS_E$) as the denominator, so that the common interaction variance cancels and the test isolates the main-effect quantity. This gives a more powerful and statistically valid test.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

The following data are collected for a study on the tensile strength (in MPa) of a polymer composite. Factor A (Catalyst Type) is **fixed** with 3 levels; Factor B (Production Batch) is **random** with 4 levels; $n = 2$ replications per cell.

| Catalyst \ Batch | B₁ | B₂ | B₃ | B₄ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A₁ | 215, 220 | 218, 222 | 212, 216 | 220, 224 |
| A₂ | 230, 234 | 232, 236 | 226, 230 | 234, 238 |
| A₃ | 205, 209 | 208, 212 | 202, 206 | 210, 214 |

**(a)** [7 marks] Formulate the linear model, state the hypotheses, and construct the complete ANOVA table for the mixed model. Compute the F-statistics and test for significance at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**(b)** [7 marks] Estimate the variance components $\hat{\sigma}^{2}$, $\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$, and $\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2}$. Interpret the practical significance of each.

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Model and Hypotheses:**

Model: $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$, $i=1,2,3$; $j=1,2,3,4$; $k=1,2$.

Hypotheses:
- $H_0^{(A)}: \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = 0$
- $H_0^{(B)}: \sigma_{\beta}^{2} = 0$
- $H_0^{(AB)}: \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = 0$

**Row and Column Totals (computed from data):**

| Catalyst \ Batch | B₁ | B₂ | B₃ | B₄ | Row Sum $T_{i\cdot\cdot}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A₁ | 435 | 440 | 428 | 444 | 1747 |
| A₂ | 464 | 468 | 456 | 472 | 1860 |
| A₃ | 414 | 420 | 408 | 424 | 1666 |
| **Col Sum** | **1313** | **1328** | **1292** | **1340** | **G = 5273** |

Correction Factor:

$$CF = \frac{(5273)^2}{(3)(4)(2)} = \frac{27{,}804{,}529}{24} = 1{,}158{,}522.04$$

Total Sum of Squares: $\sum\sum\sum Y_{ijk}^{2} = 215^2 + 220^2 + \ldots$

Computing each cell sum of squares:

- A₁B₁: $215^2 + 220^2 = 46225 + 48400 = 94625$
- A₁B₂: $218^2 + 222^2 = 47524 + 49284 = 96808$
- A₁B₃: $212^2 + 216^2 = 44944 + 46656 = 91600$
- A₁B₄: $220^2 + 224^2 = 48400 + 50176 = 98576$
- A₂B₁: $230^2 + 234^2 = 52900 + 54756 = 107656$
- A₂B₂: $232^2 + 236^2 = 53824 + 55696 = 109520$
- A₂B₃: $226^2 + 230^2 = 51076 + 52900 = 103976$
- A₂B₄: $234^2 + 238^2 = 54756 + 56644 = 111400$
- A₃B₁: $205^2 + 209^2 = 42025 + 43681 = 85706$
- A₃B₂: $208^2 + 212^2 = 43264 + 44944 = 88208$
- A₃B₃: $202^2 + 206^2 = 40804 + 42436 = 83240$
- A₃B₄: $210^2 + 214^2 = 44100 + 45796 = 89896$

$$\sum\sum\sum Y^2 = 1{,}181{,}211$$

$$SS_{Total} = 1{,}181{,}211 - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 22{,}688.96$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1747^2 + 1860^2 + 1666^2}{8} - CF$$

$$= \frac{3{,}052{,}009 + 3{,}459{,}600 + 2{,}775{,}556}{8} - 1{,}158{,}522.04$$

$$= \frac{9{,}287{,}165}{8} - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 1{,}160{,}895.625 - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 2373.58$$

$$SS_B = \frac{1313^2 + 1328^2 + 1292^2 + 1340^2}{6} - CF$$

$$= \frac{1{,}723{,}969 + 1{,}763{,}584 + 1{,}669{,}264 + 1{,}795{,}600}{6} - 1{,}158{,}522.04$$

$$= \frac{6{,}952{,}417}{6} - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 1{,}158{,}736.17 - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 214.13$$

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{\sum_{ij} T_{ij\cdot}^2}{2} - CF$$

Cell sums squared:
- $435^2 + 440^2 + 428^2 + 444^2 = 189225 + 193600 + 183184 + 197136 = 763145$
- $464^2 + 468^2 + 456^2 + 472^2 = 215296 + 219024 + 207936 + 222784 = 865040$
- $414^2 + 420^2 + 408^2 + 424^2 = 171396 + 176400 + 166464 + 179776 = 694036$

$$\sum T_{ij\cdot}^2 = 763145 + 865040 + 694036 = 2{,}322{,}221$$

$$SS_{Cells} = \frac{2{,}322{,}221}{2} - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 1{,}161{,}110.50 - 1{,}158{,}522.04 = 2588.46$$

$$SS_{AB} = SS_{Cells} - SS_A - SS_B = 2588.46 - 2373.58 - 214.13 = 0.75$$

$$SS_E = SS_{Total} - SS_{Cells} = 22{,}688.96 - 2588.46 = 20{,}100.50$$

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F-Ratio | Denominator | $F_{0.05}$ Critical | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Fixed) | 2373.58 | 2 | 1186.79 | 9494.32 | $MS_{AB}$ | $F_{2,6} = 5.14$ | Reject $H_0$ |
| B (Random) | 214.13 | 3 | 71.38 | 571.04 | $MS_{AB}$ | $F_{3,6} = 4.76$ | Reject $H_0$ |
| AB (Interaction) | 0.75 | 6 | 0.125 | 0.0000746 | $MS_E$ | $F_{6,12} = 3.00$ | Fail to reject |
| Error | 20100.50 | 12 | 1675.04 | — | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **22688.96** | **23** | | | | | |

**Part (b) — Variance Components:**

$$\hat{\sigma}^{2} = MS_E = 1675.04$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = \frac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{0.125 - 1675.04}{2} = -837.46 \Rightarrow 0$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} = \frac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an} = \frac{71.38 - 0.125}{6} = 11.876$$

**Interpretation:** Catalyst choice dominates the response (fixed-effect main effect is enormous). Batch-to-batch variability is real ($\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} \approx 11.88$) but is **dwarfed by the within-batch measurement noise** ($\hat{\sigma}^{2} \approx 1675$). The interaction is essentially zero — the catalyst effect is consistent across batches. Engineering takeaway: focus QC on reducing within-batch measurement variability rather than on cross-batch standardization.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

A fertilizer company tests the effect of **3 fertilizer formulations** (Factor A, fixed) on plant growth. The formulations are applied in **5 randomly selected greenhouses** (Factor B, random) with **3 plants per cell** ($n=3$).

**(a)** [7 marks] Write the mathematical model, derive the Expected Mean Squares (EMS) for all four sources, and clearly state which F-tests use $MS_{AB}$ vs $MS_E$ as denominator. Justify your choice using the EMS table.

**(b)** [7 marks] Given the following partial ANOVA output, complete the table and draw engineering conclusions:

| Source | df | SS | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Fixed) | 2 | 450.00 | 225.00 | ? |
| B (Random) | 4 | 80.00 | 20.00 | ? |
| A×B | 8 | 24.00 | 3.00 | ? |
| Error | 30 | 180.00 | 6.00 | |
| **Total** | **44** | | | |

Use $\alpha = 0.05$. Critical values: $F_{0.05,2,8} = 4.46$, $F_{0.05,4,8} = 3.84$, $F_{0.05,8,30} = 2.27$.

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Model and EMS Derivation:**

Model: $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$

where $\alpha_i$ fixed, $\sum \alpha_i = 0$; $\beta_j \sim N(0, \sigma_{\beta}^{2})$; $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2})$; $\epsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^{2})$.

EMS Derivation (using the rule: each MS = its own variance + sum of random effects nested within it multiplied by their replication counts):

- $E(MS_E) = \sigma^{2}$
- $E(MS_{AB}) = \sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$
- $E(MS_B) = \sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + an\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$
- $E(MS_A) = \sigma^{2} + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2} + bn \cdot \theta_{\alpha}$, where $\theta_{\alpha} = \sum \alpha_i^2 / (a-1)$

**F-test Denominators:**

- For A (Fixed): denominator = $MS_{AB}$, because $E(MS_A) - E(MS_{AB}) = bn\theta_{\alpha}$ (isolates the fixed effect).
- For B (Random): denominator = $MS_{AB}$, because $E(MS_B) - E(MS_{AB}) = an\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ (isolates $\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$).
- For AB (Random): denominator = $MS_E$, because $E(MS_{AB}) - E(MS_E) = n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ (isolates $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$).

**Part (b) — Completing the F-tests:**

$$F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_{AB}} = \frac{225.00}{3.00} = 75.00$$

$$F_B = \frac{MS_B}{MS_{AB}} = \frac{20.00}{3.00} = 6.67$$

$$F_{AB} = \frac{MS_{AB}}{MS_E} = \frac{3.00}{6.00} = 0.50$$

**Completed Table and Conclusions:**

| Source | df | SS | MS | F | Critical | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Fixed) | 2 | 450.00 | 225.00 | 75.00 | 4.46 | **Significant** |
| B (Random) | 4 | 80.00 | 20.00 | 6.67 | 3.84 | **Significant** |
| A×B | 8 | 24.00 | 3.00 | 0.50 | 2.27 | Not significant |
| Error | 30 | 180.00 | 6.00 | — | — | — |

**Engineering Conclusion:**

1. At least one fertilizer formulation produces a different mean growth — choose the best.
2. Greenhouses (batches) are significantly different — environmental control is a real source of variability.
3. No interaction means the **best fertilizer is consistently the best across all greenhouses** — the formulation effect is **portable** and can be confidently deployed across all sites.
4. Variance components: $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = 6.00$; $\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = (3-6)/3 = -1 \Rightarrow 0$; $\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} = (20-3)/9 = 1.89$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Two-Factor Mixed Model Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong denominator for F-tests** (most common; loses 3-4 marks). Students use $MS_E$ for main effects. **Always use $MS_{AB}$ when interaction is in the model and Factor B is random.**
> 2. **Reporting a negative variance component literally**. The KTU board expects you to set negatives to zero; failure to do so loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the random nature of interaction**. In a mixed model, the interaction is always random. Writing "fixed interaction" costs 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing the constraint** $\sum \alpha_i = 0$ for fixed A. The constraint is on A only — not on B or AB (since they are random with mean zero automatically).
> 5. **Wrong degrees of freedom for $MS_{AB}$**: must be $(a-1)(b-1)$, not $ab - 1$.
> 6. **Forgetting the $n$ divisor** when estimating $\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$ and $\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2}$.
> 7. **Confusing $SS_{Cells}$ with $SS_{AB}$**. $SS_{Cells}$ is the sum over all cell totals; the interaction SS requires subtracting $SS_A$ and $SS_B$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Two-Factor Mixed Model):**
>
> - [ ] **Model:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$ with $\alpha_i$ fixed, all else random
> - [ ] **Three null hypotheses** — one for A (means), two for variances ($\sigma_{\beta}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^{2}$)
> - [ ] **Interaction is ALWAYS random** when one parent is random — non-negotiable
> - [ ] **EMS key signatures:** A has $\theta_{\alpha}$ (fixed), B and AB have $\sigma^{2}$ components (random)
> - [ ] **F-test denominators:** A → $MS_{AB}$; B → $MS_{AB}$; AB → $MS_E$
> - [ ] **Variance component formulas:** $\hat{\sigma}^{2} = MS_E$; $\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^{2} = (MS_{AB} - MS_E)/n$; $\hat{\sigma}_{\beta}^{2} = (MS_B - MS_{AB})/(an)$
> - [ ] **Negative variance → set to 0**
> - [ ] **ANOVA identity:** $SS_{Total} = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$ (always verify)
> - [ ] **Degrees of freedom:** $a-1$, $b-1$, $(a-1)(b-1)$, $ab(n-1)$ for A, B, AB, E respectively
> - [ ] **Assumption of normality** for all random components ($\beta$, $(\alpha\beta)$, $\epsilon$)
> - [ ] **Balance is critical** — unbalanced mixed models require Type III SS (different from balanced)
> - [ ] **Practical insight:** Significant interaction = treat data per cell; Non-significant interaction = pool and trust main effects
> - [ ] **Engineering implication:** Random factor significance means you should generalize cautiously — inference is about the variance, not specific batches

---

*End of Module 4 — Two Factor Mixed Model Notes. Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PEPET525 syllabus outcomes aligned with Bloom's Levels L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), and L5 (Evaluate).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rules for Expected Mean Square
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rules for Expected Mean Square

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expected Mean Square (EMS):** The Expected Mean Square of a source of variation in an Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) table is the theoretical (population) average value of its computed mean square, expressed as a linear combination of the true variance components of the experiment.

Mathematically, for any source $S$ in the ANOVA decomposition,

$$E(MS_S) = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \, \sigma_i^2$$

where $\lambda_i$ are non-negative **coefficients (multipliers)** and $\sigma_i^2$ are the true **variance components** associated with each random effect and the error term.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Salary Account"

Imagine a joint family account that receives monthly contributions from three sources: **Father's salary** (fixed), **Mother's freelance income** (random), and a **shared investment pool** (mixed). At the end of every month, the family accountant (the *Mean Square*) reports a number — but the *true* expected contribution of each source to that number is hidden inside an average.

The **Rules for Expected Mean Square** are like the accountant's reconciliation method: given only the labeled deposits (the sources in the ANOVA table), we deduce *how much of the variance is genuinely due to that source* versus *how much is just noise mixed in from other sources*. Knowing this lets the family (the experimenter) decide whether a source is a "real" contributor (significant) or merely a "pass-through" channel.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need EMS Rules?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Critical Purposes of EMS:**
> 1. **Correct F-test construction** — The denominator of an F-statistic must be a mean square whose EMS contains *only* the variance component being tested (otherwise the F-ratio is inflated/deflated).
> 2. **Variance component estimation** — Solving the EMS equations yields unbiased estimators (ANOVA estimators) of $\sigma^2$.
> 3. **Model diagnosis** — EMS reveals whether the model is **Model I (Fixed)**, **Model II (Random)**, or **Model III (Mixed)** and guides interpretation.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** Outcome-Based framework, this topic directly supports **CO4**: *"Formulate and analyze experimental designs using ANOVA techniques and interpret EMS for hypothesis testing."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Algebra of EMS

Every observation in a designed experiment can be written as a sum of identifiable "effects" plus an error. When we compute $E(MS)$ for a source, we are asking:

> *"If we could repeat the experiment infinitely many times and average the resulting mean squares, what linear combination of the underlying true variances would we obtain?"*

This expectation is taken with respect to the **random** components only; fixed effects drop out because $E(\text{fixed effect}) = 0$ (deviation from its mean is zero).

## 2.2 The Standard Rules for Computing EMS

The **Correspondences / Brinton's Rules** (commonly taught in KTU DOE modules) provide a procedural shortcut:

### **Rule 1 — Write the model and identify each effect.**
List every effect of the form (subscript that varies) or interaction.

### **Rule 2 — Subscript Dropping Rule.**
For any effect $A$ (or $AB$, etc.), the EMS of that effect contains:
- Its own variance component $\sigma_A^2$ (or $\sigma_{AB}^2$)
- The variance component of every effect that is **nested within** it
- The error variance $\sigma^2$

### **Rule 3 — Coefficient Multiplication.**
Each variance component in the EMS is multiplied by the **number of levels (or combinations of levels) of the subscripts that are NOT present in the effect label** in the rule.

### **Rule 4 — Fixed vs Random Treatment.**
- **Random effect** → its true variance $\sigma^2_{\text{effect}}$ is unknown; we *estimate* it from MS.
- **Fixed effect** → its "variance" is replaced by a **quadratic form** in the fixed effects: $Q[\text{effect}]/n$.

### **Rule 5 — Error Term.**
The EMS of the **Error** is always simply $\sigma^2$ (the residual variance).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Design / Source | Model | Expected Mean Square $E(MS)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-Way ANOVA, Treatment (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\tau^2$ |
| 2 | One-Way ANOVA, Error | II | $\sigma^2$ |
| 3 | Two-Way (CRD), Factor A (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\alpha^2 + bn\,\sigma_{\beta(\alpha)}^2$ |
| 4 | Two-Way (CRD), Factor B within A (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_{\beta(\alpha)}^2$ |
| 5 | Two-Way Factorial, Main A (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + b\,\sigma_\alpha^2 + ab\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ |
| 6 | Two-Way Factorial, Main B (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + a\,\sigma_\beta^2 + ab\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ |
| 7 | Two-Way Factorial, Interaction A×B (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ |
| 8 | Two-Way Factorial, Error | II | $\sigma^2$ |
| 9 | Nested (Two-Stage), A (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\,\sigma_\alpha^2$ |
| 10 | Nested, B within A (Random) | II | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2$ |
| 11 | Factorial Main A (Fixed) | I | $\sigma^2 + \frac{bn \sum \alpha_i^2}{a-1}$ |
| 12 | Mixed A(Random), B(Fixed), Main A | III | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + bn\,\sigma_\alpha^2$ |
| 13 | Mixed A(Random), B(Fixed), Main B | III | $\sigma^2 + \frac{an\sum\beta_j^2}{b-1}$ |
| 14 | Mixed A(Random), B(Fixed), Interaction | III | $\sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:** $a, b$ = number of levels; $n$ = replications; $\sigma^2$ = error variance; $\sigma_\alpha^2, \sigma_\beta^2$ = main-effect variance components; $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ = interaction variance component.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Quality Control** — Random-effects ANOVA with EMS rules tells the engineer whether batch-to-batch variation dominates within-batch variation.
- **Agricultural Field Trials** — Nested designs use EMS rules to allocate variance between sites, plots within sites, and subplots.
- **Pharmaceutical Bioassays** — Mixed models use EMS to separate lab-to-lab variation from treatment effect.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: EMS for One-Way ANOVA (Random Model)

### Model Statement

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij}, \quad i=1,\dots,a, \;\; j=1,\dots,n$$

with $\tau_i \sim N(0,\sigma_\tau^2)$, $\epsilon_{ij} \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$, and $\tau_i, \epsilon_{ij}$ mutually independent.

### Derivation of $E(MS_{\text{Error}})$

$$E(MS_E) = E\!\left[\frac{SS_E}{a(n-1)}\right] = \sigma^2$$

The error mean square estimates only the residual variance because the $\tau_i$'s are centered.

### Derivation of $E(MS_{\text{Treatment}})$

$$SS_T = n \sum_{i=1}^{a} (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$$

Take the expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(SS_T) &= n \sum_{i=1}^{a} E(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Each $\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot}$ is a linear combination of the $\tau_i$'s and $\epsilon_{ij}$'s. The variance of $\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}$ is $\sigma^2/n + \sigma_\tau^2$, and the covariance between two different levels $i \neq k$ is $-\sigma_\tau^2/a$ (it is a random sample of size $a$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(SS_T) &= n \sum_{i=1}^{a} \left[ \text{Var}(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}) - \text{Covariance averaging} \right] \\
&= n \cdot a \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\cdot\frac{n-1}{a} \;+\; (a-1)\sigma_\tau^2 \cdot n \\
&= (a-1)\sigma^2 + n(a-1)\sigma_\tau^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore,

$$E(MS_T) = \frac{E(SS_T)}{a-1} = \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\tau^2$$

This confirms **Rule 1**: the treatment MS contains the error variance $\sigma^2$ plus $n$ times the treatment variance component.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: EMS for a Two-Factor Nested Design

### Model Statement

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_{j(i)} + \epsilon_{k(ij)}, \quad i=1,\dots,a, \; j=1,\dots,b, \; k=1,\dots,n$$

with $\alpha_i \sim N(0,\sigma_\alpha^2)$, $\beta_{j(i)} \sim N(0,\sigma_\beta^2)$, $\epsilon_{k(ij)} \sim N(0,\sigma^2)$.

### EMS for Factor A

The factor A mean square is computed from the $a$ group means. The variation among them reflects both $\sigma_\alpha^2$ and the nested $\sigma_\beta^2$ because each $\alpha_i$ level contains $b$ sub-levels of $\beta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\,\sigma_\alpha^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic behind the coefficients:**
- $bn$ = total observations per level of A → multiplies $\sigma_\alpha^2$
- $n$ = observations per level of B(within A) → multiplies $\sigma_\beta^2$
- $1$ = multiplies $\sigma^2$

### EMS for Factor B(A)

Variation among B's within each A level reflects only the B variance and the error.

$$E(MS_{B(A)}) = \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2$$

### EMS for Error

$$E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EMS for Two-Factor Factorial (Random Model)

### Model Statement

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$$

### Step-by-Step Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + b\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + ab\,\sigma_\alpha^2 \\
E(MS_B) &= \sigma^2 + a\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + ab\,\sigma_\beta^2 \\
E(MS_{AB}) &= \sigma^2 + \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 \\
E(MS_E) &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why $ab$ multiplies the main effect?**
- Each level of A is observed across all $b$ levels of B, with $n$ replications per cell → $bn$ *total* observations per level of A, but the **per-cell average** is used, so the coefficient is $bn/n = b$ per cell-batch summing → $ab$ when summed over $b$ levels of B.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Estimating Variance Components

**Problem:** A two-factor nested design has $a = 3$ levels of A, $b = 4$ levels of B within A, $n = 2$ replications. The computed mean squares are:

| Source | df | MS |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 80.0 |
| B(A) | 9 | 20.0 |
| Error | 12 | 4.0 |

**EMS equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 + 2\,\sigma_\beta^2 + 8\,\sigma_\alpha^2 &= 80.0 \\
\sigma^2 + 2\,\sigma_\beta^2 &= 20.0 \\
\sigma^2 &= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solve sequentially (this is the EMS rule in action):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_E = 4.0 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 &= \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{20.0 - 4.0}{2} = 8.0 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 &= \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn} = \frac{80.0 - 20.0}{8} = 7.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **General Procedure:** Start from the bottom of the EMS table and work upward, using the mean square of each level as the "denominator" for the level above it. This is the cornerstone rule of EMS-based variance component estimation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow — Applying EMS Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify Experimental Design"] --> B["Write the Statistical Model"]
    B --> C{"Model Type?"}
    C -- "Fixed Model I" --> D1["Replace variance components with Q[Effect]/df"]
    C -- "Random Model II" --> D2["All effects random, use variance components"]
    C -- "Mixed Model III" --> D3["Combine: Random effects have variance components, Fixed effects have Q-form"]
    D1 --> E["List all Sources of Variation"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F["Apply Rule 2: Subscript Dropping"]
    F --> G["For each Source, identify which subscripts vary"]
    G --> H["Apply Rule 3: Coefficient = number of combinations of varying subscripts"]
    H --> I["Construct EMS for each source as sum of variance components times coefficients"]
    I --> J["EMS_Computation_Complete"]
    J --> K["Use EMS to form F-tests and estimate variance components"]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture — How EMS Connects to F-Tests

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        A1["ANOVA Table: SS and df"]
    end
    subgraph EMS["EMS Computation Engine"]
        B1["Model: Fixed / Random / Mixed"]
        B2["Subscript Dropping Rule"]
        B3["Coefficient Multiplication Rule"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        C1["EMS Expressions E(MS) for each source"]
        C2["F-test Denominator Selection"]
        C3["Variance Component Estimates"]
        C4["Expected Mean Squares for Mixed Models"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Nested vs Crossed Design — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Experimental Structure"] --> Q{"Are levels of one factor the SAME for all levels of the other factor?"}
    Q -- "Yes" --> R["CROSSED (Factorial) Design: EMS contains interaction term"]
    Q -- "No" --> S["NESTED Design: EMS does NOT contain interaction term"]
    R --> R1["Use Subscript Rule with crossed indices, e.g. (alpha beta) sub i j"]
    S --> S1["Use Subscript Rule with nested indices, e.g. beta sub j of i"]
    R1 --> T["Compare with EMS Table"]
    S1 --> T
    T --> U["F-test Formulation"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Expected Mean Square (EMS). Why is it important in the analysis of a designed experiment?**

**Model Answer:**
The Expected Mean Square (EMS) of a source of variation is the theoretical mean of its computed mean square over all possible random samples, expressed as a linear combination of the underlying variance components and the error variance. It is essential because it guides the correct construction of F-tests (ensuring the denominator contains only the variance component under test) and enables the estimation of variance components in random and mixed models.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**State the "subscript dropping rule" used for computing EMS.**

**Model Answer:**
The subscript dropping rule states that for any effect whose label contains certain subscripts, its EMS will include its own variance component plus the variance component of every effect whose subscripts are a subset of (i.e., are "dropped" from) the subscripts in the label. For example, the EMS of factor $A$ in a two-factor nested design contains $\sigma_\alpha^2$, $\sigma_\beta^2$ (because $B$ is dropped/nested within $A$), and $\sigma^2$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For a **two-factor crossed factorial experiment (Random Model II)** with factors $A$ (levels = $a$) and $B$ (levels = $b$) with $n$ replications, derive the EMS for all sources of variation including the interaction.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A chemical engineer studies the effect of **Catalyst Type (A: 3 levels)** and **Temperature (B: 4 levels)** on yield, with $n = 2$ replications in a fully randomized factorial design (Random Model). The computed mean squares are:

| Source | df | MS |
|---|---|---|
| A (Catalyst) | 2 | 120.0 |
| B (Temperature) | 3 | 60.0 |
| AB (Interaction) | 6 | 25.0 |
| Error | 12 | 5.0 |

Estimate the variance components and test the significance of each effect using the appropriate F-tests (use $\alpha = 0.05$).

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### **Solution to (a):**

Model: $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \epsilon_{ijk}$ where all effects are random with variances $\sigma_\alpha^2, \sigma_\beta^2, \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2, \sigma^2$.

By applying the subscript dropping rule and coefficient multiplication:

- For $A$: subscript $A$ is "kept", $B$ and cell vary → coefficient on $\sigma_\alpha^2$ is $bn$ (number of $B$ levels × replications); the interaction $AB$ also varies → coefficient $n$ on $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$.

- For $B$: symmetric reasoning.

- For $AB$: the interaction label is the "broadest" varying set → only error remains.

- For Error: only $\sigma^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + bn\,\sigma_\alpha^2 \\
E(MS_B) &= \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + an\,\sigma_\beta^2 \\
E(MS_{AB}) &= \sigma^2 + \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 \\
E(MS_E) &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1: Identify the F-test denominators from the EMS.**

- Test $A$: numerator $MS_A$ vs denominator $MS_{AB}$ (the latter contains only $\sigma^2$ and $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ — the components of the numerator apart from $\sigma_\alpha^2$).
- Test $B$: numerator $MS_B$ vs denominator $MS_{AB}$.
- Test $AB$: numerator $MS_{AB}$ vs denominator $MS_E$.

**Step 2: Compute F-statistics.**

$$
F_A = \frac{120.0}{25.0} = 4.80
$$

$$
F_B = \frac{60.0}{25.0} = 2.40
$$

$$
F_{AB} = \frac{25.0}{5.0} = 5.00
$$

**Step 3: Compare with critical values $F_{0.05}$.**

- $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$ → $F_A = 4.80 < 5.14$ → Catalyst is **not significant** at 5%.
- $F_{0.05, 3, 6} = 4.76$ → $F_B = 2.40 < 4.76$ → Temperature is **not significant**.
- $F_{0.05, 6, 12} = 3.00$ → $F_{AB} = 5.00 > 3.00$ → Interaction $AB$ is **highly significant**.

**Step 4: Estimate variance components.**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E = 5.0
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_{\alpha\beta}^2 = \frac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{25.0 - 5.0}{2} = 10.0
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = \frac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{bn} = \frac{120.0 - 25.0}{4 \times 2} = \frac{95.0}{8} = 11.875
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = \frac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an} = \frac{60.0 - 25.0}{3 \times 2} = \frac{35.0}{6} \approx 5.833
$$

**Interpretation:** Although main effects are not individually significant, the significant interaction implies that the *combined* effect of Catalyst and Temperature matters — engineers should examine the interaction plot for optimal combinations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question based on 2024 Scheme pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **General Rules for computing EMS** with a clear algorithm. Distinguish between **Fixed, Random, and Mixed Models** in the context of EMS.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A **nested design** with $a = 4$ factories, $b = 3$ batches within each factory, and $n = 2$ observations per batch yields the following ANOVA summary:

| Source | df | MS |
|---|---|---|
| Factories (A) | 3 | 64.0 |
| Batches within A | 8 | 12.0 |
| Error | 12 | 3.0 |

Using EMS rules, (i) write the EMS for each source, (ii) estimate the variance components, and (iii) test whether factory-to-factory variation is significant at $\alpha = 0.05$.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**General Rules Algorithm:**

1. **Identify the design and write the linear model.**
2. **Classify each effect** as Fixed, Random, or Mixed:
   - **Fixed (Model I):** Levels are deliberately chosen; inference is restricted to these levels. In EMS, $\sigma_\tau^2$ is replaced by the quadratic form $Q[\tau]/(a-1)$.
   - **Random (Model II):** Levels are a random sample from a population; inference extends to the whole population. EMS contains the true variance component $\sigma_\tau^2$.
   - **Mixed (Model III):** At least one effect is random and at least one is fixed. EMS contains variance components for random effects and quadratic forms for fixed effects.
3. **Apply the Subscript Dropping Rule** to list all variance components contributing to each effect.
4. **Apply the Coefficient Rule** to find the multiplier for each variance component (number of combinations of subscripts not in the effect label).
5. **Verify** that the EMS of Error is always $\sigma^2$ and that the total coefficient of $\sigma^2$ in $E(MS_A)$ is consistent with the design.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**(i) EMS Expressions (Nested Design, Random Model):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\,\sigma_\alpha^2 \\
E(MS_{B(A)}) &= \sigma^2 + n\,\sigma_\beta^2 \\
E(MS_E) &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Variance Component Estimates:**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 = 3.0
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{12.0 - 3.0}{2} = 4.5
$$

$$
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn} = \frac{64.0 - 12.0}{3 \times 2} = \frac{52.0}{6} \approx 8.667
$$

**(iii) F-test for Factory Effect:**

Test $H_0: \sigma_\alpha^2 = 0$ vs $H_1: \sigma_\alpha^2 > 0$.

The denominator of F must be a mean square whose EMS contains $\sigma^2$ and $\sigma_\beta^2$ (i.e., the components of $MS_A$ *other than* $\sigma_\alpha^2$). Thus, $MS_{B(A)}$ is the correct denominator.

$$
F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_{B(A)}} = \frac{64.0}{12.0} \approx 5.333
$$

Critical value: $F_{0.05, 3, 8} = 4.07$.

Since $5.333 > 4.07$, **reject $H_0$** — factory-to-factory variation is significant at the 5% level.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong F-test denominator:** Students frequently use Error MS as the denominator for all main effects. In random/mixed models, the *correct* denominator is the mean square of the highest-order random effect that *contains* the effect being tested.
> 2. **Confusing Nested and Crossed designs:** In a nested design, there is **no** interaction term. Writing $E(MS_A)$ with a $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ term in a nested design costs 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by replication number $n$** in variance component estimates.
> 4. **Sign confusion with negative estimates:** If a computed variance component is negative, it is **set to zero** — not reported as a negative number. This is a standard convention in ANOVA estimation.
> 5. **Mixing up fixed-effect and random-effect EMS:** In a fixed model, EMS is written in terms of $Q[\text{effect}]$; in a random model, in terms of $\sigma^2$. Examiners check this carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **EMS = Linear combination of variance components** — each multiplied by a coefficient determined by the *number of varying subscripts*.
- ✅ **Always start with $E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$** — this is the foundation for estimating all other variance components.
- ✅ **Use the Subscript Dropping Rule** to identify which variance components appear in each effect's EMS.
- ✅ **Coefficient Rule:** The multiplier on $\sigma^2$ for a given effect equals the *number of observations per level* of that effect.
- ✅ **F-test Denominator Rule:** The denominator MS must have an EMS that contains *exactly* the variance components of the numerator *except* the one being tested.
- ✅ **Solve EMS equations sequentially from bottom to top** to estimate variance components (ANOVA method).
- ✅ **Negative variance estimates are set to zero** by convention.
- ✅ **Model I = Fixed** (replace $\sigma^2$ with $Q$-form); **Model II = Random** (use $\sigma^2$); **Model III = Mixed** (combination).
- ✅ **Nested designs do not have interaction terms**; their EMS structure is therefore *simpler* than crossed factorial designs.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic carries direct weightage in **Module 4** and is a frequent component of **ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B questions** worth 7–14 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approximate F-Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Approximate F-Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Approximate F-Test** is a statistical hypothesis-testing procedure employed in **two-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)** under the constraint that only **one observation per cell (treatment combination)** is available. Under this constraint, the classical interaction sum of squares and the error sum of squares are *mathematically confounded* (lumped together as the "remainder" or "residual" sum of squares). Because the true interaction degrees of freedom cannot be isolated, an *approximate* F-distribution (rather than the exact F-distribution) is used to draw inferences about the presence of interaction effects.

The two most widely referenced variants within the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

1. **Tukey's One-Degree-of-Freedom Test for Non-Additivity** (Tukey, 1949).
2. **Johnson–Graybill's Approximate F-Test** (Johnson & Graybill, 1972), built on the *random interaction model* and **Satterthwaite's** degrees-of-freedom approximation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Approximate F-Test is *only* invoked when replication is absent in a two-way layout. The KTU board examiners specifically look for the correct sequence: (i) compute the remainder SS, (ii) extract a testable portion of interaction, (iii) re-pool to form the cleaned error, and (iv) apply the F-statistic.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are tasting **9 different ice-cream flavour & topping combinations** (3 flavours × 3 toppings), but you only have budget to taste **each combination exactly once** (a "blind tasting"). After tasting, you compute the *average* rating for each flavour (row effect) and each topping (column effect). The "remainder" leftover variation could be due to two very different things:

- **Random chance** (e.g., a noisy day, a distracted palate), or
- A genuine **flavour × topping synergy** (chocolate + chilli creates a magic that the additive prediction misses).

The **Approximate F-Test** is the statistical tool that helps you decide, with a single number, whether that leftover "magic" is statistically real or just noise. The adjective *approximate* comes from the fact that, with only one tasting per cell, we cannot perfectly separate the synergy from the noise — so we rely on a *plausible model* (either one-DOF non-additivity, or random interactions) to obtain an F-like ratio.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Tabulated F-values:** $F_{\alpha, \, d_1, \, d_2}$ at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ (or 0.01).
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$ is the KTU default for board examinations.
- **Degrees of freedom rules:**
  - Rows: $a - 1$
  - Columns: $b - 1$
  - Remainder (lumped): $(a-1)(b-1)$
  - Tukey non-additivity: $1$ df
  - Johnson–Graybill (Satterthwaite): $\widehat{f}$ (non-integer, *estimated*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Idea:** A *remainder* mean square without further modelling can never be tested against a *clean* error mean square. The Approximate F-Test *creates* that clean error by borrowing structure (either deterministic non-additivity or random interaction variance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Approximate F-Test is Needed

In a two-way layout with one observation $Y_{ij}$ per cell ($i = 1, \dots, a$; $j = 1, \dots, b$), the full fixed-effects model is:

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

subject to the usual constraints $\sum \alpha_i = 0$, $\sum \beta_j = 0$, $\sum_i (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$, $\sum_j (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$.

Because there is **only one observation per cell**, the within-cell variability — which would normally give us an *independent* estimate of $\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$ — **does not exist**. Consequently, the interaction sum of squares and the error sum of squares become *one and the same quantity* (the **remainder SS**). The classical F-test for interaction is therefore *algebraically undefined* — and the Approximate F-Test fills this gap.

### 2.2 Tukey's One-Degree-of-Freedom Test for Non-Additivity

#### 2.2.1 Core Idea

Tukey proposed that the interaction, if any, could be modelled as **one specific degree of freedom of non-additivity** of the form:

$$(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \lambda \cdot \alpha_i \cdot \beta_j$$

This is essentially the *first principal component* of interaction. The hypothesis tested is:

- $H_0$: Data are **additive**, i.e., $\lambda = 0$ (no interaction of this form).
- $H_1$: $\lambda \neq 0$ (the data contain at least one degree of freedom of non-additivity / interaction).

#### 2.2.2 Operational Steps

1. Fit the **additive model** (rows + columns only). Compute residuals:
$$e_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$$
2. Construct the **column vector** of *row effects* and *column effects*:
$$\hat{\alpha}_i = \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}, \quad \hat{\beta}_j = \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}$$
3. Compute the **non-additivity cross-product**:
$$Q = \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} e_{ij} \, \hat{\alpha}_i \, \hat{\beta}_j$$
4. Compute the **normalising denominator**:
$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \, \hat{\beta}_j^2$$
5. The non-additivity sum of squares is:
$$SS_N = \dfrac{Q^2}{D} \quad \text{with 1 degree of freedom}$$
6. **Pool** the remainder SS by removing the non-additivity portion:
$$SS_{E}^{\prime} = SS_{E} - SS_N \quad \text{with } (a-1)(b-1) - 1 \text{ degrees of freedom}$$
7. The F-statistic:
$$F = \dfrac{MS_N}{MS_{E}^{\prime}} = \dfrac{SS_N / 1}{SS_{E}^{\prime} / \left[(a-1)(b-1) - 1\right]}$$

#### 2.2.3 Engineering Interpretation

- If $F \le F_{0.05,\, 1,\, (a-1)(b-1)-1}$: Accept additivity; the row and column main effects are the **only meaningful signals**.
- If $F > F_{0.05,\, 1,\, (a-1)(b-1)-1}$: Reject additivity; the **interaction must be modelled explicitly** (often by replicating the design or by a transformation such as $\sqrt{Y}$ or $\log Y$).

### 2.3 Johnson–Graybill Approximate F-Test

#### 2.3.1 Core Idea

The Johnson–Graybill procedure assumes that the interaction terms are themselves **random**, i.e., $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{AB})$ independently. Under this **random-effects (mixed) model**, the remainder mean square satisfies:

$$E[MS_R] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB}$$

The testable hypothesis is $H_0 : \sigma^2_{AB} = 0$.

#### 2.3.2 Expected Mean Square Components

$$E[MS_A] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB} + b\,\sigma^2_{\alpha}$$
$$E[MS_B] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB} + a\,\sigma^2_{\beta}$$
$$E[MS_R] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB}$$

The catch: we *cannot* estimate $\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$ independently when there is no replication.

#### 2.3.3 Satterthwaite's Approximation

We need an F-ratio, so we *approximate* the distribution of $MS_R / MS_{\varepsilon}$ using **Satterthwaite's rule**:

$$F^{*} = \dfrac{MS_R}{MS_{\varepsilon}}$$

with the *approximate* denominator degrees of freedom:

$$\widehat{f}_2 = \dfrac{\left[MS_R\right]^2}{\dfrac{(MS_A)^2}{a-1} + \dfrac{(MS_B)^2}{b-1} + \dfrac{(MS_R)^2}{(a-1)(b-1)}}$$

This $\widehat{f}_2$ is generally **non-integer** and is the price of approximation. The numerator degrees of freedom remain $(a-1)(b-1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** The Johnson–Graybill test is a *conservative* test by construction — it tends to under-reject $H_0$, which is the *safe* side for engineering applications (better to miss a weak interaction than to falsely manufacture one).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Expression** | **Definition** | **Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Y_{ij}$ | Single observation in row $i$, column $j$ | — | — |
| $\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}$ | Row mean | $\dfrac{1}{b}\sum_j Y_{ij}$ | — |
| $\bar{Y}_{\cdot j}$ | Column mean | $\dfrac{1}{a}\sum_i Y_{ij}$ | — |
| $\bar{Y}$ | Grand mean | $\dfrac{1}{ab}\sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}$ | — |
| $SS_A$ | Row sum of squares | $b \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y})^2$ | $a - 1$ |
| $SS_B$ | Column sum of squares | $a \sum_j (\bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y})^2$ | $b - 1$ |
| $SS_T$ | Total sum of squares | $\sum_{i,j} (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y})^2$ | $ab - 1$ |
| $SS_R$ | Remainder SS | $SS_T - SS_A - SS_B$ | $(a-1)(b-1)$ |
| $e_{ij}$ | Additive-model residual | $Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$ | — |
| $Q$ | Cross-product (Tukey) | $\sum_{i,j} e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$ | — |
| $D$ | Normalising factor (Tukey) | $\sum_{i,j} \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2$ | — |
| $SS_N$ | Non-additivity SS (Tukey) | $\dfrac{Q^2}{D}$ | $1$ |
| $SS_{E}^{\prime}$ | Cleaned error SS (Tukey) | $SS_R - SS_N$ | $(a-1)(b-1) - 1$ |
| $F_{Tukey}$ | Tukey F-statistic | $\dfrac{SS_N / 1}{SS_{E}^{\prime} / \left[(a-1)(b-1)-1\right]}$ | $(1, (a-1)(b-1)-1)$ |
| $\widehat{f}_2$ | Satterthwaite df (J–G) | $\dfrac{MS_R^2}{\dfrac{MS_A^2}{a-1} + \dfrac{MS_B^2}{b-1} + \dfrac{MS_R^2}{(a-1)(b-1)}}$ | non-integer |
| $F_{J-G}$ | Johnson–Graybill F | $\dfrac{MS_R}{MS_{\varepsilon}}$ (with $\varepsilon$ from prior / pilot) | $((a-1)(b-1), \widehat{f}_2)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - All sums of squares are in **(units of response)²** (e.g., $\text{N}^2$, $\text{mm}^2$).
> - Mean squares = SS / df → units match SS.
> - $Q$ and $D$ must be computed using the **same residual form** $e_{ij}$; otherwise the test statistic is invalid.
> - Satterthwaite's $\widehat{f}_2$ is **not rounded** — use it as a real number when consulting F-tables (interpolate or use software).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Agriculture:** Field trials where each (fertiliser × irrigation) combination is tested on a *single plot*; approximate F-test detects synergism.
- **Manufacturing:** Test of (machine × operator) interaction in a process where running each combination twice would be prohibitively expensive.
- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Drug × dose-response with one animal per cell — the approximate F-test guards against overlooking toxic synergies.
- **Materials Science:** (Temperature × catalyst) studies with single-batch runs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tukey Non-Additivity Sum of Squares

We begin with the additive model:

$$\hat{Y}_{ij} = \bar{Y} + \hat{\alpha}_i + \hat{\beta}_j, \quad \text{where} \quad \hat{\alpha}_i = \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}, \quad \hat{\beta}_j = \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}$$

The residual is:

$$e_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \hat{Y}_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$$

Tukey proposes that the *systematic* (non-random) part of the interaction is proportional to the product of the main effects:

$$(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \lambda \cdot \hat{\alpha}_i \cdot \hat{\beta}_j$$

Substituting into the residual equation:

$$e_{ij} = \lambda \cdot \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

To estimate $\lambda$ by **least squares**, we minimise the sum of squared residuals with respect to $\lambda$:

$$S(\lambda) = \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \left(e_{ij} - \lambda \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j\right)^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $\lambda$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dS}{d\lambda} = -2 \sum_{i,j} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j \left(e_{ij} - \lambda \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j\right) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\lambda \sum_{i,j} \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2 = \sum_{i,j} e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$$

Therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{\sum_{i,j} e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j}{\sum_{i,j} \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2} = \frac{Q}{D}$$

The non-additivity sum of squares is the regression-explained variation:

$$SS_N = \lambda^2 \cdot D = \left(\frac{Q}{D}\right)^2 \cdot D = \frac{Q^2}{D}$$

This single degree of freedom captures the *largest possible* one-DOF departure from additivity, justifying the "**one degree of freedom for non-additivity**" terminology.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Tukey's Test

Consider a $3 \times 3$ two-way layout (3 operators, 3 machines), with one yield observation per cell:

| | **M1** | **M2** | **M3** | **Row Total** | **Row Mean** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Op 1** | 25 | 31 | 28 | 84 | 28.00 |
| **Op 2** | 30 | 36 | 33 | 99 | 33.00 |
| **Op 3** | 28 | 35 | 30 | 93 | 31.00 |
| **Col Total** | 83 | 102 | 91 | **276** | — |
| **Col Mean** | 27.67 | 34.00 | 30.33 | — | $\bar{Y} = 30.67$ |

**Step 1 — Compute the basic ANOVA components:**

$$SS_T = \sum_{i,j} Y_{ij}^2 - \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot}^2}{ab} = (25^2 + 31^2 + 28^2 + \dots) - \frac{276^2}{9}$$

Let's expand carefully:

$$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 625 + 961 + 784 + 900 + 1296 + 1089 + 784 + 1225 + 900 = 8564$$

$$\text{Correction term} = \frac{276^2}{9} = \frac{76176}{9} = 8464$$

$$SS_T = 8564 - 8464 = 100 \quad (\text{df} = 8)$$

$$SS_A = \frac{1}{b}\sum T_{i\cdot}^2 - \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot}^2}{ab} = \frac{84^2 + 99^2 + 93^2}{3} - 8464$$

$$= \frac{7056 + 9801 + 8649}{3} - 8464 = \frac{25506}{3} - 8464 = 8502 - 8464 = 38 \quad (\text{df} = 2)$$

$$SS_B = \frac{1}{a}\sum T_{\cdot j}^2 - \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot}^2}{ab} = \frac{83^2 + 102^2 + 91^2}{3} - 8464$$

$$= \frac{6889 + 10404 + 8281}{3} - 8464 = \frac{25574}{3} - 8464 = 8524.67 - 8464 = 60.67 \quad (\text{df} = 2)$$

$$SS_R = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B = 100 - 38 - 60.67 = 1.33 \quad (\text{df} = 4)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the row and column effect estimates:**

$$\hat{\alpha}_1 = 28.00 - 30.67 = -2.67, \quad \hat{\alpha}_2 = 33.00 - 30.67 = 2.33, \quad \hat{\alpha}_3 = 31.00 - 30.67 = 0.33$$

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = 27.67 - 30.67 = -3.00, \quad \hat{\beta}_2 = 34.00 - 30.67 = 3.33, \quad \hat{\beta}_3 = 30.33 - 30.67 = -0.33$$

**Step 3 — Compute the residuals $e_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$:**

| | **M1** | **M2** | **M3** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Op 1** | $25 - 28 - 27.67 + 30.67 = 0$ | $31 - 28 - 34 + 30.67 = -0.33$ | $28 - 28 - 30.33 + 30.67 = 0.33$ |
| **Op 2** | $30 - 33 - 27.67 + 30.67 = 0$ | $36 - 33 - 34 + 30.67 = -0.33$ | $33 - 33 - 30.33 + 30.67 = 0.33$ |
| **Op 3** | $28 - 31 - 27.67 + 30.67 = 0$ | $35 - 31 - 34 + 30.67 = 0.67$ | $30 - 31 - 30.33 + 30.67 = -0.67$ |

**Step 4 — Compute $Q = \sum_{i,j} e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= (0)(-2.67)(-3) + (-0.33)(-2.67)(3.33) + (0.33)(-2.67)(-0.33) \\
&\quad + (0)(2.33)(-3) + (-0.33)(2.33)(3.33) + (0.33)(2.33)(-0.33) \\
&\quad + (0)(0.33)(-3) + (0.67)(0.33)(3.33) + (-0.67)(0.33)(-0.33)
\end{aligned}$$

Computing term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0 + 2.933 + 0.291 + 0 - 2.560 - 0.254 + 0 + 0.736 - 0.073 \\
&\approx 1.073
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $D = \sum_{i,j} \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= (-2.67)^2(-3)^2 + (-2.67)^2(3.33)^2 + (-2.67)^2(-0.33)^2 \\
&\quad + (2.33)^2(-3)^2 + (2.33)^2(3.33)^2 + (2.33)^2(-0.33)^2 \\
&\quad + (0.33)^2(-3)^2 + (0.33)^2(3.33)^2 + (0.33)^2(-0.33)^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 7.13 \cdot 9 + 7.13 \cdot 11.09 + 7.13 \cdot 0.11 + 5.43 \cdot 9 + 5.43 \cdot 11.09 + 5.43 \cdot 0.11 + 0.11 \cdot 9 + 0.11 \cdot 11.09 + 0.11 \cdot 0.11 \\
&= 64.17 + 79.07 + 0.78 + 48.87 + 60.22 + 0.60 + 0.99 + 1.22 + 0.01 \\
&\approx 255.93
\end{aligned}$$
