**Step 6 — Compute the non-additivity SS and cleaned error:**

$$SS_N = \frac{Q^2}{D} = \frac{(1.073)^2}{255.93} = \frac{1.151}{255.93} \approx 0.0045 \quad (\text{df} = 1)$$

$$SS_E' = SS_R - SS_N = 1.33 - 0.0045 \approx 1.33 \quad (\text{df} = 3)$$

**Step 7 — Form the F-statistic:**

$$F = \frac{SS_N / 1}{SS_E' / 3} = \frac{0.0045 / 1}{1.33 / 3} = \frac{0.0045}{0.443} \approx 0.010$$

**Step 8 — Decision:**

From F-tables, $F_{0.05, 1, 3} = 10.13$. Since $F = 0.010 \ll 10.13$, we **accept the additive model**. The data show *no evidence* of interaction; row (operator) and column (machine) main effects alone explain the variation.

### 3.3 Derivation of Satterthwaite's $\widehat{f}_2$ for Johnson–Graybill

In a random-effects two-way model, the remainder mean square $MS_R$ is a linear combination of independent chi-square variables scaled by the unknown variance components. **Satterthwaite (1941)** showed that if:

$$V = c_1 \chi^2_{f_1} + c_2 \chi^2_{f_2} + \dots + c_k \chi^2_{f_k}$$

then $V / E[V]$ is approximately $\chi^2_{\widehat{f}}$ with:

$$\widehat{f} = \frac{(E[V])^2}{\sum_k c_k^2 f_k \cdot (\text{var-coeff})} \Rightarrow \widehat{f} = \frac{\left(\sum c_k f_k\right)^2}{\sum c_k^2 f_k}$$

Applied to the case where $MS_A$, $MS_B$, and $MS_R$ are themselves estimators of variance components, and using the relationships $MS_A \sim \sigma^2 \chi^2_{a-1}/(a-1)$ etc., the resulting approximate df for the denominator of the F-ratio (which compares $MS_R$ against an estimate of $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ extracted from $MS_A$ and $MS_B$ being free of $\sigma^2_{AB}$) is:

$$\widehat{f}_2 = \frac{MS_R^2}{\dfrac{MS_A^2}{a-1} + \dfrac{MS_B^2}{b-1} + \dfrac{MS_R^2}{(a-1)(b-1)}}$$

This is the **Johnson–Graybill formula** quoted in Section 2.4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this approximation works:** Satterthwaite's rule matches the first two moments (mean and variance) of a chi-square to the corresponding moments of the linear combination, yielding the closest integer-equivalent df.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Decision Flow of the Approximate F-Test

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture** of how the Approximate F-Test is integrated into a standard two-way ANOVA workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Two-way layout, ONE observation per cell] --> B[Compute Grand Mean Y-bar and Row / Column Means]
    B --> C[Compute Total SS, Row SS-A, Column SS-B]
    C --> D[Compute Remainder SS-R = SST - SSA - SSB]
    D --> E{Which Approximate F-Test?}
    E --> F1[Tukey 1-DOF Non-Additivity Test]
    E --> F2[Johnson-Graybill Random Interaction Test]
    F1 --> G1[Compute residuals e-ij = Y-ij - Y-i-bar - Y-j-bar + Y-bar]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Q and D using hat-alpha and hat-beta]
    H1 --> I1[Non-additivity SS-N = Q-squared over D, df = 1]
    I1 --> J1[Cleaned Error SS-E' = SS-R - SS-N, df = (a-1)(b-1) - 1]
    J1 --> K1[Compute F-Tukey = MS-N over MS-E']
    K1 --> L1{F greater than F-critical at alpha 0.05?}
    L1 --> M1[Yes: Reject Additivity; Interaction Exists]
    L1 --> N1[No: Accept Additive Model; Pool MS-E' as Error]
    F2 --> G2[Assume random interaction: var-gamma-AB exists]
    G2 --> H2[Compute MS-A, MS-B, MS-R from two-way ANOVA]
    H2 --> I2[Apply Satterthwaite formula for hat-f-2]
    I2 --> J2[Compute F-JG = MS-R over MS-epsilon estimate]
    J2 --> K2[Reference F-table with d1 = (a-1)(b-1), d2 = hat-f-2]
    K2 --> L2{F-JG greater than F-critical?}
    L2 --> M2[Yes: Reject sigma-AB-squared = 0; Random Interaction Present]
    L2 --> N2[No: Conclude No Significant Interaction]
    M1 --> O[End: Report Findings and Recommend Model]
    N1 --> O
    M2 --> O
    N2 --> O
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Sources of Variation in Two-Way ANOVA (No Replication)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SST[Total Sum of Squares - df = ab - 1] --> SSA[Row Sum of Squares - df = a - 1]
    SST --> SSB[Column Sum of Squares - df = b - 1]
    SST --> SSR[Remainder Sum of Squares - df = (a-1)(b-1)]
    SSR --> S1[Contains both Error and Interaction]
    S1 --> S2[Approximate F-Test extracts 1 DOF of Interaction]
    S2 --> S3[Remaining SS becomes Cleaned Error]
    S3 --> S4[F-test becomes possible on Rows, Columns, and Cleaned Error]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Tukey Test Computational Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: Matrix Y-ij with a rows and b columns] --> P2[Step 1: Compute marginal means and Y-bar]
    P2 --> P3[Step 2: Compute e-ij residuals from additive fit]
    P3 --> P4[Step 3: Compute hat-alpha and hat-beta]
    P4 --> P5[Step 4: Form Q = sum of e-ij times hat-alpha times hat-beta]
    P5 --> P6[Step 5: Form D = sum of hat-alpha-squared times hat-beta-squared]
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: SS-N = Q-squared over D]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: SS-E' = SS-R - SS-N]
    P8 --> P9[Step 8: F-statistic = MS-N over MS-E']
    P9 --> P10[Step 9: Compare with F-table at alpha and conclude]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams are deliberately written with **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** and **plain uppercase labels** to ensure clean Mermaid compilation. They map the *data flow* and *decision logic* of the Approximate F-Test, replacing what would otherwise be physical scatter-plot or matrix sketches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1.** *State clearly the situations in which the **Approximate F-Test** is employed in the analysis of a two-way classification. Why is the standard F-test for interaction not applicable in such situations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The Approximate F-Test is employed when a two-way layout has **only one observation per cell** (no replication). *(1 mark)*
- In such a layout, the **interaction sum of squares** and the **error sum of squares** are confounded into a single *remainder* SS, because the within-cell variability is not estimable. *(1 mark)*
- Hence, the standard F-test for interaction, $F = MS_{AB} / MS_E$, is algebraically undefined. The Approximate F-Test resolves this by imposing a model on the interaction (Tukey's one-DOF non-additivity or Johnson–Graybill's random interaction) and recovering a testable F-statistic. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** *Briefly explain **Tukey's One Degree of Freedom Test for Non-Additivity**. What is the null hypothesis tested?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Tukey's test assumes that any non-additive (interaction) structure in the data can be captured by a *single* degree of freedom of the form $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} = \lambda \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$, where $\hat{\alpha}_i$ and $\hat{\beta}_j$ are the estimated row and column main effects. *(1 mark)*
- The **null hypothesis** is $H_0 : \lambda = 0$ (i.e., the data are purely additive). *(1 mark)*
- The test statistic is $F = \dfrac{SS_N / 1}{SS_E' / [(a-1)(b-1)-1]}$, where $SS_N = Q^2 / D$. A significant $F$ indicates at least one degree of freedom of interaction is present. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Model Question Paper Pattern]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks and may be subdivided as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice is provided.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Tukey's Test Application*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A study is conducted to investigate the effect of **3 catalyst types** (A, B, C) and **3 reaction temperatures** (low, medium, high) on the percentage yield of a chemical process. Only one experimental run is performed for each catalyst-temperature combination, yielding the following data:

| | **Low Temp** | **Medium Temp** | **High Temp** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Catalyst A** | 42 | 50 | 47 |
| **Catalyst B** | 38 | 45 | 41 |
| **Catalyst C** | 45 | 55 | 52 |

Perform a **two-way ANOVA without replication** and tabulate the results. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply **Tukey's One-Degree-of-Freedom Test for Non-Additivity** to the data in part (a) at the 5% level of significance and interpret the result. State your conclusion about the appropriateness of the additive model. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Two-Way ANOVA (7 marks):**

Compute totals and means:
- Row totals: $T_{1\cdot} = 139$, $T_{2\cdot} = 124$, $T_{3\cdot} = 152$; Row means: $46.33, 41.33, 50.67$
- Column totals: $T_{\cdot 1} = 125$, $T_{\cdot 2} = 150$, $T_{\cdot 3} = 140$; Column means: $41.67, 50.00, 46.67$
- Grand total: $T_{\cdot\cdot} = 415$; $\bar{Y} = 415 / 9 = 46.11$

Correction term: $C = T_{\cdot\cdot}^2 / (ab) = 415^2 / 9 = 172225 / 9 = 19136.11$

$SS_T = \sum Y_{ij}^2 - C = (42^2 + 50^2 + 47^2 + 38^2 + 45^2 + 41^2 + 45^2 + 55^2 + 52^2) - 19136.11$

$\sum Y_{ij}^2 = 1764 + 2500 + 2209 + 1444 + 2025 + 1681 + 2025 + 3025 + 2704 = 19377$

$SS_T = 19377 - 19136.11 = 240.89 \quad (df = 8)$

$SS_A = \frac{1}{b}\sum T_{i\cdot}^2 - C = \frac{139^2 + 124^2 + 152^2}{3} - 19136.11 = \frac{19321 + 15376 + 23104}{3} - 19136.11$

$= \frac{57801}{3} - 19136.11 = 19267.00 - 19136.11 = 130.89 \quad (df = 2)$

$SS_B = \frac{1}{a}\sum T_{\cdot j}^2 - C = \frac{125^2 + 150^2 + 140^2}{3} - 19136.11 = \frac{15625 + 22500 + 19600}{3} - 19136.11$

$= \frac{57725}{3} - 19136.11 = 19241.67 - 19136.11 = 105.56 \quad (df = 2)$

$SS_R = SS_T - SS_A - SS_B = 240.89 - 130.89 - 105.56 = 4.44 \quad (df = 4)$

**ANOVA Table:**

| **Source** | **SS** | **df** | **MS** | **F** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Catalysts (Rows) | 130.89 | 2 | 65.45 | $65.45 / 1.11 = 58.96$ |
| Temperatures (Columns) | 105.56 | 2 | 52.78 | $52.78 / 1.11 = 47.55$ |
| Remainder (Error) | 4.44 | 4 | 1.11 | — |
| **Total** | **240.89** | **8** | | |

*Note: F-values shown for illustration; for hypothesis testing on rows/columns, the test is $F_A = MS_A / MS_R$ and $F_B = MS_B / MS_R$ under the additive assumption.*

**Part (b) — Tukey's Test (7 marks):**

Compute $\hat{\alpha}_i = \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}$: $(0.22, -4.78, 4.56)$
Compute $\hat{\beta}_j = \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} - \bar{Y}$: $(-4.44, 3.89, 0.56)$

Compute residuals $e_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$:

| | Low | Medium | High |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | $42 - 46.33 - 41.67 + 46.11 = 0.11$ | $50 - 46.33 - 50.00 + 46.11 = -0.22$ | $47 - 46.33 - 46.67 + 46.11 = 0.11$ |
| **B** | $38 - 41.33 - 41.67 + 46.11 = 1.11$ | $45 - 41.33 - 50.00 + 46.11 = -0.22$ | $41 - 41.33 - 46.67 + 46.11 = -0.89$ |
| **C** | $45 - 50.67 - 41.67 + 46.11 = -1.22$ | $55 - 50.67 - 50.00 + 46.11 = 0.44$ | $52 - 50.67 - 46.67 + 46.11 = 0.78$ |

Compute $Q = \sum e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= (0.11)(0.22)(-4.44) + (-0.22)(0.22)(3.89) + (0.11)(0.22)(0.56) \\
&\quad + (1.11)(-4.78)(-4.44) + (-0.22)(-4.78)(3.89) + (-0.89)(-4.78)(0.56) \\
&\quad + (-1.22)(4.56)(-4.44) + (0.44)(4.56)(3.89) + (0.78)(4.56)(0.56) \\
&= -0.107 - 0.188 + 0.013 + 23.554 + 4.090 + 2.382 + 24.694 + 7.808 + 1.991 \\
&\approx 64.24
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $D = \sum \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= (0.22)^2 \left[(-4.44)^2 + (3.89)^2 + (0.56)^2\right] + (-4.78)^2 \left[(-4.44)^2 + (3.89)^2 + (0.56)^2\right] \\
&\quad + (4.56)^2 \left[(-4.44)^2 + (3.89)^2 + (0.56)^2\right] \\
&= \left[(-4.44)^2 + (3.89)^2 + (0.56)^2\right] \left[(0.22)^2 + (4.78)^2 + (4.56)^2\right] \\
&= [19.71 + 15.13 + 0.31] \cdot [0.05 + 22.85 + 20.79] \\
&= 35.15 \cdot 43.69 = 1535.70
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
$$SS_N = \frac{Q^2}{D} = \frac{(64.24)^2}{1535.70} = \frac{4126.78}{1535.70} \approx 2.687 \quad (df = 1)$$

$$SS_E' = SS_R - SS_N = 4.44 - 2.687 = 1.753 \quad (df = 3)$$

$$F_{Tukey} = \frac{SS_N / 1}{SS_E' / 3} = \frac{2.687}{0.584} \approx 4.60$$

**Decision:** $F_{0.05, 1, 3} = 10.13$. Since $4.60 < 10.13$, we **accept $H_0$**. The additive model is appropriate; the data do not show statistically significant non-additivity at the 5% level. The catalyst and temperature effects can be interpreted *independently* without an interaction term.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Theoretical & Comparative*

**(a)** Distinguish between the **fixed-effects model** and the **random-effects model** for a two-way layout with one observation per cell. Explain how the choice of model leads to two different approximate F-tests. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Fixed-Effects Model** | **Random-Effects Model** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature of factors | Levels are *specific* (e.g., the 3 actual machines) | Levels are a *random sample* from a population |
| Interaction terms $(\alpha\beta)_{ij}$ | Fixed unknown constants; $\sum (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$ | Random variables: $(\alpha\beta)_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{AB})$ |
| Testable hypothesis | $H_0: (\alpha\beta)_{ij} = 0$ for all $i,j$ | $H_0: \sigma^2_{AB} = 0$ |
| Approximate test used | **Tukey's test** (deterministic structure) | **Johnson–Graybill test** (variance-component test) |
| Error df | $(a-1)(b-1) - 1$ (integer) | $\widehat{f}_2$ (non-integer, Satterthwaite) |
| Conservatism | Moderate | Conservative |

The **choice of model** fundamentally changes what "no interaction" means: in the fixed model, it means *every* interaction term is literally zero; in the random model, it means the *variance* of the random interaction is zero (i.e., interactions fluctuate only because of pure error). The Tukey and Johnson–Graybill tests are therefore not interchangeable — they answer subtly different scientific questions. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Satterthwaite approximate degrees of freedom** $\widehat{f}_2$ used in the Johnson–Graybill approximate F-test, clearly stating the assumption about the nature of the interaction term. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Assumption:** The interaction terms are random, i.i.d. $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{AB})$ and independent of the error terms $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{\varepsilon})$.

**Derivation Sketch:**

Under the random model, the expected mean squares are:

$$E[MS_A] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB} + b\sigma^2_{\alpha}, \quad E[MS_B] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB} + a\sigma^2_{\beta}, \quad E[MS_R] = \sigma^2_{\varepsilon} + \sigma^2_{AB}$$

Satterthwaite's rule states that if $V$ is a weighted sum of independent chi-squares $V = \sum c_k \chi^2_{f_k}$, then $V$ is approximately distributed as $c \cdot \chi^2_{\widehat{f}}$ with:

$$\widehat{f} = \frac{\left(\sum f_k\right)^2}{\sum f_k^2 / c_k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \widehat{f} = \frac{(E[V])^2}{\text{Var}(V)}$$

Applying this to the linear combination that estimates $\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}$ from $MS_A$, $MS_B$, and $MS_R$ (with weights chosen to cancel the $\sigma^2_{AB}$ and the main-effect variances), one obtains the *denominator* of the approximate F-ratio. The resulting effective degrees of freedom is:

$$\widehat{f}_2 = \frac{MS_R^2}{\dfrac{MS_A^2}{a-1} + \dfrac{MS_B^2}{b-1} + \dfrac{MS_R^2}{(a-1)(b-1)}}$$

This $\widehat{f}_2$ is generally **non-integer**, and the F-distribution with these (non-integer) degrees of freedom is consulted (often via software interpolation). The F-statistic itself is $F_{J-G} = MS_R / \widehat{\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}}$ with numerator df $= (a-1)(b-1)$ and denominator df $= \widehat{f}_2$. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Valuation Key Patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constraint** $\sum_i \hat{\alpha}_i = 0$ and $\sum_j \hat{\beta}_j = 0$ while computing $Q$ — *loses 1 mark*.
> 2. **Using $SS_R$ directly** as the denominator of the F-ratio without removing the non-additivity portion — *loses 2 marks*; the very point of the test is the *splitting* of $SS_R$ into $SS_N$ and $SS_E'$.
> 3. **Reporting the wrong df** for $SS_E'$: it is $(a-1)(b-1) - 1$, **not** $(a-1)(b-1)$ — *loses 1 mark*.
> 4. **Confusing Tukey and Johnson–Graybill**: writing $F = MS_R / MS_A$ (which is the random-effects F for *main effects*, not interaction) — *loses 2–3 marks*.
> 5. **Rounding $\widehat{f}_2$ to the nearest integer** in Johnson–Graybill — *loses 1 mark*; it must be used as a real number.
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation step**: a numerical F without a written conclusion ("Since $F < F_{0.05}$, we accept $H_0$ and conclude ...") is incomplete — *loses 1 mark*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **When to use:** Only in **two-way layouts with one observation per cell**, where the classical F-test for interaction breaks down.
- **Tukey's test** assumes the interaction is *deterministic* and can be represented by **one degree of freedom** of the form $\lambda \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$.
- **Johnson–Graybill test** assumes the interaction is *random* with $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_{AB})$ and uses **Satterthwaite's** approximation for degrees of freedom.
- **Core quantities to memorise:**
  - $e_{ij} = Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot j} + \bar{Y}$
  - $Q = \sum e_{ij} \hat{\alpha}_i \hat{\beta}_j$
  - $D = \sum \hat{\alpha}_i^2 \hat{\beta}_j^2$
  - $SS_N = Q^2 / D$
  - $SS_E' = SS_R - SS_N$
- **Degrees of freedom cheat:** Rows $\to a-1$; Columns $\to b-1$; Remainder $\to (a-1)(b-1)$; Non-additivity $\to 1$; Cleaned error $\to (a-1)(b-1) - 1$.
- **Satterthwaite $\widehat{f}_2$** is *not* an integer; report the F-statistic *with* the fractional df when using software.
- **Decision rule:** $F_{calc} > F_{table} \Rightarrow$ reject $H_0$ (interaction is significant / data are non-additive).
- **KTU-typical answer structure:** (i) State assumptions, (ii) Compute the basic ANOVA, (iii) Compute $Q$ and $D$ explicitly, (iv) Form $SS_N$ and $SS_E'$, (v) Compute the F-ratio, (vi) Compare with table value, (vii) Conclude in plain English.
- **Real-world flag:** Tukey's test is more common in undergraduate engineering curricula; Johnson–Graybill is a graduate-level refinement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nested Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nested Design (Hierarchical / Multi-Stage Design):** A randomized experimental layout in which the levels of one factor (the *sub-factor* or *nested factor*) are **unique to a single level** of another factor (the *main factor* or *parent factor*). In other words, every level of factor $B$ appears under **only one** level of factor $A$ — there is no crossing between them.

Formally, in a **two-stage nested design** the linear statistical model with random effects is written as:

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

where the index notation $B_{j(i)}$ explicitly states that the $j$-th level of factor $B$ exists **only inside** the $i$-th level of factor $A$.

| Symbol | Meaning | Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mu$ | Grand (overall) mean | Constant |
| $A_i$ | Random effect of $i$-th level of factor $A$ | $i = 1, 2, \dots, a$ |
| $B_{j(i)}$ | Random effect of $j$-th level of $B$ nested in $A_i$ | $j = 1, 2, \dots, b$ |
| $\varepsilon_{ijk}$ | Random experimental error | $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$ |
| $a, b, n$ | Number of levels of $A$, $B$ within each $A$, replicates within each $B$ | — |

The design is also called a **Random Effects Model** because $A_i$ and $B_{j(i)}$ are both treated as random samples drawn from larger populations of suppliers, machines, batches, etc.

## 1.2 Assumptions of the Model

The validity of all F-tests and variance-component estimates rests on the following Gaussian assumptions:

- $A_i \sim N(0, \sigma_\alpha^2)$ — supplier/machine effect is random with variance $\sigma_\alpha^2$.
- $B_{j(i)} \sim N(0, \sigma_\beta^2)$ — batch/operator effect is random with variance $\sigma_\beta^2$.
- $\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ — error term is independent and identically distributed.
- All three random components are **mutually independent** and also independent of $\mu$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** Whenever you state the model, **always** mention the four independence + normality assumptions. Examiners allocate dedicated marks for them.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — A Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Schools within Districts" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a researcher studying student performance. The districts chosen are **Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata**. The researcher can only pick schools that **belong to each district** — say *DPS Delhi, KV Delhi* under Delhi, and *Campion Mumbai, Don Bosco Mumbai* under Mumbai. The schools under Delhi have **nothing to do** with the schools under Mumbai. This is *nesting*: *Schools (B) are nested within Districts (A)*.
>
> **Why not crossed?** A crossed design would require the *same* schools to appear under *every* district — which is geographically and physically **impossible**. This impossibility is the precise reason nested designs exist.

### Other Engineering Examples

| Industry | Factor $A$ (Parent) | Factor $B$ (Nested in $A$) | Replicates |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Manufacturing | Machine (3 units) | Operator (only on that machine) | Yield readings |
| Pharmaceuticals | Raw-material supplier | Production batch (only from that supplier) | Tablet assay values |
| Civil/Concrete | Cement brand | Mix batch (only from that brand) | Compressive-strength cubes |
| Electronics | Wafer lot | Die (only on that wafer) | Resistance measurements |

## 1.4 Nested vs Crossed Design — A Sharp Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossed Design:** Every level of $B$ is combined with **every** level of $A$ → Total cells = $a \times b$.
>
> **Nested Design:** Every level of $B$ is combined with **only one** level of $A$ → Total cells = $a \times b$, but the $b$ levels under $A_1$ are **different** from the $b$ levels under $A_2$.

| Feature | Crossed Design | Nested Design |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Interaction term $AB$ | Estimable | **Not estimable** |
| $F$-test for $B$ | $MS_B / MS_E$ | $MS_{B(A)} / MS_E$ |
| Practical reason | All combinations are physically possible | Combinations are physically/logistically impossible |
| Typical use | Fixed effects | Random / variance components |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step

The hierarchical logic of a two-stage nested design unfolds in the following sequence:

1. **Select $a$ levels** of factor $A$ at random from a large population (machines, suppliers, schools).
2. **Within each $A_i$, select $b$ levels** of factor $B$ at random — these are *different* for every $A_i$.
3. **Within each $(A_i, B_{j(i)})$ cell, take $n$ replicate observations**.
4. The total number of observations is $N = a \times b \times n$.
5. Compute the **grand mean** $\bar{Y}_{\dots}$, the **level means** $\bar{Y}_{i..}$, the **cell means** $\bar{Y}_{ij.}$, and the **raw observations** $Y_{ijk}$.
6. Decompose total variability into three orthogonal components:
   - **Between-$A$ variability** (SS_A)
   - **Between-$B$ within-$A$ variability** (SS_B(A))
   - **Within-cell / Error variability** (SS_E)
7. Form the **ANOVA table**, derive the **Expected Mean Squares (EMS)**, and conduct the **F-tests**.
8. Estimate the **variance components** $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2$, $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2$, $\hat{\sigma}^2$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Decomposition

- **SS_A** captures how much the $a$ parent levels differ *as a whole* (averaged over their sub-levels).
- **SS_B(A)** captures the variability of the sub-levels *after removing* the parent-level effect — i.e., it measures the *internal* heterogeneity of each parent.
- **SS_E** captures the irreducible random variation among replicates within a single $(A_i, B_{j(i)})$ cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the interaction $AB$ not estimable?**
>
> In a crossed design, the same $B$ level appears in every $A$ level, so the *change* in $B$'s effect across $A$ can be measured. In a nested design, the $B$ levels are **different** in different $A$'s, so there is no common $B$ to compare across $A$'s. Therefore $AB$ interaction is **structurally unidentifiable** and is absorbed into $B(A)$.

## 2.3 Sum of Squares — Computational Formulas

Let:
- $T_{\dots} = \sum_{i,j,k} Y_{ijk}$ (grand total)
- $T_{i..} = \sum_{j,k} Y_{ijk}$ (total for the $i$-th $A$)
- $T_{ij.} = \sum_k Y_{ijk}$ (total for the $j$-th $B$ inside the $i$-th $A$)
- $CF$ = Correction Factor $= T_{\dots}^2 \,/\, (abn)$

Then the four sums of squares are:

| Quantity | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $SS_T$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{i,j,k} Y_{ijk}^2 - CF$ | Total sum of squares |
| $SS_A$ | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{bn}\sum_{i=1}^{a} T_{i..}^2 - CF$ | Between-$A$ sum of squares |
| $SS_{B(A)}$ | $\displaystyle\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b} T_{ij.}^2 - \frac{1}{bn}\sum_{i=1}^{a} T_{i..}^2$ | Between-$B$-within-$A$ sum of squares |
| $SS_E$ | $SS_T - SS_A - SS_{B(A)}$ | Error (within-cell) sum of squares |

## 2.4 ANOVA Table — Master Template (Two-Stage Nested, Random Model)

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F-ratio |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Factor $A$ | $SS_A$ | $a-1$ | $MS_A$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2$ | $F_A = MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ |
| $B$ within $A$ | $SS_{B(A)}$ | $a(b-1)$ | $MS_{B(A)}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2$ | $F_{B(A)} = MS_{B(A)} / MS_E$ |
| Error | $SS_E$ | $ab(n-1)$ | $MS_E$ | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| **Total** | $SS_T$ | $abn-1$ | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** In a nested design, the F-test for $A$ uses $MS_{B(A)}$ in the denominator — **not** $MS_E$ — because the EMS of $A$ contains an extra $bn\sigma_\alpha^2$ term. The F-test for $B(A)$ correctly uses $MS_E$.

## 2.5 Expected Mean Squares (EMS) — Derivation Logic

The EMS for each source is obtained by expressing $E(MS)$ in terms of the underlying variances:

$$E(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2$$

$$E(MS_{B(A)}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2$$

$$E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "nesting" introduces the extra term $n\sigma_\beta^2$ in $E(MS_A)$?**
>
> Each $A_i$ mean $\bar{Y}_{i..}$ averages over $bn$ observations — $b$ cells × $n$ replicates. Therefore, the variance of $\bar{Y}_{i..}$ accumulates contributions from $\sigma_\beta^2$ (once per cell, $b$ times, weighted by $n$) and from $\sigma^2$ (over $bn$ replicates). This is the algebraic fingerprint of nesting.

## 2.6 Variance Component Estimators

Setting each MS equal to its EMS and solving the system of linear equations:

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n}$$

$$\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative estimates:** If $MS_{B(A)} \le MS_E$ or $MS_A \le MS_{B(A)}$, the corresponding variance component estimate becomes **negative**. By physical reasoning, variances cannot be negative, so we set the offending estimate to **zero** and re-evaluate the partitioning of total variance.

## 2.7 Three-Stage Nested Design (Brief Extension)

The model extends to a third nesting layer as:

$$Y_{ijkl} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + C_{k(ij)} + \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}$$

with the hierarchy: $C$ is nested inside $B$, $B$ is nested inside $A$. The total degrees of freedom and SS structure follow the same recursive pattern, with one extra source of variation $C(B(A))$ added to the ANOVA.

| Source | df | EMS (Random Model) | F |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | $a-1$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2 + nc\sigma_\beta^2 + nbc\sigma_\alpha^2$ | $MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ |
| $B(A)$ | $a(b-1)$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2 + nc\sigma_\beta^2$ | $MS_{B(A)} / MS_{C(B(A))}$ |
| $C(B(A))$ | $ab(c-1)$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2$ | $MS_{C(B(A))} / MS_E$ |
| Error | $abc(n-1)$ | $\sigma^2$ | — |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Nested Design |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Engineering (Six Sigma)** | Variance partitioning: how much of process variability comes from supplier vs. batch vs. measurement |
| **Pharmaceutical Validation** | Establishing batch-to-batch and lot-to-lot variability of a drug product |
| **Software Reliability** | Modeling variability between projects, modules within projects, and runs within modules |
| **Agriculture / Bio-statistics** | Studying yield variation between fields, plots within fields, and sub-plots within plots |
| **Industrial Manufacturing** | Pinpointing whether to control variability at the machine level or operator level |

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Model | $Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$ | $i \in [1,a],\; j \in [1,b],\; k \in [1,n]$ |
| Total observations | $N = abn$ | — |
| Correction factor | $CF = T_{\dots}^2 / (abn)$ | — |
| Total SS | $SS_T = \sum Y_{ijk}^2 - CF$ | df = $abn-1$ |
| SS for $A$ | $SS_A = \frac{\sum T_{i..}^2}{bn} - CF$ | df = $a-1$ |
| SS for $B(A)$ | $SS_{B(A)} = \frac{\sum\sum T_{ij.}^2}{n} - \frac{\sum T_{i..}^2}{bn}$ | df = $a(b-1)$ |
| SS for Error | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_{B(A)}$ | df = $ab(n-1)$ |
| $F_A$ | $MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ | $F_{(a-1),\; a(b-1)}$ |
| $F_{B(A)}$ | $MS_{B(A)} / MS_E$ | $F_{a(b-1),\; ab(n-1)}$ |
| $\hat{\sigma}^2$ | $MS_E$ | Always $\ge 0$ |
| $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2$ | $(MS_{B(A)} - MS_E)/n$ | Truncate to 0 if negative |
| $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2$ | $(MS_A - MS_{B(A)})/(bn)$ | Truncate to 0 if negative |
| Total variance | $\hat{\sigma}_T^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 + \hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 + \hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2$ | Sum of all positive components |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Expected Mean Squares (EMS)

The EMS values given in Section 2.5 are *not* arbitrary — they fall out rigorously from the model equations. Here is the full derivation.

### Step 1: Variance of a single observation

From $Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$, and the independence assumptions:

$$V(Y_{ijk}) = V(A_i) + V(B_{j(i)}) + V(\varepsilon_{ijk}) = \sigma_\alpha^2 + \sigma_\beta^2 + \sigma^2$$

### Step 2: Variance of the cell mean $\bar{Y}_{ij.}$

The cell mean is the average of $n$ independent replicates within the same $(i,j)$ cell:

$$\bar{Y}_{ij.} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_{ijk}$$

Only the error term is averaged (since $A_i$ and $B_{j(i)}$ are constant within the cell):

$$V(\bar{Y}_{ij.}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

### Step 3: Variance of the $A$-level mean $\bar{Y}_{i..}$

The $A$-level mean averages $b$ cells × $n$ replicates = $bn$ observations. The $b$ cell means are independent, and within each cell the variance is $\sigma^2 / n$. The $B$-effect inside $A_i$ contributes $\sigma_\beta^2$ to each cell mean, and the $A$-effect contributes $\sigma_\alpha^2$:

$$V(\bar{Y}_{i..}) = \sigma_\alpha^2 + \frac{\sigma_\beta^2}{b} + \frac{\sigma^2}{bn}$$

This is the key identity — note the presence of $\sigma_\beta^2$.

### Step 4: Derive $E(MS_{B(A)})$ — the within-$A$ mean square

$MS_{B(A)}$ is constructed as the average of within-$A$ cell-to-cell variability. Its expected value is:

$$E(MS_{B(A)}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2$$

*Why $n\sigma_\beta^2$?* Because the sum $\sum_k Y_{ijk}$ has variance $n(\sigma^2 + \sigma_\beta^2)$ (within the cell), and the cell sum of squares inherits this term. Dividing by the cell degrees of freedom $n-1$ and then averaging over $b$ cells within each $A_i$ yields the multiplier $n$ in front of $\sigma_\beta^2$.

### Step 5: Derive $E(MS_A)$

The mean $\bar{Y}_{i..}$ carries the variance derived in Step 3. The sum of squares $SS_A$ is a weighted sum of squared deviations of $\bar{Y}_{i..}$ from the grand mean. Carrying through the expectation:

$$E(MS_A) = bn \cdot V(\bar{Y}_{i..}) = bn\left[\sigma_\alpha^2 + \frac{\sigma_\beta^2}{b} + \frac{\sigma^2}{bn}\right]$$

$$\boxed{E(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2}$$

### Step 6: Derive $E(MS_E)$

$MS_E$ is the within-cell variance, so trivially:

$$E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$$

This completes the EMS system, fully consistent with the table in Section 2.4.

## 3.2 Derivation of Variance Component Estimators

The system of EMS equations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2 \\
E(MS_{B(A)}) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 \\
E(MS_E) &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equation (3) immediately gives:**

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$$

**Subtract Equation (3) from Equation (2) to isolate $\sigma_\beta^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_{B(A)}) - E(MS_E) &= n\sigma_\beta^2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 &= \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subtract Equation (2) from Equation (1) to isolate $\sigma_\alpha^2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) - E(MS_{B(A)}) &= bn\sigma_\alpha^2 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 &= \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Truncation Rule:** If the numerator in either estimator is negative, the corresponding variance component is set to zero. The "Method of Moments" estimators (ANOVA estimators) derived above are **unbiased** but **not** non-negative — hence the truncation.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (B.Tech Manufacturing Lab):** A plant engineer studies the variability of the tensile strength (kg/mm²) of a polymer. Three raw-material suppliers ($A_1, A_2, A_3$) are chosen at random. From each supplier, **two different batches** $B_{1(i)}, B_{2(i)}$ are sampled, and **three specimens** are tested from each batch. The complete data set is given below.

### Step 1: Tabulate the Data and Compute Cell Totals

| Supplier ($A_i$) | Batch ($B_{j(i)}$) | Obs 1 | Obs 2 | Obs 3 | Cell Total $T_{ij.}$ | Cell Mean $\bar{Y}_{ij.}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 23 | 25 | 27 | **75** | 25.00 |
| 1 | 2 | 31 | 29 | 33 | **93** | 31.00 |
| 2 | 1 | 26 | 24 | 22 | **72** | 24.00 |
| 2 | 2 | 28 | 30 | 32 | **90** | 30.00 |
| 3 | 1 | 22 | 24 | 20 | **66** | 22.00 |
| 3 | 2 | 30 | 28 | 26 | **84** | 28.00 |

**Supplier Totals:**

$$T_{1..} = 75 + 93 = 168, \quad T_{2..} = 72 + 90 = 162, \quad T_{3..} = 66 + 84 = 150$$

**Grand Total:**

$$T_{\dots} = 168 + 162 + 150 = 480$$

**Correction Factor (with $a=3, b=2, n=3, abn=18$):**

$$CF = \frac{T_{\dots}^2}{abn} = \frac{480^2}{18} = \frac{230400}{18} = 12800$$

### Step 2: Compute Total Sum of Squares

$$\sum Y_{ijk}^2 = 529+625+729+961+841+1089+676+576+484+784+900+1024+484+576+400+900+784+676 = 13038$$

$$SS_T = 13038 - 12800 = 238, \quad df_T = 18 - 1 = 17$$

### Step 3: Compute Sum of Squares for $A$ (Suppliers)

$$\sum T_{i..}^2 = 168^2 + 162^2 + 150^2 = 28224 + 26244 + 22500 = 76968$$

$$SS_A = \frac{76968}{bn} - CF = \frac{76968}{6} - 12800 = 12828 - 12800 = 28$$

$$df_A = a - 1 = 2, \quad MS_A = \frac{28}{2} = 14$$

### Step 4: Compute Sum of Squares for $B(A)$ (Batches within Suppliers)

$$\sum_{i,j} T_{ij.}^2 = 75^2 + 93^2 + 72^2 + 90^2 + 66^2 + 84^2 = 5625+8649+5184+8100+4356+7056 = 38970$$

$$SS_{B(A)} = \frac{38970}{n} - \frac{76968}{bn} = \frac{38970}{3} - 12828 = 12990 - 12828 = 162$$

$$df_{B(A)} = a(b-1) = 3 \times 1 = 3, \quad MS_{B(A)} = \frac{162}{3} = 54$$

### Step 5: Compute Error Sum of Squares

$$SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_{B(A)} = 238 - 28 - 162 = 48$$

$$df_E = ab(n-1) = 6 \times 2 = 12, \quad MS_E = \frac{48}{12} = 4$$

### Step 6: Construct the ANOVA Table

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F-ratio |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A$ (Supplier) | 28 | 2 | 14 | $\sigma^2 + 3\sigma_\beta^2 + 6\sigma_\alpha^2$ | $14/54 = 0.259$ |
| $B(A)$ (Batch) | 162 | 3 | 54 | $\sigma^2 + 3\sigma_\beta^2$ | $54/4 = 13.500$ |
| Error | 48 | 12 | 4 | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| **Total** | **238** | **17** | — | — | — |

### Step 7: Conduct F-Tests at $\alpha = 0.05$

**Critical values:**

- $F_{0.05,\, 2,\, 3} = 9.55$
- $F_{0.05,\, 3,\, 12} = 3.49$

**Test for Supplier effect (Factor A):**

$$F_A = \frac{MS_A}{MS_{B(A)}} = \frac{14}{54} = 0.259 < 9.55$$

> **Decision:** **Fail to reject $H_0$.** The variability **between suppliers** is **NOT statistically significant**.

**Test for Batch-within-Supplier effect (Factor B(A)):**

$$F_{B(A)} = \frac{MS_{B(A)}}{MS_E} = \frac{54}{4} = 13.50 > 3.49$$

> **Decision:** **Reject $H_0$.** The variability **between batches within a supplier** is **statistically significant**.

### Step 8: Estimate the Variance Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_E = 4 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 &= \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{54 - 4}{3} = \frac{50}{3} = 16.667 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 &= \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn} = \frac{14 - 54}{6} = \frac{-40}{6} = -6.667 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{set to } 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The batch-to-batch variation ($\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = 16.67$) dominates, while supplier-to-supplier variation is effectively zero. The total estimated variance is $\hat{\sigma}_T^2 = 4 + 16.67 + 0 = 20.67$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Since batch variation is dominant, the engineer should focus on **controlling batch quality** (e.g., incoming-material inspection, in-process checks) rather than switching suppliers.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Two-Stage Nested Design

The diagram below maps the exact functional structure of a two-stage nested experiment with $a=3$ parent levels, $b=2$ sub-levels per parent, and $n=2$ replicates per sub-level. The top-down flow visualises the nesting hierarchy, the data-collection flow, and the ANOVA decomposition.

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Experimenter / Researcher] -->|Selects a levels at random| A1[Factor A Level 1]
    R -->|Selects a levels at random| A2[Factor A Level 2]
    R -->|Selects a levels at random| A3[Factor A Level 3]

    A1 -->|Selects b levels nested in A1| B11[B Level 1 inside A1]
    A1 -->|Selects b levels nested in A1| B12[B Level 2 inside A1]

    A2 -->|Selects b levels nested in A2| B21[B Level 1 inside A2]
    A2 -->|Selects b levels nested in A2| B22[B Level 2 inside A2]

    A3 -->|Selects b levels nested in A3| B31[B Level 1 inside A3]
    A3 -->|Selects b levels nested in A3| B32[B Level 2 inside A3]

    B11 --> R111[Replicate 1]
    B11 --> R112[Replicate 2]
    B12 --> R121[Replicate 1]
    B12 --> R122[Replicate 2]

    B21 --> R211[Replicate 1]
    B21 --> R212[Replicate 2]
    B22 --> R221[Replicate 1]
    B22 --> R222[Replicate 2]

    B31 --> R311[Replicate 1]
    B31 --> R312[Replicate 2]
    B32 --> R321[Replicate 1]
    B32 --> R322[Replicate 2]

    R111 --> ANO[ANOVA Decomposition]
    R112 --> ANO
    R121 --> ANO
    R122 --> ANO
    R211 --> ANO
    R212 --> ANO
    R221 --> ANO
    R222 --> ANO
    R311 --> ANO
    R312 --> ANO
    R321 --> ANO
    R322 --> ANO

    ANO -->|SS_A| SS1[Between A Variability]
    ANO -->|SS_B of A| SS2[Between B within A Variability]
    ANO -->|SS_E| SS3[Within Cell Error Variability]

    SS1 --> FT[F Test for A]
    SS2 --> FT2[F Test for B of A]
    SS3 --> FT2

    FT --> VC[Variance Components Estimation]
    FT2 --> VC
    SS3 --> VC

    VC --> OUT1[Sigma squared alpha estimate]
    VC --> OUT2[Sigma squared beta estimate]
    VC --> OUT3[Sigma squared estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The downward arrows show the *nesting hierarchy* (A → B → Replicates). The arrows at the bottom show the *inferential flow*: raw data → ANOVA decomposition → F-tests → variance-component estimation → engineering decisions.

## 4.2 Crossed vs Nested — A Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CROSSED
        C1[A1] --- C2[A2]
        C2 --- C3[A3]
        C3 --- C4[A4]
        B1[B1] --- B2[B2]
        B2 --- B3[B3]
        B3 --- B4[B4]
    end
```

> In a **crossed** layout, the same $B_j$ connects to every $A_i$ — this is the full factorial geometry.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph NESTED
        N1[A1] --> NB1[B1 inside A1]
        N1 --> NB2[B2 inside A1]
        N1 --> NB3[B3 inside A1]
        N2[A2] --> NB4[B1 inside A2]
        N2 --> NB5[B2 inside A2]
        N2 --> NB6[B3 inside A2]
        N3[A3] --> NB7[B1 inside A3]
        N3 --> NB8[B2 inside A3]
        N3 --> NB9[B3 inside A3]
    end
```

> In a **nested** layout, the $B$'s under $A_1$ are entirely **different** from the $B$'s under $A_2$ or $A_3$ — there is no horizontal connection.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> Define a **nested design**. How does it differ from a **crossed (factorial) design**? Give one example of a real engineering situation where a nested design is *necessary* (not merely convenient).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **nested design** is an experimental layout in which the levels of one factor (the sub-factor) are unique to a single level of another factor (the parent factor) — i.e., $B_{j(i)}$ exists only inside $A_i$.

**Key differences (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Crossed | Nested |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Combination of $B$ across $A$ | Every $B_j$ appears with every $A_i$ | Each $B_j$ appears with only one $A_i$ |
| Interaction $AB$ | Estimable | Not estimable |
| Common usage | Fixed effects | Random effects / variance components |

**Example (1 mark):** Studying the yield of a chemical process where *batches* are nested within *raw-material suppliers* — each supplier delivers distinct batches, and the same batch cannot physically come from two different suppliers.

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> State the statistical model for a **two-stage nested design with random effects** and write down the **Expected Mean Square (EMS)** for each source of variation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Model (1 mark):**

$$Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$$

with $A_i \sim N(0, \sigma_\alpha^2)$, $B_{j(i)} \sim N(0, \sigma_\beta^2)$, $\varepsilon_{ijk} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$, and all three mutually independent.

**EMS Table (2 marks):**

| Source | EMS |
| :--- | :--- |
| $A$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2$ |
| $B(A)$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2$ |
| Error | $\sigma^2$ |

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each 14-mark question carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the variance component estimators $\hat{\sigma}^2$, $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2$, and $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2$ for a two-stage nested design with random effects. Clearly state the EMS equations and the truncation rule for negative estimates.

**(b) [7 marks]** A manufacturing engineer studies the variability of weld strength (in MPa) using **4 welding machines** ($a=4$), **3 operators nested within each machine** ($b=3$), and **2 weld specimens per operator** ($n=2$). The following partial sums are recorded:

- Grand total $T_{\dots} = 720$
- $\sum T_{i..}^2 = 132000$
- $\sum\sum T_{ij.}^2 = 44000$
- $\sum Y_{ijk}^2 = 29600$

Construct the ANOVA table, test the significance of the machine and operator effects at $\alpha = 0.05$, and estimate the variance components.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of variance component estimators (7 marks)**

The EMS equations for a two-stage nested random model are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2 \quad \text{...(1)} \\
E(MS_{B(A)}) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 \quad \text{...(2)} \\
E(MS_E) &= \sigma^2 \quad \text{...(3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting each sample MS equal to its EMS (Method of Moments) and solving the triangular system:

- **From (3):** $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$
- **Subtract (3) from (2):** $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = \dfrac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n}$
- **Subtract (2) from (1):** $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn}$

(3 marks for the derivation, 2 marks for the EMS, 2 marks for the truncation rule.)

**Truncation rule:** If $MS_{B(A)} \le MS_E$ then $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = 0$. If $MS_A \le MS_{B(A)}$ then $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = 0$. Negative variance estimates are physically impossible and are replaced by zero.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

Given: $a=4$, $b=3$, $n=2$, $N=abn=24$, $T_{\dots}=720$.

**Step 1: Correction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{T_{\dots}^2}{abn} = \frac{720^2}{24} = \frac{518400}{24} = 21600$$

**Step 2: Sum of Squares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_T &= \sum Y_{ijk}^2 - CF = 29600 - 21600 = 8000 \\
SS_A &= \frac{\sum T_{i..}^2}{bn} - CF = \frac{132000}{6} - 21600 = 22000 - 21600 = 400 \\
SS_{B(A)} &= \frac{\sum\sum T_{ij.}^2}{n} - \frac{\sum T_{i..}^2}{bn} = \frac{44000}{2} - 22000 = 22000 - 22000 = 0 \\
SS_E &= SS_T - SS_A - SS_{B(A)} = 8000 - 400 - 0 = 7600
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is computing $SS_{B(A)}$ using the formula $\frac{\sum\sum T_{ij.}^2}{bn} - CF$. The correct denominator for the first term is **$n$**, not $bn$. Note carefully: there are $n$ replicates per cell, so the cell total is divided by $n$.

**Step 3: Degrees of Freedom**

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_A &= a-1 = 3 \\
df_{B(A)} &= a(b-1) = 4 \times 2 = 8 \\
df_E &= ab(n-1) = 12 \times 1 = 12 \\
df_T &= 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Mean Squares**

$$MS_A = \frac{400}{3} = 133.33, \quad MS_{B(A)} = \frac{0}{8} = 0, \quad MS_E = \frac{7600}{12} = 633.33$$

**Step 5: ANOVA Table**

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $A$ (Machine) | 400 | 3 | 133.33 | $\sigma^2 + 2\sigma_\beta^2 + 6\sigma_\alpha^2$ | $133.33 / 0 = \infty$ |
| $B(A)$ (Operator) | 0 | 8 | 0 | $\sigma^2 + 2\sigma_\beta^2$ | $0 / 633.33 = 0$ |
| Error | 7600 | 12 | 633.33 | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| Total | 8000 | 23 | — | — | — |

**Step 6: F-Tests at $\alpha = 0.05$**

- $F_{0.05, 3, 8} = 4.07$
- $F_{0.05, 8, 12} = 3.44$

For $A$: $F_A = \infty > 4.07$ → **Reject $H_0$** — machine effect is significant.
For $B(A)$: $F_{B(A)} = 0 < 3.44$ → **Fail to reject $H_0$** — operator effect (within machine) is **not significant**.

**Step 7: Variance Components**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_E = 633.33 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 &= \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{0 - 633.33}{2} = -316.67 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 &= \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn} = \frac{133.33 - 0}{6} = 22.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Almost all the variability comes from **error** (within-operator), with a small contribution from **machine-to-machine** variation. Operators are essentially interchangeable within a machine. The factory should focus on reducing **weld-specimen variation** (better fixtures, calibration, training) rather than on operator selection.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a clear schematic, explain the structure of a **three-stage nested design**. Write the model, construct the ANOVA table (with df, MS, and EMS columns), and identify the correct F-ratios for testing each factor.

**(b) [7 marks]** A quality engineer at a semiconductor plant wants to estimate the variance components of resistance measurements. She uses **3 wafer lots** ($a=3$), **4 wafers within each lot** ($b=4$), **3 chips within each wafer** ($c=3$), and **2 readings per chip** ($n=2$). The resulting ANOVA gives the following mean squares:

| Source | MS |
| :--- | :---: |
| Lots ($A$) | 18.20 |
| Wafers within Lots ($B(A)$) | 5.40 |
| Chips within Wafers within Lots ($C(B(A))$) | 1.20 |
| Error | 0.45 |

Estimate the four variance components and interpret the dominant source of variation.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Three-Stage Nested Design Structure (7 marks)**

**Model (1 mark):**

$$Y_{ijkl} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + C_{k(ij)} + \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}$$

where the nesting order is: *Reading $\subset$ Chip $\subset$ Wafer $\subset$ Lot*.

**Schematic (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Lot 1] --> W11[Wafer 1 inside Lot 1]
    L1 --> W12[Wafer 2 inside Lot 1]
    L2[Lot 2] --> W21[Wafer 1 inside Lot 2]
    L2 --> W22[Wafer 2 inside Lot 2]
    W11 --> C111[Chip 1 inside Wafer 1 inside Lot 1]
    W11 --> C112[Chip 2 inside Wafer 1 inside Lot 1]
    W21 --> C211[Chip 1 inside Wafer 1 inside Lot 2]
    W21 --> C212[Chip 2 inside Wafer 1 inside Lot 2]
    C111 --> RD1[Reading 1]
    C111 --> RD2[Reading 2]
    C211 --> RD3[Reading 1]
    C211 --> RD4[Reading 2]
```

**ANOVA Table for Three-Stage Nested Design (Random Model) (3 marks):**

| Source | df | MS | EMS | F |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A$ (Lot) | $a-1$ | $MS_A$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2 + nc\sigma_\beta^2 + nbc\sigma_\alpha^2$ | $MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ |
| $B(A)$ (Wafer) | $a(b-1)$ | $MS_{B(A)}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2 + nc\sigma_\beta^2$ | $MS_{B(A)} / MS_{C(B(A))}$ |
| $C(B(A))$ (Chip) | $ab(c-1)$ | $MS_{C(B(A))}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_\gamma^2$ | $MS_{C(B(A))} / MS_E$ |
| Error (Reading) | $abc(n-1)$ | $MS_E$ | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| Total | $abcn - 1$ | — | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cascading EMS rule:** The EMS of each level always contains the variances of **all lower-level** random terms (those nested inside it). The denominator of each F-test is the MS of the **immediately lower** level in the hierarchy.

**Part (b) — Variance Component Estimation (7 marks)**

Given: $a=3$, $b=4$, $c=3$, $n=2$, $N=72$. Using the ANOVA estimator formulas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_E = 0.45 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\gamma^2 &= \frac{MS_{C(B(A))} - MS_E}{n} = \frac{1.20 - 0.45}{2} = \frac{0.75}{2} = 0.375 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 &= \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_{C(B(A))}}{nc} = \frac{5.40 - 1.20}{2 \times 3} = \frac{4.20}{6} = 0.700 \\
\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 &= \frac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{nbc} = \frac{18.20 - 5.40}{2 \times 4 \times 3} = \frac{12.80}{24} = 0.533
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total estimated variance:**

$$\hat{\sigma}_T^2 = 0.45 + 0.375 + 0.700 + 0.533 = 2.058$$

**Percentage contribution of each source (variance partitioning):**

| Source | $\hat{\sigma}^2$ | % of Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Error (Reading) | 0.450 | 21.9% |
| Chip within Wafer | 0.375 | 18.2% |
| Wafer within Lot | 0.700 | **34.0%** |
| Lot | 0.533 | 25.9% |
| **Total** | **2.058** | **100%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A frequently observed error in the board exam is using the wrong denominator in the variance component formulas. For the three-stage case, the correct denominators are $n$, $nc$, and $nbc$ for $\sigma_\gamma^2$, $\sigma_\beta^2$, and $\sigma_\alpha^2$ respectively — **not** $nbc$ for all three. The pattern is *product of all lower-level replication numbers*.

> [!WARNING]
> **General Examiner's Warning — Common Marks-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that $AB$ interaction is not estimable** in nested designs (loss: 2 marks).
> 2. **Using the wrong denominator in the F-ratio for Factor $A$** — students often write $MS_A / MS_E$, but the correct ratio is $MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ because $E(MS_A) \ne E(MS_E)$. (loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Negative variance components not truncated to zero** — a strict KTU examiner deducts 1 mark for not stating the truncation rule.
> 4. **Confusing $df$ for $B(A)$** as $ab-1$ instead of $a(b-1)$. The correct formula recognises that **only $b-1$** batches are free to vary within each of the $a$ suppliers. (loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Writing the model without the parentheses** $B_{j(i)}$ — losing the very signal that this is a *nested* design. (loss: 1 mark)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for the Night Before the Exam!)**

- **Model:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$ — *always* write the nesting index in parentheses.
- **Four assumptions:** Normality + independence for $A_i$, $B_{j(i)}$, $\varepsilon_{ijk}$, and mutual independence of all random terms.
- **Total $N = a \times b \times n$.**
- **Degrees of freedom:** $df_A = a-1$, $df_{B(A)} = a(b-1)$, $df_E = ab(n-1)$, $df_T = abn-1$.
- **EMS (the cascade rule):**
  - $E(MS_E) = \sigma^2$
  - $E(MS_{B(A)}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2$
  - $E(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\beta^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2$
- **F-ratios:**
  - For $A$: $F_A = MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ — *not* $MS_A / MS_E$!
  - For $B(A)$: $F_{B(A)} = MS_{B(A)} / MS_E$.
- **Variance component estimators (ANOVA / Method of Moments):**
  - $\hat{\sigma}^2 = MS_E$
  - $\hat{\sigma}_\beta^2 = (MS_{B(A)} - MS_E) / n$
  - $\hat{\sigma}_\alpha^2 = (MS_A - MS_{B(A)}) / (bn)$
- **Truncation rule:** Negative estimates set to **zero**.
- **Why nested?** When combinations are physically, logistically, or geographically impossible — e.g., operators on machines, batches from suppliers, schools in districts.
- **Interaction $AB$ is NOT estimable** in a nested design — never write an $AB$ column in the ANOVA.
- **Three-stage extension:** $Y_{ijkl} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + C_{k(ij)} + \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}$ with cascading EMS and corresponding $F$-ratios at each level.
- **Variance partitioning interpretation:** Calculate the % contribution of each $\hat{\sigma}^2$ to the total $\hat{\sigma}_T^2$ to identify the **dominant source of variability** and target engineering process improvement accordingly.
- **Quick sanity check:** $SS_T = SS_A + SS_{B(A)} + SS_E$ must hold exactly — always verify before computing MS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statistical Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Statistical Analysis** in the context of Design of Experiments (DOE) is the systematic application of mathematical and probabilistic methods to **collect, organize, analyze, interpret, and present** experimental data so that valid, objective, and reproducible conclusions can be drawn about the effects of factors on a response variable.

It is the **engine of inference** in DOE — without statistical analysis, an experiment is merely a collection of numbers. It transforms raw observations into actionable engineering knowledge by quantifying:

- **Variation** present in the process (random error)
- **Significance** of factor effects (signal vs. noise)
- **Confidence** in the conclusions (probability of correctness)
- **Relationships** between variables (regression/correlation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> Statistical analysis is the methodology of applying tests of significance (F-test, t-test), partitioning total variation (ANOVA), and performing mean separation procedures (LSD, Tukey's HSD, Duncan's MRT) to draw objective conclusions from experimental data collected under controlled designs (CRD, RBD, LSD).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Courtroom Trial 🎯

Imagine an experiment is a **courtroom trial** and statistical analysis is the **judge's deliberation process**:

| Trial Element | DOE Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Crime scene evidence | Experimental observations (response data) |
| Suspects | Treatment levels (factor levels) |
| Forensic analysis | ANOVA (Analysis of Variance) |
| "Beyond reasonable doubt" | p-value < significance level (α = **0.05**) |
| Witness testimony | Replication of measurements |
| Final verdict | Reject or accept Null Hypothesis |

> The **defense attorney** is the **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** — it always says "no effect, no difference, nothing happened." The **prosecution** is the **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)** — it claims "there IS a real effect." Statistical analysis is the **judge** who, by computing the F-statistic, decides whether the evidence (data) is strong enough to convict (reject H₀) or acquit (fail to reject H₀).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> We never "prove" H₁. We only assess whether the data provide **sufficient evidence** to reject H₀. This is the philosophical foundation of Fisher's significance testing.

---

## 1.3 Why Statistical Analysis? — The Engineering Motivation

In real engineering processes, every measurement is contaminated by **inherent random variation** (noise). Consider measuring the tensile strength of a steel rod:

- Even under identical conditions, two specimens will yield slightly different values.
- A new heat treatment **may** increase strength — but how do we know it's not just luck?
- Statistical analysis answers: *"Is the observed difference REAL or just RANDOM NOISE?"*

### Core Functions of Statistical Analysis in DOE:

1. **Quantify Variation** — Decompose total variability into meaningful components (treatment + error).
2. **Test Hypotheses** — Objectively decide if factor effects are statistically significant.
3. **Compare Treatments** — Identify which specific means differ using post-hoc tests.
4. **Estimate Effects** — Quantify the magnitude of treatment effects with confidence intervals.
5. **Model Relationships** — Establish mathematical relationships (regression) between factors and response.
6. **Predict Outcomes** — Forecast response for untested conditions using fitted models.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** "Discuss the role of statistical analysis in DOE" — always mention all 6 functions above and emphasize that it **transforms data into decisions**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Statistical Analysis

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                │   STATISTICAL ANALYSIS      │
                └──────────────┬──────────────┘
                               │
            ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
            │                  │                  │
     ┌──────▼──────┐    ┌──────▼──────┐    ┌──────▼──────┐
     │   ANOVA     │    │  REGRESSION │    │ HYPOTHESIS  │
     │  (Variance  │    │ (Cause-Effect│   │   TESTING   │
     │ Partition)  │    │  Modeling)  │    │ (Decisions) │
     └──────┬──────┘    └──────┬──────┘    └──────┬──────┘
            │                  │                  │
       Compares            Quantifies         Answers:
       MEANS               SLOPES             "Is it REAL?"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three pillars are **interconnected** — ANOVA is a special case of regression; hypothesis testing is the decision framework that uses ANOVA/regression output.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Statistical Analysis

The entire framework rests on **four foundational principles**:

### Principle 1: Partitioning of Total Variation (Variation Accounting)

The cornerstone of ANOVA. The **Total Sum of Squares (SST)** is decomposed into meaningful components:

$$SST = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Error}$$

For a two-factor design:

$$SST = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_{Error}$$

> **Logic:** Variation either comes from **deliberate changes** (treatments) or from **uncontrolled random causes** (error). We need to separate them to judge significance.

### Principle 2: Replication (Degrees of Freedom for Error)

**Replication** is the repetition of experimental runs under identical conditions. It is the **only** mechanism that provides an independent estimate of experimental error.

- With *n* replicates per treatment and *k* treatments, total observations: $N = k \times n$
- **Degrees of Freedom for Error:** $df_E = k(n-1) = N - k$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Without replication, you CANNOT compute error variance. The experiment is then useless for hypothesis testing. Always plan for replication BEFORE running the experiment.

### Principle 3: Randomization (Validity of Error Estimate)

**Randomization** ensures that the error term is **independent** and **unbiasedly estimated**. It neutralizes the effect of lurking variables (time trends, environmental drift, instrument fatigue).

- Every experimental run is assigned in a **random order** (using random number tables or software).
- This guarantees that the error MS is a valid estimate of $\sigma^2$.

### Principle 4: Blocking (Reduction of Known Nuisance Variability)

A **block** is a grouping of experimental units that are **homogeneous within** but potentially **heterogeneous between**. Blocking removes known sources of variability (e.g., batches of raw material, shifts, days) from the error term, increasing the **power** of the test.

---

## 2.2 The ANOVA Framework — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses

For single-factor experiments:

$$H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2 = \ldots = \mu_k \quad \text{(All treatment means are equal)}$$

$$H_1: \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{for at least one pair } (i,j)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

The grand mean $\bar{\bar{y}}$ is computed once and used to "correct" all sums of squares:

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N}$$

where $G$ = grand total of all observations, $N$ = total number of observations.

### Step 3: Partition the Sum of Squares

| Source | Formula | Degrees of Freedom |
|---|---|---|
| **Total (Corrected)** | $SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n}y_{ij}^2 - CF$ | $N-1$ |
| **Treatments (Between)** | $SS_{Tr} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$ | $k-1$ |
| **Error (Within)** | $SSE = SST - SS_{Tr}$ | $N-k$ |

where $T_i$ = total of $i$-th treatment, $n_i$ = number of replicates in $i$-th treatment.

### Step 4: Compute Mean Squares (Variances)

Mean Square = Sum of Squares / Degrees of Freedom

$$MS_{Tr} = \frac{SS_{Tr}}{k-1}, \quad MS_E = \frac{SSE}{N-k}$$

### Step 5: Compute the F-Statistic

The F-statistic is the **ratio of explained variance to unexplained variance**:

$$F_0 = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$$

### Step 6: Decision Rule

Compare $F_0$ with the **critical value** $F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ from the F-distribution table:

- If $F_0 > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ → **Reject $H_0$** (treatments have a significant effect)
- If $F_0 \leq F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ → **Fail to reject $H_0$** (no significant effect detected)

Equivalently, if **p-value** $< \alpha$ → reject $H_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Always state BOTH the critical value approach AND the p-value approach. Examiners reward completeness.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the only formulas you need for 90% of KTU problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Correction Factor | $CF = \dfrac{G^2}{N}$ | $G$ = grand total, $N$ = total obs. |
| 2 | Total SS | $SST = \sum y_{ij}^2 - CF$ | Sum of squared raw observations minus CF |
| 3 | Treatment SS | $SS_{Tr} = \sum \dfrac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$ | $T_i$ = $i$-th treatment total |
| 4 | Error SS | $SSE = SST - SS_{Tr}$ | Computed by subtraction |
| 5 | Block SS (RBD) | $SS_{Bl} = \sum \dfrac{B_j^2}{k} - CF$ | $B_j$ = $j$-th block total, $k$ = treatments |
| 6 | Treatment MS | $MS_{Tr} = \dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{k-1}$ | Variance among treatments |
| 7 | Error MS | $MS_E = \dfrac{SSE}{N-k}$ | Pooled within-treatment variance |
| 8 | F-statistic | $F_0 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E}$ | Test statistic |
| 9 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{\bar{y}}} \times 100$ | Measures experimental precision (%) |
| 10 | Standard Error of Mean | $SE = \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_E}{n}}$ | Used in LSD and confidence intervals |
| 11 | LSD (Least Sig. Diff.) | $LSD = t_{\alpha/2, N-k} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 MS_E}{n}}$ | For equal replicates $n$ |
| 12 | Tukey's HSD | $HSD = q_{\alpha, k, N-k} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MS_E}{n}}$ | $q$ from studentized range table |
| 13 | Regression Slope | $\beta_1 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | $b_1$ in simple linear regression |
| 14 | Regression Intercept | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | $b_0$ in simple linear regression |
| 15 | Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Strength of linear relationship |
| 16 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = \dfrac{SS_R}{SST} = 1 - \dfrac{SSE}{SST}$ | Proportion of variation explained |
| 17 | Lack of Fit SS | $SS_{LOF} = SS_{PE} - SSE$ | $SS_{PE}$ = pure error SS |
| 18 | Pure Error SS | $SS_{PE} = \sum \sum(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_i)^2$ | From replicated runs only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbolic Reminder:** All vertical bars in formulas (e.g., $\vert t \vert$) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Hypothesis Testing — The Decision Framework

### Type I and Type II Errors

| Decision | $H_0$ is TRUE | $H_0$ is FALSE |
|---|---|---|
| **Fail to reject $H_0$** | Correct decision (Probability $1-\alpha$) | **Type II Error** (Probability $\beta$) — Missed effect |
| **Reject $H_0$** | **Type I Error** (Probability $\alpha$) — False alarm | Correct decision (Power $= 1-\beta$) |

- **Significance level $\alpha$** (typically **0.05**) = probability of falsely rejecting a true $H_0$
- **Power** ($1-\beta$) = probability of correctly detecting a true effect

> [!WARNING]
> Decreasing $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.01) makes the test more **conservative** (harder to reject $H_0$) — this increases $\beta$. There is always a trade-off.

---

## 2.5 Mean Comparison (Post-Hoc) Tests

After rejecting $H_0$ in ANOVA, we know **at least one** mean differs, but not **which ones**. Post-hoc tests identify the specific differences.

### A. Least Significant Difference (LSD) Test

- **Most liberal** test — controls comparison-wise error rate, not experiment-wise.
- Formula: $LSD = t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot MS_E / n}$
- Compare $\vert \bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j \vert$ with $LSD$. If $\geq LSD$, means differ.

### B. Tukey's Honestly Significant Difference (HSD) Test

- **Most conservative** — controls experiment-wise error rate.
- Formula: $HSD = q_{\alpha, k, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{MS_E / n}$
- Uses the **studentized range** statistic $q$.

### C. Duncan's Multiple Range Test (DMRT)

- **Intermediate** in conservatism.
- Uses a **protected** approach: only proceed if ANOVA F-test is significant.
- Compares ranges of ordered means against tabulated significant ranges.

| Test | Conservatism | Use When |
|---|---|---|
| LSD | Low (liberal) | Few planned comparisons, exploratory work |
| Tukey's HSD | High (strict) | Many pairwise comparisons, confirmatory |
| Duncan's MRT | Medium | Multiple comparisons, balance of both |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite:** "Compare the means using an appropriate post-hoc test" — Tukey's HSD is the **safest default** and most accepted in industry.

---

## 2.6 Regression Analysis in DOE

While ANOVA answers *"is there an effect?"*, regression answers *"how much effect per unit change?"* and *"what is the functional relationship?"*

### Simple Linear Regression Model

$$Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $Y$ = response (dependent variable)
- $X$ = predictor (independent variable, e.g., factor level)
- $\beta_0$ = intercept (response when $X=0$)
- $\beta_1$ = slope (change in $Y$ per unit change in $X$)
- $\varepsilon$ = random error, assumed $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$

### Significance of Regression (t-test on slope)

$$t_0 = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)}$$

where $SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{MS_E / \sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$.

If $\vert t_0 \vert > t_{\alpha/2, N-2}$, the regression is significant.

### Lack of Fit Test

Decomposes $SSE$ into **Lack of Fit** and **Pure Error**:

$$SSE = SS_{LOF} + SS_{PE}$$

- $SS_{PE}$ comes **only from replicated runs** (within-treatment variation due to replication).
- If $SS_{LOF}$ is significant ($F_{LOF} = MS_{LOF}/MS_{PE} > F_{crit}$), the linear model is **inadequate** — try a higher-order model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Lack of fit testing is a powerful diagnostic — it tells you whether your model is **structurally correct**, not just whether the parameters are significant.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Statistical Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Compare alternative processes, optimize machine settings |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Bioequivalence studies, drug formulation screening |
| **Materials Science** | Compare alloy compositions, heat treatment effects |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Yield optimization in reactors, catalyst screening |
| **Civil Engineering** | Compare concrete mixes, soil stabilization methods |
| **Electronics** | Compare soldering methods, PCB layout effects |
| **Agriculture** | Compare fertilizers, irrigation regimes (classical origin) |
| **Quality Engineering** | Validate Six Sigma improvements, process capability |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single Factor ANOVA (CRD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context (KTU-style):**
> A textile engineer investigates the effect of **4 different dye concentrations** (treatments A, B, C, D) on fabric colorfastness. Each treatment is replicated **5 times** (20 observations total). Conduct a one-way ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$ and interpret the results.

### Given Data Table (Hypothetical Values)

| Treatment | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | Rep 3 | Rep 4 | Rep 5 | Total $T_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (2%) | 65 | 68 | 66 | 67 | 64 | 330 |
| **B** (4%) | 72 | 75 | 70 | 73 | 70 | 360 |
| **C** (6%) | 78 | 80 | 76 | 79 | 77 | 390 |
| **D** (8%) | 85 | 88 | 84 | 86 | 82 | 425 |
| | | | | | **Grand Total $G$** | **1505** |

- Number of treatments: $k = 4$
- Replicates per treatment: $n = 5$
- Total observations: $N = 20$
- Grand Mean: $\bar{\bar{y}} = 1505 / 20 = 75.25$

---

### Step 1: State the Hypotheses

$$H_0: \mu_A = \mu_B = \mu_C = \mu_D \quad \text{(dye concentration has no effect)}$$

$$H_1: \mu_i \neq \mu_j \quad \text{for at least one pair } (i,j)$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Correction Factor (CF)

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(1505)^2}{20} = \frac{2265025}{20} = 113251.25$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Total Sum of Squares (SST)

$$SST = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_{ij}^2 - CF$$

First, sum of all squared observations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum y_{ij}^2 = &\, (65^2 + 68^2 + 66^2 + 67^2 + 64^2) \\
&+ (72^2 + 75^2 + 70^2 + 73^2 + 70^2) \\
&+ (78^2 + 80^2 + 76^2 + 79^2 + 77^2) \\
&+ (85^2 + 88^2 + 84^2 + 86^2 + 82^2) \\
= &\, (4225 + 4624 + 4356 + 4489 + 4096) \\
&+ (5184 + 5625 + 4900 + 5329 + 4900) \\
&+ (6084 + 6400 + 5776 + 6241 + 5929) \\
&+ (7225 + 7744 + 7056 + 7396 + 6724) \\
= &\, 21790 + 25938 + 30430 + 36145 \\
= &\, 114303
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$SST = 114303 - 113251.25 = 1051.75$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Treatment Sum of Squares (SS_Tr)

$$SS_{Tr} = \sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{T_i^2}{n_i} - CF$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{Tr} = &\, \frac{(330)^2}{5} + \frac{(360)^2}{5} + \frac{(390)^2}{5} + \frac{(425)^2}{5} - 113251.25 \\
= &\, \frac{108900}{5} + \frac{129600}{5} + \frac{152100}{5} + \frac{180625}{5} - 113251.25 \\
= &\, 21780 + 25920 + 30420 + 36125 - 113251.25 \\
= &\, 114245 - 113251.25 \\
= &\, 993.75
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5: Compute the Error Sum of Squares (SSE)

$$SSE = SST - SS_{Tr} = 1051.75 - 993.75 = 58.00$$

**Verification check:** This should be positive. ✓

---

### Step 6: Compute Degrees of Freedom

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_{Tr} &= k - 1 = 4 - 1 = 3 \\
df_E &= N - k = 20 - 4 = 16 \\
df_T &= N - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19 \\
\text{Check: } &\, df_{Tr} + df_E = 3 + 16 = 19 = df_T \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7: Compute Mean Squares

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_{Tr} &= \frac{SS_{Tr}}{df_{Tr}} = \frac{993.75}{3} = 331.25 \\
MS_E &= \frac{SSE}{df_E} = \frac{58.00}{16} = 3.625
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 8: Compute the F-Statistic

$$F_0 = \frac{MS_{Tr}}{MS_E} = \frac{331.25}{3.625} = 91.379$$

---

### Step 9: Critical Value and Decision

From F-table at $\alpha = 0.05$, $df_1 = 3$, $df_2 = 16$:

$$F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 > F_{crit}$

Since $F_0 = 91.38 \gg 3.24$, we **strongly reject $H_0$**.

> **Engineering Conclusion:** Dye concentration has a **highly significant** effect on fabric colorfastness. The variation between concentration levels is 91 times larger than the random experimental error — this is not chance!

---

### Step 10: Construct the Complete ANOVA Table

| Source of Variation | SS | df | MS | F₀ | F_crit | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Between Treatments | 993.75 | 3 | 331.25 | **91.38** | 3.24 | **Significant** |
| Within (Error) | 58.00 | 16 | 3.625 | | | |
| **Total** | **1051.75** | **19** | | | | |

---

### Step 11: Coefficient of Variation (Precision Indicator)

$$CV = \frac{\sqrt{MS_E}}{\bar{\bar{y}}} \times 100 = \frac{\sqrt{3.625}}{75.25} \times 100 = \frac{1.904}{75.25} \times 100 = 2.53\%$$

> **Interpretation:** A CV of 2.53% indicates **excellent experimental precision** (typically CV < 10% is good in industrial experiments).

---

## 3.2 Pairwise Mean Comparison — Tukey's HSD

### Treatment Means

$$\bar{y}_A = 66.0, \quad \bar{y}_B = 72.0, \quad \bar{y}_C = 78.0, \quad \bar{y}_D = 85.0$$

### Tukey's HSD Calculation

From the studentized range table at $\alpha = 0.05$, $k = 4$ treatments, $df_E = 16$:

$$q_{0.05, 4, 16} = 4.05$$

$$HSD = q_{\alpha, k, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{MS_E}{n}} = 4.05 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3.625}{5}} = 4.05 \cdot \sqrt{0.725} = 4.05 \cdot 0.8515 = 3.448$$

### Pairwise Comparison Table

| Comparison | $\vert \bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j \vert$ | > HSD (3.448)? | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert A - B \vert$ | $\vert 66 - 72 \vert = 6.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert A - C \vert$ | $\vert 66 - 78 \vert = 12.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert A - D \vert$ | $\vert 66 - 85 \vert = 19.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert B - C \vert$ | $\vert 72 - 78 \vert = 6.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert B - D \vert$ | $\vert 72 - 85 \vert = 13.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert C - D \vert$ | $\vert 78 - 85 \vert = 7.0$ | Yes ✓ | **Significantly different** |

> **Conclusion:** **All four dye concentrations produce significantly different colorfastness**. The ranking from best to worst is: D > C > B > A. Concentration D (8%) yields the highest colorfastness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why F-Test Works (Conceptual)

The F-statistic compares two variance estimates:

$$F_0 = \frac{\hat{\sigma}^2_{Treatment}}{\hat{\sigma}^2_{Error}}$$

**Under $H_0$ (no treatment effect):**
- $MS_{Tr}$ estimates $\sigma^2$ (same as $MS_E$)
- The ratio $F_0 \approx 1$ (sampling fluctuation around 1)
- $F_0$ follows an F-distribution with $(k-1, N-k)$ degrees of freedom

**Under $H_1$ (treatment effect exists):**
- $MS_{Tr}$ estimates $\sigma^2 + n\sum(\tau_i)^2/(k-1)$ where $\tau_i = \mu_i - \mu$
- The extra term $\sum(\tau_i)^2$ inflates $MS_{Tr}$
- $F_0$ becomes much larger than 1 → upper tail of F-distribution

**The decision:** If $F_0$ falls in the upper $\alpha$ tail of $F(k-1, N-k)$, conclude that the inflation is too large to be due to chance alone → **reject $H_0$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Assumption:** The F-test requires that all treatment groups have **equal variances** (homoscedasticity) and that errors are **normally distributed**. KTU students should mention these assumptions explicitly.

---

## 3.4 Regression Analysis — Worked Example

> **Problem:** Fit a simple linear regression $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ to the following data, test the significance of the slope, and compute $R^2$.

| $x$ (Temperature °C) | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (Yield %) | 45 | 52 | 58 | 65 | 71 |

### Step 1: Compute Means

$$\bar{x} = \frac{100+150+200+250+300}{5} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{45+52+58+65+71}{5} = \frac{291}{5} = 58.2$$

### Step 2: Compute Sums of Squares and Cross-Products

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = &\, (-100)^2 + (-50)^2 + 0^2 + 50^2 + 100^2 \\
= &\, 10000 + 2500 + 0 + 2500 + 10000 = 25000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = &\, (-100)(45-58.2) + (-50)(52-58.2) + (0)(58-58.2) \\
&+ (50)(65-58.2) + (100)(71-58.2) \\
= &\, (-100)(-13.2) + (-50)(-6.2) + 0 + (50)(6.8) + (100)(12.8) \\
= &\, 1320 + 310 + 0 + 340 + 1280 = 3250
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Regression Coefficients

$$\hat{\beta_1} = \frac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{3250}{25000} = 0.130$$

$$\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta_1}\bar{x} = 58.2 - (0.130)(200) = 58.2 - 26.0 = 32.2$$

### Step 4: Fitted Regression Equation

$$\boxed{\hat{y} = 32.2 + 0.130\, x}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

- **Slope:** For every 1°C increase in temperature, yield increases by 0.130%.
- **Intercept:** At 0°C, predicted yield is 32.2% (may not be physically meaningful if 0°C is outside the experimental range).
- The **practical range** of the model is 100°C to 300°C.

### Step 6: Compute R² (Coefficient of Determination)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2 = &\, (-13.2)^2 + (-6.2)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (6.8)^2 + (12.8)^2 \\
= &\, 174.24 + 38.44 + 0.04 + 46.24 + 163.84 = 422.8
\end{aligned}
$$

$$SSR = \hat{\beta_1} \cdot \sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) = 0.130 \times 3250 = 422.5$$

$$R^2 = \frac{SSR}{SST} = \frac{422.5}{422.8} = 0.9993 = 99.93\%$$

> **Conclusion:** 99.93% of the variation in yield is explained by temperature — an **excellent fit**. The linear model is highly adequate for this data.

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Statistical Analysis Workflow in DOE

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Raw Experimental Data] --> stepB[Step 1: Data Cleaning and Validation]
    stepB --> stepC{Step 2: Check Assumptions}
    stepC -->|Normality| testC1[Shapiro-Wilk / QQ-Plot]
    stepC -->|Homogeneity of Variance| testC2[Levene's / Bartlett's Test]
    stepC -->|Independence| testC3[Run Test / Durbin-Watson]

    testC1 --> stepD[Step 3: Choose Statistical Test]
    testC2 --> stepD
    testC3 --> stepD

    stepD -->|Compare Means| pathE1[ANOVA Single / Multifactor]
    stepD -->|Quantify Relationship| pathE2[Regression Analysis]
    stepD -->|Rank Treatments| pathE3[Non-parametric Tests]

    pathE1 --> stepF[Step 4: Construct ANOVA Table]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 5: Compute F-Statistic]
    stepG --> stepH{Step 6: Compare F0 with F critical}

    stepH -->|F0 greater than Fcrit| stepI[Reject H0: Significant Effect]
    stepH -->|F0 less or equal Fcrit| stepJ[Fail to Reject H0: No Effect]

    stepI --> stepK[Step 7: Post-Hoc Mean Comparison]
    stepK --> stepL[Tukey HSD / LSD / DMRT]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 8: Engineering Interpretation and Recommendations]

    stepJ --> stepM

    pathE2 --> regA[Fit Regression Model]
    regA --> regB[Test Significance of Slope]
    regB --> regC[Check R-squared and Residual Plots]
    regC --> stepM

    stepM --> endZ[End: Validated Conclusions and Decisions]
```
