## 4.3 Hypothesis Testing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hyStart[State H0 and H1] --> hyChoose{Select Test}
    hyChoose -->|Compare 2 Means| ttest[Two-Sample t-Test]
    hyChoose -->|Compare 3+ Means| anovaPath[One-Way ANOVA]
    hyChoose -->|Relationship| regPath[Regression t-Test]
    hyChoose -->|Categorical Data| chiPath[Chi-Square Test]

    ttest --> tCalc[Compute t-statistic]
    anovaPath --> fCalc[Compute F-statistic]
    regPath --> bCalc[Compute t for slope]
    chiPath --> cCalc[Compute Chi-square]

    tCalc --> tComp{Compare with t critical}
    fCalc --> fComp{Compare with F critical}
    bCalc --> bComp{Compare with t critical}
    cCalc --> cComp{Compare with Chi critical}

    tComp -->|Reject| rejH0[Reject H0]
    tComp -->|Fail| keepH0[Fail to Reject H0]
    fComp -->|Reject| rejH0
    fComp -->|Fail| keepH0
    bComp -->|Reject| rejH0
    bComp -->|Fail| keepH0
    cComp -->|Reject| rejH0
    cComp -->|Fail| keepH0

    rejH0 --> engImp[Engineering Meaning: Effect is Real]
    keepH0 --> engNoImp[Engineering Meaning: Insufficient Evidence of Effect]

    style keepH0 fill:#f94144,color:#fff

    style engNoImp fill:#f8961e,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Variation Partitioning — The Core Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    sstBox[Total Variation SST] --> sstrBox[Treatment Variation SS Tr]
    sstBox --> sseBox[Error Variation SSE]
```

## 4.5 Post-Hoc Test Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startP[ANOVA Rejected H0: Now What?] --> qP1{Number of Comparisons?}

    qP1 -->|Few Planned| pathLSD[Use LSD Test]
    qP1 -->|All Pairs| qP2{Need Strict Control?}

    qP2 -->|Yes| pathTukey[Use Tukey HSD]
    qP2 -->|No, Want Balance| pathDuncan[Use Duncan MRT]

    pathLSD --> recP1[Liberal: Best for Exploratory]
    pathTukey --> recP2[Conservative: Best for Confirmatory]
    pathDuncan --> recP3[Intermediate: Best for General Use]

    style startP fill:#277da1,color:#fff
    style pathLSD fill:#90be6d,color:#000
    style pathTukey fill:#f94144,color:#fff
    style pathDuncan fill:#f9c74f,color:#000
```

# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** pattern: Part A (3 marks each), Part B (14 marks each with internal choice between two questions A and B, each having sub-parts of 7 marks).

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the following terms with respect to Design of Experiments:**
**(a)** Replication
**(b)** Randomization
**(c)** Blocking

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Replication:** The independent repetition of an experimental run under identical treatment conditions. It provides an estimate of the **experimental error variance** and increases the **precision** of treatment mean estimates. For example, if a treatment is replicated 5 times, we get 5 independent measurements of the response under that treatment.

**(b) Randomization:** The process of allocating experimental runs to treatment combinations in a **random order** using random number tables or software. It ensures that the estimates of treatment effects and experimental error are **unbiased** and **validly estimated**. Randomization neutralizes the effect of **lurking variables** such as environmental drift, instrument fatigue, and time trends.

**(c) Blocking:** A design technique in which experimental units are grouped into **homogeneous blocks** (e.g., batches of raw material, days of testing, shifts) to remove a known source of **nuisance variability** from the error term. Blocking increases the **power** of hypothesis tests by reducing the within-group variability. In RBD, the block effect is removed from the error.

> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for each definition with example. Examiners look for engineering examples.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the fundamental principles of Design of Experiments.**

#### Model Answer:

The three fundamental principles of DOE (Box, Hunter, and Hunter) are:

**1. Replication:** Repetition of experimental runs enables estimation of experimental error variance and improves precision of treatment mean estimates. The standard error of a mean decreases with $\sqrt{n}$, so 4 replications give twice the precision of 1 replication.

**2. Randomization:** Allocation of treatments to experimental units in a random order ensures that the error estimate is **independent** of treatment effects and that the experiment is **valid** for statistical inference. It protects against systematic biases from unknown or uncontrolled factors.

**3. Blocking:** Grouping of homogeneous experimental units into blocks removes known sources of variability (nuisance factors) from the error term, increasing the **sensitivity** (power) of the experiment. Blocking is a form of local control.

> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for each principle with a one-line explanation. Mention that these three principles are **synergistic** — working together they produce efficient, valid, and powerful experiments.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** The following data were collected in a CRD experiment to study the effect of **three catalysts** on reaction yield (in %). Each catalyst was tested 4 times. Analyze the data using ANOVA at $\alpha = 0.05$.

| Catalyst | Observation 1 | Observation 2 | Observation 3 | Observation 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 50 | 52 | 48 | 51 |
| **B** | 56 | 58 | 55 | 57 |
| **C** | 60 | 62 | 61 | 63 |

**(b)** If the F-test indicates significance, perform **Tukey's HSD test** to identify which catalysts differ significantly. Use $\alpha = 0.05$.

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Step 1: Tabulate Treatment Totals and Grand Total**

$$
T_A = 50+52+48+51 = 201, \quad T_B = 56+58+55+57 = 226, \quad T_C = 60+62+61+63 = 246
$$

$$G = 201 + 226 + 246 = 673, \quad N = 12, \quad \bar{\bar{y}} = 673/12 = 56.083$$

**Step 2: Compute Correction Factor (CF)**

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(673)^2}{12} = \frac{452929}{12} = 37744.083$$

**Step 3: Compute SST**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum y_{ij}^2 = &\, (50^2+52^2+48^2+51^2) + (56^2+58^2+55^2+57^2) + (60^2+62^2+61^2+63^2) \\
= &\, (2500+2704+2304+2601) + (3136+3364+3025+3249) + (3600+3844+3721+3969) \\
= &\, 10109 + 12774 + 15134 = 38017
\end{aligned}
$$

$$SST = 38017 - 37744.083 = 272.917$$

**Step 4: Compute SS_Tr**

$$SS_{Tr} = \frac{201^2}{4} + \frac{226^2}{4} + \frac{246^2}{4} - CF = 10100.25 + 12769.00 + 15129.00 - 37744.083 = 244.167$$

**Step 5: Compute SSE**

$$SSE = SST - SS_{Tr} = 272.917 - 244.167 = 28.750$$

**Step 6: Degrees of Freedom**

$$df_{Tr} = 3-1 = 2, \quad df_E = 12-3 = 9, \quad df_T = 11$$

**Step 7: Mean Squares**

$$MS_{Tr} = 244.167/2 = 122.083, \quad MS_E = 28.750/9 = 3.194$$

**Step 8: F-Statistic**

$$F_0 = 122.083/3.194 = 38.22$$

**Step 9: Critical Value and Decision**

From F-table: $F_{0.05, 2, 9} = 4.26$. Since $F_0 = 38.22 > 4.26$, **reject $H_0$**.

**ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F₀ | F_crit | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Catalysts | 244.167 | 2 | 122.083 | **38.22** | 4.26 | **Significant** |
| Error | 28.750 | 9 | 3.194 | | | |
| **Total** | **272.917** | **11** | | | | |

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Treatment Means:**

$$\bar{y}_A = 201/4 = 50.25, \quad \bar{y}_B = 226/4 = 56.50, \quad \bar{y}_C = 246/4 = 61.50$$

**Tukey's HSD:**

From the studentized range table at $\alpha = 0.05$, $k = 3$ treatments, $df_E = 9$:

$$q_{0.05, 3, 9} = 3.95$$

$$HSD = 3.95 \times \sqrt{3.194/4} = 3.95 \times \sqrt{0.7985} = 3.95 \times 0.8936 = 3.530$$

**Pairwise Comparisons:**

| Comparison | $\vert \bar{y}_i - \bar{y}_j \vert$ | > HSD (3.530)? | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\vert A - B \vert$ | 6.25 | Yes | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert A - C \vert$ | 11.25 | Yes | **Significantly different** |
| $\vert B - C \vert$ | 5.00 | Yes | **Significantly different** |

**Conclusion:** All three catalysts produce **significantly different** yields. Ranking: C (61.50%) > B (56.50%) > A (50.25%). **Catalyst C is recommended** for maximum yield.

---

### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **ANOVA procedure** in detail for a **Completely Randomized Design (CRD)** with a suitable example. State clearly the assumptions underlying the analysis.

**(b)** Distinguish between **Type I and Type II errors** in hypothesis testing. A new manufacturing process claims to reduce defects by 20%. How would you set up the null and alternative hypotheses to test this claim? What is the meaning of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in this context?

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks):

**ANOVA Procedure for CRD:**

**Step 1: Model Statement**

The statistical model for a CRD with one factor is:

$$y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where $y_{ij}$ = $j$-th observation on $i$-th treatment, $\mu$ = overall mean, $\tau_i$ = effect of $i$-th treatment, $\varepsilon_{ij}$ = random error $\sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 2: Hypotheses**

$H_0: \tau_1 = \tau_2 = \ldots = \tau_k = 0$ (all treatment means equal)
$H_1: \tau_i \neq 0$ for at least one $i$

**Step 3: Computational Procedure**

1. Compute correction factor: $CF = G^2/N$
2. Compute SST = $\sum y_{ij}^2 - CF$ with $df = N-1$
3. Compute $SS_{Tr} = \sum (T_i^2/n_i) - CF$ with $df = k-1$
4. Compute SSE = SST - SS_Tr with $df = N-k$
5. Compute $MS_{Tr} = SS_{Tr}/(k-1)$ and $MS_E = SSE/(N-k)$
6. Compute $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$
7. Compare with $F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$ from tables

**Step 4: Example Illustration**

Using the dye concentration example from Section 3.1:
- $k = 4$ treatments, $n = 5$ replicates, $N = 20$
- Obtained $F_0 = 91.38 > F_{0.05, 3, 16} = 3.24$ → Significant

**Assumptions of CRD-ANOVA:**

1. **Normality:** Errors $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are normally distributed: $\varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$
2. **Homogeneity of variance:** All treatments have the same error variance $\sigma^2$ (homoscedasticity)
3. **Independence:** Observations and errors are statistically independent
4. **Additivity:** The model is additive: $y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$

> **Note:** If the additivity assumption is violated, a transformation (e.g., log, square root) or a non-parametric test (Kruskal-Wallis) may be used.

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks):

**Type I vs Type II Errors:**

| | $H_0$ is True | $H_0$ is False |
|---|---|---|
| **Fail to reject $H_0$** | Correct (Probability $1-\alpha$) | **Type II Error** (Probability $\beta$) |
| **Reject $H_0$** | **Type I Error** (Probability $\alpha$) | Correct (Power $= 1-\beta$) |

- **Type I Error ($\alpha$):** Concluding that the new process reduces defects when in reality it does not (false alarm).
- **Type II Error ($\beta$):** Failing to detect that the new process reduces defects when in reality it does (missed opportunity).

**Hypothesis Setup for the Defect Reduction Claim:**

Let $p_0$ = current defect rate, $p$ = new process defect rate (expected 20% reduction → $p = 0.8 p_0$)

$$H_0: p = p_0 \quad \text{(New process has NO effect on defect rate)}$$

$$H_1: p < p_0 \quad \text{(New process reduces defects by 20%)}$$

This is a **one-tailed (left-tailed) test**.

**Meaning of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in this context:**

- **$\alpha$ (Significance Level):** The probability of **incorrectly rejecting** $H_0$, i.e., wrongly concluding that the new process is better when it is actually the same as the old. If $\alpha = 0.05$, there is a 5% chance of adopting a useless process change.
- **$\beta$ (Type II Error Probability):** The probability of **incorrectly failing to reject** $H_0$, i.e., missing the real improvement. The **power** ($1-\beta$) is the probability of correctly detecting the 20% reduction. If $\beta = 0.20$, there is a 20% chance of failing to adopt a beneficial process change.

> **Trade-off:** Decreasing $\alpha$ (making the test more stringent) generally **increases** $\beta$ (increases the chance of missing a real effect). Sample size can be increased to reduce both.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**

1. **Forgetting the CF (Correction Factor):** Many students compute SS_Tr and SST but forget to subtract CF. The CF is a **mandatory** step in the manual ANOVA computation. Marks deducted: 1-2 per omission.

2. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom:** For CRD: $df_{Tr} = k-1$, $df_E = N-k$, $df_T = N-1$. For RBD: $df_{Bl} = b-1$, $df_{Tr} = k-1$, $df_E = (k-1)(b-1)$. Mixing up $k$ and $b$ is a very common error.

3. **No Interpretation:** Simply computing F₀ and not stating the **engineering conclusion** ("reject H₀" or "treatments have a significant effect"). Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this.

4. **Confusing LSD and HSD Formulas:** LSD uses the $t$-statistic, HSD uses the $q$ (studentized range) statistic. They have **different tables**. Using $t$ instead of $q$ (or vice versa) loses 2-3 marks.

5. **Skipping Assumption Checks:** ANOVA assumes normality and homoscedasticity. For 14-mark questions, **always** list the assumptions and briefly mention diagnostic tests (Shapiro-Wilk for normality, Levene's for variance equality).

6. **Incorrect Mean Computation:** Writing $T_i$ (treatment total) instead of $\bar{y}_i$ (treatment mean) in the comparison table. The HSD/LSD formula uses $n$ (replicates per treatment) and $MS_E$.

7. **No Conclusion on Post-Hoc Test:** After pairwise comparisons, students often forget to state the **ranking** and **practical recommendation**. Always end with: "Catalyst X is recommended because..."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🧠

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

### ✅ Core Concepts

- **Statistical Analysis** transforms raw experimental data into valid, objective engineering decisions through ANOVA, regression, and hypothesis testing.
- The **three fundamental principles** of DOE: Replication, Randomization, Blocking — all are essential for valid inference.
- **Variation Partitioning:** $SST = SS_{Treatment} + SS_{Error}$ (or $SST = SS_A + SS_B + SS_{AB} + SS_E$ for two-factor designs).

### ✅ ANOVA Essentials

- **CRD** (Completely Randomized Design): One factor, $k$ treatments, $n$ replicates. $df_E = N - k$. Used when experimental units are **homogeneous**.
- **RBD** (Randomized Block Design): One factor + one blocking variable. $df_E = (k-1)(b-1)$. Used to remove **nuisance variability**.
- **F-test** decision: Reject $H_0$ if $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E > F_{\alpha, k-1, N-k}$.
- **CV** (Coefficient of Variation) $< 10\%$ indicates good experimental precision.

### ✅ Post-Hoc Tests

- **LSD** = $t_{\alpha/2, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{2 MS_E / n}$ — liberal, uses t-table.
- **Tukey HSD** = $q_{\alpha, k, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{MS_E / n}$ — conservative, uses q-table.
- **Duncan MRT** — intermediate, uses protected approach.

### ✅ Hypothesis Testing

- **$H_0$** = "no effect" (status quo, defense). **$H_1$** = "real effect exists" (prosecution).
- **Type I error ($\alpha$):** False positive — detecting an effect that isn't there.
- **Type II error ($\beta$):** False negative — missing a real effect.
- **Power** = $1 - \beta$ = ability to detect a true effect.

### ✅ Regression Essentials

- **Model:** $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \varepsilon$
- **Slope** $\hat{\beta_1} = \sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) / \sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$
- **Intercept** $\hat{\beta_0} = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta_1}\bar{x}$
- **$R^2$** = proportion of variation explained; closer to 1 = better fit.
- **Lack of Fit test:** If significant, the chosen model form is inadequate → try higher-order terms.

### ✅ Formulas to Memorize (Top 5)

1. $CF = G^2/N$
2. $SST = \sum y^2 - CF$
3. $SS_{Tr} = \sum (T_i^2/n_i) - CF$
4. $F_0 = MS_{Tr}/MS_E$
5. $HSD = q_{\alpha, k, df_E} \cdot \sqrt{MS_E/n}$

### ✅ Engineering Translation Phrases

- "**Reject $H_0$**" → "The factor has a **statistically significant effect** on the response."
- "**Fail to reject $H_0$**" → "There is **insufficient evidence** of an effect at the 5% level."
- "**$R^2 = 0.95$**" → "95% of the variation in $Y$ is explained by $X$ — the model is highly adequate."
- "**CV = 2.5%**" → "Experimental precision is **excellent** (CV < 10% is the industry benchmark)."

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In any KTU 14-mark question on statistical analysis, the **model answer structure** should be:
> 1. Hypotheses (1 mark)
> 2. Computation of CF, SST, SS_Tr, SSE (3 marks)
> 3. ANOVA table with df, MS, F₀ (2 marks)
> 4. Critical value and decision (1 mark)
> 5. Post-hoc test with pairwise comparisons (4 marks)
> 6. Engineering interpretation and recommendation (2 marks)
> 7. Assumption mention (1 mark)

**All the best for your KTU examinations! 🚀**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diagnostic Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diagnostic Checking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Diagnostic Checking** is the systematic post-hoc verification procedure performed after a statistical model (regression or ANOVA) has been fitted to experimental data. Its purpose is to validate the underlying assumptions of the model — namely **normality, independence, homoscedasticity (constant variance), and adequacy of functional form** — so that inferences drawn (p-values, confidence intervals, predictions) remain statistically valid and defensible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "Diagnostic checking is the process of examining the residuals from a fitted model to verify that the assumptions of the statistical analysis (normality, independence, and equal variance) are satisfied."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Checkup" Intuition

Imagine you have just finished building a **car engine** (the statistical model). Before driving it across a country, you must:

| Engineering Step | Statistical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Check oil level & temperature | Test residuals for **normality** |
| Listen for knocking sounds | Test residuals for **independence** (autocorrelation) |
| Ensure all cylinders fire equally | Test residuals for **equal variance** (homoscedasticity) |
| Test the car's handling on hills | Test **lack of fit** vs. pure error |
| Scan for loose bolts | Detect **outliers & influential points** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a doctor runs blood tests *after* a treatment to confirm it worked, a statistician runs diagnostic checks *after* fitting a model to confirm the model is trustworthy. The "blood samples" of a model are the **residuals** — the differences between observed and predicted values.

### 1.3 The Central Role of Residuals

The **residual** for the $i^{th}$ observation is formally defined as:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

where $y_i$ is the observed response and $\hat{y}_i$ is the predicted response from the fitted model. If the model is correct, residuals should behave like **random noise** — centered at zero, normally distributed, independent, and with constant variance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Residuals are the fingerprint of model adequacy.** A correctly specified model leaves residuals that look like pure random error.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Diagnostic Checking

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures diagnostic checking around **four major assumption checks**:

#### Pillar 1 — Normality of Residuals
Residuals should follow a **Normal distribution** $N(0, \sigma^2)$.

- **Why?** ANOVA F-tests and t-tests assume residuals are normally distributed.
- **How to check?**
  - **Graphical:** Normal Probability (Q-Q) plot, Histogram, Box plot
  - **Formal Tests:** Shapiro-Wilk test, Anderson-Darling test, Kolmogorov-Smirnov test
- **Remedies if violated:** Data transformation (log, square root, Box-Cox), non-parametric methods

#### Pillar 2 — Independence of Residuals
Residuals should be **uncorrelated** with each other.

- **Why?** Independence ensures each observation contributes unique information.
- **How to check?**
  - **Graphical:** Plot residuals vs. **run order** (time sequence)
  - **Formal Test:** **Durbin-Watson test** (most common in KTU papers)
- **Remedies if violated:** Time series models (ARIMA), include lagged variables

#### Pillar 3 — Homoscedasticity (Constant Variance)
Residuals should have **equal variance** across all levels of the predictor(s).

- **Why?** Unequal variance invalidates pooled variance estimates and F-tests.
- **How to check?**
  - **Graphical:** Plot residuals vs. fitted values; plot residuals vs. each predictor
  - **Formal Tests:** **Bartlett's test**, **Levene's test**
- **Remedies if violated:** Weighted least squares, variance-stabilizing transformations (log, sqrt)

#### Pillar 4 — Adequacy of Functional Form (Lack of Fit)
The chosen model form (linear, quadratic, etc.) must be **appropriate**.

- **Why?** A misspecified model leads to biased estimates.
- **How to check?**
  - **Formal Test:** **Lack of Fit F-test** (requires replicates)
  - **Remedial:** Add higher-order terms, transform predictors

### 2.2 The Shapiro-Wilk Test (Normality)

For a sample of $n$ residuals sorted in ascending order $e_{(1)} \le e_{(2)} \le \cdots \le e_{(n)}$:

$$W = \frac{\left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i \, e_{(i)} \right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (e_i - \bar{e})^2}$$

where the coefficients $a_i$ are tabulated constants derived from the expected values of the order statistics of a standard normal distribution. The test is valid for $3 \le n \le 50$.

- **Hypotheses:** $H_0$: residuals are normal vs. $H_1$: residuals are not normal
- **Decision:** Reject $H_0$ if $W < W_{\alpha, n}$ (critical value from tables)

### 2.3 The Durbin-Watson Test (Independence)

For ordered residuals $e_1, e_2, \ldots, e_n$:

$$d = \frac{\sum_{i=2}^{n} (e_i - e_{i-1})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2}$$

- Range: $0 \le d \le 4$
- $d \approx 2$ → No autocorrelation (ideal)
- $d < d_L$ → Positive autocorrelation
- $d > 4 - d_L$ → Negative autocorrelation
- $d_L$ and $d_U$ are lower and upper critical values from DW tables

### 2.4 The Lack of Fit F-Test

When **replicates** are available at some design points, the residual sum of squares can be partitioned:

$$SS_{Residual} = SS_{Lack\,of\,Fit} + SS_{Pure\,Error}$$

The test statistic is:

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{SS_{Lack\,of\,Fit} / (k - p)}{SS_{Pure\,Error} / (n - k)}$$

where $k$ = number of distinct design points, $p$ = number of model parameters.

- $H_0$: Model is adequate (no lack of fit)
- $H_1$: Model is inadequate
- Reject $H_0$ if $F_{LoF} > F_{\alpha,\,k-p,\,n-k}$

### 2.5 The Bartlett's Test (Equal Variances)

For $k$ groups with sample sizes $n_i$ and sample variances $s_i^2$:

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(N-k)\ln(s_p^2) - \sum_{i=1}^{k}(n_i - 1)\ln(s_i^2)}{1 + \frac{1}{3(k-1)}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k}\frac{1}{n_i-1} - \frac{1}{N-k}\right]}$$

where $N = \sum n_i$ and $s_p^2 = \frac{\sum (n_i-1)s_i^2}{N-k}$ is the pooled variance.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Test | Statistic Formula | Critical Distribution | Degrees of Freedom | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Shapiro-Wilk** | $W = \frac{(\sum a_i e_{(i)})^2}{\sum e_i^2}$ | Tables of $W$ | $n$ | Reject if $W < W_{\alpha,n}$ |
| **Durbin-Watson** | $d = \frac{\sum (e_i - e_{i-1})^2}{\sum e_i^2}$ | DW Tables | $n$, # predictors | Compare with $d_L, d_U$ |
| **Lack of Fit** | $F_{LoF} = \frac{SS_{LoF}/(k-p)}{SS_{PE}/(n-k)}$ | F-distribution | $(k-p,\, n-k)$ | Reject if $F > F_{\alpha}$ |
| **Bartlett's** | $\chi^2 = \frac{(N-k)\ln s_p^2 - \sum (n_i-1)\ln s_i^2}{1+\frac{1}{3(k-1)}[\sum \frac{1}{n_i-1} - \frac{1}{N-k}]}$ | $\chi^2$ | $k-1$ | Reject if $\chi^2 > \chi^2_{\alpha, k-1}$ |
| **Levene's** | $F = \frac{(N-k)\sum n_i(\bar{Z}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Z})^2}{(k-1)\sum \sum (Z_{ij} - \bar{Z}_{i\cdot})^2}$ | F-distribution | $(k-1, N-k)$ | Reject if $F > F_{\alpha}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **In Levene's test**, $Z_{ij} = \vert y_{ij} - \tilde{y}_i \vert$ where $\tilde{y}_i$ is the median of group $i$ (or mean for Brown-Forsythe variant).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Diagnostic checks on a quality control regression model ensure process capability indices ($C_p, C_{pk}$) are valid.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA requires residual analysis as part of 21 CFR Part 11 validation.
- **Reliability Engineering:** Checking homoscedasticity is critical when modeling Weibull or lognormal lifetimes.
- **Civil Engineering:** Lack of fit testing in concrete strength models prevents under-designed structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Partitioning SSE into Lack of Fit and Pure Error

Consider $n$ experimental runs grouped into $k$ **distinct treatment combinations** (design points). Let $n_i$ be the number of replicates at the $i^{th}$ design point, so $\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i = n$.

**Step 1: Definition of the Total Residual Sum of Squares**

$$SS_{Residual} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (y_{ij} - \hat{y}_{ij})^2$$

**Step 2: Partitioning the Total Variability Within Groups**

For each design point $i$, the deviation of an observation $y_{ij}$ from the **grand mean of that design point** $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$ can be decomposed as:

$$(y_{ij} - \hat{y}_{ij}) = (\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \hat{y}_{ij}) + (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})$$

Squaring and summing over all observations:

$$\sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \hat{y}_{ij})^2 = n_i(\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \hat{y}_{ij})^2 + \sum_{j}(y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2$$

**Step 3: Identifying the Two Components**

- **Pure Error (PE):** Variation *within* replicates of the same design point. It measures the **inherent experimental noise**, independent of the model.

$$SS_{PE} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{j=1}^{n_i} (y_{ij} - \bar{y}_{i\cdot})^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i - 1) s_i^2$$

- **Lack of Fit (LoF):** Variation *between* the design-point mean and the model prediction.

$$SS_{LoF} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i (\bar{y}_{i\cdot} - \hat{y}_{i\cdot})^2$$

**Step 4: Final Partition**

$$SS_{Residual} = SS_{LoF} + SS_{PE}$$

with degrees of freedom: $n - p = (k - p) + (n - k)$, where $p$ = number of model parameters.

**Step 5: Forming the F-Statistic**

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{SS_{LoF} / (k - p)}{SS_{PE} / (n - k)} \sim F_{k-p,\, n-k} \text{ under } H_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rejection of $H_0$** means the systematic discrepancy between group means and fitted values is *larger* than what random noise alone can explain — the model is **inadequate**.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Lack of Fit Test in a 2-Level Factorial with Center Points

**Problem:** A $2^2$ factorial with **3 center point replicates** is run. The fitted model is the first-order (main effects + interaction) model. Test for lack of fit at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Given Data:**

| Design Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $n_i$ | $y$ values | $\bar{y}_{i\cdot}$ | $\hat{y}_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1) | -1 | -1 | 1 | 12.1 | 12.10 | 11.95 |
| a | +1 | -1 | 1 | 15.3 | 15.30 | 15.55 |
| b | -1 | +1 | 1 | 13.7 | 13.70 | 13.45 |
| ab | +1 | +1 | 1 | 17.0 | 17.00 | 17.05 |
| Center | 0 | 0 | 3 | 14.5, 14.9, 14.7 | 14.70 | 14.50 |

**Step 1: Compute Pure Error Sum of Squares**

At the 4 factorial points, $n_i = 1$, so $n_i - 1 = 0$ → no contribution. At the center: $n_i - 1 = 2$:

$$SS_{PE} = (14.5 - 14.7)^2 + (14.9 - 14.7)^2 + (14.7 - 14.7)^2 = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.00 = 0.08$$

Degrees of freedom for PE: $n - k = 7 - 5 = 2$.

**Step 2: Compute Total Residual SS**

$$SS_{Res} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$
$$= (12.1-11.95)^2 + (15.3-15.55)^2 + (13.7-13.45)^2 + (17.0-17.05)^2 + (14.5-14.5)^2 + (14.9-14.5)^2 + (14.7-14.5)^2$$
$$= 0.0225 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 + 0.0025 + 0 + 0.16 + 0.04 = 0.35$$

Degrees of freedom for Res: $n - p = 7 - 3 = 4$.

**Step 3: Compute Lack of Fit SS**

$$SS_{LoF} = SS_{Res} - SS_{PE} = 0.35 - 0.08 = 0.27$$

Degrees of freedom for LoF: $(n - p) - (n - k) = k - p = 5 - 3 = 2$.

**Step 4: Compute Mean Squares**

$$MS_{LoF} = \frac{0.27}{2} = 0.135, \qquad MS_{PE} = \frac{0.08}{2} = 0.04$$

**Step 5: Compute F-Statistic**

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{MS_{LoF}}{MS_{PE}} = \frac{0.135}{0.04} = 3.375$$

**Step 6: Critical Value**

$$F_{0.05,\, 2,\, 2} = 19.00$$

**Step 7: Decision**

Since $F_{LoF} = 3.375 < 19.00$, **fail to reject $H_0$**. The first-order model is **adequate** — no significant lack of fit.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Durbin-Watson Calculation

**Problem:** Five residuals in run order: $e_1 = 0.5, e_2 = -0.3, e_3 = 0.8, e_4 = -0.2, e_5 = 0.1$. Compute the DW statistic.

**Step 1: Numerator**

$$\sum_{i=2}^{5}(e_i - e_{i-1})^2 = (-0.3-0.5)^2 + (0.8-(-0.3))^2 + (-0.2-0.8)^2 + (0.1-(-0.2))^2$$
$$= 0.64 + 1.21 + 1.00 + 0.09 = 2.94$$

**Step 2: Denominator**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5}e_i^2 = 0.25 + 0.09 + 0.64 + 0.04 + 0.01 = 1.03$$

**Step 3: DW Statistic**

$$d = \frac{2.94}{1.03} = 2.854$$

Since $d \approx 2.85$ is close to 2 (slightly above 2 suggests mild negative autocorrelation), with $n=5$ and 1 predictor, the DW tables would be consulted for the final conclusion. With such a small sample, the test is inconclusive, illustrating a **practical limitation** of DW with small datasets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagnostic Checking Workflow (Master Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fitted Statistical Model] --> B[Compute Residuals e_i = y_i - y_hat_i]
    B --> C{Pillar 1: Normality Check}
    C --> C1[Q-Q Plot]
    C --> C2[Histogram]
    C --> C3[Shapiro-Wilk Test]
    C --> D{Pillar 2: Independence Check}
    D --> D1[Residual vs Order Plot]
    D --> D2[Durbin-Watson Test]
    D --> E{Pillar 3: Equal Variance Check}
    E --> E1[Residual vs Fitted Plot]
    E --> E2[Bartlett's Test]
    E --> E3[Levene's Test]
    E --> F{Pillar 4: Model Adequacy}
    F --> F1[Lack of Fit F-Test]
    F --> F2[Outlier Detection Cook's Distance]
    C1 --> G{All Assumptions Met?}
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Model is Valid - Proceed with Inference]
    G -->|No| I[Apply Remedies: Transform Data, Add Terms, Use Robust Methods]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Residual Diagnostic Logic Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Residual Diagnostic Suite] --> R1[Visual Diagnostics]
    R --> R2[Formal Statistical Tests]
    R1 --> R1a[Q-Q Plot for Normality]
    R1 --> R1b[Residuals vs Fitted for Homoscedasticity]
    R1 --> R1c[Residuals vs Order for Independence]
    R2 --> R2a[Shapiro-Wilk - Normality]
    R2 --> R2b[Durbin-Watson - Autocorrelation]
    R2 --> R2c[Bartlett and Levene - Variance Equality]
    R2 --> R2d[Lack of Fit F - Functional Form]
    R1a --> DEC{Accept Model?}
    R1b --> DEC
    R1c --> DEC
    R2a --> DEC
    R2b --> DEC
    R2c --> DEC
    R2d --> DEC
    DEC -->|All Pass| ACC[Model Adequate]
    DEC -->|Any Fail| REJ[Diagnose and Remediate]
```

### 4.3 Partitioning of Sum of Squares (Lack of Fit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SS_TOT[SS Total] --> SS_REG[SS Regression]
    SS_TOT --> SS_RES[SS Residual]
    SS_RES --> SS_LOF[SS Lack of Fit]
    SS_RES --> SS_PE[SS Pure Error]
    SS_LOF --> MS_LOF[MS LoF = SS LoF / k-p]
    SS_PE --> MS_PE[MS PE = SS PE / n-k]
    MS_LOF --> FTEST[F LoF = MS LoF / MS PE]
    MS_PE --> FTEST
    FTEST --> COMPARE{Compare with F alpha k-p, n-k}
    COMPARE -->|Reject H0| ADEQ[Model Inadequate]
    COMPARE -->|Fail to Reject| OK[Model Adequate]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**  
*What is meant by diagnostic checking in the context of experimental design? List any two diagnostic tools used for checking the adequacy of a fitted model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Diagnostic checking refers to the post-fitting verification procedure used to confirm that the statistical assumptions underlying an ANOVA or regression model are satisfied by the observed data. It ensures that the inferences derived (such as p-values, confidence intervals, and predictions) are statistically valid. **Two diagnostic tools** are:  
(i) **Residual plots** (residuals vs. fitted values, normal probability plot) — graphical method.  
(ii) **Formal statistical tests** such as the Shapiro-Wilk test (normality) and the Lack of Fit F-test (functional form).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for naming two tools = 3 marks)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**  
*State the null and alternative hypotheses for the Lack of Fit F-test. Under what experimental condition is this test applicable?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
**Hypotheses:**  
$H_0$: The fitted model is adequate (no lack of fit)  
$H_1$: The fitted model is inadequate (significant lack of fit exists)  

**Condition:** This test is applicable **only when replicates are available** at one or more design points, so that the pure error can be estimated separately from the lack of fit component.  
Test statistic: $F_{LoF} = \frac{MS_{LoF}}{MS_{PE}}$ with degrees of freedom $(k-p, n-k)$, where $k$ = distinct design points and $p$ = number of model parameters.

*(Award 1 mark for hypotheses, 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for statistic = 3 marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 4 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the four major assumptions that must be verified through diagnostic checking of a fitted model. For each assumption, mention one graphical and one formal test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following residuals (in run order) were obtained from a regression analysis:  
$e_1 = 1.2, e_2 = 0.8, e_3 = -0.5, e_4 = -1.1, e_5 = 0.3, e_6 = -0.7$.  
Compute the Durbin-Watson statistic and comment on the independence of residuals. Use $\alpha = 0.05$ with $n=6$ and 1 predictor (from DW tables: $d_L = 0.61, d_U = 1.40$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

| Assumption | Graphical Test | Formal Test | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Normality** | Normal Q-Q plot / Histogram | Shapiro-Wilk test | 1.5 |
| **2. Independence** | Residuals vs. Run Order plot | Durbin-Watson test | 1.5 |
| **3. Homoscedasticity** (Equal Variance) | Residuals vs. Fitted Values plot | Bartlett's / Levene's test | 2.0 |
| **4. Adequacy of Functional Form** | Residual vs. Predictor plot | Lack of Fit F-test | 2.0 |

**Discussion (detailed, 7 marks):**

**(i) Normality:** Residuals should follow $N(0, \sigma^2)$. Visualized using a Q-Q plot (points should lie on a straight line). Formally tested using the Shapiro-Wilk test, which computes:

$$W = \frac{(\sum a_i e_{(i)})^2}{\sum e_i^2}$$

If $W$ is too small, normality is rejected.

**(ii) Independence:** Residuals should be uncorrelated. Plotted against run order; random scatter indicates independence. Tested using Durbin-Watson:

$$d = \frac{\sum_{i=2}^{n}(e_i - e_{i-1})^2}{\sum e_i^2}$$

**(iii) Homoscedasticity:** The variance of residuals should be constant across all fitted values. A funnel-shaped pattern in the residuals-vs-fitted plot indicates heteroscedasticity. Tested using Bartlett's test (sensitive to normality) or Levene's test (more robust).

**(iv) Adequacy of Functional Form:** The chosen model (linear, quadratic, etc.) should capture the underlying trend. When replicates exist, the Lack of Fit F-test is used:

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{MS_{LoF}}{MS_{PE}}$$

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Tabulate consecutive differences**

| $i$ | $e_i$ | $e_{i-1}$ | $e_i - e_{i-1}$ | $(e_i - e_{i-1})^2$ | $e_i^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.2 | — | — | — | 1.44 |
| 2 | 0.8 | 1.2 | -0.4 | 0.16 | 0.64 |
| 3 | -0.5 | 0.8 | -1.3 | 1.69 | 0.25 |
| 4 | -1.1 | -0.5 | -0.6 | 0.36 | 1.21 |
| 5 | 0.3 | -1.1 | 1.4 | 1.96 | 0.09 |
| 6 | -0.7 | 0.3 | -1.0 | 1.00 | 0.49 |
| | | | **Sum** | **5.17** | **4.12** |

**Step 2: Compute DW statistic**

$$d = \frac{5.17}{4.12} = 1.255$$

**Step 3: Decision**

With $n = 6$, 1 predictor, and $\alpha = 0.05$: $d_L = 0.61$, $d_U = 1.40$.

Since $d_L < d = 1.255 < d_U = 1.40$, the test is **inconclusive**. We cannot definitively conclude the presence or absence of positive autocorrelation at the 5% level. A larger sample size or different test (e.g., runs test) is recommended.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 4 Choice Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the Lack of Fit F-test with its hypotheses, test statistic, and ANOVA partition. Why is the presence of replicates essential for this test? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $2^2$ factorial experiment with 3 center point replicates was conducted. The fitted first-order model has $p = 3$ parameters. The following sums of squares are computed: $SS_{Total} = 50.0$, $SS_{Model} = 42.0$, $SS_{Pure\,Error} = 1.2$. If $k = 5$ distinct design points, $n = 7$ total runs, perform the Lack of Fit F-test at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use $F_{0.05, 2, 2} = 19.00$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Solution (7 marks):**

The Lack of Fit (LoF) F-test determines whether the systematic variation in the data is fully captured by the chosen model. It is essentially an extra-sum-of-squares test that isolates the **deterministic misfit** of the model from the **inherent experimental noise**.

**Hypotheses:**  
$H_0$: Model is adequate ($\beta$-coefficients capture all systematic variation)  
$H_1$: Model is inadequate (systematic variation remains unexplained)

**ANOVA Partition:**

$$SS_{Total} = SS_{Model} + SS_{Residual} = SS_{Model} + SS_{LoF} + SS_{PE}$$

**Test Statistic:**

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{SS_{LoF} / (k-p)}{SS_{PE} / (n-k)}$$

where $k$ = number of distinct design points and $p$ = number of model parameters.

**Degrees of Freedom:**  
- $df_{LoF} = k - p$  
- $df_{PE} = n - k$  
- $df_{Residual} = n - p$

**Why replicates are essential:** Without replicates, $n = k$, which gives $df_{PE} = n - k = 0$. Pure error cannot be estimated (every design point is observed exactly once), and the LoF test becomes mathematically impossible. Replicates provide an independent estimate of experimental noise against which the systematic misfit is compared.

---

**(b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**  
$SS_{Model} = 42.0$, $SS_{Total} = 50.0$, $SS_{PE} = 1.2$, $p = 3$, $k = 5$, $n = 7$, $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 1: Compute $SS_{Residual}$**

$$SS_{Residual} = SS_{Total} - SS_{Model} = 50.0 - 42.0 = 8.0$$

**Step 2: Compute $SS_{LoF}$**

$$SS_{LoF} = SS_{Residual} - SS_{PE} = 8.0 - 1.2 = 6.8$$

**Step 3: Degrees of Freedom**

$$df_{LoF} = k - p = 5 - 3 = 2$$
$$df_{PE} = n - k = 7 - 5 = 2$$

**Step 4: Mean Squares**

$$MS_{LoF} = \frac{6.8}{2} = 3.4$$
$$MS_{PE} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6$$

**Step 5: F-Statistic**

$$F_{LoF} = \frac{3.4}{0.6} = 5.667$$

**Step 6: Critical Value and Decision**

$$F_{0.05,\, 2,\, 2} = 19.00$$

Since $F_{LoF} = 5.667 < 19.00$, **fail to reject $H_0$**. The first-order model is **adequate** at the 5% significance level.

**ANOVA Summary Table (for full marks):**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Model | 42.0 | 3 | 14.0 | — |
| Lack of Fit | 6.8 | 2 | 3.4 | 5.67 |
| Pure Error | 1.2 | 2 | 0.6 | — |
| Total | 50.0 | 6 | — | — |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $p$ and $k$:** Many students swap the degrees of freedom formulas. Remember: $df_{LoF} = k - p$ (design points minus parameters), $df_{PE} = n - k$ (total minus design points).
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract $SS_{PE}$ from $SS_{Res}$:** A common error is using $SS_{Res}$ as the numerator in the LoF F-statistic instead of $SS_{LoF}$.
> 3. **Misapplying the Durbin-Watson rule:** The inconclusive region ($d_L < d < d_U$) is frequently ignored. Always check all three regions: $< d_L$, between $d_L$ and $d_U$, and $> 4 - d_U$.
> 4. **Using Bartlett's test on non-normal data:** Bartlett's test is **highly sensitive to non-normality**. If normality is violated, prefer Levene's test (more robust).
> 5. **Mixing up "Lack of Fit" direction:** A *small* F-statistic means good fit; a *large* F-statistic means poor fit. Some students reverse this.
> 6. **Not stating the critical value:** KTU examiners typically deduct 1 mark when the critical F-value (or p-value) is not explicitly mentioned in the decision step.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Diagnostic checking = post-fitting verification of model assumptions using residuals.
- **Core Tool:** Residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ — they are the "fingerprint" of model adequacy.
- **Four Pillars:**
  - **Normality** → Shapiro-Wilk test, Q-Q plot
  - **Independence** → Durbin-Watson test, residuals vs. order
  - **Homoscedasticity** → Bartlett's / Levene's test, residuals vs. fitted
  - **Adequacy of Form** → Lack of Fit F-test
- **Lack of Fit F-statistic:** $F = \frac{SS_{LoF}/(k-p)}{SS_{PE}/(n-k)}$
- **DW Statistic Range:** $0 \le d \le 4$; $d \approx 2$ is ideal
- **Bartlett's Test:** Sensitive to non-normality; use Levene's as a robust alternative
- **Shapiro-Wilk:** Valid only for $3 \le n \le 50$; use Anderson-Darling for larger samples
- **Replicates are mandatory** for the Lack of Fit test — without them, $df_{PE} = 0$.
- **Transformation remedies:** Log, square root, Box-Cox for variance issues; differencing for autocorrelation.
- **Decision rule for LoF:** Reject $H_0$ (model inadequate) if $F_{LoF} > F_{\alpha,\, k-p,\, n-k}$.
- **Decision rule for DW (positive autocorrelation):** Reject independence if $d < d_L$.
- **Outlier detection complements** diagnostic checking — use Cook's distance, leverage ($h_{ii}$), DFFITS.
- **Visual + Formal = Robust:** Always pair graphical plots with formal tests for comprehensive validation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variance Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variance Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Variance Components: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance Components** are the portions of the total observed variability in an experimental response that are attributable to each random source (factor, interaction, or error) in a **Random Effects Model** (also called a *Model II* or *Components-of-Variance Model*). They are estimated using the **Expected Mean Squares (EMS)** derived from the corresponding ANOVA table, allowing the experimenter to *quantify* how much variation each factor contributes to the total process variance.

Mathematically, for a general linear random-effects model

$$Y = \mu + Z_1 u_1 + Z_2 u_2 + \dots + Z_k u_k + \varepsilon$$

where each random vector $u_i$ has $\mathrm{E}(u_i) = 0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(u_i) = \sigma_i^2 I$, and $\varepsilon$ has $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2 I$, the **total response variance** is the sum of all the **variance components**:

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \dots + \sigma_k^2 + \sigma^2$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍕

Imagine you bake pizzas in **5 different ovens** in your factory. You want to know: *"Why aren't all pizzas the same weight?"* The total weight variation among pizzas can be split into:

- **Oven-to-oven variation** (some ovens run hotter/colder) → component $\sigma_{\text{oven}}^2$
- **Within-oven variation** (uneven dough, humidity, operator timing) → component $\sigma^2$

A **variance component** simply tells you the *percentage* of total weight-fluctuation that comes from each source. If 80% comes from oven differences, you'd better **fix your ovens** rather than retrain the workers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** When the factor levels are a *random sample* from a larger population (e.g., 5 randomly selected ovens out of 50 in the company), we use a **Random Effects Model** and estimate variance components. This is the precise scenario tested in PEPET525.

## 1.3 Why Random Effects & Not Fixed Effects?

| Aspect | Fixed Effects (Model I) | Random Effects (Model II) |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Compare *specific* treatment means | Estimate *variability* due to factor |
| Factor levels | Deliberately chosen | Random sample from a population |
| Inference | About the chosen levels only | About the entire population of levels |
| Output | Treatment mean differences | **Variance components** $\sigma_i^2$ |
| KTU Use | Most Module 2/3 designs | **Module 4 — this chapter** |

## 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Random effect**: a factor whose levels are randomly drawn from a large population.
- **Expected Mean Square (EMS)**: $\mathrm{E}(\text{MS}_i) = $ linear combination of variance components.
- **ANOVA estimator**: variance component obtained by equating observed MS to its EMS and solving.
- **Negative estimate**: when MS of factor < MS of error; set to 0 in practice.
- **Intraclass correlation (ICC)**: $\rho = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{between}}^2}{\sigma_{\text{between}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{within}}^2}$, measure of within-group homogeneity.
- **Confidence interval (CI)**: interval estimate for a variance component using $\chi^2$ distribution (Satterthwaite approximation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Module 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General Random-Effects Model

For a balanced design with $a$ factor levels and $n$ replicates per level (one-way case):

$$Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$$

- $i = 1, 2, \dots, a$ (levels of the random factor)
- $j = 1, 2, \dots, n$ (replications within level $i$)
- $\tau_i \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\tau}^2)$ — random effect
- $\varepsilon_{ij} \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ — random error
- $\tau_i$ and $\varepsilon_{ij}$ are mutually **independent**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Total variability of any observation: $\sigma_Y^2 = \sigma_{\tau}^2 + \sigma^2$. The **goal** of variance component estimation is to separate these two.

## 2.2 ANOVA Partitioning for One-Way Random Model

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Treatments (Between) | $SS_{Tr} = n \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2$ | $a-1$ | $MS_{Tr} = \dfrac{SS_{Tr}}{a-1}$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ |
| Error (Within) | $SS_E = \sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot})^2$ | $a(n-1)$ | $MS_E = \dfrac{SS_E}{a(n-1)}$ | $\sigma^2$ |
| **Total** | $SS_T$ | $an - 1$ | — | — |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ⭐

> [!NOTE]
> Use this cheat sheet for all Module-4 numerical problems. Memorize the EMS column and the estimator column.

| Model / Source | EMS Expression | ANOVA Estimator | % of Total Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| One-Way — Treatments | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\tau}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n}$ | $\dfrac{\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2}{\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \hat\sigma^2}$ |
| One-Way — Error | $\sigma^2$ | $\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E$ | $\dfrac{\hat\sigma^2}{\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \hat\sigma^2}$ |
| Two-Way (Random) — A | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + bn\sigma_{\alpha}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\alpha}^2 = \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{bn}$ | — |
| Two-Way (Random) — B | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + an\sigma_{\beta}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\beta}^2 = \dfrac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an}$ | — |
| Two-Way (Random) — AB | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n}$ | — |
| Two-Way (Random) — Error | $\sigma^2$ | $\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E$ | — |
| Nested (B within A) — A | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\beta(A)}^2 + bn\sigma_{\alpha}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\alpha}^2 = \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn}$ | — |
| Nested — B(A) | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\beta(A)}^2$ | $\hat\sigma_{\beta(A)}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n}$ | — |
| Nested — Error | $\sigma^2$ | $\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E$ | — |

> **Standard notation:** In the two-way random model $Y_{ijk} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$, the indexing is $i = 1,\dots,a$; $j = 1,\dots,b$; $k = 1,\dots,n$.

## 2.4 Confidence Intervals (Satterthwaite / Satterthwaite–Welch)

For a single variance component whose EMS is a linear combination of components, the **approximate 95% CI** is

$$L = \dfrac{df \cdot MS}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\, df}}, \qquad U = \dfrac{df \cdot MS}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\, df}}$$

where the **effective degrees of freedom** are computed by Satterthwaite's formula:

$$df \approx \dfrac{(\text{EMS})^2}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{(c_i \, MS_i)^2}{df_i + 2}}$$

## 2.5 Intraclass Correlation Coefficient (ICC)

For the one-way random model:

$$\hat\rho = \dfrac{\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2}{\hat\sigma_{\tau}^2 + \hat\sigma^2} = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{MS_{Tr} + (n-1)MS_E}$$

- $\hat\rho \approx 0$ → groups are heterogeneous (no clustering).
- $\hat\rho \approx 1$ → observations within a group are nearly identical.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Variance Components

- **Process Control (SPC):** Identify the largest source of variability, then attack it (Six-Sigma DMAIC).
- **Reliability Engineering:** Partition failure times into batch, operator, machine, and unit components.
- **Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA):** Quantify *Gage R&R* (repeatability + reproducibility) vs part-to-part.
- **Mixed Models in Agriculture/Biotech:** Estimate heritability $H^2 = \sigma_G^2 / (\sigma_G^2 + \sigma_E^2/n)$.
- **Quality Engineering:** Determine whether tightening a supplier (factor) or tightening inspection (error) will reduce product variance more.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 Module 4 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** All derivations shown below are in the *exact* step pattern expected in the 14-mark Part-B solutions. Show **every** algebraic step; partial credit is given liberally when the logic is clear.

## 3.1 Derivation: EMS for One-Way Random Model

**Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \varepsilon_{ij}$ with $i = 1,\dots,a$, $j = 1,\dots,n$.

### Step 1 — Define the cell mean

$$\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \bar{\varepsilon}_{i\cdot}$$

### Step 2 — Variance of the cell mean

Since $\tau_i \sim (0,\sigma_\tau^2)$ and $\bar\varepsilon_{i\cdot} \sim (0,\sigma^2/n)$ are independent:

$$\mathrm{Var}(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot}) = \sigma_\tau^2 + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}$$

### Step 3 — Construct the Treatments MS

$$MS_{Tr} = \dfrac{n \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{Y}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{\cdot\cdot})^2}{a-1}$$

The numerator is $n$ times a sum of squared independent normal variates each with variance $(\sigma_\tau^2 + \sigma^2/n)$. Hence:

$$\mathrm{E}(MS_{Tr}) = n \left(\sigma_\tau^2 + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{n}\right) = n\sigma_\tau^2 + \sigma^2$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Error MS

$$MS_E = \dfrac{\sum_i \sum_j (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_{i\cdot})^2}{a(n-1)}$$

This is the pooled within-cell variance, so

$$\mathrm{E}(MS_E) = \sigma^2$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the variance components

Equate observed $MS$ to expected $MS$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat\sigma^2 &= MS_E \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_\tau^2 &= \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** $\hat\sigma_\tau^2$ is the **between-group** variance (factor contribution) and $\hat\sigma^2$ is the **within-group** variance (unexplained error). If $MS_{Tr} < MS_E$, then $\hat\sigma_\tau^2 < 0$; the convention is to **report 0** and conclude that the factor contributes negligibly.

## 3.2 Derivation: EMS for Two-Way Random Model (No Replicates? Use Interaction)

**Model:** $Y_{ij} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_j + (\alpha\beta)_{ij} + \varepsilon_{ij}$ with $i=1,\dots,a$, $j=1,\dots,b$ (a *single* replicate per cell, $n=1$). Use this when $ab > df$ left over.

### Step 1 — Compute variance of a row mean $\bar Y_{i\cdot}$

$$\mathrm{Var}(\bar Y_{i\cdot}) = \sigma_\alpha^2 + \dfrac{\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2}{b} + \dfrac{\sigma^2}{b}$$

### Step 2 — Expected MS for Factor A

$$MS_A = \dfrac{b \sum_i (\bar Y_{i\cdot} - \bar Y_{\cdot\cdot})^2}{a-1} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathrm{E}(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + b\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + b\sigma_\alpha^2$$

### Step 3 — Expected MS for Factor B (by symmetry)

$$\mathrm{E}(MS_B) = \sigma^2 + a\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + a\sigma_\beta^2$$

### Step 4 — Expected MS for AB interaction

$$\mathrm{E}(MS_{AB}) = \sigma^2 + \sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$$

### Step 5 — Solve the triangular system

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat\sigma^2 &= MS_E \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 &= \dfrac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{1} \quad (\text{since } n=1) \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_\alpha^2 &= \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{b} \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_\beta^2 &= \dfrac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{a}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the *replicated* two-way model (each cell has $n$ replicates, indices $i,j,k$ with $k=1,\dots,n$), the same pattern holds **with the extra $n$ multiplier** on the high-order terms — see Section 2.3.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nested Random Model

**Model:** $Y_{ijk} = \mu + A_i + B_{j(i)} + \varepsilon_{ijk}$ with $i=1,\dots,a$, $j=1,\dots,b$, $k=1,\dots,n$. Here $B_{j(i)}$ is the $j$-th sub-level **nested within** level $i$ of factor $A$.

### Step 1 — Variance decomposition

$$\sigma_Y^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_{B(A)}^2 + \sigma^2$$

### Step 2 — Expected Mean Squares

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{E}(MS_A) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{B(A)}^2 + bn\sigma_A^2 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{E}(MS_{B(A)}) &= \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{B(A)}^2 \\[4pt]
\mathrm{E}(MS_E) &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Solve

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat\sigma^2 &= MS_E \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_{B(A)}^2 &= \dfrac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_E}{n} \\[4pt]
\hat\sigma_A^2 &= \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{B(A)}}{bn}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (One-Way, KTU Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — June 2023]:** A quality engineer measures the diameter (mm) of a critical shaft from 4 randomly selected lathes, with 5 shafts measured per lathe. The ANOVA yields $MS_{Tr} = 12.80$ and $MS_E = 4.50$. Estimate the variance components, the total variance, and the intraclass correlation.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the model parameters**

$$a = 4 \text{ lathes},\quad n = 5 \text{ shafts/lathe},\quad MS_{Tr}=12.80,\quad MS_E=4.50$$

**Step 2 — Estimate the error (within-lathe) component**

$$\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E = 4.50 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Estimate the between-lathe component**

$$\hat\sigma_\tau^2 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n} = \dfrac{12.80 - 4.50}{5} = \dfrac{8.30}{5} = 1.66 \; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Total variance and standard deviation**

$$\hat\sigma_Y^2 = 1.66 + 4.50 = 6.16 \; \text{mm}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \hat\sigma_Y = \sqrt{6.16} \approx 2.48 \; \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Intraclass correlation (fraction of variance due to lathes)**

$$\hat\rho = \dfrac{\hat\sigma_\tau^2}{\hat\sigma_\tau^2 + \hat\sigma^2} = \dfrac{1.66}{6.16} \approx 0.2695 \;(26.95\%)$$

> **Conclusion:** About **27%** of the total diameter variation comes from *lathe-to-lathe* differences; the remaining **73%** is *within-lathe* variation. The engineer should focus process improvement on individual lathes (calibration, tooling) rather than treat lathes as identical.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Two-Way Random Model

> **Problem [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]:** A study uses $a=3$ randomly chosen operators and $b=4$ randomly chosen machines, with $n=2$ replicates per cell. From the ANOVA: $MS_A = 58.0$, $MS_B = 42.0$, $MS_{AB} = 9.0$, $MS_E = 4.0$. Estimate all variance components.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Variance of error**

$$\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E = 4.0$$

**Step 2 — Operator × Machine interaction**

$$\hat\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n} = \dfrac{9.0 - 4.0}{2} = 2.5$$

**Step 3 — Operator main effect**

$$\hat\sigma_\alpha^2 = \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{bn} = \dfrac{58.0 - 9.0}{4 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{49.0}{8} = 6.125$$

**Step 4 — Machine main effect**

$$\hat\sigma_\beta^2 = \dfrac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an} = \dfrac{42.0 - 9.0}{3 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{33.0}{6} = 5.5$$

**Step 5 — Total variance and percentage decomposition**

$$\begin{aligned}
\hat\sigma_Y^2 &= 6.125 + 5.5 + 2.5 + 4.0 = 18.125 \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Operator} &= \dfrac{6.125}{18.125} \times 100 \approx 33.79\% \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Machine} &= \dfrac{5.5}{18.125} \times 100 \approx 30.34\% \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Interaction} &= \dfrac{2.5}{18.125} \times 100 \approx 13.79\% \\[4pt]
\% \text{ Error} &= \dfrac{4.0}{18.125} \times 100 \approx 22.07\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Operator and machine each contribute ~⅓ of variance; the interaction is moderate (≈14%); the residual unexplained error is ~22%. Both operator training *and* machine maintenance are necessary.

## 3.6 Negative Variance Component — What To Do

If any estimator produces a negative value, the convention is:

1. **Set it to zero**: $\hat\sigma_i^2 \leftarrow \max\!\left(0, \text{formula value}\right)$.
2. **Re-test with a better design** (more replicates, blocking).
3. **Use REML** (Restricted Maximum Likelihood) — it is *less* prone to negative estimates than ANOVA Method I.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 Module 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow of Variance Component Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Random Effects Model Specified] --> B[Construct ANOVA Table for Design]
    B --> C[Compute SS and MS for each Source]
    C --> D[Derive Expected Mean Squares EMS using Random-Effect Rules]
    D --> E{Equate Observed MS to EMS}
    E --> F[Solve the Triangular System of Linear Equations]
    F --> G{Any Negative Estimate?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Set Negative Components to Zero - Report 0]
    G -- No --> I[Keep Positive Estimates]
    H --> J[Compute Total Variance and Percentage Contribution]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute ICC and Confidence Intervals]
    K --> L[Engineering Interpretation and Action]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — One-Way Random Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Total Observation Y sub ij] --> nodeB[Overall Mean mu]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Random Effect tau sub i]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Random Error epsilon sub ij]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Between Variance sigma tau squared]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Within Variance sigma squared]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Total Variance sigma Y squared]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[ANOVA Estimators]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Variance Component 1: hat sigma tau squared]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Variance Component 2: hat sigma squared]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Intraclass Correlation ICC]
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.3 Nested vs Crossed Random Models — Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS[Identify Factor Structure] --> nodeQ1{Levels of Factor B appear in only one level of A?}
    nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeN[Nested Model Y sub ijk equals mu plus A sub i plus B sub j of i plus epsilon]
    nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeC[Crossed Model Y sub ijk equals mu plus alpha sub i plus beta sub j plus alpha-beta sub ij plus epsilon]
    nodeN --> nodeN1[EMS: A component has sigma squared plus n sigma B A squared plus b n sigma A squared]
    nodeN --> nodeN2[EMS: B A component has sigma squared plus n sigma B A squared]
    nodeN --> nodeN3[EMS: Error has sigma squared]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[EMS: Factor A has sigma squared plus n sigma AB squared plus b n sigma alpha squared]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[EMS: Factor B has sigma squared plus n sigma AB squared plus a n sigma beta squared]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[EMS: Interaction AB has sigma squared plus n sigma AB squared]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[EMS: Error has sigma squared]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — From Data to Action

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Collect Balanced Data] --> stepB[Verify Random Sampling Assumption]
    stepB --> stepC[Run ANOVA in Software or by Hand]
    stepC --> stepD[Tabulate MS Values]
    stepD --> stepE[Apply EMS Solver Formulas]
    stepE --> stepF[Check for Negative Variance Components]
    stepF --> stepG[Set Negatives to Zero if Found]
    stepG --> stepH[Compute Total Variance and Percentages]
    stepH --> stepI[Compute ICC for Clustering Strength]
    stepI --> stepJ[Identify Largest Variance Source]
    stepJ --> stepK[Recommend Process Improvement to Target Largest Source]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 Module 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define variance components. When is a random-effects model used instead of a fixed-effects model?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Variance components are the portions of the total observed response variance that are attributed to each random source in a random-effects (Model II) ANOVA.* A random-effects model is used when the factor levels are a *random sample* from a larger population and the goal is to estimate the *variability* contributed by the factor — not to compare specific means. *Example: estimating variability due to 5 randomly selected batches out of 100 produced.*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the Expected Mean Square (EMS)? State the EMS for treatments and error in a one-way random-effects model.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *The EMS is the long-run average value of a Mean Square, expressed as a linear combination of variance components.* For a one-way random model with $a$ levels and $n$ replicates:
> $$\mathrm{E}(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\tau^2, \qquad \mathrm{E}(MS_E) = \sigma^2$$
> The factor-level variance is then estimated as $\hat\sigma_\tau^2 = (MS_{Tr} - MS_E)/n$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A textile mill studies yarn strength. **5 looms** are randomly selected from a large population, and **4 yarn samples** are tested from each loom. The ANOVA table is:

| Source | df | SS | MS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Looms | 4 | 36.00 | 9.00 |
| Error | 15 | 30.00 | 2.00 |
| Total | 19 | 66.00 | — |

**(a)** Estimate the variance components $\sigma^2$ and $\sigma_\tau^2$ and the total variance. Compute the percentage contribution of loom-to-loom variation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the intraclass correlation coefficient. Interpret its value in the context of yarn-strength clustering within a loom. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**Part (a) — Variance Component Estimation (7 marks)**

Step 1: Identify parameters. $a=5$ looms, $n=4$ samples/loom, $MS_{Tr}=9.00$, $MS_E=2.00$.

Step 2: Estimate the within-loom (error) variance component:

$$\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E = 2.00$$

Step 3: Estimate the between-loom variance component:

$$\hat\sigma_\tau^2 = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{n} = \dfrac{9.00 - 2.00}{4} = \dfrac{7.00}{4} = 1.75$$

Step 4: Total variance:

$$\hat\sigma_Y^2 = \hat\sigma_\tau^2 + \hat\sigma^2 = 1.75 + 2.00 = 3.75$$

Step 5: Percentage contribution of loom-to-loom variation:

$$\%\text{Loom} = \dfrac{1.75}{3.75} \times 100 \approx 46.67\%$$

$$\%\text{Within Loom} = \dfrac{2.00}{3.75} \times 100 \approx 53.33\%$$

**Part (b) — Intraclass Correlation (7 marks)**

Step 1: Apply the ICC formula for the one-way random model:

$$\hat\rho = \dfrac{MS_{Tr} - MS_E}{MS_{Tr} + (n-1)MS_E} = \dfrac{9.00 - 2.00}{9.00 + (4-1)(2.00)} = \dfrac{7.00}{9.00 + 6.00} = \dfrac{7.00}{15.00} = 0.4667$$

Step 2: Interpretation. An ICC of $\approx 0.467$ means that **~47% of the total variance** in yarn strength is attributable to *differences between looms*. Yarn samples from the *same* loom are moderately similar (clustered) — fixing the looms (calibration, tension, maintenance) will substantially reduce overall yarn-strength variability.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A manufacturing engineer investigates surface roughness (Ra) of turned shafts. **3 operators** (A) and **4 machines** (B) are randomly chosen, with **2 replicates** per operator–machine cell. The ANOVA yields:

| Source | df | MS |
|---|---|---|
| Operator (A) | 2 | 60.0 |
| Machine (B) | 3 | 45.0 |
| A×B Interaction | 6 | 8.0 |
| Error | 12 | 5.0 |
| Total | 23 | — |

**(a)** Estimate all four variance components ($\sigma^2$, $\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2$, $\sigma_\alpha^2$, $\sigma_\beta^2$) and the total variance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the percentage contribution of each source. State which source dominates and recommend a process-improvement action. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**Part (a) — Variance Component Estimation (7 marks)**

Step 1: Identify parameters. $a=3$, $b=4$, $n=2$. Read MS values: $MS_A=60.0$, $MS_B=45.0$, $MS_{AB}=8.0$, $MS_E=5.0$.

Step 2: Error variance:

$$\hat\sigma^2 = MS_E = 5.0$$

Step 3: Operator × Machine interaction:

$$\hat\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 = \dfrac{MS_{AB} - MS_E}{n} = \dfrac{8.0 - 5.0}{2} = 1.5$$

Step 4: Operator (A) main effect:

$$\hat\sigma_\alpha^2 = \dfrac{MS_A - MS_{AB}}{bn} = \dfrac{60.0 - 8.0}{4 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{52.0}{8} = 6.5$$

Step 5: Machine (B) main effect:

$$\hat\sigma_\beta^2 = \dfrac{MS_B - MS_{AB}}{an} = \dfrac{45.0 - 8.0}{3 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{37.0}{6} \approx 6.1667$$

Step 6: Total variance:

$$\hat\sigma_Y^2 = 6.5 + 6.1667 + 1.5 + 5.0 = 19.1667$$

**Part (b) — Percentage Decomposition & Recommendation (7 marks)**

Step 1: Compute percentages:

$$\% \text{ Operator} = \dfrac{6.5}{19.1667} \times 100 \approx 33.91\%$$

$$\% \text{ Machine} = \dfrac{6.1667}{19.1667} \times 100 \approx 32.17\%$$

$$\% \text{ Interaction} = \dfrac{1.5}{19.1667} \times 100 \approx 7.83\%$$

$$\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{5.0}{19.1667} \times 100 \approx 26.09\%$$

Step 2: Dominant source. **Operator (~34%)** is the largest single contributor, narrowly followed by **Machine (~32%)**. The interaction is small, and the residual error is moderate.

Step 3: Recommendation. Implement a **two-pronged action plan**:
- **Operator training program** (standardize feed rate, tool-handling, finishing passes) — addresses the largest source.
- **Machine preventive maintenance** (spindle runout check, tool-wear compensation) — addresses the second-largest source.
- Interaction is small, so combined operator-machine effects are minimal; **focus on the main effects separately**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Variance-Component questions (PEPET525):**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $n$ (or $bn$, $an$) in the estimator formula** — most common error; loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Treating a random factor as fixed** — applies the *wrong EMS* (fixed EMS has only the highest-order component). Check whether the question says *"randomly selected"* or *"randomly chosen"*.
> 3. **Reporting a negative variance component as negative** — must be set to **zero**; failing to do so loses a mark in interpretation.
> 4. **Confusing nested and crossed models** — nested: $B$ levels appear in *only one* level of $A$; crossed: each $B$ level appears with *every* $A$ level. EMS formulas differ!
> 5. **Forgetting total variance** — examiners expect $\sigma_Y^2 = \sum \hat\sigma_i^2$ *and* a percentage decomposition in 14-mark problems.
> 6. **Skipping the interpretation step** — always end with a 1–2 line engineering recommendation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this box. Memorize the EMS table. Practice at least 3 numericals before the exam.**

- ✅ Variance components partition the **total response variance** into contributions from each random factor, interaction, and error.
- ✅ Use **random-effects model** when factor levels are a *random sample*; goal is to estimate variability, not compare means.
- ✅ The **ANOVA Method** (Method of Moments) equates observed $MS$ to its EMS and solves a *triangular* linear system.
- ✅ **One-way EMS**: $E(MS_{Tr}) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_\tau^2$; estimator $\hat\sigma_\tau^2 = (MS_{Tr}-MS_E)/n$.
- ✅ **Two-way random EMS**: $E(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{\alpha\beta}^2 + bn\sigma_\alpha^2$, with similar forms for B, AB, and Error.
- ✅ **Nested model EMS**: $E(MS_A) = \sigma^2 + n\sigma_{B(A)}^2 + bn\sigma_A^2$; no interaction term because $B$ is *nested within* $A$.
- ✅ **Negative estimates** must be **set to 0**; consider REML or a better-balanced design.
- ✅ **Intraclass correlation** $\hat\rho = \hat\sigma_\tau^2 / (\hat\sigma_\tau^2 + \hat\sigma^2)$ measures within-group homogeneity.
- ✅ **Total variance** $\hat\sigma_Y^2 = \sum_i \hat\sigma_i^2$; report **percentage decomposition** in exams.
- ✅ **Engineering action**: target the **largest** variance source first for maximum variance reduction.
- ✅ Always **interpret** the result in plain engineering language; numbers alone do not earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Staggered Nested Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Staggered Nested Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Staggered Nested Design (SND)** is a specialized class of experimental design used in industrial experimentation when one or more process factors are **hard-to-change (HTC)** while others are **easy-to-change (ETC)**. The design arranges experimental runs in a *multi-level nested (staggered) hierarchy*, where each level corresponds to a different factor whose setting is held constant over a contiguous block of runs. The nesting creates a hierarchy of experimental units (whole plots → sub-plots → sub-sub-plots), and the degree of nesting "stagger" corresponds to the number of HTC factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *"A Staggered Nested Design is a multi-level nested factorial arrangement in which factors are grouped according to their rate of change — hardest-to-change at the outermost (Level 1) and easy-to-change at the innermost (Level k) — producing a hierarchical randomization structure analogous to a multi-stratum split-plot design, but with all interactions between HTC factors deliberately confounded with whole-plot errors."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **chemical engineer optimizing a batch reactor**:

- **Hard-to-change factor (HTC):** Reactor *temperature setting*. Once you heat the reactor jacket to **180 °C**, you cannot realistically change it for every single run — it would take hours to re-equilibrate. So you lock it for, say, 4 consecutive runs.
- **Easy-to-change factor (ETC):** *Catalyst type* or *stirring speed*. You can switch these between runs in minutes.

So you do **4 runs at 180 °C**, varying the ETC factors in random order; then you do **4 runs at 200 °C**, again varying ETC factors randomly. The "stagger" emerges because if there are *two* HTC factors (say, temperature AND pressure), then temperature holds over 4 runs, but within those 4 runs, pressure may only hold over 2 runs each — creating a 2-level stagger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The design is called *staggered* because the experimental units (runs) are "staggered" across multiple levels of constraint. It looks like a staircase — each step (stagger) represents a different HTC factor.

### 1.3 Why We Need It — KTU 2024 Motivation

| Practical Constraint | Design Implication |
|---|---|
| Industrial batch processes | HTC factors must be held across multiple runs |
| Cost of resetting equipment | Reduces number of factor level changes |
| Time pressure in manufacturing | Makes full randomization infeasible |
| Pilot plant studies | Operational realism demands restricted randomization |

A **fully randomized** design would be statistically optimal but **operationally impossible** in such scenarios. SND offers a *practically feasible compromise* that preserves valid statistical inference through appropriate (restricted) randomization and stratum-specific error terms.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Number of staggers (s):** Equals the number of HTC factors
- **Number of whole-plot units per level:** Product of ETC levels divided appropriately
- **Degrees of freedom per stratum:** Governed by the hierarchical structure
- **Standard F-distribution critical values:** $F_{\alpha,(df_1,df_2)}$ at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ unless otherwise stated

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of a Staggered Nested Design

Consider a design with **two HTC factors** (A and B) and **one ETC factor** (C). The nesting cascade is:

$$
\underbrace{A}_{\text{Level 1 (slowest)}} \rightarrow \underbrace{B(i)}_{\text{Level 2 (nested in A)}} \rightarrow \underbrace{C(ij)}_{\text{Level 3 (nested in A and B)}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\varepsilon_{ijk}}_{\text{residual}}
$$

This produces **three strata** of randomization:

1. **Stratum 1 (Whole-plot for A):** Between levels of A — randomization is restricted.
2. **Stratum 2 (Sub-plot for B):** Within each level of A, levels of B form sub-plots.
3. **Stratum 3 (Sub-sub-plot for C):** Within each combination of A and B, levels of C are randomized.

### 2.2 The General Linear Model

The general linear model for an SND with $s$ staggers and replication $r$ is:

$$
Y_{i_1 i_2 \cdots i_s \, k} = \mu + \alpha_{i_1} + \beta_{i_2(i_1)} + \gamma_{i_3(i_1 i_2)} + \cdots + \theta_{i_s(i_1 \cdots i_{s-1})} + \tau_k + \varepsilon_{i_1 i_2 \cdots i_s k}
$$

where:

- $\mu$ = overall mean
- $\alpha_{i_1}$ = main effect of HTC factor 1 (Level 1)
- $\beta_{i_2(i_1)}$ = main effect of HTC factor 2 (nested in level 1)
- $\gamma_{i_3(i_1 i_2)}$ = main effect of ETC factor (nested in levels 1 and 2)
- $\tau_k$ = replication / blocking effect (if present)
- $\varepsilon_{i_1 \cdots k}$ = residual error

**Constraints (for identifiability):**

$$
\sum_{i_1} \alpha_{i_1} = 0, \quad \sum_{i_2} \beta_{i_2(i_1)} = 0 \ \forall i_1, \quad \sum_{i_3} \gamma_{i_3(i_1 i_2)} = 0 \ \forall i_1, i_2
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** the examiner expects you to remember for KTU 2024 ESE on Staggered Nested Designs. Use this as your last-night revision sheet.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Sum of Squares | $SS_T = \sum_{i,j,k}(Y_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2$ | Total variability |
| 2 | Stratum 1 (A) SS | $SS_A = n_{B} n_{C} r \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2$ | Between levels of HTC-1 |
| 3 | Stratum 2 (B in A) SS | $SS_{B(A)} = n_C r \sum_{i,j}(\bar{Y}_{ij\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet})^2$ | Sub-plot stratum |
| 4 | Stratum 3 (C in A,B) SS | $SS_{C(AB)} = r \sum_{i,j,k}(\bar{Y}_{ijk} - \bar{Y}_{ij\bullet})^2$ | Sub-sub-plot stratum |
| 5 | Replicate / Block SS | $SS_R = \sum_{i,j,k}(\bar{Y}_{ijk\cdot} - \bar{Y}_{ijk})^2$ | Within cells |
| 6 | Error SS (residual) | $SS_E = SS_T - SS_A - SS_{B(A)} - SS_{C(AB)} - SS_R$ | All remaining |
| 7 | Total df | $N - 1$ | Where $N$ = total runs |
| 8 | Stratum-1 df | $a - 1$ | $a$ = levels of A |
| 9 | Stratum-2 df | $a(b - 1)$ | $b$ = levels of B |
| 10 | Stratum-3 df | $ab(c - 1)$ | $c$ = levels of C |
| 11 | Mean Square (MS) | $MS = SS / df$ | Per stratum |
| 12 | F-test for HTC-1 | $F_A = MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ | Tests $\alpha$ at Stratum 1 |
| 13 | F-test for HTC-2 | $F_B = MS_{B(A)} / MS_{C(AB)}$ | Tests $\beta$ at Stratum 2 |
| 14 | F-test for ETC | $F_C = MS_{C(AB)} / MS_E$ | Tests $\gamma$ at Stratum 3 |
| 15 | Variance Component A | $\hat{\sigma}^2_A = (MS_A - MS_{B(A)}) / (bcr)$ | REML-type estimator |
| 16 | Variance Component B | $\hat{\sigma}^2_B = (MS_{B(A)} - MS_{C(AB)}) / (cr)$ | REML-type estimator |
| 17 | Variance Component C | $\hat{\sigma}^2_C = (MS_{C(AB)} - MS_E) / r$ | REML-type estimator |
| 18 | Standard Error of Mean | $SE(\bar{Y}_{i}) = \sqrt{MS_{B(A)} / (bcr)}$ | For HTC-1 contrasts |
| 19 | Restricted Likelihood | $\ell_R(\sigma^2) = -\tfrac{1}{2}\left[(N-s)\ln\sigma^2 + \sum_j \ln \vert X_j^\top V_j^{-1} X_j \vert \right]$ | REML log-likelihood |
| 20 | Power of test | $1 - \beta = P(F > F_{\text{crit}} \vert \text{true } \lambda)$ | Non-central F |

> **Important:** The vertical bar `\vert` is used in the REML formula (Item 19) for matrix determinant — this is a LaTeX convention, **NOT** a markdown table separator.

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of the Analysis

1. **Identify the stagger hierarchy:** Order factors from hardest-to-change to easiest-to-change.
2. **Set up the linear model:** Use the nested form with appropriate subscripts.
3. **Compute stratum-specific sums of squares:** Each level has its own error term.
4. **Form the ANOVA table** with separate strata (multi-line ANOVA).
5. **Run F-tests** with the **correct denominator (error) MS** for each effect.
6. **Estimate variance components** for each level using REML or ANOVA estimators.
7. **Interpret** with caution: HTC factors are estimated with less precision due to fewer degrees of freedom for whole-plot error.

### 2.5 Why Each Step Matters (Engineering Context)

| Step | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| Stratum identification | Matches operational reality of the process |
| Stratum-specific SS | Honors the restricted randomization structure |
| F-test with correct error | Prevents inflated Type-I error (critical in QC) |
| Variance components | Quantifies how much variability lives in each "layer" of the process |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stratum-Specific Sum of Squares

We start from the total sum of squares for a two-HTC, one-ETC design with $r$ replicates:

$$
SS_T = \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{c}\sum_{l=1}^{r}(Y_{ijkl} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2
$$

We can decompose this into orthogonal components using the *law of total variance* applied hierarchically:

**Step 1 — Decompose by factor A (outermost):**

$$
SS_A = bcr \sum_{i=1}^{a}(\bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2
$$

This captures variability *between* levels of A, weighted by the number of observations per level ($bcr$).

**Step 2 — Decompose by factor B within A:**

$$
SS_{B(A)} = cr \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}(\bar{Y}_{ij\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2
$$

This captures variability *between* levels of B, computed *within* each level of A. The weight $cr$ accounts for the number of observations in each (i,j) cell.

**Step 3 — Decompose by factor C within A and B:**

$$
SS_{C(AB)} = r \sum_{i=1}^{a}\sum_{j=1}^{b}\sum_{k=1}^{c}(\bar{Y}_{ijk\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{ij\bullet\bullet})^2
$$

This captures variability between levels of C within each (i,j) combination.

**Step 4 — Replicate (within-cell) variability:**

$$
SS_{\text{rep}} = \sum_{i,j,k}\sum_{l=1}^{r}(Y_{ijkl} - \bar{Y}_{ijk\bullet})^2
$$

**Step 5 — Total:**

$$
SS_T = SS_A + SS_{B(A)} + SS_{C(AB)} + SS_{\text{rep}}
$$

> **Why this is exact (orthogonal decomposition):** Because the nesting is *complete and balanced*, the four sums of squares are mutually orthogonal, so the sum of components equals the total SS.

### 3.2 Derivation of Expected Mean Squares (EMS)

The expected mean square for each stratum determines **which F-test is valid**. We derive EMS for factor A:

$$
