E[MS_A] = \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C + rc\sigma^2_B + rcb\sigma^2_A
$$

**Derivation line by line:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[MS_A] &= E\left[\frac{SS_A}{a-1}\right] \\
&= E\left[\frac{bcr \sum_i (\bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet})^2}{a-1}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding $\bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet}$ using the model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Y}_{i\bullet\bullet\bullet} - \bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet} &= \frac{1}{bcr}\sum_{j,k,l} Y_{ijkl} - \frac{1}{abcr}\sum_{i,j,k,l} Y_{ijkl} \\
&= \alpha_i + \bar{\beta}_{\bullet(i)} + \bar{\gamma}_{\bullet\bullet(i)} + \bar{\varepsilon}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet(i)} - \text{grand means (all }0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking expectations and squaring:

$$
E[MS_A] = \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C + rc\sigma^2_B + rcb\sigma^2_A
$$

Similarly:

$$
E[MS_{B(A)}] = \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C + rc\sigma^2_B
$$

$$
E[MS_{C(AB)}] = \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C
$$

$$
E[MS_{\text{rep}}] = \sigma^2
$$

### 3.3 Proper F-Test Construction

The denominator of each F-test is the MS of the **next lower stratum** (or residual):

| Effect | Numerator | Denominator | df of Denominator |
|---|---|---|---|
| A (HTC-1) | $MS_A$ | $MS_{B(A)}$ | $a(b-1)$ |
| B (HTC-2) | $MS_{B(A)}$ | $MS_{C(AB)}$ | $ab(c-1)$ |
| C (ETC) | $MS_{C(AB)}$ | $MS_{\text{rep}}$ | $abc(r-1)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Valuation Rule:** The **wrong denominator** is the #1 mistake students make on staggered nested ANOVA problems. You **must** use the EMS structure to identify the appropriate error term.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Setup:** A pharmaceutical company studies a tablet-coating process with two HTC factors (A: drying temperature with 2 levels, B: spray rate with 2 levels nested in A) and one ETC factor (C: polymer concentration with 2 levels nested in A and B), with $r = 2$ replicates. Total $N = 16$ runs.

**Hypothetical data table (mean tablet hardness, kg):**

| A | B | C | Rep 1 | Rep 2 | $\bar{Y}$ |
|---|---|---|-------|-------|-----------|
| 60°C | Low | X1 | 7.2 | 7.4 | 7.30 |
| 60°C | Low | X2 | 8.1 | 7.9 | 8.00 |
| 60°C | High | X1 | 8.5 | 8.3 | 8.40 |
| 60°C | High | X2 | 9.0 | 9.2 | 9.10 |
| 80°C | Low | X1 | 7.8 | 8.0 | 7.90 |
| 80°C | Low | X2 | 8.4 | 8.6 | 8.50 |
| 80°C | High | X1 | 9.1 | 9.3 | 9.20 |
| 80°C | High | X2 | 9.8 | 9.6 | 9.70 |

**Step 1 — Compute grand mean:**

$$
\bar{Y}_{\bullet\bullet\bullet\bullet} = \frac{1}{16}\sum Y = \frac{132.4}{16} = 8.275
$$

(We assume the sum equals 132.4 for illustration.)

**Step 2 — Stratum 1 (A) SS:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Y}_{1\bullet\bullet\bullet} &= \frac{7.30+8.00+8.40+9.10}{4} = 8.20 \\
\bar{Y}_{2\bullet\bullet\bullet} &= \frac{7.90+8.50+9.20+9.70}{4} = 8.325 \\
SS_A &= 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \left[(8.20-8.275)^2 + (8.325-8.275)^2\right] \\
&= 8 \left[0.005625 + 0.0025\right] = 8(0.008125) = 0.065
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Stratum 2 (B within A) SS:**

For A=1: $\bar{Y}_{11\bullet\bullet} = (7.30+8.00)/2 = 7.65$, $\bar{Y}_{12\bullet\bullet} = (8.40+9.10)/2 = 8.75$

For A=2: $\bar{Y}_{21\bullet\bullet} = (7.90+8.50)/2 = 8.20$, $\bar{Y}_{22\bullet\bullet} = (9.20+9.70)/2 = 9.45$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SS_{B(A)} &= 2 \cdot 2 \left[(7.65-8.20)^2 + (8.75-8.20)^2 + (8.20-8.325)^2 + (9.45-8.325)^2\right] \\
&= 4 \left[0.3025 + 0.3025 + 0.015625 + 1.265625\right] \\
&= 4(1.88625) = 7.545
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Stratum 3 (C within A and B) SS:**

For A=1, B=1: $\bar{Y}_{111\bullet} = 7.30$, $\bar{Y}_{112\bullet} = 8.00$, mean = 7.65

Difference squared: $(7.30-7.65)^2 + (8.00-7.65)^2 = 0.1225 + 0.1225 = 0.245$

Similarly for other cells (each contributing 0.245):

$$
SS_{C(AB)} = 4 \times 0.245 = 0.98
$$

**Step 5 — Replicate SS:**

For each (i,j,k) cell, $SS = (Y_{ijkl} - \bar{Y}_{ijk\bullet})^2$ summed.

For cell (1,1,1): $(7.2-7.3)^2 + (7.4-7.3)^2 = 0.01 + 0.01 = 0.02$. Total across 8 cells = $0.16$.

$$
SS_{\text{rep}} = 0.16
$$

**Step 6 — Total SS and ANOVA Table:**

| Source | SS | df | MS | EMS | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (HTC-1) | 0.065 | 1 | 0.0650 | $\sigma^2 + 2\sigma^2_C + 4\sigma^2_B + 8\sigma^2_A$ | $0.0650 / 0.3773 = 0.172$ |
| B(A) (HTC-2) | 7.545 | 2 | 3.7725 | $\sigma^2 + 2\sigma^2_C + 4\sigma^2_B$ | $3.7725 / 0.245 = 15.40$ |
| C(AB) (ETC) | 0.980 | 4 | 0.2450 | $\sigma^2 + 2\sigma^2_C$ | $0.2450 / 0.020 = 12.25$ |
| Replicate | 0.160 | 8 | 0.0200 | $\sigma^2$ | — |
| **Total** | **8.750** | **15** | | | |

**Step 7 — Variance Components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_{\text{rep}} = 0.0200 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_C &= (MS_{C(AB)} - MS_{\text{rep}}) / r = (0.2450 - 0.0200)/2 = 0.1125 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_B &= (MS_{B(A)} - MS_{C(AB)}) / (cr) = (3.7725 - 0.2450)/(2 \cdot 2) = 0.8819 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_A &= (MS_A - MS_{B(A)}) / (bcr) = (0.0650 - 3.7725)/8 < 0 \rightarrow \text{truncate to } 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**

- B(A) is **highly significant** ($F = 15.40 > F_{0.05, 2, 4} = 6.94$).
- C(AB) is **significant** ($F = 12.25 > F_{0.05, 4, 8} = 3.84$).
- A is **not significant** ($F = 0.172 < F_{0.05, 1, 2} = 18.51$).
- $\hat{\sigma}^2_B$ dominates → most variability lies in the *spray rate* (HTC-2) stratum.

### 3.5 REML Estimation (Conceptual Derivation)

REML maximizes the **restricted log-likelihood**:

$$
\ell_R(\sigma^2_A, \sigma^2_B, \sigma^2_C, \sigma^2) = -\frac{1}{2}\left[(N-r)\ln(2\pi) + \ln \vert V \vert + \ln \vert X^\top V^{-1} X \vert + (Y-X\hat{\beta})^\top V^{-1}(Y-X\hat{\beta})\right]
$$

where $V$ is the variance-covariance matrix with block-diagonal structure reflecting the hierarchy. REML produces **unbiased** estimates by adjusting for the loss of df due to estimating fixed effects.

> [!NOTE]
> REML is preferred over ANOVA estimators in KTU 2024 contexts whenever **unbalanced data** is encountered or when estimating **variance components** is the primary goal.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of the Staggered Nesting Structure

The following block diagram illustrates how experimental runs are arranged in a 2-stagger design (2 HTC factors + 1 ETC factor):

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef htc1 fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef htc2 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef etc fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef err fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    A1["A = Level 1 (HTC-1)"]:::htc1
    A2["A = Level 2 (HTC-1)"]:::htc1

    B1A1["B = L1 in A1 (HTC-2)"]:::htc2
    B2A1["B = L2 in A1 (HTC-2)"]:::htc2
    B1A2["B = L1 in A2 (HTC-2)"]:::htc2
    B2A2["B = L2 in A2 (HTC-2)"]:::htc2

    C1B1A1["C = X1 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C2B1A1["C = X2 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C1B2A1["C = X1 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C2B2A1["C = X2 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C1B1A2["C = X1 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C2B1A2["C = X2 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C1B2A2["C = X1 (ETC)"]:::etc
    C2B2A2["C = X2 (ETC)"]:::etc

    E1["e1"]:::err
    E2["e2"]:::err
    E3["e3"]:::err
    E4["e4"]:::err
    E5["e5"]:::err
    E6["e6"]:::err
    E7["e7"]:::err
    E8["e8"]:::err

    A1 --> B1A1
    A1 --> B2A1
    A2 --> B1A2
    A2 --> B2A2

    B1A1 --> C1B1A1
    B1A1 --> C2B1A1
    B2A1 --> C1B2A1
    B2A1 --> C2B2A1
    B1A2 --> C1B1A2
    B1A2 --> C2B1A2
    B2A2 --> C1B2A2
    B2A2 --> C2B2A2

    C1B1A1 --> E1
    C2B1A1 --> E2
    C1B2A1 --> E3
    C2B2A1 --> E4
    C1B1A2 --> E5
    C2B1A2 --> E6
    C1B2A2 --> E7
    C2B2A2 --> E8
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Randomization Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef startNode fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef stratNode fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    START["Select HTC-1 Level"]:::startNode
    S1["Set HTC-1 = constant"]:::stratNode
    S2["Randomize HTC-2 within HTC-1"]:::stratNode
    S3["Randomize ETC within HTC-2"]:::stratNode
    RUN["Execute experimental run"]:::stratNode
    NEXT["Move to next ETC"]:::stratNode
    BEND["Move to next HTC-2"]:::stratNode
    AEND["Move to next HTC-1"]:::stratNode
    DONE["All runs complete"]:::endNode

    START --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> RUN
    RUN --> NEXT
    NEXT -->|more ETC| RUN
    NEXT -->|done| BEND
    BEND -->|more HTC-2| S3
    BEND -->|done| AEND
    AEND -->|more HTC-1| S1
    AEND -->|done| DONE
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: When to Use SND vs Other Designs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef qnode fill:#277da1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef anode fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    Q1{"All factors are ETC?"}:::qnode

    A1["Use full factorial or fractional factorial design"]:::anode

    A5["Use response surface methodology"]:::anode
    C1["Proceed with Staggered Nested Design"]:::cnode

    Q1 -->|Yes| A1
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|1| Q3
    Q2 -->|2 or more| Q4
    Q3 -->|No, only main effects| A2
    Q3 -->|Yes| A3
    Q4 -->|Batch| C1
    Q4 -->|Continuous| A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In the decision tree, when you reach the green "Proceed" node, the design choice is finalized as an SND. The preceding questions (yellow nodes) are the screening criteria.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*Define a Staggered Nested Design. Under what practical conditions is it preferred over a fully randomized design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Staggered Nested Design is a multi-level hierarchical experimental design in which factors are grouped by their rate of change — hardest-to-change (HTC) at the outermost level and easy-to-change (ETC) at the innermost. Each level creates a "stagger" of nesting. (2 marks)
It is preferred when: (i) some factors are operationally difficult to vary between runs, (ii) full randomization is infeasible due to cost or time, and (iii) the process is industrial/batch in nature. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*Distinguish between a Staggered Nested Design and a Split-Plot Design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a split-plot design, there is typically **one** whole-plot factor and the analysis uses standard split-plot ANOVA. (1 mark)
In a staggered nested design, there are **multiple** HTC factors creating a cascade of nested strata — the design has $s \geq 2$ staggers. (1 mark)
The SND generalizes the split-plot: a one-stagger SND is essentially a split-plot, but multi-stagger SNDs cannot be reduced to a standard split-plot. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adapted]**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Explain the linear model for a Staggered Nested Design with **two** HTC factors and **one** ETC factor. Write down all EMS equations and clearly identify the appropriate denominator for testing each main effect.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* For a 2-stagger SND with factor A (2 levels), factor B (2 levels, nested in A), factor C (3 levels, nested in A and B), and $r = 2$ replicates, compute the total number of runs and the degrees of freedom for each stratum.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Model and EMS:**

The linear model is:

$$
Y_{ijkl} = \mu + \alpha_i + \beta_{j(i)} + \gamma_{k(ij)} + \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}
$$

where:
- $\alpha_i$ = effect of A, $i = 1, 2$
- $\beta_{j(i)}$ = effect of B nested in A, $j = 1, 2$ for each $i$
- $\gamma_{k(ij)}$ = effect of C nested in A and B, $k = 1, 2, 3$ for each $(i,j)$
- $\varepsilon$ = residual

**Expected Mean Squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[MS_A] &= \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C + rc\sigma^2_B + rcb\sigma^2_A \\
E[MS_{B(A)}] &= \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C + rc\sigma^2_B \\
E[MS_{C(AB)}] &= \sigma^2 + r\sigma^2_C \\
E[MS_E] &= \sigma^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**F-test structure (denominator for each effect):**

| Effect | F = MS_numerator / MS_denominator |
|---|---|
| A (HTC-1) | $F_A = MS_A / MS_{B(A)}$ |
| B(A) (HTC-2) | $F_B = MS_{B(A)} / MS_{C(AB)}$ |
| C(AB) (ETC) | $F_C = MS_{C(AB)} / MS_E$ |

**(7 marks: 3 for model, 2 for EMS, 2 for F-test structure)**

---

**Part (b) — Total runs and df calculation:**

Total runs: $N = a \times b \times c \times r = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$ runs

Degrees of freedom:

$$
\begin{aligned}
df_A &= a - 1 = 1 \\
df_{B(A)} &= a(b-1) = 2(1) = 2 \\
df_{C(AB)} &= ab(c-1) = 4(2) = 8 \\
df_{\text{error}} &= abc(r-1) = 12(1) = 12 \\
df_{\text{total}} &= N - 1 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $1 + 2 + 8 + 12 = 23 = df_{\text{total}}$ ✓

**(7 marks: 3 for total runs, 4 for df computation and verification)**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Adapted]**

**(a)** *[7 marks]* An SND experiment on a polymer extrusion process has factor A (barrel temperature, 2 levels = HTC-1), factor B (screw speed, 2 levels = HTC-2, nested in A), and factor C (additive concentration, 3 levels = ETC, nested in A and B), with $r = 2$. The following partial ANOVA was obtained:

| Source | SS | df |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4.20 | 1 |
| B(A) | 18.60 | 2 |
| C(AB) | 7.50 | 6 |
| Error | 2.40 | 12 |
| **Total** | **32.70** | **23** |

Complete the ANOVA table, perform F-tests at $\alpha = 0.05$, and state conclusions.

**(b)** *[7 marks]* Estimate the variance components using ANOVA estimators.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Completed ANOVA table and F-tests:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MS_A &= 4.20 / 1 = 4.20 \\
MS_{B(A)} &= 18.60 / 2 = 9.30 \\
MS_{C(AB)} &= 7.50 / 6 = 1.25 \\
MS_E &= 2.40 / 12 = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

| Source | SS | df | MS | F | $F_{\text{crit}, 0.05}$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 4.20 | 1 | 4.20 | $4.20/9.30 = 0.452$ | $F_{1,2,0.05} = 18.51$ | Not significant |
| B(A) | 18.60 | 2 | 9.30 | $9.30/1.25 = 7.44$ | $F_{2,6,0.05} = 5.14$ | **Significant** |
| C(AB) | 7.50 | 6 | 1.25 | $1.25/0.20 = 6.25$ | $F_{6,12,0.05} = 2.99$ | **Significant** |
| Error | 2.40 | 12 | 0.20 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **32.70** | **23** | | | | |

**Conclusions:**
- A (barrel temperature) is **not significant** at $\alpha = 0.05$.
- B (screw speed) is **significant** — it affects the response.
- C (additive concentration) is **significant** — it affects the response.

**(7 marks: 2 for MS, 2 for F, 2 for comparison, 1 for conclusion)**

---

**Part (b) — Variance component estimates:**

For this design, $a=2, b=2, c=3, r=2$, so $cr=6, bcr=12, r=2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\sigma}^2 &= MS_E = 0.20 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_C &= (MS_{C(AB)} - MS_E) / r = (1.25 - 0.20)/2 = 0.525 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_B &= (MS_{B(A)} - MS_{C(AB)}) / (cr) = (9.30 - 1.25)/6 = 1.342 \\
\hat{\sigma}^2_A &= (MS_A - MS_{B(A)}) / (bcr) = (4.20 - 9.30)/12 < 0 \rightarrow \text{truncate to } 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance component summary:**

| Component | Estimate | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $\hat{\sigma}^2_A$ | 0.000 | No variability attributable to A |
| $\hat{\sigma}^2_B$ | 1.342 | Dominant source — screw speed differences are large |
| $\hat{\sigma}^2_C$ | 0.525 | Moderate effect of additive concentration |
| $\hat{\sigma}^2$ | 0.200 | Pure experimental error |

**Total variance:** $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{total}} = 0 + 1.342 + 0.525 + 0.200 = 2.067$

**Proportion explained by B:** $1.342 / 2.067 \approx 64.9\%$

**(7 marks: 2 per variance component, 1 for summary interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong denominator MS:** Using $MS_E$ for testing factor A or B. Always use the **next lower stratum's MS** as the denominator.
> 2. **Negative variance components:** A negative $\hat{\sigma}^2$ is mathematically possible (especially with small samples). **Truncate to 0** — do NOT report negative variance.
> 3. **Confusing "nested" with "crossed":** In SND, B is nested in A (B's levels differ between A's levels). This is NOT a factorial interaction.
> 4. **Forgetting to use $r$ (replication) when calculating df:** $df_{\text{error}} = abc(r-1)$, not $abc - 1$.
> 5. **Misinterpreting "stagger":** Each HTC factor adds ONE stagger. So 2 HTC factors = 2 staggers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition:** SND = multi-level nested design honoring operational constraints (HTC vs ETC factors).
- **Structure:** Stagger $s$ = number of HTC factors. Each HTC factor adds one level of nesting.
- **Model:** $Y = \mu + \alpha + \beta(A) + \gamma(A,B) + \cdots + \varepsilon$ with nested subscripts.
- **Randomization:** Restricted at the outermost (HTC) levels; full within the innermost (ETC) level.
- **ANOVA:** Multi-stratum table with one row per level of nesting.
- **EMS Equations:** Carry only the variance components from the **same or higher** strata.
- **F-test denominator:** The MS of the **next lower stratum** (or residual for the lowest stratum).
- **df rules:**
  - $df_{\text{Stratum }k} = (\text{product of higher factor levels}) \times (\text{current factor levels} - 1)$
  - $df_{\text{error}} = N - 1 - \sum df_{\text{strata}}$
- **Variance components (ANOVA method):** $(\text{MS}_{\text{current}} - \text{MS}_{\text{next}}) / (\text{replication factor at this level})$.
- **REML:** Preferred for unbalanced data and unbiased estimation.
- **Truncation rule:** Negative variance components → set to 0.
- **Comparison:** 1-stagger SND ≡ split-plot; multi-stagger SND is a generalization.
- **Engineering use cases:** Batch reactors, extrusion, heat treatment, annealing, coating — anywhere factor change is costly in time/energy.
- **Total runs:** $N = \prod (\text{levels of each factor}) \times r$.
- **Critical KTU 2024 takeaway:** Always show the EMS structure explicitly to justify your F-test choice.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design with Both Nested and Factorial Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET525 - DESIGN OF EXPERIMENTS AND STATISTICAL ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design with Both Nested and Factorial Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Design of Experiments (DOE)**, a **Design with Both Nested and Factorial Factors** is a *mixed* or *hybrid* experimental structure in which the experimental run-sheet simultaneously contains:

1. **Crossed (Factorial) Factors** — Two or more factors whose every level is combined with every level of the other factor(s). These are denoted by writing them side-by-side, e.g., a $2 \times 3$ factorial of factors $A$ and $B$.

2. **Nested Factors** — A factor whose levels appear *only* inside one specific level of another factor and are *meaningless outside* it. These are denoted with parentheses, e.g., $B(A)$ meaning "Factor $B$ is nested inside Factor $A$."

The combined notation, e.g., a $2 \times 3$ factorial in $A$ and $C$ with $B$ nested in $A$, is written as:

$$[A \times C] \text{ with } B(A)$$

or, in run count form, $a \times b(a) \times c \times n$ replicates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A factor is *crossed* with another if it appears in every level of the other; it is *nested* within another if its levels are unique sub-categories of one specific parent level. The **interaction** between a crossed factor and a nested factor is **not estimable in the usual sense** — it is absorbed into the nested factor's main effect or its interaction with other crossed factors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **pharmaceutical quality-control lab** with the following setup:

- **Factor A** = Two manufacturing **plants** (Plant-1, Plant-2). This is a *crossed* factor because we will study every plant.
- **Factor C** = Three **shift timings** (Morning, Evening, Night). This is *crossed* with A — every plant runs all three shifts.
- **Factor B(A)** = Two **operators** available at *each* plant. *Operator "Ravi"* at Plant-1 is a *different person* from *Operator "Ravi"* at Plant-2. Operators are therefore **nested within plants**, because the identity of the operator only makes sense relative to the plant they belong to.

The experiment measures tablet hardness from each *operator × shift* combination with some replicates. The analysis must therefore recognise:

- Plants are *comparable* across shifts (crossed).
- Operators are *not comparable* across plants (nested) — you cannot average the effect of "Ravi" because "Ravi" means two different people.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory hook for KTU exams:** *Crossed = comparable everywhere; Nested = comparable only inside one parent level.*

---

### 1.3 Classification of the Design

A design with both nested and factorial factors is generally classified under the family of **Mixed Models** or **Hierarchical Models with Crossed Strata**. Two widely-used sub-classes appear repeatedly in KTU Module 4:

| Sub-class | Description | Typical KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|
| **Factorial–Nested Design** | All main effects are crossed; one or more sub-factors are nested inside a main factor | $a \times b \times c(b) \times n$ |
| **Staggered Nested-Factorial Design** | Levels of the nested factor do not all appear in every crossed cell; common in industrial / reliability testing | UNEQUAL replications per cell |

> [!TIP]
> The **standard textbook metric** for distinguishing the two: if every level of the nested factor occurs in *each* level of its parent crossed factor, the design is **balanced**; otherwise it is **unbalanced/staggered** — both require *Type III* Sum-of-Squares (SS) in commercial software.

---

### 1.4 Why This Design Exists — Real Engineering Motivation

In real industrial experimentation, the levels of certain nuisance or secondary factors are *inherently tied* to one specific level of a primary factor:

- **Welders** are tied to a specific fabrication bay.
- **Batches of raw material** are produced per supplier lot.
- **Operators** are assigned per machine.
- **Days** are nested within weeks, while **treatments** are applied across weeks.

Forcing such factors to be crossed would either be *logistically impossible* or *scientifically meaningless*. The mixed nested-factorial design is the formal statistical answer to this engineering reality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of the Design

A KTU-typical design of this type follows the logical cascade:

1. **Select the crossed (outer) factors** — these define the *experimental cells* of interest (the *design points*).
2. **Identify the nested (inner) factor** — its levels are unique *within each cell* of the outer factors.
3. **Decide the replication level** $n$ — independent repeated measurements within each innermost cell.
4. **Choose the run order** — must be **randomised** within the constraints of nesting; partial randomisation (e.g., split-plot) is acceptable only when hard-to-change factors exist.
5. **Compute the ANOVA** — with the *correct* degrees of freedom and the *correct* Expected Mean Squares (EMS) for the F-tests.

### 2.2 The Core Statistical Model

For a design with crossed factors $A$ and $C$, a nested factor $B(A)$, and $n$ replicates per cell, the standard linear model is:

$$
y_{ijk\ell} \;=\; \mu \;+\; \alpha_i \;+\; \gamma_k \;+\; (\alpha\gamma)_{ik} \;+\; \beta_{j(i)} \;+\; (\beta\gamma)_{j(i)k} \;+\; \varepsilon_{\ell(ijk)}
$$

**Why each term exists (the 'Why' and 'How'):**

- $\mu$ — the **grand mean** of the response.
- $\alpha_i$ — main effect of level $i$ of crossed factor $A$ (with $\sum_i \alpha_i = 0$).
- $\gamma_k$ — main effect of level $k$ of crossed factor $C$.
- $(\alpha\gamma)_{ik}$ — *crossed interaction* between $A$ and $C$ — **fully estimable**.
- $\beta_{j(i)}$ — main effect of level $j$ of factor $B$, *nested within level $i$ of $A$*. Note the subscript $j(i)$ — the levels of $B$ are *indexed by* $i$.
- $(\beta\gamma)_{j(i)k}$ — interaction of the nested factor with the crossed factor $C$. **This interaction is partially confounded** with the nested effect in unbalanced designs.
- $\varepsilon_{\ell(ijk)}$ — random replicate error, i.i.d. $N(0, \sigma^2)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU fact:** Because $B$ is nested in $A$, the term $B \times A$ (as a crossed interaction) **does not exist**; the part of $B$'s variability that *would have been* a $B \times A$ interaction is *absorbed* into $\beta_{j(i)}$.

### 2.3 Degrees-of-Freedom (df) Decomposition

For an $a \times b(a) \times c \times n$ design (i.e., $a$ levels of $A$, $b$ levels of $B$ inside each $A$, $c$ levels of $C$, $n$ replicates):

| Source | Degrees of Freedom (df) |
|---|---|
| Factor $A$ (crossed) | $a - 1$ |
| Factor $B(A)$ (nested) | $a(b - 1)$ |
| Factor $C$ (crossed) | $c - 1$ |
| Interaction $A \times C$ | $(a-1)(c-1)$ |
| Interaction $B(A) \times C$ | $a(b-1)(c-1)$ |
| Pure (Replicate) Error | $abc(n-1)$ |
| **Total** | $abcn - 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most **exam-favoured KTU line of reasoning** is verifying that the df of $B(A)$ is $a(b-1)$ and **not** $b-1$ — the multiplication by $a$ comes from the $a$ parent levels of $A$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Item | Formula / Identity | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total df | $N - 1$ where $N = abcn$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | df of nested factor $B(A)$ | $a(b-1)$ | **multiplied by $a$** |
| 3 | df of $A \times C$ | $(a-1)(c-1)$ | standard crossed interaction |
| 4 | df of $B(A) \times C$ | $a(b-1)(c-1)$ | error term for $A \times C$ in many designs |
| 5 | Total SS (TSS) | $\sum y^2 - \dfrac{(\sum y)^2}{N}$ | correction factor $CF = (\sum y)^2 / N$ |
| 6 | SS due to $A$ | $\dfrac{\sum_i T_{i\cdot\cdot\cdot}^2}{bcn} - CF$ | divide by number of obs per level of $A$ |
| 7 | SS due to $B(A)$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{i,j} T_{ij\cdot\cdot}^2}{cn} - \dfrac{\sum_i T_{i\cdot\cdot\cdot}^2}{bcn}$ | subtract SS($A$) |
| 8 | SS due to $C$ | $\dfrac{\sum_k T_{\cdot\cdot k\cdot}^2}{abn} - CF$ |  |
| 9 | SS due to $A \times C$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{i,k} T_{i\cdot k\cdot}^2}{bn} - SS_A - SS_C - CF$ |  |
| 10 | SS due to $B(A) \times C$ | $\dfrac{\sum_{i,j,k} T_{ijk\cdot}^2}{n} - SS_{B(A)} - SS_{A\times C} - SS_C - SS_A - CF$ |  |
| 11 | Pure Error SS | TSS $-$ (sum of all model SS) | df = $abc(n-1)$ |
| 12 | Mean Square | MS = SS / df | unit = (response)$^2$ |
| 13 | F-statistic for $A$ | $F_A = \dfrac{MS_A}{MS_{B(A)}}$ | **denominator is the nested MS** |
| 14 | F-statistic for $C$ | $F_C = \dfrac{MS_C}{MS_{B(A) \times C}}$ |  |
| 15 | F-statistic for $A \times C$ | $F_{A \times C} = \dfrac{MS_{A \times C}}{MS_{B(A) \times C}}$ |  |
| 16 | Variance component of nested factor | $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta} = \dfrac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_{B(A) \times C}}{cn}$ | for crossed $C$, $n$ rep. |
| 17 | EMS of $B(A)$ | $\sigma^2 + n \sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn \sigma^2_{\beta}$ | $B$ and $A$ are **random** assumption |

> **Notation convention used above:** $T_{i \cdot \cdot \cdot}$ = row total across all other indices; dots are summation placeholders.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Design Shines

- **Semiconductor manufacturing:** Wafer (nested inside lot) tested across tools (crossed) for yield.
- **Automotive crash testing:** Car models (crossed) with crash-test labs (nested within model) and replicates.
- **Textile quality:** Looms (nested within factory) producing fabric tested under multiple humidity conditions (crossed).
- **Clinical trials:** Patients (nested within hospital) receiving treatments (crossed) with repeated measurements.

The design's primary value is **separating the variability attributable to the nested factor from the variability of the crossed factors**, which is essential for honest Generalised Linear / Mixed Model (GLMM) inference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — df of the Nested Factor

**Setup.** Factor $A$ has $a$ levels, and inside level $i$ of $A$, factor $B$ has $b$ levels. The total number of $B$-level instances is $ab$, but the levels of $B$ are *not* shared across different $i$.

**Step 1 — Total df of the "B" stratum ignoring nesting:**

$$
\text{df}_{B,\text{naive}} = ab - 1
$$

This would be correct if the $b$ levels of $B$ were *the same* across all $a$ levels of $A$ (a fully crossed model).

**Step 2 — Subtract the $A$ main-effect df (which is the part confounded with $B$ when crossed):**

$$
\text{df}_{B(A)} \;=\; (ab - 1) \;-\; (a - 1)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\text{df}_{B(A)} \;=\; ab - 1 - a + 1 \;=\; ab - a \;=\; a(b - 1)
$$

Hence the **df of a nested factor equals the number of parent levels × (number of nested levels − 1)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Sum of Squares for a Nested Factor

**Setup.** The $A \times B$ crossed layout would yield:

$$
SS_{A} + SS_{B \text{ (crossed)}} + SS_{A \times B}
$$

Because $B$ is **nested in $A$**, the levels of $B$ are *distinct* for each $A$. The variability among $B$ levels inside a fixed $A$ is what we want; the variability *between* the $A$-level means is $SS_A$, which must be removed first.

**Step 1 — Cell totals (over replicates) for level $(i,j)$ of the nested layout:**

$$
T_{ij\cdot} \;=\; \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} y_{ij\ell}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squares using cell totals (over the $n$ replicates within $(i,j)$):**

$$
SS_{\text{within cells}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \sum_{\ell=1}^{n} y_{ij\ell}^2 \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \frac{T_{ij\cdot}^2}{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Add back the between-cells sum of squares, partitioned into $A$ and $B(A)$:**

$$
SS_{\text{between cells}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \frac{T_{ij\cdot}^2}{n} \;-\; \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot\cdot}^2}{abn}
$$

**Step 4 — Within-cell "B-inside-A" decomposition. The $A$ part is:**

$$
SS_{A} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \frac{T_{i\cdot\cdot}^2}{bn} \;-\; \frac{T_{\cdot\cdot\cdot}^2}{abn}
$$

**Step 5 — Therefore, the $B(A)$ part is the remainder:**

$$
SS_{B(A)} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \sum_{j=1}^{b} \frac{T_{ij\cdot}^2}{n} \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{a} \frac{T_{i\cdot\cdot}^2}{bn}
$$

This subtracts the between-$A$-level variability and isolates the *within-$A$* variation across the $b$ nested levels.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Expected Mean Square (EMS) for $B(A)$ Under Random Model

**Random-model assumption:** $A$, $B(A)$, and $\varepsilon$ are all random, mutually independent, with zero means and variances $\sigma^2_A$, $\sigma^2_{\beta}$, $\sigma^2$ respectively.

**Step 1 — Model (random-effects version):**

$$
y_{ijk\ell} \;=\; \mu \;+\; A_i \;+\; B_{j(i)} \;+\; \varepsilon_{\ell(ijk)}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean Square is a quadratic form in the $y$'s. Its expectation is obtained by expanding $E[\text{MS}] = E[y' P y] / \text{df}$ for the orthogonal projection matrix $P$ corresponding to the $B(A)$ term.**

**Step 3 — For a balanced design with $n$ replicates per cell, the EMS of $B(A)$ is:**

$$
E[MS_{B(A)}] \;=\; \sigma^2 \;+\; n \sigma^2_{\beta}
$$

**Step 4 — Logical reasoning behind the formula:**

- Each $\sigma^2_{\beta}$ contribution is multiplied by the number of replicates $n$ per $(i,j)$ cell because that many observations share the same random $B_{j(i)}$.
- The residual $\sigma^2$ always appears with coefficient **1** because every observation contributes one unit of pure error.
- The $A$ variance $\sigma^2_A$ **does not appear** in $E[MS_{B(A)}]$ because $B$ is *nested* in $A$ — the $A$ variability has already been removed by the subtraction of $SS_A$.

**Step 5 — General EMS table (for KTU-style random-model design $a \times b(a) \times c \times n$):**

| Source | Expected Mean Square |
|---|---|
| $A$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn\sigma^2_{\beta} + bcn\sigma^2_{\alpha}$ |
| $B(A)$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn\sigma^2_{\beta}$ |
| $C$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + abn\sigma^2_{\gamma}$ |
| $A \times C$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + bn\sigma^2_{\alpha\gamma}$ |
| $B(A) \times C$ | $\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma}$ |
| Error | $\sigma^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This EMS table is the **single most important KTU Module 4 output** — without it, one cannot construct the correct F-tests. Note that the appropriate denominator for testing $A$ is $MS_{B(A)}$, and for testing $A \times C$ it is $MS_{B(A) \times C}$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — F-Statistic for Factor $A$

**Step 1 — From the EMS table, the $F$-test for $A$ is:**

$$
F_A \;=\; \frac{MS_A}{MS_{B(A)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the EMS values:**

$$
F_A \;=\; \frac{\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn\sigma^2_{\beta} + bcn\sigma^2_{\alpha}}{\sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn\sigma^2_{\beta}}
$$

**Step 3 — Under the null hypothesis $H_0 : \sigma^2_{\alpha} = 0$:**

$$
F_A \;\sim\; F_{(a-1),\, a(b-1)} \quad \text{approximately}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision rule (KTU convention):**

> Reject $H_0$ at significance level $\alpha$ if $F_{\text{computed}} > F_{\text{critical},\,(a-1),\,a(b-1),\,1-\alpha}$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Sketch (No Real Data — Symbolic)

Consider a $2 \times 3(2) \times 4 \times 2$ design:
- $A$: 2 levels (crossed)
- $B(A)$: 3 levels nested within each level of $A$
- $C$: 4 levels (crossed)
- $n$: 2 replicates per innermost cell

**Total observations** $N = 2 \times 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 48$.

**Degrees of freedom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{df}_A &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\
\text{df}_{B(A)} &= 2(3 - 1) = 4 \\
\text{df}_C &= 4 - 1 = 3 \\
\text{df}_{A \times C} &= (2-1)(4-1) = 3 \\
\text{df}_{B(A) \times C} &= 2(3-1)(4-1) = 12 \\
\text{df}_{\text{error}} &= 2 \times 3 \times 4 \times (2-1) = 24 \\
\text{df}_{\text{total}} &= 48 - 1 = 47
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (sum check):**

$$
1 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 12 + 24 \;=\; 47 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This sanity check (sum of df = total df) is the **fastest KTU exam trick** to catch arithmetic errors in df decomposition.

---

### 3.6 The Hierarchy of Constraints

A KTU-favourite topic: when an interaction $A \times C$ is significant, **interpret the simple (main) effects of $A$ and $C$ *within* each level of the other**, not their marginal averages. For mixed models with nested factors, the same hierarchy applies but the "levels of the other" are restricted to the *parent* levels of the nested factor.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Tree of Factors (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block renders a hierarchical block diagram of a $2 \times 3(2) \times 4$ mixed nested-factorial design. The diagram maps the *interactions* between factors rather than attempting a physical sketch.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["EXPERIMENTAL DESIGN a x b a x c x n"]
    nodeA["FACTOR A CROSSED a levels"]
    nodeC["FACTOR C CROSSED c levels"]
    nodeBnested["FACTOR B NESTED IN A b levels per A"]
    nodeAC["INTERACTION A x C estimable crossed x crossed"]
    nodeBnestedC["INTERACTION B A x C nested x crossed"]
    nodeRep["REPLICATES n per cell"]

    nodeRoot --> nodeA
    nodeRoot --> nodeC
    nodeRoot --> nodeBnested
    nodeA --> nodeAC
    nodeC --> nodeAC
    nodeA --> nodeBnested
    nodeBnested --> nodeBnestedC
    nodeC --> nodeBnestedC
    nodeBnestedC --> nodeRep
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU interpretation):**

- `nodeRoot` is the entire experiment.
- `nodeA` and `nodeC` are *peers* — they are both crossed.
- `nodeBnested` is drawn as a *child* of `nodeA`, reflecting the **parent-child relationship** of nesting.
- `nodeAC` is the only **fully crossed** interaction; it sits between `nodeA` and `nodeC`.
- `nodeBnestedC` connects the *child* `nodeBnested` with the *peer* `nodeC`, mirroring the partially-confounded $B(A) \times C$ interaction.
- `nodeRep` is the *innermost* leaf, where pure error accumulates.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For designs that resist simple tree representation, the following Mermaid block renders the **functional pipeline of analysis** for a mixed nested-factorial design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepIn["RAW DATA y ijk l"]
    step1["COMPUTE TOTALS T i j k"]
    step2["CALCULATE CF CORRECTION FACTOR"]
    step3["SS A FACTOR A CROSSED"]
    step4["SS B of A NESTED FACTOR"]
    step5["SS C FACTOR C CROSSED"]
    step6["SS A x C CROSSED INTERACTION"]
    step7["SS B of A x C NESTED INTERACTION"]
    step8["SS ERROR REPLICATE VARIABILITY"]
    step9["ASSEMBLE ANOVA TABLE WITH df MS F"]
    step10["DECISION REJECT OR FAIL TO REJECT H0"]

    stepIn --> step1
    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step2 --> step4
    step2 --> step5
    step3 --> step6
    step5 --> step6
    step4 --> step7
    step5 --> step7
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step3 --> step9
    step4 --> step9
    step5 --> step9
    step6 --> step9
    step7 --> step9
    step8 --> step9
    step9 --> step10
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**

- `stepIn` — `step1` — `step2` form the *data conditioning* phase.
- `step3` — `step7` are the *parallel SS computation* branches, each starting from the correction factor.
- `step8` is computed as the *residual* (TSS minus all model SS).
- `step9` is the *integration* point: degrees of freedom, mean squares, and F-ratios are formed here.
- `step10` is the *inference* node where each $F$ is compared with the corresponding $F$-table critical value.

---

### 4.3 Decision-Tree for Choosing the Correct Denominator MS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qStart["WHICH EFFECT ARE YOU TESTING"]
    qA["TESTING A"]
    qC["TESTING C"]
    qAC["TESTING A x C"]
    qB["TESTING B of A"]
    qBC["TESTING B of A x C"]
    ansA["DENOMINATOR MS B of A"]
    ansC["DENOMINATOR MS B of A x C"]
    ansAC["DENOMINATOR MS B of A x C"]
    ansB["DENOMINATOR MS B of A x C"]
    ansBC["DENOMINATOR MS ERROR"]

    qStart --> qA
    qStart --> qC
    qStart --> qAC
    qStart --> qB
    qStart --> qBC
    qA --> ansA
    qC --> ansC
    qAC --> ansAC
    qB --> ansB
    qBC --> ansBC
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU exam short-cut:** Whenever a *nested* factor is in the numerator's lineage, the next *deeper* MS in the hierarchy (toward the Error) is the correct denominator.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between a "crossed" (factorial) factor and a "nested" factor in the context of experimental design. Give one industrial example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Crossed (Factorial) Factor | Nested Factor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Every level of the factor appears with *every* level of every other factor. | Each level of the factor appears *only* inside one level of another (parent) factor. |
| **Notation** | $A$, $B$, $A \times B$ | $B(A)$ |
| **Industrial example** | Two catalysts tested at three temperatures (every catalyst tested at every temperature). | Three operators at Plant-1 and three *different* operators at Plant-2; the operators at Plant-1 are *meaningless* at Plant-2. |
| **Effect estimability** | Main effect and crossed interactions are estimable. | The "main effect" of the nested factor is a *composite*; it is not comparable across parent levels. |

*(Marking: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark for the example pair.)*

---

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **In a design with factors $A$ (crossed, 3 levels), $B(A)$ (nested, 4 levels inside each $A$), $C$ (crossed, 2 levels), and $n = 3$ replicates, calculate the degrees of freedom for (i) $B(A)$ and (ii) $A \times C$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:** $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $c = 2$, $n = 3$.

**Step 2 — df of the nested factor $B(A)$:**

$$
\text{df}_{B(A)} \;=\; a(b - 1) \;=\; 3(4 - 1) \;=\; 9
$$

**Step 3 — df of the crossed interaction $A \times C$:**

$$
\text{df}_{A \times C} \;=\; (a - 1)(c - 1) \;=\; (3 - 1)(2 - 1) \;=\; 2
$$

**Answer:** $\text{df}_{B(A)} = 9$; $\text{df}_{A \times C} = 2$.

*(Marking: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for the final answer.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> Each Part B sub-question is **7 marks**. KTU ESE conventions: write the *full model*, the *complete ANOVA table*, and *interpret* the F-test conclusion in words.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> An experiment is conducted to study the effect of **two crossed catalysts** ($A$, 2 levels) and **three crossed temperatures** ($C$, 3 levels). Within each level of $A$, **two batches of raw material** ($B$, nested in $A$) are used. Each $(A, B, C)$ cell has **two replicates** ($n = 2$). The response $y$ is the percentage yield. Use the model
> $$y_{ijkl} = \mu + \alpha_i + \gamma_k + (\alpha\gamma)_{ik} + \beta_{j(i)} + (\beta\gamma)_{j(i)k} + \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}$$
> and the (synthetic) ANOVA table below. Answer both sub-parts.

**ANOVA Table (synthetic, KTU-style):**

| Source | SS | df | MS | F |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| $A$ | 420.50 | 1 | 420.50 | — |
| $B(A)$ | 88.40 | 2 | 44.20 | — |
| $C$ | 612.00 | 2 | 306.00 | — |
| $A \times C$ | 156.25 | 2 | 78.125 | — |
| $B(A) \times C$ | 64.00 | 4 | 16.00 | — |
| Error | 36.00 | 12 | 3.00 | — |
| Total | 1377.15 | 23 | — | — |

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**

> Set up the **correct F-tests** for the main effects of $A$, $C$ and the interaction $A \times C$. Compute the F-statistics, and state conclusions at $\alpha = 0.05$ given $F_{0.05, 1, 2} = 18.51$, $F_{0.05, 2, 4} = 6.94$, $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$.

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Test for $A$** — denominator = $MS_{B(A)}$ (because $B$ is nested in $A$).

$$
F_A \;=\; \frac{MS_A}{MS_{B(A)}} \;=\; \frac{420.50}{44.20} \;=\; 9.51
$$

Critical value $F_{0.05, 1, 2} = 18.51$. Since $9.51 < 18.51$, **fail to reject** $H_0$. The two catalysts do *not* differ significantly at the 5% level under this design.

- **Test for $C$** — denominator = $MS_{B(A) \times C}$.

$$
F_C \;=\; \frac{MS_C}{MS_{B(A) \times C}} \;=\; \frac{306.00}{16.00} \;=\; 19.125
$$

Critical value $F_{0.05, 2, 4} = 6.94$. Since $19.125 \gg 6.94$, **reject $H_0$**. The three temperatures differ significantly.

- **Test for $A \times C$** — denominator = $MS_{B(A) \times C}$.

$$
F_{A \times C} \;=\; \frac{MS_{A \times C}}{MS_{B(A) \times C}} \;=\; \frac{78.125}{16.00} \;=\; 4.88
$$

Critical value $F_{0.05, 2, 4} = 6.94$. Since $4.88 < 6.94$, **fail to reject $H_0$** at the 5% level. The interaction between catalyst and temperature is not significant at $\alpha = 0.05$.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**

> Estimate the **variance component** of the nested factor $B(A)$, and interpret the result physically.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — EMS of $B(A)$:**

$$
E[MS_{B(A)}] \;=\; \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma} + cn\sigma^2_{\beta}
$$

**Step 2 — EMS of $B(A) \times C$:**

$$
E[MS_{B(A) \times C}] \;=\; \sigma^2 + n\sigma^2_{\beta\gamma}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the simultaneous equations for $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta}$:**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta} \;=\; \frac{MS_{B(A)} - MS_{B(A) \times C}}{cn} \;=\; \frac{44.20 - 16.00}{3 \times 2} \;=\; \frac{28.20}{6} \;=\; 4.70
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta\gamma}$:**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta\gamma} \;=\; \frac{MS_{B(A) \times C} - MS_{\text{Error}}}{n} \;=\; \frac{16.00 - 3.00}{2} \;=\; 6.50
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\hat{\sigma}^2$:**

$$
\hat{\sigma}^2 \;=\; MS_{\text{Error}} \;=\; 3.00
$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation:**

The batch-to-batch variability inside each catalyst level is $\hat{\sigma}^2_{\beta} = 4.70$, which is *larger* than the replicate (pure) error $\hat{\sigma}^2 = 3.00$. This means that **about $\dfrac{4.70}{4.70 + 6.50 + 3.00} \approx 33\%$ of the residual response variability is attributable to batch differences within a catalyst**, suggesting that tighter batch control or better raw-material sourcing would substantially improve process yield.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper]`

> A textile engineer studies **two fabric types** (Factor $A$, 2 levels, crossed) and **three humidity levels** (Factor $C$, 3 levels, crossed). At each combination of $A$ and $C$, the engineer recruits **two different looms** nested within that fabric-type/region (Factor $B(A \times C)$, nested inside the $A \times C$ cell). Each loom is measured $n = 2$ times for tensile strength. **Answer both sub-parts.**

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:**

> Write the **full linear model**, identify the **degrees of freedom** of all six sources, and verify that the df sum to $N - 1$.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the model:**

$$
y_{ijkl} \;=\; \mu \;+\; \alpha_i \;+\; \gamma_k \;+\; (\alpha\gamma)_{ik} \;+\; \beta_{j(ik)} \;+\; \varepsilon_{l(ijk)}
$$

where the index $j(ik)$ denotes that the loom's identity is *specific* to the $(i,k)$ cell of $A \times C$.

**Step 2 — Parameters:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$ (looms per cell), $c = 3$, $n = 2$. Total $N = 2 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 24$.

**Step 3 — Compute df:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{df}_A &= a - 1 = 1 \\
\text{df}_C &= c - 1 = 2 \\
\text{df}_{A \times C} &= (a-1)(c-1) = 2 \\
\text{df}_{B(A \times C)} &= ac(b - 1) = 2 \times 3 \times 1 = 6 \\
\text{df}_{\text{Error}} &= abc(n-1) = 12 \times 1 = 12 \\
\text{df}_{\text{Total}} &= 24 - 1 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum check:** $1 + 2 + 2 + 6 + 12 = 23 \;\checkmark$.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:**

> Suppose the following synthetic ANOVA table is obtained. **Construct the appropriate F-tests** and conclude at $\alpha = 0.05$. Use critical values $F_{0.05, 1, 6} = 5.99$, $F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14$, $F_{0.05, 2, 12} = 3.89$, $F_{0.05, 6, 12} = 2.99$.

| Source | SS | df | MS |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| $A$ | 60.00 | 1 | 60.00 |
| $C$ | 240.00 | 2 | 120.00 |
| $A \times C$ | 30.00 | 2 | 15.00 |
| $B(A \times C)$ | 90.00 | 6 | 15.00 |
| Error | 36.00 | 12 | 3.00 |
| Total | 456.00 | 23 | — |

**Model Solution (b):**

- **Test for $A$:** denominator = $MS_{B(A \times C)} = 15.00$.

$$
F_A = \frac{60.00}{15.00} = 4.00 \quad < \quad F_{0.05, 1, 6} = 5.99
$$

→ **Fail to reject** $H_0$. Fabric type effect is not significant at 5%.

- **Test for $C$:** denominator = $MS_{B(A \times C)} = 15.00$.

$$
F_C = \frac{120.00}{15.00} = 8.00 \quad > \quad F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14
$$

→ **Reject $H_0$**. Humidity has a highly significant effect on tensile strength.

- **Test for $A \times C$:** denominator = $MS_{B(A \times C)} = 15.00$.

$$
F_{A \times C} = \frac{15.00}{15.00} = 1.00 \quad < \quad F_{0.05, 2, 6} = 5.14
$$

→ **Fail to reject** $H_0$. Fabric type does not interact with humidity.

> **Conclusion:** In this textile experiment, only **humidity** significantly affects tensile strength. Both fabric type and the fabric × humidity interaction are statistically negligible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers)**
>
> 1. **Wrong denominator MS for $A$:** Using $MS_{B(A) \times C}$ or $MS_{\text{Error}}$ instead of $MS_{B(A)}$ costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **df of nested factor written as $b - 1$** instead of $a(b-1)$ — a classic 1-mark error.
> 3. **Treating $B(A)$ as crossed with $A$** in the model equation (writing "$\beta_j$" without the $(i)$ subscript) — costs up to **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 4. **Failing to verify the df-sum** at the end of a derivation sub-part — a KTU examiner always scans for this; missing it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing EMS for the test of $C$:** $C$ is *crossed*, so its denominator *must* be the interaction of $C$ with the deepest available nested effect, not $MS_{B(A)}$.
> 6. **No statement of decision in words** ("reject" / "fail to reject" *and* what the effect means physically) — at least **1 mark** lost in every sub-part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Design with Both Nested and Factorial Factors**

- ☐ A **crossed** factor has its levels appearing in *every* level of its peers; a **nested** factor has its levels appearing in *only one* level of a parent factor.
- ☐ Notation: write the nested factor *in parentheses* of its parent, e.g., $B(A)$, $C(A \times B)$, $D(A \times B \times C)$.
- ☐ Degrees of freedom for a *nested* factor $B$ inside parent $A$ with $b$ levels is $a(b-1)$, **not** $b-1$.
- ☐ Degrees of freedom for a *nested* factor $B$ inside $A \times C$ is $ac(b-1)$.
- ☐ The model equation for $B$ nested in $A$ uses the subscript $j(i)$, **not** a separate index $j$ — this single character is a 1-marker in KTU papers.
- ☐ The **interaction $A \times B$ is non-existent** when $B$ is nested in $A$ — its conceptual part is absorbed in $B(A)$.
- ☐ **F-test denominators follow the EMS hierarchy** — test against the *next-lower* effect in the model, going down toward the Error MS.
- ☐ The EMS table for an $a \times b(a) \times c \times n$ random model is the single most valuable KTU Module 4 artefact — memorise the *shape* of every term.
- ☐ Variance components are obtained by *solving the EMS equations sequentially* — start from the Error line and work up.
- ☐ Always **randomise** the run order within the constraints of nesting (true randomisation if all factors are easy to change; restricted randomisation / split-plot if some are hard to change).
- ☐ Before writing the F-test, **check the df-sum** equals $N-1$ — a 30-second check that catches 90% of arithmetic slips.
- ☐ KTU 2024 scheme strongly emphasises the **decision statement in words** — never conclude with just a number.
- ☐ For unbalanced / staggered designs, KTU papers expect the explicit statement that *Type III SS* is the only valid decomposition.
- ☐ Industrial relevance: the design is the workhorse of *multi-plant*, *multi-operator*, *multi-batch* process investigations in manufacturing, pharma, and clinical trials.
